Code is available at https://github.com/QuantitativeImagingLaboratory/GeoStack.

</details>


### [103] [3D MRI Image Pretraining via Controllable 2D Slice Navigation Task](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06487)
*Yu Wang,Qingchao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces an action-conditioned SSL objective for MRI by turning 3D volumes into controllable 2D slice navigation sequences; a tokenizer + latent dynamics model predict feature evolution, outperforming static-volume baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of static-volume SSL by exploiting a controllable navigation signal that encodes 3D structure and spatial relations through continuous slice transformations.

Method: Render slices at continuous positions, orientations, and scales to create dense video-like sequences. A tokenizer encodes slice observations and a latent dynamics model predicts the evolution of latent features, conditioned on action trajectories. Pretraining is evaluated against static-volume baselines, tokenizer-only, and action-unaligned dynamics.

Result: Across representative anatomical and spatial tasks, the action-conditioned pretraining outperforms standard static-volume baselines, tokenizer-only pretraining, and dynamics variants without aligned actions.

Conclusion: Controllable MRI slice navigation provides a useful, complementary pretraining signal for learning anatomical and spatial representations from large unlabeled MRI collections.

Abstract: Self-supervised pretraining has become the mainstream approach for learning MRI representations from unlabeled scans. However, most existing objectives still treat each scan primarily as static aggregations of slices, patches or volumes. We ask whether there exists an intrinsic form of self-supervision signal that is different from reconstructing the masked patches, through transforming the 3D volumes into controllable 2D rendered sequences: by rendering slices at continuous positions, orientations, and scales, a 3D volume can be converted into dense video-action sequences whose controls are the action trajectories. We study this formulation with an action-conditioned pretraining objective, where a tokenizer encodes slice observations and a latent dynamics model predicts the evolution of latent features. Across representative anatomical and spatial downstream tasks, the proposed pretraining is evaluated against standard static-volume baselines, tokenizer-only pretraining, and dynamics variants without aligned actions. These results suggest that controllable MRI slice navigation provides a useful complementary pretraining interface for learning anatomical and spatial representations from large unlabeled MRI collections.

</details>


### [104] [MARBLE: Multi-Aspect Reward Balance for Diffusion RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06507)
*Canyu Zhao,Hao Chen,Yunze Tong,Yu Qiao,Jiacheng Li,Chunhua Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MARBLE proposes a gradient-space, multi-reward optimization framework for diffusion-model alignment, using per-reward advantage estimators and a Quadratic Program to synthesize a unified update without manual reward weighting; an amortized variant lowers backward-pass cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the difficulty of aligning diffusion models along multiple human-preference dimensions with naive weighted sums, which causes sample-level mismatch and suboptimal joint optimization.

Method: Maintain independent advantage estimators for each reward, compute per-reward policy gradients, and solve a Quadratic Program to obtain a single update direction. An amortized formulation exploits the affine structure of the DiffusionNFT loss to reduce per-step cost from K+1 backward passes to near baseline. EMA smoothing regularizes balancing coefficients for stability.

Result: On SD3.5 Medium with five rewards, MARBLE improves all reward dimensions simultaneously; worst-aligned reward gradient turns from negative (under weighted sum) to consistently positive in 80% of mini-batches; training runs at 0.97x the baseline speed.

Conclusion: MARBLE enables joint, weight-free optimization of multiple rewards for diffusion-model alignment, delivering simultaneous improvements across dimensions with near-baseline training efficiency.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning fine-tuning has become the dominant approach for aligning diffusion models with human preferences. However, assessing images is intrinsically a multi-dimensional task, and multiple evaluation criteria need to be optimized simultaneously. Existing practice deal with multiple rewards by training one specialist model per reward, optimizing a weighted-sum reward $R(x)=\sum_k w_k R_k(x)$, or sequentially fine-tuning with a hand-crafted stage schedule. These approaches either fail to produce a unified model that can be jointly trained on all rewards or necessitates heavy manually tuned sequential training. We find that the failure stems from using a naive weighted-sum reward aggregation. This approach suffers from a sample-level mismatch because most rollouts are specialist samples, highly informative for certain reward dimensions but irrelevant for others; consequently, weighted summation dilutes their supervision. To address this issue, we propose MARBLE (Multi-Aspect Reward BaLancE), a gradient-space optimization framework that maintains independent advantage estimators for each reward, computes per-reward policy gradients, and harmonizes them into a single update direction without manually-tuned reward weighting, by solving a Quadratic Programming problem. We further propose an amortized formulation that exploits the affine structure of the loss used in DiffusionNFT, to reduce the per-step cost from K+1 backward passes to near single-reward baseline cost, together with EMA smoothing on the balancing coefficients to stabilize updates against transient single-batch fluctuations. On SD3.5 Medium with five rewards, MARBLE improves all five reward dimensions simultaneously, turns the worst-aligned reward's gradient cosine from negative under weighted summation in 80% of mini-batches to consistently positive, and runs at 0.97X the training speed of baseline training.

</details>


### [105] [FreeSpec: Training-Free Long Video Generation via Singular-Spectrum Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06509)
*Fangda Chen,Shanshan Zhao,Longrong Yang,Chuanfu Xu,Zhigang Luo,Long Lan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FreeSpec offers a training-free spectral reconstruction approach for long-video generation that uses SVD to split global (low-rank) and local (high-rank) features, addressing spectral concentration caused by large attention windows to preserve both long-range consistency and rich dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-video diffusion models suffer from content drift, temporal inconsistency, and over-smoothed dynamics when extending to long sequences. Existing global/local decompositions rely on rigid, predefined criteria that fail when appearance and motion are tightly coupled; a spectral perspective suggests enlarged attention concentrates energy in a few low-rank directions, suppressing details and motion.

Method: Apply singular value decomposition to decompose global and local features. Treat the global branch as low-rank spectral guidance and the local branch as a high-rank reconstruction basis. Perform spectrum-level fusion in a training-free manner, avoiding explicit per-feature decomposition rules.

Result: On Wan2.1 and LTX-Video, FreeSpec improves long-video generation, notably temporal dynamics, while maintaining strong visual quality and temporal consistency, demonstrated via quantitative/qualitative results and a project demo.

Conclusion: Spectrum-level fusion via FreeSpec provides a flexible, training-free mechanism to preserve long-range consistency and retain spatial and temporal details, mitigating spectral concentration issues and improving long-video synthesis.

Abstract: Video diffusion models perform well in short-video synthesis, but their training-free extension to long videos often suffers from content drift, temporal inconsistency, and over-smoothed dynamics. Existing methods improve temporal consistency by combining a global branch with a local branch, but they often further decompose appearance consistency and temporal dynamics within each branch using predefined criteria. This assignment is unreliable when appearance and action progression are tightly coupled, such as in camera motion and sequential motion. We analyze the video temporal extension issue from a singular-spectrum perspective and show that enlarged self-attention windows induce spectral concentration: spectral energy becomes dominated by a few low-rank singular directions, preserving coarse structure but suppressing high-rank spatial details and motion-rich temporal variations. To mitigate this problem, we propose FreeSpec, a training-free spectral reconstruction framework for long-video generation. FreeSpec decomposes global and local features with singular value decomposition, and uses the global branch as low-rank spectral guidance and the local branch as a high-rank reconstruction basis. This spectrum-level fusion avoids the rigid feature partitioning of previous decomposition rules, preserving long-range consistency while better retaining spatial details and temporal dynamics. Experiments on Wan2.1 and LTX-Video demonstrate that FreeSpec improves long-video generation, especially for temporal dynamics, while maintaining strong visual quality and temporal consistency. Project demo: https://fdchen24.github.io/FreeSpec-Website/.

</details>


### [106] [DCR: Counterfactual Attractor Guidance for Rare Compositional Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06512)
*Taewon Kang,Matthias Zwicker*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free method (Default Completion Repulsion, DCR) suppresses default completion bias in diffusion models by creating a counterfactual attractor and a projection-based repulsion, improving rare-composition fidelity without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models frequently default to common semantic configurations when prompted with rare but plausible compositions (e.g., snowy beach, rainbow at night), due to implicit attractors in denoising trajectories. This default completion bias degrades compositional fidelity and existing guidance lacks explicit modeling of this competing tendency.

Method: DCR constructs a counterfactual attractor by relaxing the rare compositional factor while preserving surrounding semantics; defines counterfactual drift as the discrepancy between target and attractor trajectories; applies a projection-based repulsion to remove guidance components aligned with the drift during standard diffusion sampling, requiring no retraining or architectural changes.

Result: Empirical evaluations on rare compositional prompts show improved compositional fidelity without sacrificing visual quality; analysis indicates the framework also reveals and counteracts intrinsic model biases, offering a new angle on controllable generation beyond explicit constraint enforcement.

Conclusion: DCR provides a simple, training-free mechanism to counter default completion bias, enhancing rare-composition generation while preserving quality, and suggesting a broader viewpoint on leveraging counterfactuals to influence diffusion-based generation.

Abstract: Diffusion models generate realistic visual content, yet often fail to produce rare but plausible compositions. When prompted with combinations that are valid but underrepresented in training data, such as a snowy beach or a rainbow at night, the generation process frequently collapses toward more common alternatives. We identify this failure mode as default completion bias, where denoising trajectories are implicitly attracted toward high-frequency semantic configurations. Existing guidance mechanisms do not explicitly model this competing tendency and therefore struggle to prevent such collapse. We introduce Default Completion Repulsion (DCR), a training-free framework that explicitly models and suppresses default completion behavior. DCR constructs a counterfactual attractor by relaxing the rare compositional factor while preserving surrounding semantics, inducing an alternative denoising trajectory reflecting the model's preferred completion. We define the discrepancy between target and attractor trajectories as a counterfactual drift, and propose a projection-based repulsion mechanism that removes guidance components aligned with this drift direction. This suppresses undesired frequent completions while preserving other semantic components. DCR operates entirely within the standard diffusion sampling process without retraining or architectural modification. Experiments on rare compositional prompts show that DCR improves compositional fidelity while maintaining visual quality. Our analysis further shows that the framework exposes and counteracts intrinsic model biases, offering a new perspective on controllable generation beyond explicit constraint enforcement.

</details>


### [107] [Sparkle: Realizing Lively Instruction-Guided Video Background Replacement via Decoupled Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06535)
*Ziyun Zeng,Yiqi Lin,Guoqiang Liang,Mike Zheng Shou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Sparkle, a scalable foreground/background guidance pipeline and a ~140K video-pair dataset plus Sparkle-Bench benchmark for background replacement; decoupled guidance with strict filtering; yields significant performance gains over baselines and is open-sourced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-source editing datasets exist, but background replacement remains data-poor and quality-sensitive. Lack of precise background guidance in data synthesis leads to static, unnatural backgrounds (e.g., OpenVE-3M). A high-quality, scalable data pipeline and evaluation suite are needed to advance temporally consistent background synthesis for film/advertising.

Method: A decoupled foreground/background guidance generation pipeline with strict quality filtering. Construction of Sparkle (~140K video pairs) spanning five background-change themes. Introduction of Sparkle-Bench as a large, task-specific evaluation benchmark. Training/testing of models on Sparkle showing improved performance.

Result: Models trained on Sparkle surpass all existing baselines on OpenVE-Bench and Sparkle-Bench, demonstrating the dataset's quality and the effectiveness of decoupled guidance and filtering.

Conclusion: Sparkle addresses data quality gaps for background replacement, offering a scalable dataset, a robust benchmark, and a competitive model. All resources are open-sourced at the provided URL.

Abstract: In recent years, open-source efforts like Senorita-2M have propelled video editing toward natural language instruction. However, current publicly available datasets predominantly focus on local editing or style transfer, which largely preserve the original scene structure and are easier to scale. In contrast, Background Replacement, a task central to creative applications such as film production and advertising, requires synthesizing entirely new, temporally consistent scenes while maintaining accurate foreground-background interactions, making large-scale data generation significantly more challenging. Consequently, this complex task remains largely underexplored due to a scarcity of high-quality training data. This gap is evident in poorly performing state-of-the-art models, e.g., Kiwi-Edit, because the primary open-source dataset that contains this task, i.e., OpenVE-3M, frequently produces static, unnatural backgrounds. In this paper, we trace this quality degradation to a lack of precise background guidance during data synthesis. Accordingly, we design a scalable pipeline that generates foreground and background guidance in a decoupled manner with strict quality filtering. Building on this pipeline, we introduce Sparkle, a dataset of ~140K video pairs spanning five common background-change themes, alongside Sparkle-Bench, the largest evaluation benchmark tailored for background replacement to date. Experiments demonstrate that our dataset and the model trained on it achieve substantially better performance than all existing baselines on both OpenVE-Bench and Sparkle-Bench. Our proposed dataset, benchmark, and model are fully open-sourced at https://showlab.github.io/Sparkle/.

</details>


### [108] [MedHorizon: Towards Long-context Medical Video Understanding in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06537)
*Bodong Du,Bowen Liu,Yang Yu,Xinpeng Ding,Zhiheng Wu,Shuning Wang,Shuo Nie,Naiming Liu,Qifeng Chen,Yangqiu Song,Xiaomeng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MedHorizon is a long-context medical video benchmark for full-procedure understanding with sparse evidence; current MLLMs achieve only 41.1% accuracy, underscoring retrieval-before-reasoning challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in long medical video understanding where evidence is sparse and not pre-localized, evaluating retrieval and multi-hop clinical reasoning.

Method: Dataset comprises 759 hours of full-length clinical procedures and 1,253 evidence-grounded multiple-choice questions; evaluates representative general-domain, medical-domain, and long-video MLLMs; analyzes bottlenecks in evidence retrieval and interpretation.

Result: Best model achieves 41.1% accuracy; performance does not scale with more frames; bottlenecks lie in evidence retrieval and clinical interpretation, rooted in weak procedural reasoning and attention drift due to redundancy; generic sampling methods only partially balance local detail with global coverage.

Conclusion: MedHorizon provides a rigorous testbed for sparse-evidence retrieval and reasoning over complete clinical workflows, highlighting the need for stronger procedural reasoning and retrieval strategies in medical LLMs.

Abstract: Medical multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have advanced image understanding and short-video analysis, but real clinical review often requires full-procedure video understanding. Unlike general long videos, medical procedures contain highly redundant anatomical views, while decisive evidence is temporally sparse, spatially subtle, and context dependent. Existing benchmarks often assume this evidence has already been localized through images, short clips, or pre-segmented videos, leaving the retrieval-before-reasoning problem under-tested. We introduce MedHorizon, an in-the-wild benchmark for long-context medical video understanding. MedHorizon preserves 759 hours of full-length clinical procedures and provides 1,253 evidence-grounded multiple-choice questionsthat jointly evaluate sparse evidence understanding and multi-hop clinical reasoning. Its evidence is extremely sparse, with only 0.166% evidence frames on average, requiring models to search noisy procedural streams before interpreting and aggregating findings. We evaluate representative general-domain, medical-domain, and long-video MLLMs. The best model reaches only 41.1% accuracy, showing that current systems remain far from robust full-procedure understanding. Further analysis yields four key findings: performance does not scale reliably with more frames, evidence retrieval and clinical interpretation remain primary bottlenecks; these bottlenecks are rooted in weak procedural reasoning and attention drift under redundancy, and generic sampling methods only partially balances local detail with global coverage. MedHorizon provides a rigorous testbed for MLLMs that retrieve sparse evidence and reason over complete clinical workflows.

</details>


### [109] [Solving Minimal Problems Without Matrix Inversion Using FFT-Based Interpolation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06572)
*Haidong Wu,Snehal Bhayani,Janne Heikkilä*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A sampling-based, matrix-inversion-free solver for minimal problems in camera geometry using sparse hidden-variable resultants, with FFT-based reconstruction of the determinant and GCD-based robust submatrix identification to recover unknowns; competitive for small-scale problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome computational and numerical challenges of traditional Gröbner-basis and resultant-based solvers (notably matrix inversion and symbolic expansion) by using a sparse hidden-variable framework and numerical interpolation.

Method: Build solvers via sparse hidden-variable resultants. Reconstruct the determinant polynomial in the hidden variable by inverse FFT interpolation from sampled evaluations, avoiding symbolic expansion. Solve for the hidden variable, then recover other unknowns by exploiting rank-1 deficiency and applying Cramer's rule. Use a gcd-based criterion to identify robust submatrices under noise.

Result: Empirical evaluation on diverse minimal problems shows strong numerical stability and competitive runtime, especially for small-scale problems, demonstrating the method as a practical alternative to traditional solvers.

Conclusion: Proposes an inversion-free, sampling-based solver framework that yields stable, efficient solutions for minimal problems in camera geometry and broadens practical algorithmic options beyond Gröbner-basis and resultant-based methods.

Abstract: Estimating camera geometry typically involves solving minimal problems formulated as systems of multivariate polynomial equations, which often pose computational challenges when using existing Gröbner-basis or resultant-based methods due to matrix inversion needed in the online solver. Here we propose a sampling-based, matrix inversion-free method that constructs the solvers using sparse hidden-variable resultants. The determinant polynomial in the hidden variable is efficiently reconstructed via inverse fast Fourier transform interpolation from sampled evaluations, avoiding symbolic expansion. Solving this polynomial yields the hidden variable, and the remaining unknowns are recovered by identifying rank-1 deficient submatrices and applying Cramer's rule. A greatest common divisor-based criterion ensures robust submatrix identification under noise. Experiments on diverse minimal problems demonstrate that the proposed solver achieves strong numerical stability and competitive runtime, particularly for small-scale problems, providing a practical alternative to traditional Gröbner-basis and resultant-based solvers.

</details>


### [110] [DINORANKCLIP: DINOv3 Distillation and Injection for Vision-Language Pretraining with High-Order Ranking Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06592)
*Shuyang Jiang,Nan Yu,Yiming Zhang,Zenghui Ding,Zhenyu Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DINORANKCLIP: a high-order, dual-branch framework for CLIP that injects a frozen DINOv3 teacher and uses a high-order Plackett-Luce ranking to jointly address global pooling and ordering limitations, achieving state-of-the-art gains on fine-grained and OOD tasks; optimal order is R*=3; trained end-to-end on Conceptual Captions 3M.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CLIP's symmetric InfoNCE loses relative ordering among in-batch unmatched pairs and global pooling collapses local details; prior work partially addressed one weakness but not both.

Method: Introduce frozen DINOv3 teacher into contrastive trunk via a dual-branch lightweight student and multi-scale fusion with channel-spatial attention, self-attention refiner, and conflict-aware gate preserving first-order cross-modal alignment. Propose high-order Plackett-Luce ranking with attention-parameterised pairwise and tuple-wise terms; show that CLIP and RANKCLIP are zero- and first-order special cases; determine optimal order R*=3 across benchmarks; extensive empirical study on Conceptual Captions 3M including multiple ablations.

Result: DINORANKCLIP consistently outperforms CLIP, CyCLIP, ALIP, and RANKCLIP under matched compute; greatest gains in fine-grained and out-of-distribution tests; comprehensive experiments including order sweep, Fine-grained Probe on five datasets, Modality-Gap analysis, Fusion ablations, completed in 72 hours on an 8-GPU H100 node with CC3M.

Conclusion: Jointly addressing global pooling bottleneck and in-batch ordering with high-order ranking and teacher-guided fusion yields robust cross-modal representations; optimal order is 3; feasible training regime on large-scale CC3M; framework improves local structural reasoning in vision-language pretraining.

Abstract: Contrastive language-image pretraining (CLIP) suffers from two structural weaknesses: the symmetric InfoNCE loss discards the relative ordering among unmatched in-batch pairs, and global pooling collapses the visual representation into a semantic bottleneck that is poorly sensitive to fine-grained local structure. RANKCLIP partially addresses the first issue with a list-wise Plackett-Luce ranking-consistency loss, but its model is strictly first-order and inherits the second weakness untouched. We propose DINORANKCLIP, a pretraining framework that addresses both jointly. Our principal contribution is injecting a frozen DINOv3 teacher into the contrastive trunk through a dual-branch lightweight student and a multi-scale fusion module with channel-spatial attention, a self-attention refiner, and a conflict-aware gate that preserves the cross-modal alignment up to first order. Complementarily, we introduce a high-order Plackett-Luce ranking model in which the per-position utility is augmented with attention-parameterised pairwise and tuple-wise transition terms; the family contains CLIP and RANKCLIP as nested zero-order and first-order special cases, and the optimal order on every benchmark is $R^*=3$. The full empirical study -- order sweep, Fine-grained Probe on five datasets, four-node Modality-Gap analysis, six-variant Fusion ablation -- fits in 72 hours on a single eight-GPU H100 node and trains entirely on Conceptual Captions 3M. DINORANKCLIP consistently outperforms CLIP, CyCLIP, ALIP, and RANKCLIP under matched compute, with the largest relative gains on the fine-grained and out-of-distribution evaluations that most directly stress local structural reasoning.

</details>


### [111] [DPM++: Dynamic Masked Metric Learning for Occluded Person Re-identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06637)
*Lei Tan,Yingshi Luan,Pincong Zou,Pingyang Dai,Liujuan Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DPM++ introduces a dynamic masked metric learning framework for occluded person re-ID that uses input-adaptive masking, CLIP-based supervision, and saliency-guided occlusion synthesis to achieve robust, visibility-consistent matching under realistic occlusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Occlusion causes mismatch between occluded samples and holistic identity representations; global metric learning is unreliable; there is a lack of unified framework to handle realistic occlusion patterns.

Method: 1) input-adaptive masked metric selecting reliable identity subspaces; 2) CLIP-based two-stage supervision transferring text-based ID priors into classifier-prototype space; 3) saliency-guided patch transfer to synthesize realistic occlusions; 4) occlusion-aware sample pairing and mask-guided optimization.

Result: Empirical results show DPM++ consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on both occluded and holistic re-ID benchmarks.

Conclusion: DPM++ offers robust visibility-consistent matching under occlusion by combining dynamic masking, cross-modal priors from CLIP, and realistic occlusion synthesis; demonstrates strong performance gains.

Abstract: Although person re-identification has made impressive progress, occlusion caused by obstacles remains an unsettled issue in real applications. The difficulty lies in the mismatch between incomplete occluded samples and holistic identity representations. Severe occlusion removes discriminative body cues and introduces interference from background clutter and occluders, making global metric learning unreliable. Existing methods mainly rely on extra pre-trained models to estimate visible parts for alignment or construct occluded samples via data augmentation, but still lack a unified framework that learns robust visibility-consistent matching under realistic occlusion patterns. In this paper, we propose DPM++, a Dynamic Masked Metric Learning framework for occluded person re-identification. DPM++ learns an input-adaptive masked metric that dynamically selects reliable identity subspaces for each occluded instance, enabling matching to emphasize visibility-consistent evidence while suppressing unreliable components. Built upon the classifier-prototype space, DPM++ introduces a CLIP-based two-stage supervision scheme, where ID-level semantic priors are learned from the text branch and transferred into the classifier-prototype space for dynamic masked matching. To strengthen the masked metric, we introduce a saliency-guided patch transfer strategy to synthesize controllable and photo-realistic occluded samples during training. Exploiting real scene priors, this strategy exposes the model to realistic partial observations and provides richer supervision than random erasing. In addition, occlusion-aware sample pairing and mask-guided optimization improve the stability and effectiveness of the framework. Experiments on occluded and holistic person re-identification benchmarks show that DPM++ consistently outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods in both holistic and occlusion scenarios.

</details>


### [112] [Are We Making Progress in Multimodal Domain Generalization? A Comprehensive Benchmark Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06643)
*Hao Dong,Hongzhao Li,Shupan Li,Muhammad Haris Khan,Eleni Chatzi,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduce MMDG-Bench: a unified benchmark for Multimodal Domain Generalization across six datasets, six modality combinations, nine methods, and multiple evaluation settings, including corruption robustness and missing-modality generalization. Key results suggest modest gains over ERM, no consistent best method, notable upper-bound gaps, trimodal fusion not always superior, and widespread robustness/misclassification issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inconsistent evaluation protocols across MMDG hinder reliable progress. A standardized, comprehensive benchmark is needed that covers diverse tasks, modalities, and real-world challenges (corruptions, missing modalities, trustworthiness).

Method: MMDG-Bench standardizes evaluation across six datasets spanning action recognition, mechanical fault diagnosis, and sentiment analysis. It includes six modality combinations, nine representative methods, and multiple evaluation settings. It assesses accuracy, corruption robustness, missing-modality generalization, misclassification detection, and out-of-distribution detection. The study trains 7,402 neural networks across 95 cross-domain tasks.

Result: Five key findings: (1) under fair comparisons, recent specialized MMDG methods offer only marginal improvements over ERM baseline; (2) no single method consistently outperforms others across datasets or modality combinations; (3) a substantial gap to upper-bound performance persists; MMDG remains far from solved; (4) trimodal fusion does not consistently outperform the strongest bimodal configurations; (5) all evaluated methods degrade under corruption and missing-modality scenarios, with some methods compromising model trustworthiness.

Conclusion: MMDG-Bench provides a necessary standard for evaluating Multimodal Domain Generalization. The results indicate limited progress of specialized methods under standardized, robust evaluation, highlight dataset- and modality-specific variability, and reveal critical robustness and trustworthiness challenges that must be addressed for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Despite the growing popularity of Multimodal Domain Generalization (MMDG) for enhancing model robustness, it remains unclear whether reported performance gains reflect genuine algorithmic progress or are artifacts of inconsistent evaluation protocols. Current research is fragmented, with studies varying significantly across datasets, modality configurations, and experimental settings. Furthermore, existing benchmarks focus predominantly on action recognition, often neglecting critical real-world challenges such as input corruptions, missing modalities, and model trustworthiness. This lack of standardization obscures a reliable assessment of the field's advancement. To address this issue, we introduce MMDG-Bench, the first unified and comprehensive benchmark for MMDG, which standardizes evaluation across six datasets spanning three diverse tasks: action recognition, mechanical fault diagnosis, and sentiment analysis. MMDG-Bench encompasses six modality combinations, nine representative methods, and multiple evaluation settings. Beyond standard accuracy, it systematically assesses corruption robustness, missing-modality generalization, misclassification detection, and out-of-distribution detection. With 7, 402 neural networks trained in total across 95 unique cross-domain tasks, MMDG-Bench yields five key findings: (1) under fair comparisons, recent specialized MMDG methods offer only marginal improvements over ERM baseline; (2) no single method consistently outperforms others across datasets or modality combinations; (3) a substantial gap to upper-bound performance persists, indicating that MMDG remains far from solved; (4) trimodal fusion does not consistently outperform the strongest bimodal configurations; and (5) all evaluated methods exhibit significant degradation under corruption and missing-modality scenarios, with some methods further compromising model trustworthiness.

</details>


### [113] [Relit-LiVE: Relight Video by Jointly Learning Environment Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06658)
*Weiqing Xiao,Hong Li,Xiuyu Yang,Houyuan Chen,Wenyi Li,Tianqi Liu,Shaocong Xu,Chongjie Ye,Hao Zhao,Beibei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Relit-LiVE: a diffusion-based video relighting framework that uses raw reference images and joint environment-map prediction to achieve physically consistent, temporally stable relighting without known per-frame camera poses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intrinsic decomposition for neural video rendering is unreliable in real-world footage, leading to distorted appearances, material leakage, and temporal artifacts during relighting. A method is needed that preserves physical consistency without requiring per-frame camera poses.

Method: Introduce raw reference images into the rendering process to recover scene cues lost in intrinsic decomposition. Propose an environment video prediction formulation that jointly generates relit video and per-frame environment maps aligned to each camera viewpoint within a single diffusion process, enforcing geometric-illumination alignment and supporting dynamic lighting and camera motion.

Result: Relit-LiVE outperforms state-of-the-art video relighting and neural rendering methods on synthetic and real-world benchmarks, with improved physical consistency and temporal stability. The framework also enables downstream tasks such as scene-level rendering, material editing, object insertion, and streaming relighting.

Conclusion: Relit-LiVE provides physically consistent, temporally stable relighting under broad conditions without requiring explicit per-frame camera poses, and broadens applicability to various downstream tasks; the project is publicly available.

Abstract: Recent advances have shown that large-scale video diffusion models can be repurposed as neural renderers by first decomposing videos into intrinsic scene representations and then performing forward rendering under novel illumination. While promising, this paradigm fundamentally relies on accurate intrinsic decomposition, which remains highly unreliable for real-world videos and often leads to distorted appearances, broken materials, and accumulated temporal artifacts during relighting. In this work, we present Relit-LiVE, a novel video relighting framework that produces physically consistent, temporally stable results without requiring prior knowledge of camera pose. Our key insight is to explicitly introduce raw reference images into the rendering process, enabling the model to recover critical scene cues that are inevitably lost or corrupted in intrinsic representations. Furthermore, we propose a novel environment video prediction formulation that simultaneously generates relit videos and per-frame environment maps aligned with each camera viewpoint in a single diffusion process. This joint prediction enforces strong geometric-illumination alignment and naturally supports dynamic lighting and camera motion, significantly improving physical consistency in video relighting while easing the requirement of known per-frame camera pose. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Relit-LiVE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art video relighting and neural rendering methods across synthetic and real-world benchmarks. Beyond relighting, our framework naturally supports a wide range of downstream applications, including scene-level rendering, material editing, object insertion, and streaming video relighting. The Project is available at https://github.com/zhuxing0/Relit-LiVE.

</details>


### [114] [BAMI: Training-Free Bias Mitigation in GUI Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06664)
*Borui Zhang,Bo Zhang,Bo Wang,Wenzhao Zheng,Yuhao Cheng,Liang Tang,Yiqiang Yan,Jie Zhou,Jiwen Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Bias-Aware Manipulation Inference (BAMI) is a training-free method for GUI grounding that mitigates two identified error sources—precision bias from high image resolution and ambiguity bias from complex interface elements—by coarse-to-fine focus and candidate selection. It yields improved accuracy on the ScreenSpot-Pro benchmark (e.g., TianXi-Action-7B from 51.9% to 57.8%), with robust ablations and a public code release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GUI grounding models struggle in complex interfaces and high-resolution images due to precision and ambiguity biases. There is a need for effective, training-free bias mitigation to improve task execution (clicking, dragging) on benchmarks like ScreenSpot-Pro.

Method: Introduce MPD attribution to diagnose error biases, then propose Bias-Aware Manipulation Inference (BAMI) featuring two manipulations: (1) coarse-to-fine focus to address precision bias from high-resolution imagery, and (2) candidate selection to mitigate ambiguity bias from dense interface elements. All training-free, with evaluation on GUI grounding benchmarks.

Result: Empirical results show substantial accuracy gains on GUI grounding models in a training-free setting; notably, TianXi-Action-7B improves from 51.9% to 57.8% on ScreenSpot-Pro. Ablation studies indicate robustness across parameter configurations, suggesting stability and effectiveness of BAMI.

Conclusion: BAMI effectively mitigates image-resolution and interface-element biases, enhancing GUI grounding accuracy without additional training; code is available at the provided GitHub repository.

Abstract: GUI grounding is a critical capability for enabling GUI agents to execute tasks such as clicking and dragging. However, in complex scenarios like the ScreenSpot-Pro benchmark, existing models often suffer from suboptimal performance. Utilizing the proposed \textbf{Masked Prediction Distribution (MPD)} attribution method, we identify that the primary sources of errors are twofold: high image resolution (leading to precision bias) and intricate interface elements (resulting in ambiguity bias). To address these challenges, we introduce \textbf{Bias-Aware Manipulation Inference (BAMI)}, which incorporates two key manipulations, coarse-to-fine focus and candidate selection, to effectively mitigate these biases. Our extensive experimental results demonstrate that BAMI significantly enhances the accuracy of various GUI grounding models in a training-free setting. For instance, applying our method to the TianXi-Action-7B model boosts its accuracy on the ScreenSpot-Pro benchmark from 51.9\% to 57.8\%. Furthermore, ablation studies confirm the robustness of the BAMI approach across diverse parameter configurations, highlighting its stability and effectiveness. Code is available at https://github.com/Neur-IO/BAMI.

</details>


### [115] [ActCam: Zero-Shot Joint Camera and 3D Motion Control for Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06667)
*Omar El Khalifi,Thomas Rossi,Oscar Fossey,Thibault Fouque,Ulysse Mizrahi,Philip Torr,Ivan Laptev,Fabio Pizzati,Baptiste Bellot-Gurlet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ActCam is a zero-shot video-generation method that jointly controls actor motion and camera pose by conditioning a pretrained diffusion model on pose and depth, with a two-phase conditioning schedule. It achieves geometrically consistent pose/depth, resulting in improved camera adherence and motion fidelity without training, especially under large viewpoint changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for fine-grained, joint control of character motion and cinematography in video generation; ability to transfer motion into new scenes and manipulate camera parameters in a zero-shot setting; ensure geometric consistency while enabling high-frequency detail.

Method: Leverages any pretrained image-to-video diffusion model that accepts depth and pose conditioning. Given a source video and target camera motion, compute pose and sparse depth to maintain geometry; perform a single sampling with two-phase conditioning: early denoising uses both pose and depth to enforce structure; later denoising uses pose only to refine details without over-constraining; no training required.

Result: Evaluations across benchmarks show ActCam outperforms pose-only and other pose+camera methods in camera adherence and motion fidelity; human evaluators prefer ActCam, especially with large viewpoint changes.

Conclusion: ActCam demonstrates that careful camera-consistent conditioning with staged guidance enables strong joint camera and motion control in video generation without model training; suggests potential for artistic applications and broader use with diffusion-based video models.

Abstract: For artistic applications, video generation requires fine-grained control over both performance and cinematography, i.e., the actor's motion and the camera trajectory. We present ActCam, a zero-shot method for video generation that jointly transfers character motion from a driving video into a new scene and enables per-frame control of intrinsic and extrinsic camera parameters. ActCam builds on any pretrained image-to-video diffusion model that accepts conditioning in terms of scene depth and character pose. Given a source video with a moving character and a target camera motion, ActCam generates pose and depth conditions that remain geometrically consistent across frames. We then run a single sampling process with a two-phase conditioning schedule: early denoising steps condition on both pose and sparse depth to enforce scene structure, after which depth is dropped and pose-only guidance refines high-frequency details without over-constraining the generation. We evaluate ActCam on multiple benchmarks spanning diverse character motions and challenging viewpoint changes. We find that, compared to pose-only control and other pose and camera methods, ActCam improves camera adherence and motion fidelity, and is preferred in human evaluations, especially under large viewpoint changes. Our results highlight that careful camera-consistent conditioning and staged guidance can enable strong joint camera and motion control without training. Project page: https://elkhomar.github.io/actcam/.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [116] [Scalable GPU Construction of 3D Voronoi and Power Diagrams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06408)
*Bernardo Taveira,Carl Lindström,Maryam Fatemi,Lars Hammarstrand,Fredrik Kahl*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: GPU-based, highly parallel algorithm for large-scale 3D Voronoi and power diagrams using progressive clipping of initial cells, with directional bounds culling and BV-based neighbor search; scalable to heterogeneous data and competitive with Delaunay methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Voronoi construction for large point sets is computationally expensive. CPU-based Delaunay tetrahedralization is slow for large diagrams; existing GPU approaches struggle with scale or assume homogeneous distributions. Power diagrams (weighted Voronoi) are under-explored. A general, scalable method is needed for large, diverse datasets relevant to mesh-based neural rendering.

Method: For each weighted 3D point, construct its Voronoi cell by progressively clipping an initial cell volume with bisecting planes induced by candidate neighboring points. Efficient candidate neighbor identification via a culling criterion based on directional geometric bounds of the evolving cell, combined with a hierarchical best-first traversal of bounding volumes.

Result: The method achieves performance on par with state-of-the-art Delaunay tetrahedralization on small to moderate problems, and robust scalability to large point sets with diverse spatial distributions. It naturally generalizes to power diagrams without additional assumptions.

Conclusion: A highly parallel GPU algorithm for fast construction of large-scale 3D Voronoi and power diagrams, offering scalable performance for heterogeneous data and practical applicability in large-scale applications such as mesh-based neural rendering.

Abstract: Voronoi diagrams, and their more general weighted counterpart, power diagrams, are fundamental geometric constructs with wide-ranging applications. Recently, they have gained renewed attention in mesh-based neural rendering. Despite being extensively studied, the construction of 3D Voronoi diagrams for large-scale point sets remains computationally expensive, limiting their adoption in large-scale applications. Existing CPU-based approaches typically rely on computing its dual, the Delaunay tetrahedralization, but are prohibitively slow for large diagrams, while GPU-based methods either struggle to scale efficiently to large point sets or assume homogeneous point distributions. The weighted case, power diagrams, is even less explored in this context. Existing approaches are typically tailored to the application at hand, assuming homogeneous point distributions and small weight variations, making them unsuitable for general use in more complex heterogeneous data.
  In this paper, we present a highly parallelizable GPU algorithm for the fast construction of large-scale 3D Voronoi and power diagrams. Our approach constructs each convex cell from a weighted 3D point by progressively clipping an initial cell volume against bisecting planes induced by candidate neighboring points. To efficiently identify candidate neighbors under arbitrary spatial distributions, we introduce a culling criterion based on directional geometric bounds of the evolving cell, combined with a hierarchical best-first traversal of bounding volumes.
  We achieve performance on par with state-of-the-art Delaunay tetrahedralization methods on small and moderate problem sizes, while exhibiting robust scalability to large point sets and diverse spatial distributions. Moreover, our method naturally generalizes to power diagrams without additional assumptions. See https://research.zenseact.com/publications/paragram .

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [117] [A GPU-Accelerated Hybrid Method for a Class of Multi-Depot Vehicle Routing Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05208)
*Zhenyu Lei,Jin-Kao Hao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid MDVRP solver combining learning-driven diversity-controlled route-exchange crossover and multi-depot feasible/infeasible search with multi-penalty guidance; enhanced GPU-accelerated version with multi-move updates; shows strong performance on three MDVRP variants, especially for large-scale instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MDVRP is widespread but computationally hard due to combinatorial complexity; efficient, scalable algorithms exploiting depot structure and parallel hardware are needed.

Method: A hybrid algorithm with (1) a learning-driven, diversity-controlled route-exchange crossover; (2) a multi-depot-supported feasible/infeasible search guided by a multi-penalty evaluation function; (3) two depot-related local search operators; (4) an enhanced GPU-accelerated version using a tensor-based approach and a novel multi-move update strategy.

Result: Extensive computational experiments on benchmark instances of three MDVRP variants show the proposed algorithms are highly competitive with state-of-the-art methods, especially for large-scale instances.

Conclusion: The framework effectively solves MDVRPs with improved scalability and competitive performance; GPU acceleration and depot-tailored operators enhance efficiency, making it suitable for large-scale multi-depot logistics problems.

Abstract: Multi-depot vehicle routing problems (MDVRPs) are prevalent in a variety of practical applications. However, they are computationally challenging to solve due to their inherent complexity. This paper proposes an effective hybrid algorithm for a class of MDVRPs. The algorithm integrates a learning-driven, diversity-controlled route-exchange crossover and a multi-depot-supported feasible-and-infeasible search framework guided by a multi-penalty evaluation function. Two dedicated depot-related local search operators are incorporated to further strengthen the search capability in multi-depot settings. To improve computational efficiency and scalability, an enhanced version of the algorithm is developed that uses a tensor-based GPU acceleration combined with a novel multi-move update strategy. Extensive computational experiments on benchmark instances of three MDVRP variants show that the proposed algorithms are highly competitive with state-of-the-art methods, especially for large-scale instances.

</details>


### [118] [Topology-Driven Anti-Entanglement Control for Soft Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05236)
*Haoyang Le,Shengxuan Wang,Mohan Chen,Shuo Feng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Topology-driven MARL for anti-winding control of soft robots in constrained settings improves convergence and entanglement avoidance, outperforming state-of-the-art DRL in simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses coordination and observability challenges in multi-robot unwinding within highly constrained environments; traditional distributed RL suffers from instability and poor learning due to high-density obstacles and complex interactions.

Method: Proposes TD-MARL with centralized learning in the critical network; robots share topological state to infer others' strategies while executing in a decentralized manner; integrates a topological security layer based on topological invariants to assess and mitigate entanglement risk.

Result: Simulation experiments show superior convergence and anti-winding performance compared to current advanced DRL methods.

Conclusion: Topology-driven coordination with a centralized critic and topological invariants enhances reliability and performance in constrained multi-robot unwinding tasks, reducing communication needs and improving stability.

Abstract: In the field of precision manufacturing in complex constrained environments, the role of soft robots is increasingly prominent, and the realization of anti-winding control based on multi-intelligent body reinforcement learning has become a research hotspot. One of the core problems at present is to coordinate multiple robots to complete the unwinding operation in a highly constrained environment. The existing distributed training framework faces some observability challenges in high-density barrier and unstable environments, resulting in poor learning results. This paper proposes a topology-driven Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (TD-MARL) framework to coordinate multi-robot systems to avoid entanglement. Specifically, the critical network adopts centralized learning, so that each intelligent body can perceive the strategies of other intelligent bodies by sharing the topological state, thus alleviating the training instability caused by complex interactions; eliminating the demand for communication resources between robots through distributed execution, Upgrade system reliability; the integrated topological security layer uses topological invariants to accurately assess and mitigate the risk of entanglement to avoid the strategy from falling into local difficulties. Finally, the full simulation experiments carried out in the real simulation environment show that the method is better than the current advanced deep reinforcement learning (DRL) method in terms of convergence and anti-winding effect.

</details>


### [119] [DexSim2Real: Foundation Model-Guided Sim-to-Real Transfer for Generalizable Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05241)
*Zijian Zeng,Fei Ding,Huiming Yang,Xianwei Li,Yuhao Liao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexSim2Real combines FM-DR, TVCAP, and PSC to bridge sim-to-real for dexterous manipulation via vision-language guidance, cross-modal attention, and LLM-driven curricula, achieving 78.2% real-world success and reducing the gap to 8.3%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limited generalizability of sim-to-real methods that rely on manual domain randomization or task-specific adaptation.

Method: 1) FM-DR uses a vision-language foundation model as a visual realism critic to optimize simulation parameters via closed-loop CMA-ES, complementing text-based approaches like DrEureka with direct visual feedback. 2) TVCAP adapts cross-attention visuo-tactile fusion to zero-shot sim-to-real RL. 3) PSC builds on LLM-based task decomposition with a difficulty scheduler tailored to contact-rich dexterous tasks.

Result: On six manipulation tasks with blinded evaluation, DexSim2Real achieves a 78.2% average real-world success rate and reduces the sim-to-real performance gap to 8.3%, outperforming DrEureka and DeXtreme.

Conclusion: The integrated framework demonstrates strong generalization across diverse dexterous tasks and effective bridging of the sim-to-real gap, leveraging vision-language guidance, tactile-vision fusion, and curriculum learning.

Abstract: Sim-to-real transfer remains a critical bottleneck for deploying dexterous manipulation policies learned in simulation to real-world robots. Existing approaches rely on manually designed domain randomization or task-specific adaptation, limiting their generalizability across diverse manipulation scenarios. We present DexSim2Real, an integrated framework that leverages vision-language foundation models to bridge the sim-to-real gap for dexterous manipulation. Our system combines three components: (1) Foundation Model-Guided Domain Randomization (FM-DR), which uses a vision-language model as a visual realism critic to optimize simulation parameters via closed-loop CMA-ES, complementing text-based approaches like DrEureka with direct visual feedback; (2) a Tactile-Visual Cross-Attention Policy (TVCAP) that adapts cross-attention visuo-tactile fusion to zero-shot sim-to-real RL; and (3) a Progressive Skill Curriculum (PSC) that builds on LLM-based task decomposition with a difficulty scheduler tailored to contact-rich dexterous tasks. Extensive experiments on six challenging manipulation tasks with blinded evaluation demonstrate that DexSim2Real achieves a 78.2% average real-world success rate, outperforming DrEureka and DeXtreme while reducing the sim-to-real performance gap to only 8.3%.

</details>


### [120] [Track A*: Fast Visibility-Aware Trajectory Planning for Active Target Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05338)
*Hanxuan Chen,Kangli Wang,Ji Pei*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TA star is an offline, search-based trajectory planner for visibility-aware active target tracking on a discretized 4D grid, using a layered DAG with cross-time BVH caching, per-layer beam pruning, and a multi-ray visibility evaluator. It achieves substantial speedups and near-baseline visibility at scale, sacrificing strict optimality for practicality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide repeatable, offline reference trajectories to benchmark online tracking planners and to build multi-modal datasets, enabling controlled comparisons and performance analyses.

Method: Formulates planning on a discretized spatio-temporal grid (x, y, z, t) as a layered DAG; employs cross-time obstacle distance caching via BVH, per-layer beam pruning, and a configurable multi-ray visibility evaluator; uses a beam-pruned heuristic search to produce high-quality trajectories; parallelized (32 workers) to improve throughput.

Result: On 1000-scenario stress test across eight CARLA Optimized maps: TA star converges in 45 s with 32 workers. In a 248-scenario comparison against an unoptimized BinaryHeap A*, it reduces mean planning time by 23x and worst-case by 11.8x, with convergence improving from 56.9% to 100%. On a 141-baseline-converged subset, average visibility changes by -0.15 percentage points, with no scenario dropping more than 5 pp. The planner is robust but has known limitations in challenging environments like dense vegetation (Town07).

Conclusion: TA star is a practical offline reference planner that delivers substantial speedups and near-baseline visibility for large-scale offline trajectory planning, accepting relaxed optimality for scalability and detailing failure cases in difficult environments.

Abstract: Offline reference trajectories for active target tracking are needed both for building multi-modal tracking datasets and for benchmarking online tracking planners under repeatable conditions. We present Track A star (TA star), an offline search-based trajectory planner that targets the visibility-aware target tracking objective on a discretized four-dimensional spatio-temporal grid (x, y, z, t). TA star combines a layered Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) search with three engineering optimizations: cross-time obstacle distance caching against a Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH), per-layer beam pruning, and a configurable multi-ray visibility evaluator. TA star employs a beam-pruned heuristic search on this discrete graph to efficiently find high-quality tracking trajectories. While it trades strict theoretical optimality for practical scalability, our empirical results demonstrate robust, near-baseline visibility performance at a fraction of the computational cost. On a 1000-scenario stress test across eight CARLA Optimized maps, TA star converges on all scenarios and completes in 45 s using 32 workers; on a 248-scenario controlled comparison against an unoptimized priority-queue A star baseline (BinaryHeap implementation) under identical scenario inputs and a 5 x 10^6 expansion cap, TA star reduces mean planning time by 23.0x and worst-case planning time by 11.8x, while raising convergence from 56.9% to 100%. On the n=141 baseline-converged subset, TA star changes average visibility by only -0.15 percentage points (pp), with no scenario exceeding a 5 pp drop. We position TA star as a practical offline reference planner under these specific conditions, with limitations and failure cases discussed for environments such as Town07 dense vegetation.

</details>


### [121] [Passive Fault Tolerance through Tension-to-Thrust Feed-Forward: Hybrid Input-to-State Stability for Decentralized Multi-UAV Slung-Load Transport under Abrupt Cable Severance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05339)
*Hadi Hajieghrary,Paul Schmitt*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A decentralized passive recovery mechanism for multi-UAV slung-load with abrupt severance, using local tension feed-forward into altitude thrust plus a PD anti-swing and cascade projection to maintain feasibility. It provides a hybrid practical input-to-state-stability recovery envelope and is validated in Drake with five UAVs showing robust recovery; disabling the feed-forward degrades performance, highlighting its central role.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address abrupt cable severance in multi-UAV slung-load transport, which redistributes load and alters the active constraint set, leaving little time for fault diagnosis and reconfiguration. Existing setups rely on centralized coordination or fixed topology and lack a certified decentralized recovery mechanism for unannounced severance.

Method: A passive architecture routes each vehicle’s measured cable tension directly into its altitude thrust command (T_i^ff = T_i), complemented by a surrounding PD-based anti-swing controller and a projection cascade to preserve local tracking feasibility. The main theoretical contribution is a conditional hybrid practical input-to-state-stability (ISS) certificate that combines: slack-excursion-bounded taut-cable reduction, bounded post-severance Lyapunov jumps, inter-fault decay, and per-fault-cycle contraction ρ in (0,1) into an explicit recovery envelope under actuator, slack, and dwell assumptions.

Result: Validated in Drake multibody simulation with five vehicles, a 10 kg payload, Kelvin-Voigt cables, Dryden wind, and both single- and dual-severance scenarios. The closed-loop achieves 0.312–0.328 m RMSE, 76.1–95.2 mm peak sag, and recovers within one payload-pendulum period. When the identity component is disabled, cruise error increases by 34–39% and peak sag by 3.6x–4.0x, indicating the local tension feed-forward is the dominant passive recovery mechanism in the proposed decentralized cascade.

Conclusion: The work delivers a certified decentralized recovery scheme for unannounced severance in a multi-UAV slung-load setting. It demonstrates that local tension feed-forward, embedded in a passive architecture with a hybrid ISS framework, yields robust performance and a bounded recovery envelope under practical actuator and dwell constraints; disabling this mechanism substantially degrades performance, underscoring its central role.

Abstract: Abrupt cable severance in multi-UAV slung-load transport redistributes load and changes the active constraint set, leaving limited time for fault diagnosis and reconfiguration. Existing controllers rely on coordinated force allocation, peer-state exchange, or fixed cable topology, and therefore lack a certified decentralized recovery mechanism for unannounced severance. We present a passive architecture that routes each vehicle's measured cable tension directly into its altitude thrust command, $T_i^{\mathrm{ff}}=T_i$, while a surrounding proportional-derivative, anti-swing, and projection cascade preserves local tracking feasibility. The main contribution is a conditional hybrid practical input-to-state-stability certificate that composes a slack-excursion-bounded taut-cable reduction, bounded post-severance Lyapunov jumps, inter-fault decay, and per-fault-cycle contraction $ρ\in (0,1)$ into an explicit recovery envelope under stated actuator, slack, and dwell assumptions. We validate the controller in Drake multibody simulation with five vehicles, a 10 kg payload, Kelvin-Voigt cables, Dryden wind, and single- and dual-severance schedules: the closed loop attains 0.312-0.328 m RMSE, 76.1-95.2 mm peak sag, and recovery within one payload-pendulum period. Disabling the identity inflates cruise error by 34-39% and peak sag by 3.6x-4.0x, identifying local tension feed-forward as the dominant passive recovery mechanism in the tested decentralized cascade.

</details>


### [122] [Creative Robot Tool Use by Counterfactual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05411)
*M. Tuluhan Akbulut,Varun Satheesh,Ahmed Jaafar,Alper Ahmetoglu,Shane Parr,Aditya Ganeshan,Shivam Vats,George Konidaris*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A causal reasoning framework for creative robot tool use grounded in physics, enabling tool selection and skill transfer to novel objects via simulated dynamics, VLM-based feature suggestion, and counterfactual perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots must use tools beyond their primary objectives and adapt to novel objects; grounding tool use in causal features and physics improves generalization and reliability.

Method: Decouple causal discovery into two components: (1) VLM-based feature suggestion, (2) counterfactual tool generation via targeted geometric and physical perturbations in a dynamics model. Classify novel objects by identified causal features; transfer tool-use skill via keypoint matching conditioned on these features; reconstruct tasks in dynamics model to ground physics; demonstrate on tasks like reaching distant objects with sticks, scooping candies with various items, and using boxes as stepping platforms; compare to baselines.

Result: Identifying causal features and grounding them in physical tool properties leads to more reliable tool selection and stronger keypoint transfer, outperforming baselines on the presented tasks.

Conclusion: Grounding tool use in causal features and physics-based properties enhances generalization to novel objects and tasks, enabling more robust and adaptable tool-use strategies.

Abstract: We propose a causal reasoning framework for creative robot tool use where a suitable tool for a task is correctly identified for use beyond its primary objectives. The proposed framework first discovers the causal relationships between the tool and the task by conducting simulated experiments in a dynamics model. We decouple the causal discovery problem into two complementary components: VLM-based feature suggestion and counterfactual tool generation via targeted geometric and physical feature perturbations. Then, novel objects are classified based on identified causal features, and the tool use skill is transferred via keypoint matching conditioned on the identified causal features. By reconstructing the task in a dynamics model, our approach grounds tool use in the physics of the problem. We illustrate our approach in reaching a distant object with different sticks, scooping candies from a bowl using diverse items, and using different boxes or crates as stepping platforms to retrieve an object from a high shelf. Our baseline comparisons show that identifying causal features and grounding them in physical tool properties leads to more reliable tool selection and stronger skill keypoint transfer.

</details>


### [123] [Contact-Free Grasp Stability Prediction with In-Hand Time-of-Flight Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05461)
*Kyle DuFrene,Cindy Grimm*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Contact-free grasp stability predictor using multi-zone time-of-flight sensors on a gripper; does not require grasping to predict stability; achieves ~85.5-86% accuracy on validation/test with 2,500 grasps across 15 objects; fast at 15 Hz; generalizes to unseen objects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve grasp stability assessment speed and robustness by eliminating the need to physically grasp the object. Reduce dependency on tactile contact for stability prediction and handle noisy sensing.

Method: Deploy multi-zone time-of-flight sensors on distal links of a gripper to collect contact-free sensory data. Train a classifier on >2,500 real-world grasps across 15 objects; evaluate on six unseen objects (3 for validation/model selection, 3 for testing). Classify stability at 15 Hz without grasp execution.

Result: Validation accuracy 85.5%. Test accuracy 86.0% on unseen objects. Demonstrates competitive performance and a significant speedup by avoiding grasp attempts for stability assessment.

Conclusion: A viable, faster alternative to tactile-based stability classifiers that require grasping; demonstrates generalization to unseen objects and real-time operation, potentially enabling faster planning and execution in grasping pipelines.

Abstract: Current approaches to grasp planning for robotics demonstrate high success rates, but degrade with noisy sensors and other factors. Previous works have proposed tactile-based grasp stability classifiers to detect failures, but these approaches rely on making contact and grasping the object to do so. We propose a contact-free grasp stability predictor using multi-zone time-of-flight sensors mounted in the distal links of a gripper. Our method, as it does not require grasping the object to make a prediction, significantly speeds up the stability classification process, cycling at 15 Hz. We collected over 2,500 real-world grasps across 15 objects to train a classifier. Additionally, we conducted grasp attempts over six additional unseen objects, three for validation and model selection, and three for model testing. Our approach demonstrated strong classification performance, with an accuracy of 85.5% on validation and 86.0% on test objects.

</details>


### [124] [Robust $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Controller Design For INDI-Controlled Quadrotor Using Online Parameter Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05483)
*Tom Aantjes,Till M. Blaha,Spilios Theodoulis,Ewoud J. J. Smeur*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A robust, gain-scheduled INDI-based attitude controller for quadrotors with online parameter identification is developed, achieving stable, good-tracking performance under uncertainty, validated by simulations and real flight tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work can identify INDI parameters online quickly, but there is no robust method to tune outer-loop gains as model parameters vary. A robust, gain-scheduled outer loop is needed for reliable performance.

Method: Design of a gain-scheduled cascaded attitude controller with an INDI-based inner loop and online system-parameter identification. The outer loop uses signal-based H-infinity closed-loop shaping to synthesize robust gains for a symmetric quadrotor, with a feedforward filter incorporated.

Result: The controller provides good stability margins. Nonlinear simulations show effective tracking under parameter uncertainty. Flight tests with full online parameter identification demonstrate acceptable performance even when identified parameters lie outside the nominal uncertainty, provided actuator time constants are below 40 ms.

Conclusion: A robust, gain-scheduled INDI-based attitude control framework with online parameter identification is viable for quadrotors, offering strong stability margins and good tracking under uncertainty, validated in both simulation and flight experiments.

Abstract: It has recently been shown that all physical parameters of an Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) controller can be estimated onboard a multirotor within half a second, which is fast enough to do the full identification during a throw in the air. However, a robust method to tune outer loop gains for this feedback-linearizing INDI controller depending on the model parameters is still missing. This work presents the design of a robust gain-scheduled controller for attitude control of quadrotor, using an INDI-based inner loop with online identification of its system parameters. A gain-scheduled cascaded attitude controller with a feedforward filter is synthesized for a symmetric quadrotor using signal-based $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ closed-loop shaping. The resulting controller exhibits good stability margins, with nonlinear simulations confirming effective tracking performance under uncertainty. Experimental evaluation is also conducted through flight tests with full online parameter identification. Even though the identified parameters during these tests are far outside the defined uncertainty range, acceptable flight performance comparable to simulation results is maintained for actuator time constants below 40 ms.

</details>


### [125] [Real-world Latency Analysis of Vehicular Visible Light Communication with Multiple LED Transmitters and an Event-Based Camera](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05541)
*Ryota Soga,Tsukasa Shimizu,Shintaro Shiba,Quan Kong,Shan Lu,Takaya Yamazato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Event-camera-based VLC for V2X enables low-latency, high-dynamic-range reception with positive-event-only operation, multi-transmitter detection (up to 3 LEDs), and end-to-end latency assessment in real vehicles, suggesting it can complement RF V2X.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address ultra-low latency perception needs in V2X and alleviate bandwidth saturation while handling multiple transmitters and maintaining wide FOV with event cameras.

Method: Adopt a positive-event-only mode; design a protocol to suppress event generation without sacrificing communication distance/FOV; develop a method to identify multiple transmitters; demonstrate simultaneous reception from up to three LEDs; measure end-to-end latency in real vehicular scenarios.

Result: Demonstrated simultaneous reception from up to three LEDs with maintained distance and wide FOV; end-to-end latency meets cooperative perception requirements in real-world driving scenarios; supports feasibility of event-camera VLC as a V2X complement.

Conclusion: Event-camera VLC appears to be a viable complement to RF-based V2X, offering low latency and wide dynamic range; future work could scale to more transmitters, enhance robustness, and integrate with existing V2X protocols.

Abstract: Event cameras offer high temporal resolution, low latency, and wide dynamic range, making them promising receivers for visible light communication (VLC) in vehicle-to-everything (V2X) applications. This work presents an event-camera-based VLC system addressing three key challenges: bandwidth saturation, multi-transmitter reception, and latency characterization.
  We adopt a positive-event-only mode and design a protocol that suppresses event generation while maintaining communication distance and a wide field of view. We also propose a method to identify multiple transmitters and demonstrate simultaneous reception from up to three LEDs. Finally, we evaluate end-to-end latency in real vehicular scenarios and show that the system meets cooperative perception requirements. These results demonstrate that event-camera-based VLC is a feasible complement to existing V2X technologies (e.g., RF).

</details>


### [126] [On the Emergence of Pendular Structure in Multi-Contact Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05707)
*Lingxue Lyu,Zihui Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LIPM-based control tendencies are revealed by a centroidal OCP penalizing angular-momentum rate; full-rank stance yields a pendular force pattern; 2-leg stance imposes friction-based lower bounds and a non-smooth kink; adding a task term shifts off the pendular set; results align with ZMP/DCM intuition but reveal limits in closed-loop; validated on a point-mmass quadruped and Unitree Go1 with open-loop QP and torque control, with ~16% experimental match for geometry-driven constants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Make the link between LIPM intuition and controller optimization explicit, and understand what an optimal centroidal dynamic controller would prefer, clarifying when pendular intuition emerges and where it breaks.

Method: Begin with a small centroidal OCP penalizing the rate of change of angular momentum. Analyze optimal solutions under different stance configurations: (i) full-rank stance yields a pendular-force pattern with rate governed by the SVD of the moment Jacobian; (ii) N=2 stance ( trot ) introduces friction-cone constraints that impose a lower bound on ||dot(H_G)|| and reveals a closed-form non-smooth kink at a critical horizontal acceleration; (iii) add a task term enforcing nonzero dot(H_G) to shift the optimum away from the pendular set; (iv) compare to ZMP/DCM and test on a point-mass quadruped and the Unitree Go1 in MuJoCo with open-loop QP and torque-control.

Result: The optimal solution tends toward a pendular force pattern with a rate dictated by the moment Jacobian's SVD and geometry (foot-span). For N=2 stance, the friction cone enforces a lower bound on the angular momentum rate, and a non-smooth feasibility kink is analytically derivable. A task term pushing for nonzero angular momentum rate moves the optimum off the pendular set in a predictable manner. The analysis remains close to the ZMP/DCM picture overall. Experimental validation on a point-mass quadruped and Unitree Go1 shows ~16% agreement with the predicted geometry-driven constants; asymptotic predictions degrade in closed-loop behavior.

Conclusion: LIPM can be interpreted as the optimization-driven preference of a centroidal controller, with the pendular intuition surfacing as the open-loop optimum under common stance configurations. The results clarify when and how LIPM-like control decisions arise from an OCP and highlight the limits of this view in closed-loop implementations, where friction, non-smooth dynamics, and task terms alter the optimal trajectory.

Abstract: LIPM is everywhere in legged-locomotion control, but almost always as a modeling choice rather than as something the
  controller's cost actually prefers. This note tries to make that link more explicit. Working from a small centroidal
  OCP that penalizes the rate of angular momentum, we look at what its optimum tends to look like. Three things come
  out. With full-rank stance, the optimum drifts toward a pendular force pattern at a rate determined by the SVD of the
  moment Jacobian; the constant is set by foot-span geometry and matches the experiments to within 16%. With N=2 stance,
  as in trot, the friction cone introduces a lower bound on $\|\dot{H}_G\|$ that no amount of weight tuning fixes; we
  also see a non-smooth feasibility kink at a critical horizontal acceleration that we can write in closed form. Adding
  a task term that asks for a nonzero $\dot{H}_G$ moves the optimum off the pendular set in a predictable way. None of
  this is far from the classical ZMP/DCM picture. We test these claims on a point-mass quadruped and on the Unitree Go1
  in MuJoCo (open-loop QP and a torque-level closed-loop controller), and we note where the asymptotic story stops being
  a good description of what the closed loop actually does.

</details>


### [127] [MaMi-HOI: Harmonizing Global Kinematics and Local Geometry for Human-Object Interaction Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05756)
*Hao Wang,Shiqi Wang,Qi Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MaMi-HOI is a hierarchical framework that reconciles macro-level kinematic fluidity with micro-level spatial precision for 3D Human-Object Interaction generation, addressing diffusion-model Geometric Forgetting with two adapters: Geometry-Aware Proximity Adapter (GAPA) for precise contact, and Kinematic Harmony Adapter (KHA) for natural, constraint-compatible whole-body motion; demonstrates improved contact accuracy, naturalness, and capability for long-horizon tasks; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based HOI generation tends to lose object-level geometry as models go deeper, resulting in imperfect contact between hands/objects. The authors identify Geometric Forgetting, where semantic features overwhelm geometry features in deeper diffusion steps, and aim to preserve fine-grained geometry while maintaining global motion realism.
