Method: Introduce MaMi-HOI, a two-layered hierarchical framework. Geometry-Aware Proximity Adapter (GAPA) injects dense object geometry details to perform residual snapping corrections for precise contact; Kinematic Harmony Adapter (KHA) aligns whole-body posture with spatial objectives, ensuring constraints are satisfied without sacrificing natural movement. The framework enables long-horizon trajectories by balancing macro navigation with micro manipulation constraints.

Result: Extensive experiments show MaMi-HOI achieves both natural motion and precise contact, extending generation to long-term tasks with complex trajectories. It bridges global navigation and high-fidelity manipulation in 3D scenes, outperforming baselines that struggle with accurate contact under diffusion. Code is available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: MaMi-HOI effectively reconciles macro-level fluidity with micro-level precision in 3D HOI generation, mitigating Geometric Forgetting via GAPA and preserving natural dynamics with KHA. The approach supports long-horizon, high-fidelity manipulation in 3D environments and provides a reproducible, open-source implementation.

Abstract: Generating realistic 3D Human-Object Interactions (HOI) is a fundamental task for applications ranging from embodied AI to virtual content creation, which requires harmonizing high-level semantic intent with strict low-level physical constraints. Existing methods excel at semantic alignment, however, they struggle to maintain precise object contact. We reveal a key finding termed \textit{Geometric Forgetting}: as diffusion model depth increases, semantic feature tend to overshadow object geometry feature, causing the model to lose its perception to object geometry. To address this, we propose MaMi-HOI, a hierarchical framework reconciling \textbf{Ma}cro-level kinematic fluidity with \textbf{Mi}cro-level spatial precision. First, to counteract geometric forgetting, we introduce the Geometry-Aware Proximity Adapter (GAPA), which explicitly re-injects dense object details to perform residual snapping corrections for precise contact. Nevertheless, such aggressive local enforcement can disrupt global dynamics, leading to robotic stiffness. In response, we introduce the Kinematic Harmony Adapter (KHA), which proactively aligns whole-body posture with spatial objectives, ensuring the skeleton actively accommodates constraints without compromising naturalness. Extensive experiments validate that MaMi-HOI simultaneously achieves natural motion and precise contact. Crucially, it extends generation capabilities to long-term tasks with complex trajectories, effectively bridging the gap between global navigation and high-fidelity manipulation in 3D scenes. Code is available at https://github.com/DON738110198/MaMi-HOI.git

</details>


### [128] [Resource-Constrained Robotic Planning in the face of Mixed Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05797)
*Yihao Yin,Pian Yu,Andrea Turrini,Zhiming Chi,Yong Li,Lijun Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces a Consumption MDP with Set-valued Transitions (CMDPST) to synthesize robust, resource-conserving strategies; integrates with LTLf specifications; proposes direct unrolling and pruning-based optimization; validated on a warehouse network.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots face both measurable noise and unmeasurable uncertainty while operating under hard resource constraints; there is a need for strategies that maximize task satisfaction probability without depleting resources.

Method: Model the system as CMDPST to capture nondeterministic actions, quantified/unquantified uncertainty, and resource consumption; combine with LTLf specifications; develop two synthesis algorithms: (i) a direct unrolling-based method, (ii) an optimized, state-space pruning method.

Result: Two synthesis approaches are presented; the pruning-based method offers better scalability; experimental evaluation on a warehouse transportation network demonstrates effectiveness in achieving high probability of LTLf satisfaction under resource constraints.

Conclusion: CMDPST coupled with LTLf provides a robust framework for resource-aware task synthesis under uncertainty; the pruning-based algorithm improves scalability, suggesting practical applicability, with potential for further performance enhancements and broader benchmarks.

Abstract: Robots operate under significant uncertainty, from quantifiable noise to unquantifiable unknowns, and must account for strict operational constraints, such as limited resources. In this paper, we consider the problem of synthesizing robust strategies to guide a robot's actions in fulfilling a given task, while ensuring the system never exhausts its resources. To solve this problem, we first model the robotic system as a Consumption Markov Decision Process with Set-valued Transitions(CMDPST), a unified framework modelling nondeterministic actions, quantifiable and unquantifiable uncertainty, and resource consumption. Then, we combine the CMDPST with the task specification, expressed as a Linear Temporal Logic over finite traces (LTLf ) formula. Lastly, we address the resource constrained optimal robust strategy synthesis problem, which aims to synthesize a strategy that maximizes the probability of satisfying the LTLf objective without resource exhaustion. Our solution involves two techniques: a direct unrolling-based method and a more efficient, optimized approach that leverages state-space pruning for better performance. Experiments on a warehouse transportation network show the effectiveness of the proposed solutions.

</details>


### [129] [A Comparative Study of INDI and NDI with Nonlinear Disturbance Observer for Aerial Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05825)
*Benedetta Rota,Mirko Mizzoni,Amr Afifi,Pieter van Goor,Antonio Franchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A simulation-based study comparing INDI and NDI+NDO for fully actuated aerial robots; INDI shows greater robustness to model mismatch and disturbances, while NDI+NDO tracks nominal performance but is more sensitive under non-ideal conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess robustness trade-offs between incremental nonlinear dynamic inversion (INDI) and observer-augmented inversion (NDI+NDO) to guide design choices in aerial robotics.

Method: Systematic simulation campaign across representative operating scenarios; evaluate tracking performance, robustness, and control effort under param variations, external disturbances, and measurement noise; compare INDI vs NDI+NDO.

Result: INDI demonstrates stronger robustness in several model-mismatch and combined-stress cases; NDI+NDO largely matches nominal performance but is more sensitive under several non-ideal conditions.

Conclusion: The findings provide practical guidance on the relative strengths and limitations of incremental and observer-based inversion strategies for aerial robotics, informing when to prefer INDI or NDI+NDO in design.

Abstract: This work presents a simulation-based comparative robustness analysis of Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) and Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion augmented with a nonlinear disturbance observer (NDI+NDO) for fully actuated aerial robots. A systematic simulation campaign across representative operating scenarios is conducted, where we compare tracking performance, robustness, control effort, under parametric variations, external disturbances, and measurement noise. Results show that INDI demonstrates stronger robustness in several model-mismatch and combined-stress cases, while NDI+NDO primarily matches nominal performance but exhibits greater sensitivity under several non-ideal conditions. These findings provide practical guidance on the relative strengths and limitations of incremental and observer-based inversion strategies for aerial robotic applications.

</details>


### [130] [Cycle-resolved Cephalopod-Inspired Pulsed-Jet Robot With High-Volume Expulsion and Drag-Reduced Gliding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05875)
*Yiyuan Zhang,Anye Zhong,Junkai Chen,Wenci Xin,Cecilia Laschi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Cephalopod-inspired pulsed-jet robot with a rigid-soft hybrid origami mantle enables large, actively driven deformation and reduced drag, studied via a cycle-resolved expulsion-gliding-refilling framework; demonstrates ~0.5 m/s peak speed and significant drag reduction within the first jet cycle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cephalopod pulsed-jet locomotion is cycle-based (expulsion, glide, refill). A cycle-resolved study can reveal how each phase and deformation contribute to speed and efficiency, informing bioinspired robotic propulsion.

Method: Develop a rigid-soft hybrid origami mantle that compresses the jet cavity by 75% and reduces projected drag area by ~75.7% in contraction. Integrate passive inlet valves inspired by mantle aperture. Form a cycle-resolved experimental framework to isolate effects of expelled volume, glide duration, and refill pathway on whole-cycle performance. Conduct experiments to measure speed, cycle timing, drag, and refill behavior.

Result: The robot achieved a peak speed of ~0.5 m/s (3.8 BL/s) and average speed >0.2 m/s (1.5 BL/s) within the first jetting cycle. High expelled-volume-ratio contraction enhances speed; reduced drag area supports gliding under varied glide durations; passive inlet valves assist refill. 75% cavity-volume reduction and ~75.7% drag-area reduction were demonstrated.

Conclusion: A functional cephalopod-like pulsed-jet robot with actively deformable mantle. Provides a unified platform to study expulsion-gliding-refilling dynamics and informs design of cycle-resolved propulsion systems in soft robotics.

Abstract: Cephalopod pulsed-jet locomotion is not a single isolated expulsion event, but a coordinated cycle involving jet expulsion, passive gliding, and mantle refilling. Inspired by this cycle-resolved biological strategy, this paper presents a cephalopod-inspired pulsed-jet robot with a rigid-soft hybrid origami mantle that enables large, actively driven, and geometry-guided body deformation. The proposed mantle integrates rigid folding panels with a compliant silicone framework, allowing a 75% effective cavity-volume reduction during expulsion and reducing the projected cross-sectional drag area by approximately 75.7% in the contracted gliding configuration. Using this platform, we formulate a cycle-resolved framework to separately investigate how expelled volume, glide duration, and refill pathway influence whole-cycle locomotion performance. Experiments show that the robot reaches a peak speed of approximately 0.5 m/s (3.8 BL/s) and an average speed exceeding 0.2 m/s (1.5 BL/s) within the first jetting cycle. The results further demonstrate the roles of high expelled-volume-ratio contraction in speed generation, reduced-drag-area gliding under different glide durations, and mantle-aperture-inspired passive inlet valves in assisting refill. This work provides both a robotic implementation of actively deformable cephalopod-like jet propulsion and a unified experimental platform for studying expulsion-gliding-refilling dynamics in pulsed-jet locomotion.

</details>


### [131] [Generating Roadside LiDAR Datasets from Vehicle-Side Datasets via Novel View Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05897)
*Yuhan Xia,Runxin Zhao,Hanyang Zhuang,Chunxiang Wang,Ming Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VRS synthesizes labeled roadside LiDAR data from vehicle-side data via novel view synthesis, using vehicle point cloud completion and an occupancy-based visibility constraint to manage large viewpoint changes, enabling scalable multi-view roadside data generation for improved roadside 3D detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lack of large-scale annotated roadside LiDAR datasets hampers robust roadside perception. A scalable cross-view data generation approach from vehicle-side data can fill this gap and enhance generalization to unseen viewpoints.

Method: VRS pipeline: (1) vehicle point cloud completion to recover missing geometry; (2) occupancy-based visibility constraint to handle large viewpoint changes during cross-view rendering; (3) flexible multi-view rendering to synthesize labeled roadside LiDAR from vehicle datasets; (4) evaluation on roadside 3D object detection.

Result: Synthesized roadside LiDAR data effectively complements real roadside data, mitigates limitations of limited real-world roadside data, and improves generalization to unseen roadside viewpoints.

Conclusion: VRS provides a flexible, scalable solution to generate roadside data from vehicle data, bridging the vehicle–roadside domain gap and improving roadside perception performance.

Abstract: Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) require reliable environmental perception to support safe and efficient transportation. With the rapid development of Vehicle-to-everything (V2X), roadside perception has become an effective means to extend sensing coverage and improve traffic safety. However, the scarcity of large-scale annotated roadside LiDAR datasets poses a major challenge for training high-performance roadside perception models. In this paper, we introduce Vehicle-to-Roadside LiDAR Synthesis (VRS), a data synthesis framework that generates labeled roadside LiDAR datasets from vehicle-side datasets via LiDAR novel view synthesis. To mitigate the vehicle-to-roadside domain gap, VRS employs vehicle point cloud completion to compensate for missing geometry in vehicle-side observations, and introduces an occupancy-based visibility constraint to handle large viewpoint changes during cross-view rendering. The proposed framework enables flexible multi-view rendering for scalable roadside data generation. Extensive experiments on roadside 3D object detection demonstrate that the synthesized data effectively complements real roadside data, mitigates the limitations of limited real-world roadside data, and improves generalization to unseen roadside viewpoints.

</details>


### [132] [DexSynRefine: Synthesizing and Refining Human-Object Interaction Motion for Physically Feasible Dexterous Robot Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05925)
*Hyesung Lee,Hyunwoo Jung,Si-Hwan Heo,Sungwook Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexSynRefine integrates HOI-derived motion primitives, task-space residual RL, and dynamics adaptation to enable sim-to-real dexterous manipulation from sparse HOI data, achieving strong sim and real-world performance across five tasks with large gains over retargeting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HOI data offer scalable demonstrations but are sparse and misaligned with embodiment and contact-rich dynamics; need a method to synthesize executable references and ground them for real robots.

Method: Three components: HOI-MMFP to synthesize hand-object trajectories from sparse HOI demonstrations conditioned on task and initial state; task-space residual RL policy to physically ground the synthesized reference while preserving kinematic structure; contact-and-dynamics adaptation module to enable sim-to-real transfer from proprioceptive history.

Result: In five dexterous tasks (pick-and-place, tool use, object reorientation), the task-space residual policy outperforms prior action-representation baselines in simulation and transfers to a real robot on all tasks, improving over kinematic retargeting by 50-70 percentage points.

Conclusion: An integrated framework that leverages HOI data to produce executable dexterous manipulation with robust sim-to-real transfer, yielding substantial performance gains over baselines.

Abstract: Learning dexterous manipulation from human-object interaction (HOI) data is a scalable alternative to teleoperation, but HOI demonstrations are sparse and provide only kinematic motion that is not directly executable under embodiment mismatch and contact-rich dynamics. We present DexSynRefine, a framework with three coupled components: HOI-MMFP, a task- and object-initial-state-conditioned extension of motion manifold primitives that synthesizes coordinated hand-object trajectories from sparse HOI demonstrations; a task-space residual RL policy that physically grounds the synthesized reference while inheriting its kinematic structure; and a contact-and-dynamics adaptation module that enables sim-to-real transfer from proprioceptive history. Across five dexterous manipulation tasks spanning pick-and-place, tool use, and object reorientation, our task-space residual policy outperforms prior action-representation baselines in simulations and transfers to a real robot on all five tasks, improving over kinematic retargeting by 50-70 percentage points.

</details>


### [133] [Plug-and-Play Label Map Diffusion for Universal Goal-Oriented Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05960)
*Zhixuan Shen,Yijie Zeng,Shengxiang Luo,Tianrui Li,Haonan Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces PLMD, a diffusion-based map completion method for goal-oriented navigation that completes BEV obstacle/semantic labels in unobserved regions, reducing semantic inconsistency and enabling robust localization in partially observed environments; achieves state-of-the-art on three GON tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GON requires accurate planning from incomplete BEV maps and reliable localization of unobserved goals. Existing self-centered semantic maps struggle when maps are incomplete or semantic associations are inconsistent, hindering robust navigation.

Method: Proposes a DDPM-based map completion model (PLMD) that predicts obstacle and semantic labels for unobserved regions. It enforces structural consistency between known and unknown obstacle layouts, integrates obstacle priors into the semantic denoising process, and plugs into existing semantic-map-based navigation pipelines by substituting predicted labels for unobserved areas.

Result: Extensive experiments show that PLMD (i) expands the unknown map region effectively, (ii) integrates seamlessly with existing navigation strategies relying on semantic maps, and (iii) achieves state-of-the-art performance across three GON tasks.

Conclusion: PLMD provides a principled, plug-and-play solution to incomplete BEV maps in GON, improving localization accuracy and robustness in partially observed environments while maintaining compatibility with current navigation methods.

Abstract: In embodied vision, Goal-Oriented Navigation (GON) requires robots to locate a specific goal within an unexplored environment. The primary challenge of GON arises from the need to construct a Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) map to understand the environment while simultaneously localizing an unobserved goal. Existing map-based methods typically employ self-centered semantic maps, often facing challenges such as reliance on complete maps or inconsistent semantic association. To this end, we propose Plug-and-Play Label Map Diffusion (PLMD), which defines a novel map completion diffusion model based on Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM). PLMD generates obstacle and semantic labels for unobserved regions through a diffusion-based completion process, thereby enabling goal localization even in partially observed environments. Moreover, it mitigates inconsistent semantic association by leveraging structural consistency between known and unknown obstacle layouts and integrating obstacle priors into the semantic denoising process. By substituting predicted labels for unobserved regions, robots can accurately localize the specified objects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PLMD \textbf{(I)} effectively expands the region of unknown maps, \textbf{(II)} integrates seamlessly into existing navigation strategies that rely on semantic maps, \textbf{(III)} achieves state-of-the-art performance on three GON tasks.

</details>


### [134] [Accurate Trajectory Tracking with MPCC for Flapping-Wing MAVs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06042)
*Charbel Toumieh,Jack Zeng,Niel Mistry,Dario Floreano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MPCC with a compact differentiable model enables real-time arc-length trajectory tracking for bird-scale ornithopters, achieving 6.5–9 cm tracking error at up to 3 m/s and about 10x improvement over prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the tight coupling between lift, airspeed, and turning authority in flapping-wing MAVs and overcome the limitations of cascaded controllers and predefined timing by enabling real-time, coupled-dynamics control without fixed timing profiles.

Method: Propose a Model Predictive Contouring Control (MPCC) framework that tracks arc-length parameterized trajectories and optimizes progress online. Develop a compact, continuously differentiable plant model capturing dominant aero-dynamic couplings. Apply MPCC to the XFly ornithopter on circular and 3D racing trajectories with real-time nonlinear optimization.

Result: Mean trajectory deviation from reference between 6.5 and 9 cm at speeds up to 3 m/s, representing ~10x improvement over prior ornithopter control methods.

Conclusion: Combining MPCC with a compact, differentiable model enables accurate, real-time autonomous control of bird-scale ornithopters on complex trajectories without predefined timing, significantly improving tracking performance.

Abstract: Flapping-wing micro aerial vehicles offer quieter and safer operation than rotary-wing drones, yet achieving precise autonomous control of bird-scale ornithopters remains challenging: lift, airspeed, and turning authority are tightly coupled and governed by only a few control inputs. Conventional cascaded controllers treat altitude, speed, and heading independently, producing persistent tracking errors during complex maneuvers, while time-parameterized trajectory tracking requires predefined speed profiles that existing methods cannot robustly produce for these coupled dynamics. We address both limitations simultaneously with a Model Predictive Contouring Control (MPCC) approach that tracks arc-length-parameterized trajectories while optimizing progress online, eliminating the need for predefined timing. However, MPCC requires a dynamical model that captures the coupled aerodynamics without exceeding the computational budget of real-time nonlinear optimization. Here, we propose a compact, continuously differentiable model that captures the dominant couplings of bird-scale ornithopters, enabling real-time predictive control. We validated the method with the XFly ornithopter flying along circular and three-dimensional racing trajectories and achieved a mean deviation from the reference trajectory between 6.5 and 9 cm at speeds up to 3 m/s, which represents an almost 10-fold improvement over prior ornithopter control methods.

</details>


### [135] [Monitoring autonomous persistent surveillance missions using invariance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06062)
*Vladislav Nenchev,Prodromos Sotiriadis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A scalable runtime monitor for persistent robotic surveillance with black-box autonomy, using decentralized invariant sets and a compositional monitor that is sound and complete under independence assumptions; validated on a real labyrinth case.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Guarantee runtime monitoring and safety for persistent surveillance when the autonomy stack is a black box and the environment is large, making global invariants hard to compute.

Method: Model the closed-loop as a state-dependent hybrid system with linear parameter-varying dynamics; compute an offline invariant; develop a compositional monitor by decentralized computation of low-dimensional invariants for each uncertainty region; online conjunction of regional invariants; independence assumptions ensure soundness and completeness.

Result: The compositional monitor is sound and complete with respect to the full-system invariant under the stated independence assumptions; demonstrated in a real-robot labyrinth case study, highlighting practical applicability.

Conclusion: Shows feasibility and practicality of scalable, sound/complete runtime monitoring for persistent surveillance with black-box autonomy, using compositional invariants; applicable to real-world robotic deployments.

Abstract: This paper studies runtime monitoring for persistent surveillance by autonomous robots when the autonomy stack is a black box. The environment is partitioned into finitely many parts, each carrying an uncertainty state that decreases when observed and increases otherwise. We model the closed loop as a state-dependent hybrid system with linear parameter varying dynamics and design a monitor based on an invariant computed offline. As this invariant is typically hard to obtain for large to-be-surveyed spaces, we propose a compositional monitor obtained by decentralized computation of low-dimensional invariant sets for each uncertainty region, and checking their conjunction online. Under common independence assumptions, the compositional monitor is sound and complete with respect to the full-system invariant. The approach is applied in a case study with a real robot persistently monitoring a labyrinth, emphasizing its applicability in practice.

</details>


### [136] [VLA-GSE: Boosting Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning in VLA with Generalized and Specialized Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06175)
*Yuhua Jiang,Junjie Lu,Xinyao Qin,Xiaoyu Chen,Kaixin Wang,Feifei Gao,Li Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VLA-GSE is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework for vision-language-action robotics that uses spectral decomposition of a frozen backbone to allocate generalized (shared) and specialized (routed) experts, enabling strong adaptation with a tiny update footprint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable effective adaptation of pre-trained vision-language-action models to robotic control without overfitting (FFT) and while preserving pretrained knowledge (PEFT), by increasing adaptation capacity under a fixed parameter budget.

Method: Spectrally decompose the frozen backbone to identify leading singular components. Map leading components to generalized (shared) experts and remainder residual components to specialized (routed) experts. Train only a small fraction of parameters within these expert modules under a fixed budget, effectively routing to appropriate experts.

Result: With the same parameter budget, VLA-GSE updates about 2.51% of the full model and consistently outperforms FFT and PEFT baselines. It achieves 81.2% average zero-shot success on LIBERO-Plus, preserves pre-trained VLM capabilities comparably to LoRA on multimodal benchmarks, and improves real-world manipulation under distribution shifts. Code is available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: VLA-GSE provides a principled, parameter-efficient adaptation mechanism for VLA models in robotics, balancing knowledge preservation with task-specific adaptation and demonstrating strong empirical gains across multimodal understanding and real-world manipulation tasks. Potentially extensible to other backbones and tasks with efficient fine-tuning.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models inherit rich visual-semantic priors from pre-trained vision-language backbones, but adapting them to robotic control remains challenging. Full fine-tuning (FFT) is prone to overfitting on downstream robotic data and catastrophic forgetting of pretrained vision-language capabilities. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) better preserves pre-trained knowledge, yet existing PEFT methods still struggle to adapt effectively to robot control tasks. To address this gap, we propose VLA-GSE, a parameter-efficient VLA fine-tuning framework that improves control adaptation while retaining PEFT's knowledge preservation advantage. Specifically, VLA-GSE (Generalized and Specialized Experts) is initialized by spectrally decomposing the frozen backbone, assigning leading singular components to generalized experts (shared experts) and disjoint residual components to specialized experts (routed experts). This decomposition improves adaptation capacity under a fixed trainable-parameter budget. Under a comparable parameter budget, VLA-GSE updates only 2.51% of the full model parameters and consistently outperforms strong FFT and PEFT baselines. It achieves 81.2% average zero-shot success on LIBERO-Plus, preserves pre-trained VLM capability comparably to LoRA on multimodal understanding benchmarks, and improves real-world manipulation success under multiple distribution shifts. Code is available at: https://github.com/YuhuaJiang2002/VLA-GSE

</details>


### [137] [When to Trust Imagination: Adaptive Action Execution for World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06222)
*Rui Wang,Yue Zhang,Jiehong Lin,Kuncheng Luo,Jianan Wang,Zhongrui Wang,Xiaojuan Qi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive verification-based WAM execution using FFDC with Mixture-of-Horizon Training yields robust long-horizon manipulation with strong efficiency gains and improved success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: WAMs predict future observations and actions but typically execute a fixed number of steps, causing misalignment between imagined futures and actual physics. There is a need for real-time verification to decide whether to extend or replan, enabling adaptive action chunking and better handling of contact-rich phases, guided by language instructions.

Method: Propose Future Forward Dynamics Causal Attention (FFDC), a lightweight verifier that jointly reasons over predicted future actions, predicted visual dynamics, real observations, and language instructions to assess trust in the remaining rollout. Enables adaptive action chunk sizes as an emergent property of prediction-observation consistency. Introduce Mixture-of-Horizon Training to improve long-horizon trajectory coverage for adaptive execution.

Result: Empirical results show a strong robustness-efficiency trade-off: on RoboTwin, FFDC reduces WAM forward passes by 69.10% and execution time by 34.02%, with a 2.54% improvement in success rate over a short-chunk baseline. Real-world experiments report a 35% improvement in success rate.

Conclusion: FFDC enables adaptive, reliable long-horizon WAM execution without sacrificing efficiency; Mixture-of-Horizon Training enhances long-horizon trajectory coverage and robustness in adaptive execution.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation by jointly predicting future visual observations and future actions. However, current WAMs typically execute a fixed number of predicted actions after each model inference, leaving the robot blind to whether the imagined future remains consistent with the actual physical rollout. In this work, we formulate adaptive WAM execution as a future-reality verification problem: the robot should execute longer when the WAM-predicted future remains reliable, and replan earlier when reality deviates from imagination. To this end, we propose Future Forward Dynamics Causal Attention (FFDC), a lightweight verifier that jointly reasons over predicted future actions, predicted visual dynamics, real observations, and language instructions to estimate whether the remaining action rollout can still be trusted. FFDC enables adaptive action chunk sizes as an emergent consequence of prediction-observation consistency, preserving the efficiency of long-horizon execution while restoring responsiveness in contact-rich or difficult phases. We further introduce Mixture-of-Horizon Training to improve long-horizon trajectory coverage for adaptive execution. Experiments on the RoboTwin benchmark and in the real world demonstrate that our method achieves a strong robustness-efficiency trade-off: on RoboTwin, it reduces WAM forward passes by 69.10% and execution time by 34.02%, while improving success rate by 2.54% over the short-chunk baseline; in real-world experiments, it improves success rate by 35%.

</details>


### [138] [RobotEQ: Transitioning from Passive Intelligence to Active Intelligence in Embodied AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06234)
*Kuofei Fang,Xinyi Che,Haomin Ouyang,Shufan Zhang,Xuehao Wang,Qi Liu,Liyi Liu,Chenqi Zhang,Wenxi Cai,Wenyu Dai,Jinyang Wu,Fan Zhang,Haoyu Chen,Bin He,Zheng Lian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces RobotEQ, a benchmark to evaluate active social intelligence in embodied AI by distinguishing passive instruction-following from active norm-compliant behavior. It includes RobotEQ-Data (1,900 egocentric images across 10 embodied categories with 56 subcategories; 5,353 action-judgment questions and 1,286 spatial-grounding questions) and RobotEQ-Bench for evaluation. Results show current models struggle, especially with spatial grounding; RAG with external social-norm knowledge bases can help.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable embodied agents to understand and follow social norms without explicit commands, bridging the gap between passive, user-guided manipulation and active, norm-based behavior.

Method: Create RobotEQ-Data with annotated egocentric images across diverse embodied scenarios; define action-judgment and spatial-grounding tasks; assemble RobotEQ-Bench; evaluate state-of-the-art models; explore retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) using external normative knowledge bases.

Result: Current models underperform on active intelligence tasks, particularly in spatial grounding; RAG improves performance by injecting external norm knowledge.

Conclusion: RobotEQ provides a first benchmark and dataset for active social compliance in embodied AI, highlighting gaps in current models and suggesting knowledge-augmented approaches to improve normative understanding.

Abstract: Embodied AI is a prominent research topic in both academia and industry. Current research centers on completing tasks based on explicit user instructions. However, for robots to integrate into human society, they must understand which actions are permissible and which are prohibited, even without explicit commands. We refer to the user-guided AI as passive intelligence and the unguided AI as active intelligence. This paper introduces RobotEQ, the first benchmark for active intelligence, aiming to assess whether existing models can comprehend and adhere to social norms in embodied scenarios. First, we construct RobotEQ-Data, a dataset consisting of 1,900 egocentric images, spanning 10 representative embodied categories and 56 subcategories. Through extensive manual annotation, we provide 5,353 action judgment questions and 1,286 spatial grounding questions, specifying appropriate robot actions across diverse scenarios. Furthermore, we establish RobotEQ-Bench to evaluate the performance of state-of-the-art models on this task. Experimental results show that current models still fall short in achieving reliable active intelligence, particularly in spatial grounding. Meanwhile, we observe that leveraging RAG techniques to incorporate external social norm knowledge bases can generally enhance performance. This work can facilitate the transition of robotics from user-guided passive manipulation to active social compliance.

</details>


### [139] [CKT-WAM: Parameter-Efficient Context Knowledge Transfer Between World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06247)
*Yuhua Jiang,Yijun Guo,Hongbing Yang,Guojun Lei,Nuo Chen,Yinuo Zhang,Shaoqiang Yan,Bo Lin,Feifei Gao,Biqing Qi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Context-based knowledge transfer for World Action Models (WAMs) using a compact text-embedding context, enabling parameter-efficient cross-model transfer and strong few-shot/zero-shot gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mismatched latent interfaces across heterogeneous WAMs and high adaptation cost of conventional distillation hinder cross-model knowledge transfer; need lightweight, modality-agnostic transfer in the text embedding space.

Method: CKT-WAM pipeline: extract teacher hidden states, compress tokens with learnable-query cross attention (LQCA), feed through always-on generalized adapter, router, and sparsely activated specialized adapters; generate a compact knowledge context appended to student conditioning embeddings.

Result: Zero-shot/generalization improved; LIBERO-Plus best overall performance: 86.1% total success with 1.17% trainable parameters; approaches full fine-tuning; real-world long-horizon tasks: average success 83.3% across four tasks.

Conclusion: Context-based knowledge transfer in WAMs enables effective cross-architecture transfer with minimal trainable parameters and strong real-world applicability.

Abstract: World action models (WAMs) provide a powerful generative framework for embodied control, yet transferring knowledge across heterogeneous WAMs remains challenging due to mismatched latent interfaces, high adaptation cost, and the rigidity of conventional distillation objectives. We propose \textbf{CKT-WAM}, a parameter-efficient \textbf{C}ontext \textbf{K}nowledge \textbf{T}ransfer framework that transfers teacher WAM's knowledge into a student WAM through a compact context in the text embedding space, rather than output imitation or dense hidden-state matching. Specifically, CKT-WAM extracts intermediate teacher hidden states, reduces the number of tokens via compressors' learnable-query cross attention (LQCA), and transforms them through an always-on generalized adapter, a lightweight router, and sparsely activated specialized adapters. The resulting context is then appended to the student's conditioning textual embeddings, thereby injecting the transferred knowledge into the student with minimal architectural modification. Experiments show that CKT-WAM consistently improves zero-shot generalization and achieves the best overall performance on LIBERO-Plus, reaching 86.1\% total success rate with only 1.17\% trainable parameters, while approaching full fine-tuning performance. Beyond simulation, CKT-WAM also demonstrates strong real-world long-horizon manipulation ability, achieving the best average success rate of 83.3\% across four multi-step and long-horizon tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/YuhuaJiang2002/CKT-WAM.

</details>


### [140] [Toward Visually Realistic Simulation: A Benchmark for Evaluating Robot Manipulation in Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06311)
*Yixin Zhu,Zixiong Wang,Jian Yang,Jin Xie,Jingyi Yu,Jiayuan Gu,Beibei Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VISER is a visually realistic simulation benchmark for robot manipulation built from 1k+ PBR assets, with an automated pipeline using multimodal LLMs for material-aware segmentation and retrieval. It enables diverse tasks (grasp/place/long-horizon) and shows strong real-world correlation (r=0.92) across policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks lack visual realism, resulting in a large sim-to-real visual gap. Lighting and material drastically affect geometric reasoning and spatial grounding but are often overlooked, undermining reliable simulation-based evaluation.

Method: Assemble VISER: a high-fidelity 3D asset dataset with physically-based rendering materials; generate scenes via curated layouts or generation. Implement an automated pipeline leveraging multimodal LLMs for material-aware part segmentation and material retrieval to produce plausible assets. Define evaluation tasks (grasping, placing, long-horizon) to assess Vision-Language-Action models in simulation.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows a strong sim-to-real correlation, with a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.92 across different policies.

Conclusion: VISER reduces the visual sim-to-real gap, enabling reliable, scalable, and reproducible evaluation of robot manipulation policies in simulation and fostering robust VLA benchmarking.

Abstract: Reliable simulation evaluation of robot manipulation policies serves as a high-fidelity proxy for real-world performance. Although existing benchmarks cover a wide range of task categories, they lack visual realism, creating a large domain gap between simulation and reality. This undermines the reliability of simulation-based evaluation in predicting real-world performance. To mitigate the sim-to-real visual gap, we conduct a systematic analysis to isolate the effects of lighting and material. Our results show that these factors play a critical role in geometric reasoning and spatial grounding, yet are largely overlooked in existing benchmarks. Motivated by the analysis, we propose VISER, a visually realistic benchmark for evaluating robot manipulation in simulation. VISER features a high-fidelity dataset of over 1,000 3D assets with physically-based rendering (PBR) materials, along with 3D scenes created from these assets through curated layouts or generation. To this end, we propose an automated pipeline leveraging Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for material-aware part segmentation and material retrieval, enabling scalable generation of physically plausible assets. Building on the high-fidelity 3D asset dataset, we construct diverse evaluation tasks, such as grasping, placing, and long-horizon tasks, enabling scalable and reproducible assessment of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. Our benchmark shows a strong correlation between simulation and real-world performance, achieving an average Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.92 across different policies.

</details>


### [141] [AssistDLO: Assistive Teleoperation for Deformable Linear Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06323)
*Berk Guler,Simon Manschitz,Kay Pompetzki,Jan Peters*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AssistDLO: a geometry-aware shared-autonomy controller (SA-CBF) for DLO teleoperation that combines real-time multi-view state estimation and visual assistance to preserve DLO geometry while aiding grasping; effectiveness is user- and material-dependent, highlighting the need for adaptive, user-aware, material-aware control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DLO manipulation is hard due to infinite-dimensional configuration space and nonlinear dynamics. Teleoperation suffers from depth uncertainty in perception and reaction. A robust assistive framework is needed that preserves geometry while empowering users.

Method: Integrates real-time multi-view state estimation, visual assistance (VA), and a geometry-aware shared-autonomy controller based on Control Barrier Functions (SA-CBF). Evaluated via a bimanual knot-untangling user study (N=22) with ropes of varying length and rigidity.

Result: SA-CBF significantly improves naive users' success rate from 71% to 88% and serves as a skill equalizer, especially for stiffer ropes; VA preferred by expert users; long, highly compliant ropes benefit more from visual support than from localized action assistance.

Conclusion: Adaptive, user-aware, and material-aware shared autonomy is essential for effective DLO teleoperation; fixed strategies are insufficient; SA-CBF provides a geometry-aware funnel to preserve DLO geometry while maintaining operator authority.

Abstract: Manipulating Deformable Linear Objects (DLOs) is challenging in robotics due to their infinite-dimensional configuration space and complex nonlinear dynamics. In teleoperation, depth uncertainty hinders state perception and reaction. AssistDLO addresses this challenge as an assistive teleoperation framework for DLO manipulation that combines real-time multi-view state estimation, visual assistance (VA), and a geometry-aware shared-autonomy controller based on Control Barrier Functions (SA-CBF). While traditional shared autonomy methods often rely on simple geometric attractors and may fail to preserve DLO geometry, SA-CBF acts as a geometry-aware funnel, facilitating precise grasping while preserving the operator's high-level authority. The framework is evaluated in a bimanual knot-untangling user study (N = 22) using ropes with varying length and rigidity. Results show that the effectiveness of the assistance depends strongly on operator expertise and DLO properties. SA-CBF provides the strongest gains for naive users, acting as a skill equalizer that increases task success from 71% to 88%, and is effective for stiffer ropes. Conversely, expert users prefer VA, and highly compliant, long ropes benefit more from visual support than localized action assistance. Ultimately, these findings demonstrate that effective DLO teleoperation cannot rely on a fixed strategy, highlighting the critical need for adaptive, user-aware, and material-aware shared autonomy.

</details>


### [142] [TouchDrive: Electronics-Free Tactile Sensing Interface for Assistive Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06432)
*Jing Xu,Xuezhi Niu,Didem Gurdur Broo,Klas Hjort*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A cost-effective, electronics-free tactile interface (TouchDrive) converts contact forces into pneumatic feedback via a passive valve loop, enabling assistive grasping without electronics and usable across multiple robots for delicate object handling (e.g., fruits) across ~20 items.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce system complexity and reliance on electronic sensing in assistive grasping to improve accessibility and robustness for delicate object manipulation.

Method: A pneumatic, normally closed valve–based feedback loop uses a sensing element and haptic actuator to transduce contact forces into tactile feedback without electronics, enabling integration with various robotic platforms.

Result: Demonstrated reliable, practical performance across diverse platforms, enabling precise and robust manipulation of compliant/fragile objects like fruits and everyday items (tested on up to 20 objects).

Conclusion: Electronics-free tactile sensing and feedback via a passive mechanical loop can effectively empower users to modulate grasp forces, reducing system complexity while maintaining robustness across platforms.

Abstract: Assistive robotic grasping plays an important role in enabling safe and adaptive manipulation of diverse objects. However, existing systems often rely on electronic sensing and multi-stage processing pipelines, increasing system complexity and reducing accessibility. To address these limitations, we present TouchDrive, a cost-effective, electronics-free tactile sensing interface for assistive grasping. TouchDrive directly converts contact forces into pneumatic feedback through valve-mediated switching, integrating sensing, signal generation, and feedback within a single passive mechanical loop. The system can be employed using a pneumatic normally closed valve, a compressed air tank, sensing element, and haptic feedback actuator without electronics. By delivering tactile cues, TouchDrive empowers users to modulate grasp forces, enabling precise and robust delicate manipulation of compliant and fragile objects. The interface has been validated across diverse robotic platforms, consistently demonstrating reliable performance and practical applicability in assistive grasping tasks, such as handling fruits and everyday items (up to 20 objects).

</details>


### [143] [GA3T: A Ground-Aerial Terrain Traversability Dataset for Heterogeneous Robot Teams in Unstructured Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06478)
*Siwei Cai,Knut Peterson,Quan Tran,Christian Ricks,Dhanush Parthasarathy,Amir Kaidarov,Neil Deshpande,Sukaina Najm,David Han,Lifeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GA3T is a real-world ground–aerial multi-robot perception dataset across diverse unstructured terrains, featuring synchronized 3D LiDAR, stereo, RGB, thermal imaging, IMU, and GPS data from ground and aerial platforms, designed for cross-view perception, air–ground fusion, and traversability understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of real-world multi-modal, cross-view datasets in unstructured environments, enabling research on occlusion-aware collaboration, cross-view fusion, and traversability estimation beyond SLAM or simulated driving.

Method: Data collection with a Clearpath Husky UGV and an Autel EVO II UAV across forest trails, rocky paths, muddy terrain, snow, and grass. Ground platform provides 3D LiDAR, stereo camera, IMU, GPS; aerial platform provides RGB, thermal/IR, GPS. 4 environments, ~29 minutes, 13,000 synchronized frames, and >8,000 manually labeled images; includes SAM3-based zero-shot segmentation.

Result: A large, multi-modal, real-world dataset with synchronized ground-aerial observations across unstructured terrain, enabling cross-modal alignment and cross-view perception research. Early-spring collection allows partial aerial visibility through sparse canopies, adding occlusion-aware scenarios and distinguishing GA3T from prior SLAM-focused or simulated datasets.

Conclusion: GA3T fills a gap for real off-road cross-view perception datasets, supporting research in cross-view perception, air-ground viewpoint fusion, traversability estimation, and collaborative scene understanding, with unique occlusion-aware characteristics due to early-spring collection.

Abstract: Heterogeneous air-ground robot teams combine complementary sensing modalities, mobility characteristics, and spatial viewpoints that can significantly enhance perception in complex outdoor environments. However, progress in multi-robot collaborative perception has been constrained by the lack of real-world datasets featuring overlapping multi-modal observations from platforms operating in unstructured terrain. We present GA3T (Ground-Aerial Team for Terrain Traversal), a real-world multi-robot collaborative perception dataset collected using a Clearpath Husky UGV and an Autel EVO~II UAV across diverse unstructured environments, including forest trails, rocky paths, muddy terrain, snow piles, and grass-covered fields. The ground platform provides 3D LiDAR, stereo camera, IMU, and GPS data, while the aerial platform contributes RGB imagery, thermal/infrared observations, and GPS from a complementary overhead viewpoint, allowing for rich cross-modal and cross-view perception. The dataset is collected in 4 unique environments, with over 13,000 synchronized frames across approximately 29 minutes of operation, and includes both SAM~3-based zero-shot segmentation and over 8,000 manually labeled images. A unique aspect of the dataset is its early-spring collection period, during which sparse tree canopies allow the aerial robot to partially observe the ground robot and terrain through the trees, allowing for occlusion-aware collaborative perception. Unlike prior multi-robot datasets that focus on SLAM or simulated cooperative driving, GA3T is specifically designed to support research on cross-view perception, air-ground viewpoint fusion, traversability estimation, and collaborative scene understanding in real off-road environments.

</details>


### [144] [OA-WAM: Object-Addressable World Action Model for Robust Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06481)
*Yushan Liu,Peibo Sun,Shoujie Li,Yifan Xie,Lingfeng Zhang,Xintao Chao,Shiyuan Dong,Fang Chen,Xiao-Ping Zhang,Wenbo Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OA-WAM introduces an object-addressable world model for robot manipulation by partitioning frames into robot and object slots with persistent addresses; it decouples identity from content and shows robust performance and strong object-specific control on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current World Action Models (WAMs use holistic images, videos, or global latents) struggle to address instructions tied to specific objects, especially when scene context shifts entangle identity with content. There is a need for a structured, object-addressable representation to maintain consistent object identity and enable precise object-level control.

Method: Each frame is decomposed into N+1 slots (1 robot + N objects). Each slot has a persistent address vector and a time-varying content vector. Slots are fused with text, image, proprioception, and past-action tokens in a block-causal sequence. A world head predicts next-frame slot states; a flow-matching action head decodes a 16-step action chunk in the forward pass. Object-addressability is enforced by routing cross-slot attention through address-only keys and resetting the address slice at every transformer layer, separating which object to act on from what that object currently is without adding extra tokens.

Result: OA-WAM matches strong VLA and WAM baselines on LIBERO (97.8%) and SimplerEnv (79.3%), achieves state-of-the-art on LIBERO-Plus geometric axes, and remains competitive on seven-axis aggregate. A causal slot-intervention test yields a swap-binding cosine of 0.87, far exceeding holistic baselines (≤0.09).

Conclusion: Addressable object states provide an effective interface for robust world-action modeling under scene perturbations, enabling reliable object-level control and identity preservation across changing contexts.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) enhance Vision-Language-Action policies by jointly predicting scene evolution and robot actions, but existing methods usually represent the predicted world as holistic images, video tokens, or global latents. These representations are difficult for an action decoder to address when an instruction refers to a particular object, especially under scene shifts where object identity is entangled with context. We propose OA-WAM, an Object-Addressable World Action Model for robust robot manipulation. OA-WAM decomposes each frame into N+1 slot states, with one robot slot and N object slots. Each slot contains a persistent address vector and a time-varying content vector, and is fused with text, image, proprioception, and past-action tokens in a block-causal sequence. A world head predicts next-frame slot states, while a flow-matching action head decodes a 16-step continuous action chunk in the same forward pass. Addressability is enforced by routing cross-slot attention through address-only keys and resetting the address slice at every transformer layer, separating which object to act on from what that object currently is without adding extra tokens. OA-WAM matches strong VLA and WAM baselines on LIBERO (97.8%) and SimplerEnv (79.3%), reaches state-of-the-art performance on the most relevant LIBERO-Plus geometric axes, and remains competitive on the seven-axis aggregate. A causal slot-intervention test yields a swap-binding cosine of 0.87, versus at most 0.09 for holistic baselines. These results suggest that addressable object states provide an effective interface for robust world-action modeling under scene perturbations.

</details>


### [145] [Lie Group Formulation of Recursive Dynamics Algorithms of Higher Order for Floating-Base Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06498)
*Ahmed Ali,Chiara Gabellieri,Antonio Franchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Higher-order time derivatives of floating-base tree dynamics are developed using Lie-group methods (SE(3) base, T^{n1}×R^{n2} joints) with spatial twists, yielding closed-form EOM, a passive Coriolis matrix, and invariant articulated inertia; demonstrated on a 12-DoF aerial manipulator with quadratic vs exponential scaling against AD baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable efficient, stable computation of high-order derivatives for control and analysis of floating-base robotic trees, maintaining physical properties (passivity, inertia invariance) while enabling analytic expressions.

Method: Extend Lie-group Newton–Euler, Articulated-Body Inertia, and hybrid dynamics algorithms to floating-base trees; derive recursive procedures for higher-order derivatives; convert to closed-form equations of motion; identify an admissible Coriolis matrix that satisfies passivity; prove articulated inertia tensor remains constant across derivatives; apply to a 12-DoF aerial manipulator to obtain analytical forward/inverse dynamics and first derivatives; validate with numerical simulations up to fifth order; benchmark computational cost against automatic differentiation.

Result: Derived closed-form equations of motion for higher-order derivatives; established a passive Coriolis matrix; shown articulated inertia tensor invariant with time; produced analytic expressions for geometric forward and inverse dynamics and their first derivatives for a 12-DoF system; simulations reach fifth-order derivatives; computational cost scales quadratically with derivative order, outperforming AD which scales exponentially.

Conclusion: The framework provides scalable, physically consistent, high-order dynamic derivatives for floating-base tree robotics, enabling efficient control and planning; it preserves key invariants and yields practical analytic expressions, with clear computational advantages over automatic differentiation as derivative order grows.

Abstract: In this paper, we describe procedures for computing higher-order time derivatives of the Lie-group Newton-Euler, Articulated-Body Inertia, and hybrid dynamics algorithms for floating-base trees, where the base configuration evolves on SE(3) and the attached mechanism is an open kinematic tree with configuration on the (n1+n2)-dimensional manifold T^{n1} \times R^{n2}, using spatial representation of twists. After presenting the algorithms, we collect the resulting recursions into closed-form equations of motion, identifying an admissible Coriolis matrix satisfying the passivity property, and showing that the articulated inertia tensor remains unchanged across all time derivatives. We then apply the developed methods to a 12-DoF aerial manipulator to derive analytical expressions for its geometric forward and inverse dynamics along with their first time derivatives whereas the numerical simulations successfully evaluate these dynamics up to fifth order. Finally, to demonstrate their practical utility, we benchmark the proposed extensions and show that, in the considered tests, their computational cost scales quadratically with the derivative order, whereas the automatic-differentiation baseline exhibits exponential scaling.

</details>


### [146] [ReActor: Reinforcement Learning for Physics-Aware Motion Retargeting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06593)
*David Müller,Agon Serifi,Sammy Christen,Ruben Grandia,Espen Knoop,Moritz Bächer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A bilevel optimization framework retargets human motion to diverse robot morphologies, jointly optimizing reference motion and a tracking policy via RL, using an approximate gradient and sparse semantic correspondences to avoid manual tuning, and integrating with physics simulation for physically plausible motions across embodiments (including quadrupeds).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retargeting human kinematics to robots is often fraught with physical inconsistencies (foot sliding, self-collisions, infeasibility) that hinder imitation learning. A unified, simulation-integrated approach is needed to produce feasible motions across varied morphologies and to train robust controllers.

Method: Formulates a bilevel optimization that (upper level) adapts reference motions to the robot’s morphology while (lower level) trains a tracking policy with reinforcement learning. An approximate gradient is derived for the upper-level loss to make optimization tractable. The method relies on sparse semantic rigid-body correspondences and includes a parameterization that self-tunes to preserve characteristic motion across embodiments. Retargeting is integrated with physics simulation to enforce physical plausibility.

Result: Demonstrates physically plausible motions and robust imitation learning in both simulation and hardware, including challenging retargeting onto morphologies far from human, such as quadrupeds.

Conclusion: The framework effectively mitigates kinematic-physical inconsistencies in retargeted motions, reduces manual tuning, and enables end-to-end learning of tracking policies across heterogeneous robot morphologies by coupling retargeting with physics-based simulation.

Abstract: Retargeting human kinematic reference motion onto a robot's morphology remains a formidable challenge. Existing methods often produce physical inconsistencies, such as foot sliding, self-collisions, or dynamically infeasible motions, which hinder downstream imitation learning. We propose a bilevel optimization framework that jointly adapts reference motions to a robot's morphology while training a tracking policy using reinforcement learning. To make the optimization tractable, we derive an approximate gradient for the upper-level loss. Our framework requires only a sparse set of semantic rigid-body correspondences and eliminates the need for manual tuning by identifying optimal values for a parameterization expressive enough to preserve characteristic motion across different embodiments. Moreover, by integrating retargeting directly with physics simulation, we produce physically plausible motions that facilitate robust imitation learning. We validate our method in simulation and on hardware, demonstrating challenging motions for morphologies that differ significantly from a human, including retargeting onto a quadruped.

</details>


### [147] [Cross-Modal Navigation with Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06595)
*Shuo Liu,Xinzichen Li,Christopher Amato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces CRONA, a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework for cross-modal navigation, using control-relevant auxiliary beliefs and a centralized multi-modal critic to enable collaboration among lightweight modality-specialized agents; achieves better performance and efficiency than single-agent baselines across visual-acoustic tasks, with insights on when homogeneous vs heterogeneous collaboration is beneficial and the importance of richer perception in larger environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenges of robust embodied navigation with multi-modal inputs, where training a single monolithic model is difficult due to complex representations and large policy spaces, and where high-quality, well-aligned multi-modal data is scarce.

Method: CRONA equips multiple lightweight agents specialized per modality, augmented with control-relevant auxiliary beliefs. A centralized multi-modal critic with access to a global state facilitates cross-modal coordination. The framework is evaluated on visual-acoustic navigation tasks with analyses of collaboration patterns (homogeneous vs heterogeneous) across task scales.

Result: Multi-agent methods significantly improve performance and efficiency over single-agent baselines. Homogeneous collaboration with limited modalities suffices for short-range navigation under salient cues; heterogeneous collaboration among complementary modalities is generally efficient and effective; large, complex environments require richer multi-modal perception and increased model capacity.

Conclusion: Cross-modal collaboration among modality-specialized agents is a scalable and effective paradigm for robust navigation. Task characteristics determine the optimal collaboration strategy and required model capacity, with richer perception becoming essential in larger environments.

Abstract: Robust embodied navigation relies on complementary sensory cues. However, high-quality and well-aligned multi-modal data is often difficult to obtain in practice. Training a monolithic model is also challenging as rich multi-modal inputs induce complex representations and substantially enlarge the policy space. Cross-modal collaboration among lightweight modality-specialized agents offers a scalable paradigm. It enables flexible deployment and parallel execution, while preserving the strength of each modality. In this paper, we propose \textbf{CRONA}, a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework for \textbf{Cro}ss-Modal \textbf{Na}vigation. CRONA improves collaboration by leveraging control-relevant auxiliary beliefs and a centralized multi-modal critic with global state. Experiments on visual-acoustic navigation tasks show that multi-agent methods significantly improve performance and efficiency over single-agent baselines. We find that homogeneous collaboration with limited modalities is sufficient for short-range navigation under salient cues; heterogeneous collaboration among agents with complementary modalities is generally efficient and effective; and navigation in large, complex environments requires both richer multi-modal perception and increased model capacity.

</details>


### [148] [Multi-Robot Coordination in V2X Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06662)
*John Pravin Arockiasamy,Alexey Vinel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a decentralized V2X framework for social robots in urban traffic, introducing Robot Awareness Service (RAS) and Robot Maneuver Coordination Service (RMCS) via RAM and RMCM to enable role-aware awareness and event-driven coordination, integrating VRUs including non-V2X pedestrians; validated through a real-world proof-of-concept and simulations, showing scalable, standards-aligned integration for Connected, Cooperative, and Automated Mobility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for decentralized, low-latency robot cooperation in urban traffic, leveraging ETSI Cooperative Awareness and Maneuver Coordination services, while ensuring integration of vulnerable road users (VRUs) and avoiding centralized infrastructure.

Method: Define two robot-centric services (RAS and RMCS) realized by RAM and RMCM; operate under established roles with event-driven coordination; integrate externally detected VRUs; real-world proof-of-concept with humanoid and quadruped robots using a finite-state coordination model; supplement with simulations evaluating VRU clustering.

Result: Deterministic multi-robot coordination demonstrated in a road-crossing scenario; VRU clustering simulations show integration of non-V2X VRUs and reduced channel load for V2X VRUs; demonstrates scalability and standards alignment.

Conclusion: The framework provides a scalable, standards-aligned foundation for integrating cooperative robots into future Connected, Cooperative, and Automated Mobility ecosystems, enabling decentralized, low-latency, role-based coordination in complex urban environments.

Abstract: This paper presents a Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication framework that enables decentralized cooperation among social robots operating in complex urban traffic environments. Building on ETSI Cooperative Awareness and Maneuver Coordination services, the framework introduces two robot-centric facility-layer services: the Robot Awareness Service (RAS) and the Robot Maneuver Coordination Service (RMCS), realized through the Robot Awareness Message (RAM) and the Robot Maneuver Coordination Message (RMCM), respectively. RAS enables role-aware, task-oriented robot awareness while integrating externally detected Vulnerable Road Users (VRUs), including non-V2X pedestrians, into cooperative awareness. RMCS supports event-driven, low-latency coordination of robot maneuvers under explicitly established roles, without centralized infrastructure or prior pairing. A real-world proof of concept demonstrates deterministic multi-robot coordination between a humanoid robot and a quadrupedal robot assisting a pedestrian during a road-crossing scenario, governed by a formally specified finite-state coordination model. Complementary simulations evaluate robot-mediated VRU clustering in mixed V2X environments, showing that RAS-based clustering integrates non-V2X VRUs in safety-critical areas while reducing redundant transmissions from V2X-enabled VRUs, thereby lowering channel load. Together, the proposed services provide a scalable and standards-aligned foundation for integrating cooperative robots into future Connected, Cooperative, and Automated Mobility ecosystems.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [149] [Geometry-Aware Simplicial Message Passing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06061)
*Elena Xinyi Wang,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geometric Simplicial WL (GSWL) extends the Weisfeiler–Lehman framework to geometric simplicial complexes by incorporating vertex coordinates, bounding the expressivity of geometry-aware simplicial message-passing networks by GSWL and showing parameter-constructibility to match GSWL on finite families; combined with the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT), this yields a complete geometric invariant and an approximation framework, with experiments validating a hierarchy from combinatorial to geometry-aware models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: WL/SWL ignore geometry; geometry-aware expressivity is needed for mesh-embedded data; establishes a principled, hierarchical understanding of geometric information in simplicial neural networks.

Method: Define Geometric Simplicial WL (GSWL) color refinement that uses vertex coordinates; prove (i) geometry-aware simplicial MPN expressivity ≤ GSWL; (ii) there exist parameters enabling these schemes to match GSWL discriminative power on any fixed finite family of geometric simplicial complexes; combine with Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT) to obtain a complete geometric expressivity characterization and an accompanying approximation framework; validate with experiments on synthetic and mesh datasets.

Result: Theoretically, bounds on expressivity and a universality-like property for matching discriminative power; practically, a framework combining GSWL with ECT that yields a complete invariant for geometric simplicial complexes and an experimental validation showing a hierarchy from combinatorial to geometry-aware models.

Conclusion: GSWL formalizes geometry-aware expressivity for simplicial networks and, together with ECT, provides a complete geometric invariant and practical approximation framework; experiments corroborate a progression from purely combinatorial to geometry-informed models.

Abstract: The Weisfeiler--Lehman (WL) test and its simplicial extension (SWL) characterize the combinatorial expressivity of message passing networks, but they are blind to geometry, i.e., meshes with identical connectivity but different embeddings are indistinguishable. We introduce the Geometric Simplicial Weisfeiler--Lehman (GSWL) test, which incorporates vertex coordinates into color refinement for geometric simplicial complexes. In addition, we show that (i) the expressivity of geometry-aware simplicial message passing schemes is bounded above by GSWL, and (ii) that there exist parameters such that the discriminating power of GSWL is matched by these schemes on any fixed finite family of geometric simplicial complexes. Combined with the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT), a complete invariant for geometric simplicial complexes, this yields a geometric expressivity characterization together with an approximation framework. Experiments on synthetic and mesh datasets serve to validate our theory, showing a clear hierarchy from combinatorial to geometry-aware models.

</details>


### [150] [Are Flat Minima an Illusion?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05209)
*Michael Timothy Bennett*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that 'weakness'—the volume of completions compatible with the learned function in the model's embodied language—is the true, reparameterisation-invariant driver of generalisation. Flat/sharp minima explanations are not causal; large-batch generalisation advantages fade with more data. Empirical evidence shows weakness predicts generalisation on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, while simplicity is dataset-dependent and often not predictive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reparameterisation can artificially inflate Hessians without changing predictions, undermining flatness-based explanations. The authors seek a robust, parameterisation-invariant account of generalisation that aligns with minimax and PAC-Bayes perspectives and behaves consistently across data regimes.

Method: Theoretically define weakness as a property of the network’s functional behavior (not its parameterisation) and show it is minimax-optimal under exchangeable demands; relate to PAC-Bayes bounds. Empirically evaluate 100 networks with identical architecture trained on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, assessing correlations between weakness, sharpness, and simplicity with generalisation. Extend with varying data sizes to test large-batch advantages.

Result: Weakness correlates positively with generalisation (MNIST: ρ=+0.374, p=1.2e-4; Fashion-MNIST: ρ=+0.384, p=8.15×10^-5). Sharpness shows a negative correlation on MNIST (ρ=-0.226). Simplicity is predictive on Fashion-MNIST but not on MNIST (p=0.848 on MNIST). The large-batch generalisation advantage vanishes with more data (from +1.6% at n=2,000 to +0.02% at n=60,000).

Conclusion: Flat minima are not the primary cause of generalisation. Weakness—an invariant to reparameterisation and tied to the network’s functional output—predicts generalisation robustly, while simplicity is dataset-dependent. This reframes generalisation research away from curvature-based explanations toward a functional, language-like notion of model weakness.

Abstract: Neural networks that land in flat regions of the loss landscape tend to generalise better than those in sharp regions. Sharpness-Aware Minimisation exploits this to improve generalisation. But function-preserving reparameterisation can inflate the Hessian of any minimum by two orders of magnitude without changing a single prediction. If the geometry of weight space can be manufactured from nothing, it cannot be the cause of anything. In other words, flat is simple and simplicity depends on encoding. Here I show that the actual driver is weakness, the volume of completions compatible with the learned function in the learner's embodied language. Weakness is reparameterisation-invariant because it is defined over what the network \emph{does}, not how it is parameterised. I prove weakness is minimax-optimal under exchangeable demands, and that PAC-Bayes bounds work because they correlate with it. On MNIST, the large-batch generalisation advantage \emph{vanishes} as training data grows, from $+1.6\%$ at $n = 2{,}000$ to $+0.02\%$ at $n = 60{,}000$. A quantity whose predictive power depends on how much data you have is not a cause but a confounder. I run head-to-heads on 100 networks with identical architecture and training. For MNIST weakness predicts generalisation ($ρ= +0.374$, $p = 0.00012$), sharpness anticorrelates ($ρ= -0.226$) and simplicity predicts nothing ($p = 0.848$). For Fashion-MNIST ($ρ= +0.384$, $p = 8.15 \times 10^{-5}$), though simplicity is at least somewhat predictive there. Simplicity is dataset dependent, whereas weakness is invariant. Flat minima were never the answer.

</details>


### [151] [Nationwide EHR-Based Chronic Rhinosinusitis Prediction Using Demographic-Stratified Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05213)
*Sicong Chang,Yidan Shen,Justina Varghese,Akshay R Prabhakar,Sebastian Guadarrama-Sistos-Vazquez,Jiefu Chen,Masayoshi Takashima,Omar G. Ahmed,Renjie Hu,Xin Fu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A nationwide EHR-based approach predicts CRS up to 2 years before diagnosis using a 100-feature model, achieving AUC 0.8461 and outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CRS presents with overlapping symptoms and heterogeneous phenotypes, making early identification difficult and prior single-institution studies potentially non-generalizable; leveraging large-scale longitudinal EHR data aims to improve population-level risk stratification.

Method: Hybrid feature selection combining prevalence-based screening with model-based importance ranking to compress ~110,000 candidate codes into 100 interpretable features. Demographic stratification across six adult sex/life-stage groups with subgroup-specific hyperparameters. Two years of pre-diagnostic EHR history used for prediction; evaluation via AUC improvement over baseline.

Result: AUC of 0.8461 achieved; improvement of 0.0168 over best baseline, indicating improved discrimination.

Conclusion: Routinely collected EHR data can support population-representative CRS risk stratification and enable earlier triage/referral in primary care; the hybrid feature-selection approach addresses sparsity and heterogeneity while yielding interpretable features.

Abstract: Chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) is a common heterogeneous inflammatory disorder that causes substantial morbidity and healthcare costs. CRS is difficult to identify early from routine encounters, as symptom presentations overlap with common conditions such as allergic rhinitis, and heterogeneous phenotypes further obscure risk patterns. Prior predictive studies often rely on single-institutional cohorts , which reduce population-level generalizability. To overcome this, we leveraged nationwide longitudinal EHR data from the \textit{All of Us} Research Program to predict CRS diagnosis using two years of pre-diagnostic history. To address extreme feature sparsity and dimensionality in coded EHR data, we implemented a hybrid feature-selection pipeline that combines prevalence-based statistical screening with model-based importance ranking, compressing approximately 110,000 candidate codes into 100 interpretable features. To capture demographic heterogeneity, we trained demographic stratified models across six adult sex and life-stage subgroups with subgroup-specific hyperparameter tuning. Our framework achieved an overall AUC of 0.8461, improving discrimination by 0.0168 over the best baseline. These results demonstrate that routinely collected EHR data may support population-representative CRS risk stratification and inform earlier triage and referral prioritization in primary care.

</details>


### [152] [SAT: Sequential Agent Tuning for Coordinator Free Plug and Play Multi-LLM Training with Monotonic Improvement Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05216)
*Yi Xie,Yangyang Xu,Yi Fan,Bo Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A coordinator-free, scalable training method for multi-agent LLM teams called Sequential Agent Tuning (SAT) that uses a factorized policy and block-coordinate updates. It provides monotonic improvement guarantees and plug-and-play invariance (upgrading agents without retraining others). Empirically, a 12B total system (three 4B agents) outperforms a 32B single model on AIME benchmarks, and swapping in higher-capacity agents yields further gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Jointly updating multiple agents causes distribution shifts across the team, making coordination and stability difficult. There is a need for scalable, decentralized training with theoretical guarantees and practical upgradeability.

Method: Represent the team as a factorized policy and optimize via block-coordinate updates (decentralized training). Introduce a sequence-aware, on-policy advantage estimator that conditions on the evolving team policy, and per-agent KL trust regions to bound occupancy drift. Prove monotonic improvement and plug-and-play invariance, enabling upgrading individual agents without retraining others.

Result: Empirically, a 12B-total SAT team (three 4B agents) surpasses Qwen3-32B on AIME24/25 by 3.9% on average. Replacing two agents with 8B models yields a 10.4% composite score boost, supporting the plug-and-play claim.
