Result: Found that relation reconstruction is highly sensitive to noise and benefits from clear schemas; feature reconstruction is robust and performs near or on par with clean graphs; contrastive objectives are less affected by noise but depend on task alignment; bidirectional relational message passing yields better robustness on noisy graphs, while unidirectional passes perform best on clean graphs; NATD-GSSL yields up to 7% improvement over LM baselines; code and benchmarks are publicly available.

Conclusion: Robust GSSL on text-driven graphs requires careful alignment between pretext tasks, GNN architecture, and downstream objectives. The NATD-GSSL framework provides practical guidance for deploying GSSL in noisy, real-world graphs and demonstrates tangible gains over baseline language models.

Abstract: Graph Self-Supervised Learning (GSSL) offers a powerful paradigm for learning graph representations without labeled data. However, existing work assumes clean, manually curated graphs. Recent advances in NLP enable the large-scale automatic extraction of knowledge graphs from text, opening new opportunities for GSSL while introducing substantial real-world noise. This type of noise remains largely unexplored, as prior robustness studies typically rely on synthetic perturbations. To address this gap, we present the first comprehensive evaluation of GSSL methods on text-driven graphs for unsupervised term typing. We introduce Noise-Aware Text-Driven Graph GSSL (NATD-GSSL), a unified framework that combines automatic graph construction, graph refinement, and GSSL. Our evaluation follows a dual-graph protocol that contrasts a noisy graph derived from MedMentions with a clean Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) reference graph, aligned through a shared gold standard. Our results reveal variability in robustness across both pretext tasks and Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures. Relation reconstruction is highly sensitive to noise and benefits from well-defined schemas, whereas feature reconstruction is considerably more robust, achieving performance comparable to clean-graph settings. Contrastive objectives are generally less affected by noise but depend strongly on alignment with downstream tasks. GNN architecture also plays a critical role: bidirectional relational message-passing designs are better suited to noisy, text-driven graphs, while unidirectional relational ones perform best on clean graphs. Overall, NATD-GSSL provides practical guidance for applying GSSL to real-world, noisy graphs and achieves up to a 7\% improvement over pretrained language model baselines. All code and benchmarks are publicly available at https://github.com/OthmaneKabal/MC2GAE.

</details>


### [180] [A Unified Benchmark for Evaluating Knowledge Graph Construction Methods and Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05476)
*Othmane Kabal,Mounira Harzallah,Fabrice Guillet,Hideaki Takeda,Ryutaro Ichise*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dual-purpose benchmark to jointly evaluate GNN performance on noisy, text-derived graphs and the quality of graph construction methods, using two automatically constructed graphs and a high-quality expert-curated reference in biomed, with semi-supervised node classification and an extensible evaluation framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knowledge graphs automatically constructed from text are increasingly used, but noise, fragmentation, and semantic inconsistencies hamper GNNs. It is hard to tell whether performance is due to the model or the graph. A standardized benchmark is needed to jointly assess graph quality and GNN robustness, especially in the biomedical domain.

Method: Construct two automatically derived biomedical graphs from a single corpus using different extraction methods; curate an expert high-quality reference graph as an upper bound; design controlled experiments for joint evaluation of graph construction and GNN performance via semi-supervised node classification; provide a standardized, reproducible, and extensible evaluation framework.

Result: The paper presents a benchmark design that enables controlled comparison of graph construction methods and systematic evaluation of GNN robustness on noisy graphs. It includes two auto-generated graphs and a reference graph, enabling evaluation bounds and extensibility, but does not report empirical results in the abstract.

Conclusion: This approach enables disentangled assessment of model versus data quality in knowledge-graph tasks, offers a domain-specific benchmark in biomed, and provides an extensible framework for integrating new extraction methods and learning models.

Abstract: Knowledge graphs automatically constructed from text are increasingly used in real-world applications. However, their inherent noise, fragmentation, and semantic inconsistencies significantly affect the performance of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on downstream tasks. Assessing their performance and robustness remains difficult, as it is often unclear whether observed results stem from the learning model or from the quality of the constructed graph itself. In this work, we introduce a dual-purpose benchmark designed to jointly evaluate (i) the performance of GNNs on noisy, text-derived graphs and (ii) the effectiveness of graph construction methods on a downstream task. The benchmark is built in the biomedical domain from a single textual corpus and includes two automatically constructed graphs generated using different extraction methods, alongside a high-quality reference graph curated by experts that serves as an upper performance bound. This design enables controlled comparison of construction methods and systematic evaluation of GNN robustness through semi-supervised node classification. We further provide a standardized, reproducible, and extensible evaluation framework, facilitating the integration of new graph extraction methods and learning models.

</details>


### [181] [Adaptive Q-Chunking for Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05544)
*Nandiraju Gireesh,Yuanliang Ju,He Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive Q-Chunking (AQC) adaptively selects action chunk sizes by comparing chunk advantages to a per-horizon baseline, normalized by discount, addressing bias and noise in fixed-chunk RL. It provides theoretical guarantees and achieves state-of-the-art results on OGBench, Robomimic, and RoboCasa-GR1 VLA tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline-to-online RL with action chunking typically uses a fixed chunk size, which is suboptimal across varying dynamics (reactive near contact vs. long-horizon motion). Naive critic comparison methods fail due to discount-scale mismatch and noise in low-value states, motivating an adaptive, principled approach.

Method: Introduce Adaptive Q-Chunking (AQC) that evaluates the advantage of each chunk size relative to a per-horizon baseline and normalizes by the discount factor, producing unbiased, discriminative choices. The authors provide theoretical bounds on noise immunity and on the value dominance of adaptive chunking over any fixed chunk size.

Result: Empirically, AQC achieves state-of-the-art offline and online performance on OGBench and Robomimic. It also enhances large-scale value-action (VLA) models predicting action sequences, improving RoboCasa-GR1 tasks.

Conclusion: AQC resolves bias and noise issues in fixed-chunk RL, enabling context-dependent chunking that improves planning and control, with strong theoretical guarantees and broad applicability across offline/online RL and sequence-prediction models.

Abstract: Offline-to-online reinforcement learning with action chunking eliminates multi-step off-policy bias and enables temporally coherent exploration, but all existing methods use a fixed chunk size across every state. This is suboptimal: near contact events the agent needs short chunks for reactive control, while during free-space motion long chunks provide better credit assignment. The natural solution is to train critics for several chunk sizes and select the best one at each state, but naive comparison of learned critic values systematically collapses to the shortest chunk due to discount-scale mismatch, and degrades to noise in low-value states. We propose Adaptive Q-Chunking (AQC), which resolves both failures by comparing the advantage of each chunk size relative to a per-horizon baseline, normalized by the discount factor. This criterion converts biased wrong answers into unbiased near-random choices when no genuine signal exists, and becomes discriminative when a particular scale enables better planning. We prove theoretical bounds on the advantage selector's noise immunity and on the value dominance of adaptive chunking over any fixed chunk size. We demonstrate that AQC achieves state-of-the-art offline and online success rates on OGBench and Robomimic, and can be applied to enhance the performance of large-scale VLA models that predict action sequences, significantly boosting performance on RoboCasa-GR1 tasks.

</details>


### [182] [GRALIS: A Unified Canonical Framework for Linear Attribution Methods via Riesz Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05480)
*Raimondo Fanale*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRALIS provides a canonical, representation-theoretic framework for deep learning attributions that subsumes SHAP, IG, LIME, and linearized GradCAM, with seven formal guarantees and a multi-scale extension; preliminary validation on BreaKHis shows improved faithfulness and consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify disparate attribution methods under a single, rigorous theory with complete guarantees (completeness, locality, interactions, convergence) and enable principled comparisons and multi-scale aggregation.

Method: Define additive, linear, and continuous attribution functionals on L^2(Q,μ) and prove a unique canonical representation (Q, w, Δ) via the Riesz Representation Theorem. The framework GRALIS (and MS-GRALIS) encompasses SHAP, IG, LIME and linearized GradCAM while excluding nonlinear functionals. Seven theorems establish guarantees: (T1) necessary canonical form; (T2) exact completeness; (T3) Monte Carlo convergence O(1/√m)+O(1/k); (T4) exact Shapley Interaction Values; (T5) Hoeffding ANOVA; (T6) Sobol generalization; (T7) multi-scale extension with minimum-variance weights. An algebraic appendix justifies GRALIS–SIV via the Mobius transform, avoiding circularity.

Result: GRALIS satisfies 13.5/14 axiomatic properties versus 2.5–6/14 for individual methods, including completeness, sensitivity, locality, order-k interactions, and optimal multi-scale aggregation. The framework provides a rigorous, unified representation and guarantees across attribution tasks; preliminary validation on BreaKHis (1,187 images, DenseNet-121) reports a +0.015 AUC deletion faithfulness for malignant predictions, 96% class-conditional consistency, SAL = 0.762±0.109, and sparsity 0.39. An extended comparison with baseline XAI methods is planned for a companion paper.

Conclusion: GRALIS offers a comprehensive theoretical unification of attribution methods with strong, nearly complete axiomatic guarantees and practical validation, setting the stage for robust, comparable XAI analyses and a forthcoming extended empirical study.

Abstract: The main XAI attribution methods for deep neural networks -- GradCAM, SHAP, LIME, Integrated Gradients -- operate on separate theoretical foundations and are not formally comparable. We present GRALIS (Gradient-Riesz Averaged Locally-Integrated Shapley), a mathematical framework establishing a representation theory for attributions: every additive, linear, and continuous attribution functional on L^2(Q,mu) admits a unique canonical representation (Q, w, Delta), proved necessary by the Riesz Representation Theorem. This class encompasses SHAP, IG, LIME and linearized GradCAM, but excludes nonlinear functionals such as standard GradCAM or attention maps. Seven formal theorems provide simultaneous guarantees absent in any individual method: (T1) necessary canonical form; (T2) exact completeness; (T3) Monte Carlo convergence O(1/sqrt(m))+O(1/k); (T4) exact Shapley Interaction Values; (T5) Hoeffding ANOVA decomposition; (T6) Sobol sensitivity generalization; (T7) multi-scale extension (MS-GRALIS) with minimum-variance weights. An algebraic appendix justifies the GRALIS-SIV correspondence via the Mobius transform without circularity. GRALIS satisfies 13.5/14 axiomatic properties vs. 2.5-6/14 for individual methods, including completeness, sensitivity, locality, order-k interactions and optimal multi-scale aggregation simultaneously. Preliminary validation on BreaKHis (1,187 histology images, DenseNet-121) reports deletion faithfulness AUC +0.015 (malignant), 96% class-conditional consistency, SAL = 0.762+/-0.109 and sparsity index 0.39. Extended comparison with baseline XAI methods is planned for a companion paper.

</details>


### [183] [Approximate Next Policy Sampling: Replacing Conservative Target Policy Updates in Deep RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05481)
*Dillon Sandhu,Ronald Parr*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Approximate Next Policy Sampling (ANPS) stabilizes RL policy updates by aligning training data with the next policy rather than constraining the policy update, using SV-API to gather data with a fixed target policy and SV-PPO to apply to PPO. This yields safe, sometimes improved, large-policy updates and strong performance on Atari and continuous tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the chicken-and-egg problem in policy improvement: value targets depend on the visited state distribution of the updated policy, which is unknown. Conservative updates shrink updates; ANPS proposes modifying training data distribution to approximate the next policy, enabling larger, safer updates.

Method: Introduce ANPS to modify the training distribution. Develop Stable Value Approximate Policy Iteration (SV-API) that fixes the target policy while a behavioral policy collects relevant data and only commits to a new policy after convergence. Provide safety guarantees: if stability criteria are met, updates are safe; otherwise, they are no less safe than standard API. Apply SV-API to PPO to obtain Stable Value PPO (SV-PPO).

Result: SV-API and SV-PPO achieve comparable or improved performance on high-dimensional discrete (Atari) and continuous control benchmarks, enabling substantially larger target policy updates while maintaining safety.

Conclusion: ANPS offers a viable alternative to conservative updates for policy improvement in RL, with SV-API/SV-PPO demonstrating practical effectiveness and safety under the proposed criteria.

Abstract: We revisit a classic "chicken-and-egg" problem in reinforcement learning: to safely improve a policy, the value function must be accurate on the state-visitation distribution of the updated policy. That distribution over states is unknown and cannot be sampled for the purposes of training the value function. Conservative updates solve this problem, but at the cost of shrinking the policy update. This paper explores an alternative solution, Approximate Next Policy Sampling (ANPS), which addresses the problem by modifying the training distribution rather than constraining the policy update. ANPS is satisfied if the distribution of the training data approximates that of the next policy. To demonstrate the feasibility and efficacy of ANPS, we introduce Stable Value Approximate Policy Iteration (SV-API). SV-API modifies the standard approximate policy iteration loop to hold the target policy fixed while an iteratively updated behavioral policy gathers relevant experience. It only commits to a new policy once a convergence criterion has been met. If certain stability criteria are met, the update is guaranteed to be safe; otherwise, it remains no less safe than standard approximate policy iteration. Applying SV-API to PPO yields Stable Value PPO (SV-PPO), which matches or improves performance on high-dimensional discrete (Atari) and continuous control benchmarks while executing substantially larger target policy updates. These results demonstrate the viability of ANPS as a new solution to this classic challenge in RL.

</details>


### [184] [A Robust Foundation Model for Conservation Laws: Injecting Context into Flux Neural Operators via Recurrent Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05488)
*Taeyoung Kim,Joon-Hyuk Ko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Augments Flux Neural Operator with ViT-based context injection via a hypernetwork to infer conservation laws without explicit PDEs, improving robustness and generalization for unseen fluxes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the need for explicit PDE coefficients in Flux NO and enhance long-time stability and generalization across unseen fluxes and conservative systems.

Method: A hypernetwork extracts solution dynamics over a short temporal window, encodes them with a recurrent Vision Transformer, and generates context-conditioned neural operator parameters for FVM-based Flux NO.

Result: The approach preserves the robustness, generalization, and long-time prediction benefits of Flux NO and delivers reliable numerical solutions across a broad range of conservative systems, including unseen fluxes.

Conclusion: ViT-based context injection via a hypernetwork extends Flux NO's capability to infer and solve conservation laws without explicit governing equations, broadening applicability to diverse conservative systems.

Abstract: We propose an architecture that augments the Flux Neural Operator (Flux NO), which combines the classical finite volume method (FVM) with neural operators, with ViT-based context injection. Our model is formulated as a hypernetwork: it extracts solution dynamics over a finite temporal window, encodes them with a recurrent Vision Transformer, and generates the parameters of a context-conditioned neural operator. This enables the model to infer and solve conservation laws without explicit access to the governing equation or PDE coefficients. Experimentally, we show that the proposed method preserves the robustness, generalization ability, and long-time prediction advantages of Flux NO over standard neural operators, while delivering reliable numerical solutions across a broad range of conservative systems, including previously unseen fluxes. Our code is available at https://github.com/xx257xx/CONTEXT_FLUX_NO.

</details>


### [185] [MEMOA: Massive Mixtures of Online Agents via Mean-Field Decentralized Nash Equilibria](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05492)
*Xuwei Yang,David B. Emerson,Fatemeh Tavakoli,Anastasis Kratsios*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Derives a unique optimal decentralized policy for large-scale federated/mean-field settings under a worst-case client criterion; shows convergence to centralized Nash-optimal policy in the large-population limit; enhances predictions via online weighting; empirical gains over greedy baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scalability and communication bottlenecks in federated learning by enabling decentralized agents that rely only on local state and a minimal mean-field summary, ensuring performance while reducing overhead.

Method: Closed-form derivation of the optimal decentralized policy under a worst-class (maximal) client regret (minimax) objective; analysis in the large-population (mean-field) limit; introduction of an online weighting mechanism to combine client predictions server-side; empirical validation.

Result: Found a unique decentralized policy; proved asymptotic convergence to the Nash-optimal centralized policy as population grows; online weighting improves both mean and worst-client predictions; experiments show the decentralized policy outperforms natural greedy baselines.

Conclusion: Decentralized mean-field policies can attain centralized Nash-like performance in large populations; the minimax objective enforces fairness to the weakest agent; online weighting further enhances ensemble quality; results support scalability of the approach.

Abstract: In the modern age of large-scale AI, federated learning has become an increasingly important tool for training large populations of AI agents; however, its computational and communication costs can rapidly fail to scale with the number of agents. This is precisely where decentralized agentic strategies shine: each agent acts autonomously, using only its own state together with a minimal summary of the ensemble, namely the mean-field. We derive the unique optimal decentralized policy in closed form. Optimality is characterized through a worst-client/minimax criterion: minimizing the under-performer regret, namely the maximal online cost incurred by the weakest agent in the ensemble. We further prove that the resulting decentralized policy asymptotically converges, in the large-population limit, to the Nash-optimal centralized policy, whose direct computation is not scalable. We use an online weighting mechanism to optimize the server-computed mixture of client predictions, thereby improving the mean prediction in addition to the previously optimized weakest-client prediction. Numerical experiments verify our theoretical guarantees and demonstrate that our decentralized policy typically outperforms natural greedy decentralized baselines.

</details>


### [186] [Shortcut Solutions Learned by Transformers Impair Continual Compositional Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05495)
*William T. Redman,Erik C. Johnson,Brian Robinson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study extends LEGO to continual learning and shows that ALBERT (recurrent Transformer) exhibits better continual learning behavior than BERT (feedforward), but both struggle with cross-experience composition; data-sharing strategies help ALBERT but not BERT, suggesting architecture-induced inductive biases in continual learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how Transformer architectures and learned representations support continual learning and compositional reasoning across related experiences, and to identify inductive biases that enable or hinder transfer.

Method: Extend the LEGO framework to a continual learning setting (continual LEGO) and evaluate feedforward (BERT) and recurrent (ALBERT) Transformer models on tasks requiring learning across multiple experiences, including composition across experiences; assess transfer, shortcut solutions, and the impact of cross-experience data sharing.

Result: BERT tends to learn shortcut solutions that limit generalization and forward transfer; ALBERT appears to learn a For loop–like solution, yielding better continual learning performance. However, when required to compose across experiences, both models fail. ALBERT's performance can be rescued by training strategies that mix data across experiences, whereas BERT's detrimental shortcut becomes entrenched and unmitigated by such strategies.

Conclusion: Recurrent ALBERT may have inductive biases better aligned with continual learning, motivating further study of how Transformer architecture shapes the computational strategies that models develop in continual learning and tasks requiring cross-experience composition.

Abstract: Identifying and exploiting common features across domains is at the heart of the human ability to make analogies, and is believed to be crucial for the ability to continually learn. To do this successfully, general and flexible computational strategies must be developed. While the extent to which Transformer neural network models can perform compositional reasoning has been the subject of intensive recent investigation, little work has been done to systematically understand how well these models can leverage their representations to learn new, related experiences. To address this gap, we expand the previously developed Learning Equality and Group Operations (LEGO) framework to a continual learning (CL) setting ("continual LEGO"). Using this continual LEGO experimental paradigm, we study the capability of feedforward and recurrent Transformer models to perform CL. We find that BERT, a canonical feedforward Transformer model, learns shortcut solutions that limits its ability to generalize and prevents strong forward transfer to new experiences. In contrast, we find evidence supporting the hypothesis that ALBERT, a recurrent version of BERT, learns a For loop-esque solution, which leads to better CL performance. When applying BERT and ALBERT models to a CL setting that requires composition across experiences, we find that both model families fail. Our investigation suggests that ALBERT models can have their performance drop rescued by use of training strategies that combine data across experiences, but this is not true for BERT models, where a detrimental shortcut solution becomes entrenched with initial training. Our results demonstrate that the recurrent ALBERT model may have an inductive bias better suited for CL and motivate future investigation of the interplay between Transformer architecture and computational solutions that emerge in modern models and tasks.

</details>


### [187] [Structure-Preserving Gaussian Processes Via Discrete Euler-Lagrange Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06246)
*Jan-Hendrik Ewering,Kathrin Flaßkamp,Niklas Wahlström,Thomas B. Schön,Thomas Seel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) learn energy-consistent dynamics from sparse position data using discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations, enabling uncertainty-aware, long-horizon predictions without velocity measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need data-efficient, physically accurate dynamic models that preserve geometric structure and work with only position observations in real-world robotics/vision applications.

Method: Construct linear GP conditioning operators from discretized forced Euler-Lagrange equations and variational discretization; learn dynamics from position snapshots by enforcing Lagrangian structure; handle absence of external forces by design.

Result: Demonstrates data efficiency, generalization, and stable long-term predictions on synthetic and real-world tasks, including a hysteretic soft robot, with uncertainty quantification.

Conclusion: LGPs provide physically consistent, uncertainty-aware dynamics learned from sparse data and maintain stability over long horizons; useful for motion capture and visual servoing where velocity data is unavailable.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) for probabilistic and data-efficient learning of dynamics via discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations. Importantly, the geometric structure of the Lagrange-d'Alembert principle, which governs the motion of dynamical systems, is preserved by construction in the absence of external forces. This allows learning physically consistent models that overcome erroneous drift in the system's energy, thereby providing stable long-term predictions. At the core of our approach lie linear operators for Gaussian process conditioning, constructed from discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations and variational discretization schemes. Thereby and unlike prior work, the method enables learning dynamics from discrete position snapshots, i.e., without access to a system's velocities or momenta. This is particularly relevant for a large class of practical scenarios where only position measurements are available, for instance, in motion capture or visual servoing applications. We demonstrate the data-efficiency and generalization capabilities of the LGPs in various synthetic and real-world case studies, including a real-world soft robot with hysteresis. The experimental results underscore that the LGPs learn physically consistent dynamics with uncertainty quantification solely from sparse positional data and enable stable long-term predictions.

</details>


### [188] [Online Localized Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05497)
*Yuheng Lai,Garvesh Raskutti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Online Localized Conformal Prediction (OLCP) and OLCP-Hedge for non-exchangeable online data: localizes calibration to covariates and uses online expert aggregation for bandwidth; provides valid long-run coverage with narrower prediction sets than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In online learning/time-series, exchangeability fails and global calibration (as in adaptive conformal inference, ACI) is inefficient under covariate heterogeneity. There is a need for covariate-aware, locally calibrated conformal prediction with theoretical guarantees.

Method: OLCP combines online adaptation with covariate-dependent localization for prediction sets. OLCP-Hedge introduces online bandwidth selection via constrained online convex optimization, treating bandwidth choices as online experts; aggregation yields robust, data-driven calibration.

Result: Both methods provide coverage guarantees and, in simulations and real-data experiments, achieve valid long-run coverage with narrower (more efficient) prediction sets than existing baselines.

Conclusion: The paper introduces two practical, theoretically-grounded online conformal prediction methods that address non-exchangeable data and covariate heterogeneity, offering improved efficiency while maintaining validity; OLCP-Hedge adds data-driven bandwidth selection through online optimization.

Abstract: Conformal prediction is a framework that provides valid uncertainty quantification for general models with exchangeable data. However, in the online learning and time-series settings, exchangeability is not satisfied. Existing online conformal methods, such as adaptive conformal inference (ACI), can achieve long-run validity, yet they remain inefficient under covariate heterogeneity because they rely on global calibration. We propose \emph{Online Localized Conformal Prediction (OLCP)}, which combines online adaptation with covariate-dependent localization to better reflect heterogeneity. To reduce sensitivity to the localization bandwidth, we further develop \emph{OLCP-Hedge}, which performs bandwidth selection as an online expert aggregation problem using a constrained online convex optimization framework. Importantly, we provide coverage guarantees for both algorithms and demonstrate through simulations and real-data experiments that the proposed methods attain valid long-run coverage with narrower prediction sets than existing baselines.

</details>


### [189] [Non-Myopic Active Feature Acquisition via Pathwise Policy Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05511)
*Linus Aronsson,Morteza Haghir Chehreghani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-myopic pathwise policy gradients for active feature acquisition (AFA) using continuous relaxation and straight-through rollout to enable end-to-end optimization and lower-variance gradient estimates, achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AFA incurs feature costs and requires sequential decision-making under uncertainty; POMDP provides a natural framework; existing non-myopic methods face high gradient variance with score-function estimators; need training-time and deployment-time alignment.

Method: NM-PPG: non-myopic pathwise policy gradient. Continuous relaxation of acquisition events to allow pathwise (reparameterized) gradients through the acquisition trajectory; straight-through rollout: forward pass with hard acquisitions, backward pass through soft relaxation; entropy regularization and staged temperature sharpening to stabilize training.

Result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets shows NM-PPG outperforms state-of-the-art AFA baselines.

Conclusion: End-to-end pathwise optimization with non-myopic planning improves AFA performance; the continuous relaxation plus straight-through approach reduces gradient variance and aligns training with deployment; method is robust across datasets.

Abstract: Active feature acquisition (AFA) considers prediction problems in which features are costly to obtain and the learner adaptively decides which feature values to acquire for each instance and when to stop and predict. AFA can be formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP), which naturally admits a sequential decision-making perspective. In this paper, we present non-myopic pathwise policy gradients (NM-PPG), a new AFA method built around this formulation. We introduce a continuous relaxation of the acquisition process that enables pathwise gradients through the full acquisition trajectory, avoiding the high variance of standard score-function policy gradients while allowing end-to-end optimization of a non-myopic acquisition policy. To better align training with deployment, we further develop a straight-through rollout scheme that follows hard feature acquisitions in the forward pass while backpropagating through the corresponding soft relaxation in the backward pass. We stabilize optimization with entropy regularization and staged temperature sharpening. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that NM-PPG yields superior performance relative to state-of-the-art AFA baselines.

</details>


### [190] [OpenG2G: A Simulation Platform for AI Datacenter-Grid Runtime Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05519)
*Jae-Won Chung,Zhirui Liang,Yanyong Mao,Jiasi Chen,Mosharaf Chowdhury,Vladimir Dvorkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OpenG2G is a modular simulation platform that enables comparative study of AI datacenter–grid coordination under realistic workloads, combining production-scale datacenter measurements with high-fidelity grid models to evaluate classic, optimization, and learning-based controllers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rising AI compute demand and datacenter growth stress electricity grids, causing long interconnection delays and reliability concerns. Rapid datacenter power flexibility and coordinated control are needed to alleviate grid bottlenecks and support AI deployment at scale.

Method: Develop OpenG2G with a datacenter backend driven by real production AI service measurements, a grid backend built on high-fidelity grid simulators, and a generic controller interface to close the loop. Enable the implementation and comparison of diverse control paradigms (classic, optimization, learning-based) across modular components.

Result: OpenG2G demonstrates capability to answer a wide range of coordination questions, quantify how AI model and deployment choices affect datacenter flexibility and coordination outcomes, and validate the approach using realistic grid scenarios and AI workloads.

Conclusion: OpenG2G is a versatile, extensible tool for studying AI datacenter–grid coordination, facilitating design of effective coordination strategies and enabling researchers to explore the impact of workload and model choices on grid performance.

Abstract: AI's growing compute demand and new datacenter buildouts present major capacity and reliability challenges for the electricity grid, leading to multi-year interconnection delays for new datacenters and bottlenecking AI growth. To ease this strain, datacenters increasingly offer rapid power flexibility in response to grid signals, where the datacenter can increase or decrease its power consumption by adapting its workload in real time.
  In order to understand the impact of large datacenters on the grid and to facilitate the design of effective coordination strategies, we build OpenG2G, a simulation platform for AI datacenter-grid runtime coordination. We show that OpenG2G is capable of answering a wide range of coordination questions by allowing users to implement and compare various control paradigms (including classic, optimization, and learning-based controllers), and quantify how AI model and deployment choices affect datacenter flexibility and coordination outcomes. This versatility is enabled by OpenG2G's modular and extensible architecture: a datacenter backend driven by real measurements of production-grade AI services, a grid backend built on high-fidelity grid simulators, and a generic controller interface that closes the loop between them. We describe the design of OpenG2G and demonstrate its usefulness through realistic grid scenarios and AI workloads.

</details>


### [191] [MOSAIC: Module Discovery via Sparse Additive Identifiable Causal Learning for Scientific Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05524)
*Shicheng Fan,Nour Elhendawy,Jianle Sun,Ke Fang,Kun Zhang,Yihang Wang,Lu Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MOSAIC combines regime-conditioned temporal VAE with a sparse additive decoder to align latent factors with observed semantic variables, enabling module-level interpretability and finite-sample guarantees for observable-associated latent structure in scientific time series.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identifiability of latent factors in causal representation learning does not guarantee interpretability. Scientific data have inherent semantics in observations, so aligning latent structure to observed quantities yields more meaningful, domain-relevant factors.

Method: Propose MOSAIC, a sparse temporal VAE that uses regime-conditioned identification of latent variables, and recovers for each latent a sparse set of associated observations via an additive decoder. The approach yields module-level interpretability. Theoretical results include identifiability of ANOVA main-effect supports under general smooth mixing, and finite-sample recovery guarantees for a tractable sparse-additive variant. Empirical validation across diverse scientific domains.

Result: Identifiability of latent factors up to permutation with interpretability achieved via sparse-additive decoding linking latents to observed variables. ANOVA main-effects are identifiable under broad smoothing assumptions; finite-sample recovery guarantees for the sparse-additive variant. Demonstrated across RNA molecular dynamics, solar wind, ENSO climate, Tennessee Eastman process, and a synthetic tokamak benchmark, enabling interpretable discovery of latent mechanisms in scientific time series.

Conclusion: MOSAIC provides a principled framework to transfer observation semantics to latent representations, achieving interpretable, domain-aligned latent factors with theoretical identifiability guarantees and practical utility across multiple scientific domains.

Abstract: Causal representation learning (CRL) seeks to recover latent variables with identifiability guarantees, typically up to permutation and component-wise reparameterization under appropriate assumptions. However, identifiability does not imply interpretability: latent semantics are typically assigned post hoc by alignment with known ground-truth factors. This limitation is particularly acute in scientific time series, where underlying mechanisms are unknown and discovering interpretable structure is a primary goal. In contrast, scientific observations (such as residue-pair distances, climate indices, or process sensors) are inherently semantic, as they correspond to named physical quantities. This raises a key question: can the interpretability of observations be transferred to the identifiable latent space? We propose MOSAIC (Module discovery via Sparse Additive Identifiable Causal learning), a sparse temporal VAE that integrates temporal CRL identifiability with support recovery over observed variables. MOSAIC identifies latent variables via regime-conditioned temporal variation, and recovers for each latent a sparse set of associated observations through an additive decoder, yielding module-level interpretability. We show that ANOVA main-effect supports are identifiable under general smooth mixing functions, and provide finite-sample recovery guarantees for a tractable sparse-additive variant. Empirically, MOSAIC recovers domain-consistent variable groups across RNA molecular dynamics, solar wind, ENSO climate, the Tennessee Eastman process, and a synthetic tokamak benchmark, enabling interpretable discovery of latent mechanisms in scientific time series.

</details>


### [192] [Energy Generative Modeling: A Lyapunov-based Energy Matching Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05530)
*Yixuan Wang,Wenqian Xue,Warren E. Dixon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unifies training and sampling for static scalar energy-based generative models as density transport on Wasserstein space, using KL divergence as a Lyapunov function. Provides finite-step stopping for Langevin sampling, shows no Lyapunov certificate for deterministic gradient flow, and demonstrates that additive energy compositions preserve Gibbs invariance and Lyapunov properties. Connects static energy models with nonlinear control theory and suggests barrier and contraction tools; validated on synthetic distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge static energy-based generative modeling with the full toolkit of nonlinear control theory, enabling principled convergence criteria, and enabling new capabilities (e.g., constrained generation, accelerated sampling) by treating training and sampling as manifestations of a single master dynamics.

Method: Formulates density transport on the Wasserstein space as a nonlinear control problem, with KL divergence serving as a Lyapunov function. Treats training and sampling as two instances of the same master dynamics differing only by initial condition. Proves a finite-step stopping criterion for Langevin sampling and shows absence of a Lyapunov certificate for the deterministic gradient flow. Demonstrates that additive composition of trained scalar energies preserves a Gibbs invariant measure and inherits the closed-loop Lyapunov certificate. Discusses implications for barrier functions and contraction metrics.

Result: Derives practical stopping criterion for Langevin sampling within the Lyapunov framework; proves nonexistence of Lyapunov certificate for gradient flow on the energy landscape; shows that combining energies maintains Gibbs measure and Lyapunov properties; introduces a bridge to nonlinear control tools and potential for constrained generation and faster sampling. Experimental validation on synthetic distributions corroborates the theoretical predictions.

Conclusion: Reformulating static scalar energy generative models within nonlinear control theory yields rigorous convergence and invariance guarantees, extends the design space with composition and control tools, and empirically validates the theory on synthetic data, thereby linking energy-based modeling with the full nonlinear control toolbox.

Abstract: Generative models based on static scalar energy functions represent an emerging paradigm in which a single time independent potential drives sample generation through its gradient field, eliminating the need for time conditioning entirely. We unify the training and sampling phases of this paradigm, conventionally treated as separate procedures, within a single framework: density transport on the Wasserstein space, cast as a nonlinear control problem in which the Kullback Leibler (KL) divergence serves as a Lyapunov function. Training and sampling are then two instances of this same master dynamics, differing only in initial condition. Within this autonomous framework we develop two analytic results. First, since the Lyapunov certificate is asymptotic, we derive a finite step stopping criterion for Langevin sampling and prove that no Lyapunov certificate exists for the deterministic gradient flow on the same energy landscape. Second, the reformulation brings the toolkit of nonlinear control theory to bear on static scalar energy generative modeling, that is, we show that additive composition of trained scalar energies retains an explicit Gibbs invariant measure and inherits the closed-loop Lyapunov certificate. Beyond these immediate results, this reformulation bridges static scalar energy generative models with the full toolkit of nonlinear control theory, opening the door to barrier functions for constrained generation and contraction metrics for accelerated sampling. Experiments on synthetic distributions validate the theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [193] [Adversarial Graph Neural Network Benchmarks: Towards Practical and Fair Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05534)
*Tran Gia Bao Ngo,Zulfikar Alom,Federico Errica,Murat Kantarcioglu,Cuneyt Gurcan Akcora*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Standardized, large-scale benchmark reveals that results in adversarial GNN studies are highly sensitive to evaluation protocols; fair, robust procedures alter attack/defense rankings, underscoring the need for standardized evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field lacks consistent experimental settings across adversarial GNN evaluations, leading to ambiguous or conflicting conclusions about robustness.

Method: Re-evaluate seven widely used attacks and eight defenses under both poisoning and evasion, across six graph datasets, totaling 453k experiments within a unified evaluation framework; examine factors like target node selection and training procedure of the attacked model.

Result: Significant differences in attack effectiveness emerge under fair evaluation; previously overlooked factors drastically affect outcomes, potentially distorting prior conclusions about attack/defense efficacy.

Conclusion: Highlights the urgent need for standardized evaluation protocols in adversarial graph machine learning to ensure reliable and comparable results.

Abstract: Adversarial learning and the robustness of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are topics of widespread interest in the machine learning community, as documented by the number of adversarial attacks and defenses designed for these purposes. While a rigorous evaluation of these adversarial methods is necessary to understand the robustness of GNNs in real-world applications, we posit that many works in the literature do not share the same experimental settings, leading to ambiguous and potentially contradictory scientific conclusions. In this benchmark, we demonstrate the importance of adopting fair, robust, and standardized evaluation protocols in adversarial GNN research. We perform a comprehensive re-evaluation of seven widely used attacks and eight recent defenses under both poisoning and evasion scenarios, across six popular graph datasets. Our study spans over 453,000 experiments conducted within a unified framework. We observe substantial differences in adversarial attack performance when evaluated under a fair and robust procedure. Our findings reveal that previously overlooked factors, such as target node selection and the training process of the attacked model, have a profound impact on attack effectiveness, to the extent of completely distorting performance insights. These results underscore the urgent need for standardized evaluations in adversarial graph machine learning.

</details>


### [194] [Towards Scalable One-Step Generative Modeling for Autoregressive Dynamical System Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05540)
*Tianyue Yang,Xiao Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MeLISA is a latent-free autoregressive surrogate for high-dimensional turbulence, using pixel-space MeanFlow with a blockwise stochastic transition kernel. It stabilizes long-horizon rollouts via Window-Consistency MeanFlow and Time Increment Consistency losses, evaluated on 256x256 Kolmogorov flow and 192x192 turbulent channel-flow. It outperforms neural-operator baselines in short-term and long-horizon metrics, with inference speeds comparable or faster, and parameter-efficient variants up to 150M parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve fast, accurate long-horizon forecasts for high-dimensional turbulent dynamics, addressing limitations of neural operators (drift in turbulence) and latent diffusion surrogates (inference cost and complexity).

Method: Introduce MeLISA, a latent-free autoregressive surrogate built on pixel-space MeanFlow. Use a blockwise stochastic transition kernel generating each forecast block with a single model evaluation. Avoid latent encoders and iterative diffusion at inference. Add Window-Consistency MeanFlow objective for conditional spatiotemporal generation from partial temporal windows and Time Increment Consistency loss to constrain multi-lag increments and preserve temporal correlation. Evaluate with compact UNet and scalable DiT backbones on two high-res benchmarks.

Result: MeLISA outperforms neural-operator baselines in short-term forecast accuracy and long-horizon statistics (energy spectra, turbulent kinetic energy, mixing-rate dynamics). Inference speeds comparable to or faster than neural operators. Parameter-efficient variants (3.7–5.7M params); DiT versions scalable to ~150M params. Showcases improved rollout efficiency and long-horizon statistical fidelity.

Conclusion: MeLISA provides a scalable, efficient latent-free autoregressive surrogate that maintains temporal statistical structure while offering competitive or superior performance and inference speed, with strong parameter efficiency.

Abstract: Fast surrogate modeling for high-dimensional physical dynamics requires more than low short-term error: useful models must roll out efficiently while preserving the statistical structure of long trajectories. Neural operators provide inexpensive autoregressive forecasts but can drift in turbulent regimes, whereas rolling diffusion and latent generative surrogates can represent stochastic transitions at the cost of multi-step denoising, noise-schedule design, or auxiliary compression models. We propose MeanFlow Long-term Invariant Spatiotemporal Consistency Autoregressive Models (MeLISA), a latent-free autoregressive generative surrogate built on pixel-space MeanFlow. MeLISA defines a blockwise stochastic transition kernel that generates each forecast block with a single model evaluation, avoiding latent encoders and iterative diffusion solvers at inference time. To stabilize long-horizon rollouts, MeLISA combines a Window-Consistency MeanFlow objective that learns conditional spatiotemporal generation from partially observed temporal windows with a Time Increment Consistency loss that constrains multi-lag finite increments and targets temporal-correlation structure. We evaluate MeLISA with compact UNet and scalable DiT backbones on two high-resolution benchmarks, extended 2D Kolmogorov flow at $256 \times 256$ and turbulent channel-flow slice at $192 \times 192$. MeLISA outperforms neural-operator baselines on short-term forecasting accuracy and long-horizon statistical metrics, including energy spectra, turbulent kinetic energy, and mixing-rate-related dynamics, while achieving inference speeds comparable to, and in some cases faster than, neural operators. Compact 3.7-5.7M-parameter variants already deliver strong parameter efficiency, and DiT variants provide a scalable path up to 150M parameters. Overall, MeLISA benefits both rollout efficiency and long-horizon statistical accuracy.

</details>


### [195] [FedeKD: Energy-Based Gating for Robust Federated Knowledge Distillation under Heterogeneous Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05553)
*Quang-Huy Nguyen,Jiaqi Wang,Wei-shinn Ku*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedeKD is a reliability-aware federated knowledge distillation framework that uses sample-wise trust to guide knowledge transfer without public data, improving robustness in heterogeneous FL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In federated learning, data heterogeneity causes negative transfer. FKD avoids sharing model parameters but relies on public data or assumes uniform transfer quality, which is fragile. A framework that estimates sample-level trust and weights transfer accordingly can mitigate negative transfer without extra data.

Method: Clients maintain high-capacity private models for local learning and lightweight proxy models for cross-client knowledge exchange. Proxies are aggregated on the server to form a global proxy, which guides updates to private models. An energy-based gating mechanism converts private-proxy disagreement into sample-wise trust weights for backward distillation, enabling weighted knowledge transfer without public data.

Result: Extensive experiments on six real-world datasets show that FedeKD significantly reduces negative transfer under heterogeneous settings while maintaining strong predictive performance.

Conclusion: FedeKD introduces a sample-wise reliability mechanism via energy-based gating to gate knowledge transfer in FKD, offering robustness to heterogeneity without public data and strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) operates in heterogeneous environments, where variations in data distributions and asymmetric model design often result in negative transfer. While federated knowledge distillation (FKD) avoids direct model parameter sharing, existing methods typically rely on public datasets or assume that transferred knowledge is uniformly reliable, which limits their robustness in practice. This paper presents FedeKD, a reliability-aware FKD framework that makes sample-wise trust estimation an explicit component of knowledge transfer, without relying on additional public data. Each client maintains a high-capacity private model for local learning and a lightweight shared proxy model for cross-client knowledge exchange. During training, proxy models are aggregated on the server to form a global proxy, which is then used to guide updates of the private models. At the core of FedeKD is an energy-based gating mechanism that converts task-specific private-proxy disagreement into sample-wise trust weights for backward distillation. This mechanism enables sample-wise weighting of knowledge transfer, where the proxy model contributes more to reliable samples while down-weighting unreliable ones. Extensive experiments on six real-world datasets demonstrate that FedeKD significantly reduces negative transfer under heterogeneous settings while maintaining strong predictive performance.

</details>


### [196] [Accelerating LMO-Based Optimization via Implicit Gradient Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05577)
*Won-Jun Jang,Si-Hyeon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces LMO-IGT, a stochastic LMO method using implicit gradient transport, unifying LMO theory and introducing RSF as a common stationarity metric; achieves faster iteration complexity with minimal per-iteration cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inefficiencies of variance-reduced LMO methods (high gradient evaluation overhead) and the fragmented theory between unconstrained and constrained LMO formulations; provide a unified, single-gradient-per-iteration framework with acceleration.

Method: Propose LMO-IGT by transporting gradients via implicit gradient transport; develop a unified stochastic LMO framework and a new stationarity measure called the regularized support function (RSF); evaluate gradients at transported points to accelerate convergence while preserving single-gradient-per-iteration structure.

Result: Theoretical iteration complexities: standard stochastic LMO O(ε^-4); variance-reduced LMO O(ε^-3) but with extra gradient evaluations; LMO-IGT O(ε^-3.5) using only a single stochastic gradient per iteration. Empirically, LMO-IGT improves over stochastic LMO with negligible overhead; Muon-IGT yields strongest performance across tested settings.

Conclusion: Implicit gradient transport provides a practical acceleration mechanism for LMO-based optimization, enabling a unified framework and improved convergence with minimal extra cost; Muon-IGT offers the best overall results among instantiated methods.

Abstract: Recent optimizers such as Lion and Muon have demonstrated strong empirical performance by normalizing gradient momentum via linear minimization oracles (LMOs). While variance reduction has been explored to accelerate LMO-based methods, it typically incurs substantial computational overhead due to additional gradient evaluations. At the same time, the theoretical understanding of LMO-based methods remains fragmented across unconstrained and constrained formulations. Motivated by these limitations, we propose \emph{LMO-IGT}, a new class of stochastic LMO-based methods leveraging implicit gradient transport (IGT). We further introduce a unified framework for stochastic LMO-based optimization together with a new stationarity measure, the \emph{regularized support function} (RSF), which bridges gradient-norm and Frank--Wolfe-gap notions within a common framework. By evaluating stochastic gradients at transported points, LMO-IGT accelerates convergence while retaining the single-gradient-per-iteration structure of standard stochastic LMO. Our analysis establishes that stochastic LMO achieves an iteration complexity of $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-4})$, variance-reduced LMO achieves $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-3})$ at the cost of additional gradient evaluations, and LMO-IGT achieves $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-3.5})$ using only a single stochastic gradient per iteration. Empirically, LMO-IGT consistently improves over stochastic LMO counterparts with negligible overhead. Among its instantiations, Muon-IGT achieves the strongest overall performance across evaluated settings, demonstrating that IGT provides an effective and practical acceleration mechanism for modern LMO-based optimization.

</details>


### [197] [AeroJEPA: Learning Semantic Latent Representations for Scalable 3D Aerodynamic Field Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05586)
*Francisco Giral,Abhijeet Vishwasrao,Andrea Arroyo Ramo,Mahmoud Golestanian,Federica Tonti,Adrian Lozano-Duran,Steven L. Brunton,Sergio Hoyas,Hector Gomez,Soledad Le Clainche,Ricardo Vinuesa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AeroJEPA: a latent-prediction surrogate framework for aerodynamic fields that decouples latent prediction from field resolution, enabling scalable high-resolution surrogates and semantically meaningful latent representations; validated on HiLiftAeroML and SuperWing with downstream design-analytic capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current surrogates struggle with very large 3D aerodynamic fields and rarely yield latent representations directly useful for analysis and design; there is a need for scalable, interpretable latent spaces that support design tasks.

Method: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture: use a context latent for geometry/operating conditions to predict a target latent of the flow, optionally reconstructing the field with a continuous implicit decoder; decouples latent prediction from field resolution and fosters semantically organized latent space; evaluated on HiLiftAeroML and SuperWing.

Result: AeroJEPA is competitive as a continuous surrogate, scales to high-resolution outputs, and learns latent representations encoding geometry and aerodynamic quantities beyond supervision; supports controlled interpolation, linear probing, concept-vector arithmetic, and constrained design latent optimization.

Conclusion: Predictive latent learning is a promising direction for scalable, design-meaningful aerodynamic surrogate modeling; AeroJEPA demonstrates practical benefits and latent-space utilities for design tasks.

Abstract: Aerodynamic surrogate models are increasingly used to replace repeated high-fidelity CFD evaluations in many-query design settings, but current approaches still face two important limitations: they often scale poorly to the very large fields arising in realistic 3D aerodynamics, and they rarely produce latent representations that are directly useful for analysis and design. We introduce AeroJEPA, a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for aerodynamic field modeling that addresses both issues. Rather than predicting the full flow field directly from geometry, AeroJEPA predicts a target latent representation of the flow from a context latent representation of the geometry and operating conditions, and optionally reconstructs the field through a continuous implicit decoder. This formulation decouples latent prediction from field resolution while encouraging the latent space to organize semantically. We evaluate AeroJEPA on two complementary datasets: HiLiftAeroML, which stresses the method in a high-fidelity regime with extremely large boundary-layer fields, and SuperWing, which tests large-scale generalization and latent-space optimization over a broad family of transonic wings. Across these benchmarks, AeroJEPA is competitive as a continuous surrogate for aerodynamic fields, scales naturally to high-resolution outputs, and learns context and predicted latents that encode geometry and aerodynamic quantities not used directly as supervision. We further show that the resulting latent space supports controlled interpolation, linear probing, concept-vector arithmetic, and a constrained design latent-optimization experiment. These results suggest that predictive latent learning is a promising direction for scalable and design-meaningful aerodynamic surrogate modeling.

</details>


### [198] [When Can Voting Help, Hurt, or Change Course? Exact Structure of Binary Test-Time Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05592)
*Yi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Voting under exchangeability can yield nonmonotone curves driven by latent per-example correctness; the curve encodes a signed voting signature; odd-budget curve and latent law are linked via signed Hausdorff moments; per-example data reveals the full signature, while grouped labels reveal only a finite prefix.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why simple majority voting over a fixed predictor can behave nonmonotonically and to formalize identifiability and estimation under exchangeable repeated correctness.

Method: Adopt the de Finetti representation for exchangeable correctness, construct latent mixture models for per-example accuracy, derive the resulting voting curves, define a signed voting signature, and prove a theorem equating the odd-budget curve with the signature via signed Hausdorff moments; discuss identifiability and estimation regimes.

Result: Demonstrates that voting curves can be nonmonotone with infinite trend changes; the latent law is not determined by the curve while the curve is determined by the latent law. The increments of the odd-budget curve are signed Hausdorff moments, and the curve uniquely recovers the signed voting signature. Provides explanations for shape phenomena, nonidentifiability, realizability, variation, and endpoint behavior; shows a separation of estimation regimes where per-example success probabilities reveal the full signature while fixed-depth grouped labels reveal only a finite prefix.

Conclusion: The voting process reveals a latent signature rather than a direct latent law; the odd-budget curve and the signed signature are equivalent, enabling precise characterization of what voting can recover and clarifying identifiability and estimation limits; direct per-example data yields full latent information, whereas grouped-label data yield limited, prefix information.

Abstract: Majority voting is one of the few black-box interventions that can improve a fixed stochastic predictor: repeated access can be cheaper than changing a high-capability model. Classical fixed-competence theory makes this intervention look monotone -- more votes help above the majority threshold and hurt below it. We show that this picture is fundamentally incomplete. Under the de Finetti representation for exchangeable repeated correctness, voting is governed by a latent distribution of per-example correctness probabilities. Even simple latent mixtures can generate sharply different voting curves, including nonmonotone behavior and, in an explicit construction, infinitely many trend changes. The full latent law determines the curve, but the curve does not determine the law. The exact object recovered by voting is a signed voting signature: at each binomial variance scale, it records excess latent mass above rather than below the majority threshold. Our main theorem proves that the complete odd-budget curve and this signature are equivalent: the curve increments are signed Hausdorff moments, and the full curve recovers the signature uniquely. This viewpoint explains shape phenomena, branch-symmetric nonidentifiability, realizability, variation, and endpoint rates. It also separates estimation regimes: direct per-example success-probability information targets the full signature, whereas fixed-depth grouped labels reveal only a finite prefix.

</details>


### [199] [Optimal Contextual Pricing under Agnostic Non-Lipschitz Demand](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05609)
*Jianyu Xu,Yu-Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a polynomial-time algorithm (Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing) for contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations under bounded-support agnostic noise causing non-Lipschitz, discontinuous demand; achieves ~O(T^{2/3}) regret, matching Kleinberg–Leighton (2003) lower bounds up to log factors and surpassing the prior ~O(T^{3/4}) bound; resolves the regret gap under stochastic well-conditioned contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discontinuous, non-Lipschitz demand curves due to noise destroy cross-context interpolation used by smooth-demand pricing; there is a known O(T^{3/4}) upper bound and a Kleinberg-Leighton lower bound; gap remains in linear-valuation contextual pricing with agnostic noise; need for algorithm that handles jumps.

Method: Introduces Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing combining randomized parameter estimation, conservative residual-grid probing, and confidence-based one-step redirection; ensures polynomial-time computation and robust performance against discontinuities.

Result: Achieves ~O(T^{2/3}) regret; matches lower bounds up to log factors; improves the previous O(T^{3/4}) bound of Xu & Wang (2022).

Conclusion: Under stochastic well-conditioned contexts, closes the open regret gap; advances understanding of contextual pricing with agnostic non-Lipschitz noise; provides near-optimal, efficient algorithm.

Abstract: We study contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations and bounded-support agnostic noise, whose induced demand curve may be non-Lipschitz with arbitrary jumps and atoms. Such discontinuities break the cross-context interpolation arguments used by smooth-demand pricing algorithms, while the best previous method achieved only $\tilde O(T^{3/4})$ regret. We propose Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing, a polynomial-time algorithm that combines randomized parameter estimation, conservative residual-grid probing, and confidence-based one-step redirection. Our algorithm achieves $\tilde O(T^{2/3})$ optimal regret, matching the known lower bounds of Kleinberg and Leighton (2003) up to logarithmic factors and improving over the previous upper bound of Xu and Wang (2022). Under stochastic well-conditioned contexts, this closes the long-existing open regret gap in linear-valuation contextual pricing under agnostic non-Lipschitz noise distribution.

</details>


### [200] [LLMSpace: Carbon Footprint Modeling for Large Language Model Inference on LEO Satellites](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05615)
*Lei Jiang,Adrian Ildefonso,Daniel Loveless,Fan Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMSpace provides a unified carbon accounting framework for LLM inference on AI-enabled LEO satellites, integrating embodied and operational emissions, radiation-hardened hardware, and LLM-specific workloads to compare design choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs impose rapidly growing energy demands; to address potential space-based mitigation, a holistic carbon model is needed that covers launch/manufacturing emissions, hardware radiation-hardening, operational energy, and workload characteristics.

Method: Model both embodied and operational carbon, including peripheral subsystems, radiation-hardened accelerators/memories, and LLM workload patterns (prefill-decode behavior and token generation). Use realistic satellite and GPU configurations to evaluate trade-offs.

Result: LLMSpace reveals key trade-offs among carbon footprint, inference latency, hardware design, and operational lifetime for sustainable space-based LLM inference; source code is provided.

Conclusion: First carbon modeling framework for LLM inference on AI-enabled LEO satellites, enabling carbon-aware design choices and lifecycle assessment to guide sustainable space-based AI deployments.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) impose rapidly growing energy demands, creating an emerging energy and carbon crisis driven by large-scale inference. Solar-powered, AI-enabled low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites have been proposed to mitigate terrestrial electricity consumption, but their lifecycle carbon footprint remains poorly understood due to launch emissions, satellite manufacturing, and radiation-hardened hardware requirements. This paper presents \textit{LLMSpace}, the first carbon modeling framework for LLM inference on AI-enabled LEO satellites. LLMSpace jointly models operational and embodied carbon, peripheral subsystems, radiation-hardened accelerators and memories, and LLM-specific workload characteristics such as prefill-decode behavior and token generation. Using realistic satellite and GPU configurations, LLMSpace reveals key trade-offs among carbon footprint, inference latency, hardware design, and operational lifetime for sustainable space-based LLM inference. Source code: https://github.com/UnchartedRLab/LLMSpace.

</details>


### [201] [Region-adaptable retrieval of coastal biogeochemical parameters from near-surface hyperspectral remote sensing reflectance using physics-aware meta-learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05623)
*Yiqing Guo,Nagur R. C. Cherukuru,Eric A. Lehmann,S. L. Kesav Unnithan,Tim J. Malthus,Gemma Kerrisk,Xiubin Qi,Faisal Islam,Tisham Dhar,Mark J. Doubell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-stage physics-aware meta-learning for coastal BGC retrieval from hyperspectral Rrs: generate synthetic data via forward model from Australian spectral library; pretrain region-agnostic base model with meta-learning; region-specific fine-tuning on five sites; outperforms benchmarks; captures temporal dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization across water bodies is challenging due to regional differences in bio-optical properties; need physics-informed, data-efficient models for BGC retrieval from Rrs.

Method: Stage 1: use forward model to produce large synthetic Rrs-BGC dataset from a broad Australian coastal spectral library; pretrain a base model with meta-learning to capture fundamental physical relationships. Stage 2: fine-tune the pretrained model on region-specific in situ data. Data from five Australian coastal sites; evaluation against five benchmarks; analysis of regional variations and time-series alignment.

Result: Findings include: (1) clear regional distinctions in BGC and corresponding Rrs; (2) synthetic data distributions and inter-parameter correlations align with real samples; (3) the proposed framework outperforms five benchmark models; (4) model-predicted BGC time series agree with in situ measurements in magnitude and dynamics.

Conclusion: A physics-aware meta-learning approach with synthetic pretraining and local fine-tuning improves cross-region BGC retrieval from Rrs, offering scalable coastal water quality monitoring.

Abstract: Hyperspectral in situ sensing has shown promise in retrieving aquatic biogeochemical (BGC) parameters, such as total suspended solids, dissolved organic carbon, and total chlorophyll-a, for cost-effective monitoring of coastal water quality. However, generalising such retrieval algorithms across water bodies remains challenging, as the relationship between remote sensing reflectance (Rrs) and BGC parameters can vary considerably from one region to another due to regional distinctions in environmental conditions and biogeochemistry that lead to different BGC ranges and bio-optical properties. In this study, we propose a two-stage physics-aware meta-learning framework for retrieving coastal BGC parameters from near-surface Rrs observations. In the first stage, a bio-optical forward model is used to generate a large synthetic dataset based on an in situ bio-optical spectral library with broad representativeness of Australian coastal waters. This dataset is then used to pretrain a region-agnostic base model with meta-learning, allowing the model to learn fundamental physical relationships. In the second stage, the pretrained base model is fine-tuned for specific regions with local samples. We collected in situ hyperspectral Rrs and BGC measurements from five geographically distinct sites in Australian coastal waters. Our experimental results suggest: (1) the BGC parameters and their corresponding hyperspectral Rrs signatures exhibited clear regional distinctions among the experimental sites; (2) the synthetic dataset was physically plausible and closely aligned with real-world samples in both parameter distributions and inter-parameter correlations; (3) the proposed approach outperformed five benchmark models in BGC retrieval; and (4) time series of in situ measured and model-predicted BGC parameters showed good agreement in both magnitude and temporal dynamics.

</details>


### [202] [Scaling Pretrained Representations Enables Label-Free Out-of-Distribution Detection Without Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05638)
*Brett Barkley,Preston Culbertson,David Fridovich-Keil*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Frozen pretrained representations enable label-free OOD detection with two detectors; scaling improves detection and reduces detector differences across vision and language.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Can OOD detection be achieved without supervised fine-tuning by leveraging the geometry of frozen representations? Does detector choice matter at scale?

Method: Two detectors: a global Mahalanobis estimator on unlabeled latent features and a local diffusion-based ReSCOPED typicality estimator; evaluated on 59 backbone-task pairings spanning vision and language; analysis of performance as representation quality scales.

Result: Both detectors improve as representations become higher quality; the performance gap between the two detectors narrows with model and representation scaling; label-free OOD detection is primarily governed by the geometry of frozen backbones and generalizes across modalities.

Conclusion: Label-free OOD detection can be effectively achieved using frozen pretrained backbones; detector choice becomes less critical with scale, enabling efficient deployment without fine-tuning.

Abstract: Models trained with deep learning often fail to signal when inputs fall outside their training data manifold, leading to unreliable predictions under distribution shift. Prior work suggests that effective out-of-distribution (OOD) detection often requires class-conditional modeling or specialized models obtained through supervised fine-tuning. We revisit this assumption in modern pretrained models and show that their frozen representations already encode sufficient geometric structure for accurate label-free OOD detection. Across 59 backbone-task pairings spanning vision and language, we compare two complementary label-free detectors: a global Mahalanobis estimator fit on unlabeled latent representations, and ReSCOPED, a lightweight, diffusion-based typicality estimator operating on the same features at a local level. Despite their different detection mechanisms, representation scaling reveals a consistent regime-dependent pattern: both local and global detectors' absolute performance improves with better representation quality, and performance gaps between the two detectors disappear across both language and vision tasks as representations scale. These results suggest that label-free OOD detection depends strongly on the geometry exposed by frozen pretrained backbones, reducing the importance of detector choice as backbone scale increases and enabling efficient deployment directly on frozen models.

</details>


### [203] [Information-Preserving Domain Transfer with Unlabeled Data in Misspecified Simulation-Based Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05652)
*Joon Jang,Eunho Jeong,Kyu Sung Choi,Hyeonjin Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPIN enables simulation-based inference under model misspecification by learning parameter-relevant domain transfer from unlabeled real data. It trains transfers between simulator and real domains using labeled simulator data to preserve parameter information, and applies real-to-simulator mapping at test time for posterior inference without real-world labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SBI performs amortized Bayesian inference using simulations, but misspecified models cause posterior errors. Unlabeled real data alignment methods align distributions but may not preserve parameter-relevant information needed for accurate inference.

Method: Train a domain-transfer pipeline that translates labeled simulator observations toward the real domain and back to the simulator domain, using the simulator labels to encourage preservation of parameter-relevant mutual information. At test time, map real-world observations into the simulator domain via learned transports and perform standard posterior inference; no real-world parameter labels or paired data are required.

Result: Empirical evaluation on controlled synthetic and physical real-world benchmarks shows that SPIN improves real-world posterior inference, with larger gains as model misspecification increases.

Conclusion: SPIN provides a robust SBI framework under misspecification by incorporating parameter-relevant domain transfer that preserves information essential for posterior inference, leveraging unlabeled real data for training and avoiding the need for paired data or real-world parameter labels.

Abstract: Simulation-based inference (SBI) provides amortized Bayesian parameter inference from simulator-generated data without requiring explicit likelihood evaluation. Its reliability can degrade under model misspecification, where real-world observations are not well represented by the simulator used for training. Existing methods using unlabeled real-world data often align simulated and real-world data distributions, but marginal alignment alone does not directly preserve parameter-relevant information needed for posterior inference. We propose SPIN, an SBI framework with parameter-relevant information-preserving domain transfer using unlabeled, unpaired real-world observations. During training, SPIN translates labeled simulator observations toward the real-world domain and back to the simulator domain, using the original simulator labels to encourage domain transfer that preserves parameter-relevant mutual information. At test time, the learned real-to-simulator transport maps real-world observations into the simulator domain for posterior inference, without requiring real-world parameter labels or paired real--simulator observations. Across controlled synthetic and physical real-world benchmarks, SPIN improves real-world posterior inference, with the improvement becoming clearer as misspecification increases.
