</details>


### [204] [Structural Correspondence and Universal Approximation in Diagonal plus Low-Rank Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05659)
*Ying Chen,Aoxi Li,Jihun Kim,Javad Lavaei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies the expressive power of parameter-efficient low-rank neural networks without pretrained priors, showing rank-1 layers fail for function approximation but can interpolate scalars; introduces a Diagonal plus Low-Rank (DLoR) framework via a Structural Correspondence approach to achieve universal approximation. It demonstrates reconstruction of any full-rank transform using DLoR through additive (width) or multiplicative (depth) decompositions, proves universal approximation for general activations via asymptotic Taylor remainder analysis, and argues multiplicative depth yields better parameter-to-expressivity scaling. Dense matrices and specific activations are not required for universal expressivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the theoretical gap on the expressive power of parameter-efficient low-rank architectures (e.g., LoRA, factorized layers) when no pretrained dense priors are used; determine whether universal approximation can be achieved within constrained low-rank families and how width/depth trade-offs affect efficiency.

Method: Theoretical investigation of low-rank networks, starting with rank-1 limitations; development of Structural Correspondence framework; introduction of DLoR (Diagonal+Low-Rank) to restore expressivity; formal proofs showing exact reconstruction of full-rank transforms using DLoR via width or depth trade-offs; asymptotic Taylor remainder analysis to extend universal approximation to general activations; comparison of multiplicative depth versus additive width in parameter-efficiency scaling.

Result: DLoR is sufficient for universal approximation; any full-rank transform can be reconstructed with DLoR by either widening (additive decomposition) or deepening (multiplicative decomposition); universal approximation holds for general activation functions; multiplicative depth provides superior parameter-to-expressivity scaling compared to additive width; dense matrices and specific activation prerequisites are not necessary for universal expressivity.

Conclusion: The study shows that universal expressivity can be achieved within parameter-efficient structured networks without relying on dense priors or particular activations; DLoR offers a principled path to retain expressivity while maintaining parameter efficiency, with depth-based (multiplicative) designs outperforming width-based ones in scaling.

Abstract: The massive computational costs of scaling modern deep learning architectures have driven the widespread use of parameter-efficient low-rank structures, such as LoRA and low-rank factorization. However, theoretical guarantees for their expressive power are less explored, often relying on restrictive priors like a pretrained base matrix, ReLU activations or non-verifiable singularity conditions. We first investigate the limits of neural networks constrained strictly to low-rank manifolds without pretrained dense priors. We demonstrate a theoretical paradox: while purely rank-1 layers can exactly interpolate arbitrary scalar datasets, they collapse for function approximations. To overcome this bottleneck without surrendering parameter efficiency, we introduce a unified \textit{Structural Correspondence} framework. We prove that augmenting low-rank layers with only a minimal sparse diagonal component, say a Diagonal plus Low-Rank (DLoR) structure, is sufficient to reach Universal Approximation. We show that any full-rank transformation can be exactly reconstructed using these DLoR components by trading off network width (additive decomposition) or depth (multiplicative decomposition). By tracking asymptotic Taylor remainders, we prove that DLoR neural networks fully restore the Universal Approximation Theorem for general activation functions. Finally, we establish that multiplicative depth provides superior parameter-to-expressivity scaling compared to additive width. Our results show that dense matrices and specific activation functions are not topological prerequisites for universal expressivity.

</details>


### [205] [Distributionally Robust Multi-Objective Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05660)
*Yufeng Yang,Fangning Zhuo,Ziyi Chen,Heng Huang,Yi Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes distributionally robust multi-objective optimization (DR-MOO) and Pareto-type solutions, with MGDA-based algorithms. Shows two variants: a double-loop MGDA via a Lagrangian dual reformulation achieving O(ε^{-12}) sample complexity, and a single-loop double-clip MGDA with gradient clipping achieving O(ε^{-4}). Applicable to nonconvex settings without requiring bounded objectives/gradients; experiments indicate competitiveness with state-of-the-art MGDA baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address distributional shifts in data for multi-objective learning by seeking Pareto-efficient solutions under worst-case distributions.

Method: Formulate DR-MOO via Lagrangian duality. Develop two MGDA variants: (i) a double-loop MGDA using an inner loop to estimate dual variables (with double sampling) achieving ε-stationarity; (ii) a single-loop double-clip MGDA using gradient clipping to handle generalized-smooth and biased gradient estimates, removing the need for double sampling.

Result: The double-loop variant achieves total sample complexity O(ε^{-12}) to reach an ε-Pareto-stationary point; the single-loop variant with gradient clipping reduces complexity to O(ε^{-4}). The framework applies to nonconvex problems and does not require objective/gradient boundedness; experiments show competitiveness with MGDA baselines.

Conclusion: DR-MOO with MGDA offers robust Pareto optimization under distributional uncertainty. Gradient clipping significantly boosts efficiency and practicality by avoiding double sampling, while maintaining theoretical guarantees in nonconvex settings.

Abstract: Multi-objective optimization (MOO) has received growing attention in applications that require learning under multiple criteria. However, the existing MOO formulations do not explicitly account for distributional shifts in the data. We introduce distributionally robust multi-objective optimization (DR-MOO), which minimizes multiple objectives under their respective worst-case distributions. We propose Pareto-type solution concepts for DR-MOO and develop multi-gradient descent algorithms (MGDA) with provable guarantees. Leveraging a Lagrangian dual reformulation, we first design a double-loop MGDA that uses an inner loop to estimate dual variables and achieves a total sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-12})$ for reaching an $ε$-Pareto-stationary point. To further improve efficiency, we incorporate gradient clipping to handle generalized-smooth and biased gradient estimates, removing the need for double sampling. This yields a single-loop double-clip MGDA with substantially improved sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-4})$. Our theory applies to the nonconvex setting and does not require bounded objectives or gradients. Experiments demonstrate that our methods are competitive with state-of-the-art MGDA baselines.

</details>


### [206] [Temporal Functional Circuits: From Spline Plots to Faithful Explanations in KAN Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05685)
*Naveen Mysore*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Temporal Functional Circuits provide interpretable, temporally grounded edge-level explanations for time-series forecasting by transforming KAN edge functions into gated, explainable components; they achieve competitive performance while enabling faithfulness through edge-level interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard neural predictors (e.g., MLPs) lack explicit, mechanistic edge-level explanations. Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) offer edge functions on connections, enabling mechanism-level interpretability. This work aims to convert latent KAN edge functions into faithful, temporally grounded explanations for forecasting.

Method: Introduce Temporal Functional Circuits built on a gated residual KAN with a linear base term and sparsely activated KAN correction. Map each edge to input lags via output-aware attribution, rank edges by learned activation range, and validate faithfulness through edge-level interventions (zeroing, spline removal). Test by removing learned B-spline while keeping base SiLU to assess spline value; analyze gating behavior across synthetic regimes and benchmarks.

Result: In synthetic regimes of increasing complexity, the gate opens wider as signal complexity grows. On regime-switching signals, the gated KAN achieves 59% lower MSE than linear-only models. Across eight benchmarks, the architecture is competitive with linear, attention, and MLP baselines, while yielding edge-level explanations that are not available from MLP-based corrections.

Conclusion: Temporal Functional Circuits deliver faithful, temporally grounded explanations through edge-level attribution in KANs, with spline components carrying predictive value beyond the base activation. The approach maintains competitive forecasting performance while enhancing interpretability compared to traditional models.

Abstract: Unlike MLPs, Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) expose explicit learnable edge functions on every connection, enabling mechanistic explanation in time-series forecasting. This paper introduces Temporal Functional Circuits, a framework that transforms KAN edge functions from latent visualizations into faithful, temporally grounded explanations. Built on a gated residual KAN that decomposes forecasts into a linear base and a sparsely activated KAN correction, the framework (i) maps each edge to input lags via output-aware attribution, (ii) ranks edges by learned activation range, and (iii) validates faithfulness through edge-level interventions including zeroing and spline removal. Removing the learned B-spline component while retaining the base SiLU term degrades forecasts, providing evidence that the spline shape itself carries predictive value beyond the base activation. On four synthetic regimes of increasing complexity, the learned gate opens progressively wider as signal complexity grows. On regime-switching signals, gated KAN achieves 59% lower MSE than linear-only models. Across eight benchmarks, the gated architecture is competitive with linear, attention, and MLP alternatives, while providing interpretable edge functions that MLP-based corrections cannot offer.

</details>


### [207] [Budgeted Attention Allocation: Cost-Conditioned Compute Control for Efficient Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05697)
*Amrit Nidhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Feasibility study of Budgeted Attention Allocation in transformers, showing monotone head-gating enables controllable cost–accuracy tradeoffs across budgets, with reproducible speedups on CPU benchmarks; not universally dominant but practically useful.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To obtain multiple cost-quality operating points from a single trained transformer by gating attention according to a budget, addressing deployment constraints and stability.

Method: Introduce a monotone head-gating mechanism conditioned on a requested attention budget; employ dense warm-starting for stability; explore hard-gate adaptation, budgeted structural pruning, zero-shot dense post-hoc baselines, and a recovery epoch; validate on synthetic tasks, AG News with a word-level transformer, and BERT-Mini on DBpedia14.

Result: On synthetic data: 99.7% accuracy at 0.303 cost and 100.0% at 0.504 cost. On AG News with a custom transformer: 82.1% accuracy with 1.28x speedup at budget 0.50. On BERT-Mini AG News: 87.6% with 1.20x speedup at 0.50; a zero-shot dense baseline 86.1%, improved to 87.9% after a recovery epoch. On DBpedia14: 97.4% at 0.50 vs 96.6% dense full attention. Static fixed-budget gates and recovered dense specialists also perform well.

Conclusion: The work presents a reproducible feasibility study rather than universal dominance; a single controllable checkpoint across budgets can trade attention cost for accuracy and yield measurable speedups on small CPU benchmarks, with varying gains across tasks.

Abstract: Transformers usually expose one inference cost per trained model, while deployed systems often need multiple cost-quality operating points. We study Budgeted Attention Allocation, a monotone head-gating mechanism conditioned on a requested attention budget. Dense warm-starting is important for stability: on a robust synthetic sequence task, one budgeted model reaches 99.7% accuracy at 0.303 estimated attention cost and 100.0% accuracy at 0.504 cost. On held-out AG News with a custom word-level transformer, hard-gate adaptation turns soft cost control into measured single-thread CPU speed, reaching 82.1% accuracy with 1.28x speedup at budget 0.50. In pretrained BERT-Mini AG News, budgeted structural pruning reaches 87.6% accuracy with 1.20x speedup at budget 0.50; a validation-ranked zero-shot dense post-hoc structural baseline reaches 86.1%, and one recovery epoch raises that per-budget specialist to 87.9%. On DBpedia14, BERT-Mini budgeted gates reach 97.4% at exact budget 0.50 versus 96.6% for dense full attention. Static fixed-budget gates and recovered dense specialists remain strong. The contribution is therefore not universal dominance, but a reproducible feasibility study of one controllable checkpoint across budgets that can trade attention cost for accuracy and be converted into measured structural speedups on small CPU benchmarks.

</details>


### [208] [On the Blessing of Pre-training in Weak-to-Strong Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05710)
*Wei Yao,Wang Zhaoyang,Gengze Xu,Chen Qian,Dongrui Liu,Ziqiao Wang,Yong Liu,Yunbei Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pre-training is essential for Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG). The work formalizes W2SG in a high-dimensional single-index model with spiked Gaussian data, using spectral initialization as a proxy for pre-training. They show W2SG is achievable within an “effective region” of perturbed strong-convexity, derive a generalization bound that captures initial gains and a saturation due to the weak supervisor, and validate the theory with synthetic data and a large-scale LLM checkpoint study, finding a phase transition tied to pre-training progress.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify the root cause of W2SG and connect theoretical guarantees with empirical observations, specifically the role of pre-training in enabling a strong model to outperform a weak supervisor.

Method: Theoretical analysis in a high-dimensional single-index model with spiked Gaussian data; model pre-training as a spectral initialization; prove impossibility with random initialization and sufficiency with a warm start placing the model in an effectively strongly-convex region; derive a generalization bound that reflects optimization dynamics (early gains, eventual saturation from the weak bias). Empirical validation via controlled synthetic simulations and a massive-scale evaluation across hundreds of intermediate pre-training checkpoints from large language models.

Result: W2SG is achievable given a geometrically favorable warm start provided by pre-training; the generalization bound reveals a two-phase dynamics: initial performance gains followed by saturation dictated by the weak supervisor’s bias. Empirical results corroborate that W2SG is not intrinsic but emerges through a phase transition during pre-training.

Conclusion: Pre-training is the essential prerequisite for W2SG. The emergence of W2SG is tightly coupled to the progression of pre-training, suggesting that warm-start geometry governs generalization and that achieving W2SG requires adequate pre-training-induced initialization.

Abstract: The paradigm of Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG) suggests that a pre-trained strong model can surpass its weak supervisor, yet the decisive role of pre-training remains theoretically and empirically under-explored. In this work, we identify pre-training as the essential prerequisite for the emergence of W2SG. Theoretically, we formalize the W2SG problem within a high-dimensional single-index model framework using spiked Gaussian data, modeling pre-training as a spectral initialization step. Building upon prior impossibility results regarding the failure of learning under random initialization, we prove that W2SG is achievable when pre-training provides a geometric warm start that places the model within an "effective region" characterized by a perturbed strong-convexity geometry. Within this region, we derive a rigorous generalization bound that naturally captures the optimization dynamics: an initial performance improvement followed by a saturation bottleneck dictated by the weak supervisor's bias. Empirically, we first validate all our assumptions and theoretical insights through controlled synthetic simulations. Finally, through a massive-scale evaluation of hundreds of intermediate pre-training checkpoints from large language models, we demonstrate that W2SG is not an innate capability but emerges via a phase transition tightly coupled with the progression of pre-training.

</details>


### [209] [Enabling Federated Inference via Unsupervised Consensus Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05718)
*Yui Hashimoto,Takayuki Nishio,Yuichi Kitagawa,Takahito Tanimura*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CE-FI enables privacy-preserving cooperative inference among pretrained models without sharing inputs or parameters and without requiring a common encoder, via a Consensus Embedding layer that maps heterogeneous representations to a shared space and a Cooperative Output layer that derives predictions from these embeddings; trained solely on unlabeled data; shows gains on CIFAR-10/100 under non-IID settings, with cross-domain applicability and performance dependent on ensemble strategy; representation alignment identified as the main bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for cross-model cooperation in distributed, privacy-sensitive settings where input data and model parameters must be kept private, and where no common encoder is available.

Method: Introduce a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that projects heterogeneous intermediate representations into a shared embedding space, and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer that produces predictions from these embeddings. Both layers are trained using shared unlabeled data only, enabling cooperative inference without sharing raw inputs or model parameters or requiring a common encoder. The approach supports heterogeneous models and is evaluated across image, text, and time-series tasks. Ensemble strategy affects performance.

Result: CE-FI consistently improves over solo inference and attains performance comparable to conventional methods that require stronger sharing assumptions. CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 experiments under diverse non-IID conditions demonstrate robustness; additional cross-domain evaluations on text and time-series show applicability, though performance hinges on ensemble strategy. Analysis indicates representation alignment is the primary bottleneck.

Conclusion: CE-FI provides a viable privacy-preserving framework for cooperative inference across independently deployed models, highlighting representation alignment as a key factor and suggesting avenues for improving cross-model collaboration and extending to more modalities.

Abstract: Cooperative inference across independently deployed machine learning models is increasingly desirable in distributed environments, as there is a growing need to leverage multiple models while keeping their data and model parameters private. However, existing cooperative frameworks typically rely on sharing input data, model parameters, or a common encoder, which limits their applicability in privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings. To address this challenge, we propose Consensus Embedding-based Federated Inference (CE-FI), a framework that enables pretrained models to cooperate at inference time without sharing model parameters or raw inputs and without assuming a common encoder. CE-FI introduces two components: a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that maps heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space, and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer that produces predictions from these embeddings. Both layers are trained using shared unlabeled data only, so the cooperative stage does not require additional labeled data. Experiments on image classification benchmarks -- CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 -- under diverse non-IID conditions show that CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference and performs comparably to conventional methods that require stronger sharing assumptions. Additional evaluations on text and time-series tasks indicate applicability beyond image classification, although performance depends on the ensemble strategy. Further analysis identifies representation alignment as the primary bottleneck.

</details>


### [210] [WARP: A Benchmark for Primal-Dual Warm-Starting of Interior-Point Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05728)
*Dhruv Suri,Helgi Hilmarsson,Shourya Bose*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Warm-starts for AC-OPF based on primal predictions were overstated due to a flawed baseline; when evaluated against the solver's actual default (the variable-bound midpoint) and against the full primal-dual-barrier state, primal-only warm-starts offer no iteration reduction, while access to dual and barrier variables yields substantial speedups. A benchmark suite and WARP model enable rigorous evaluation and show up to ~76-85% iteration reductions and N-1 robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Critically re-evaluate the claimed benefits of learned warm-starts for interior-point methods in AC-OPF; highlight the importance of dual variables and barrier state in convergence; provide standardized benchmarks.

Method: Analytical critique of prior baselines; experiments with corrected baseline showing no gain from primal-only warm-starts; oracle experiments demonstrating large gains only with full primal-dual-barrier state; development of a dual-labeled IPOPT-derived dataset; proposal of WARP, a topology-conditioned encoder–processor–decoder that predicts (x, λ, z, μ) on a constraint graph; evaluation across topology variations.

Result: Primal-only warm-starts do not reduce IPOPT iterations under the corrected baseline; complete primal-dual-barrier initialization reduces iterations from 23 to 3 (85% reduction); WARP achieves ~76% iteration reduction and generalizes to N-1 contingency topologies without retraining.

Conclusion: Evaluation of warm-start strategies must include dual and barrier components; primal-only approaches are inherently limited in IPMs for AC-OPF; the released benchmark and WARP framework provide a rigorous path for future research and fair comparisons.

Abstract: Solving AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) is of central importance in electricity market operations, where interior-point methods (IPMs) such as IPOPT are the standard solvers. A growing body of work uses machine learning to predict primal warm-start iterates, reporting iteration reductions of 30-46\%. We show that these reported gains rest on an inappropriate evaluation baseline: prior methods benchmark against the flat start $V_m = 1, V_a = 0$, whereas the solver's actual default - the variable-bound midpoint $(l+u)/2$ - is near-optimal for log-barrier centrality. Against this corrected baseline, no primal-only warm-start method reduces solver iterations. We trace the failure to a geometric property of interior-point methods: primal prediction accuracy is anticorrelated with convergence speed, and providing the ground-truth optimal solution $x^*$ without dual variables causes the solver to diverge. Oracle experiments establish that the complete primal-dual-barrier state $(x^*, λ^*, z^*, μ^*)$ reduces IPOPT iterations from 23 to 3 - an 85\% reduction that is structurally inaccessible to primal-only methods. To enable rigorous evaluation of warm-start methods on this task, we release a benchmark suite comprising dual-labeled AC-OPF datasets with IPOPT-extracted solutions, a corrected evaluation protocol, and WARP - a topology-conditioned encode-process-decode interaction network that predicts the full interior-point state $(\hat{x}, \hatλ, \hat{z}, \hatμ)$ on the heterogeneous constraint graph. WARP achieves a 76\% reduction in IPOPT iterations while natively accommodating N-1 contingency topology variations without retraining.

</details>


### [211] [CRAFT: Forgetting-Aware Intervention-Based Adaptation for Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05732)
*Md Anwar Hossen,Fatema Siddika,Juan Pablo Munoz,Tanya Roosta,Ali Jannesari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRAFT is a KL-divergence-based continual learning framework for LLMs that applies low-rank interventions in hidden representations, routing tasks by output-distribution divergence, updating via KL-regularization without weight updates, and merging back with KL guidance. It achieves reduced forgetting and improved performance over LoRA baselines across multiple scales and task orders.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate catastrophic forgetting when continually adapting LLMs without resorting to full fine-tuning or heavy parameter growth; leverage representation-space interventions guided by output-space signals.

Method: Three-stage process: (1) route each task to a group of similar tasks using output-distribution divergence; (2) fine-tune by minimizing KL divergence against the group's prior state, controlling forgetting and convergence; (3) merge updated interventions into the shared representation using the same KL signal. Interventions are low-rank, target hidden representations, and are learned without updating base weights.

Result: Outperforms strong LoRA-based approaches in reducing forgetting and boosting accuracy across multiple benchmarks and model scales; shows robustness to task ordering and scalable deployment.

Conclusion: Controlling adaptation in representation space with an output-space-guided, KL-based objective provides a unified, scalable approach to continual learning for LLMs, balancing plasticity and stability via low-rank, representation-space interventions.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can acquire new capabilities through fine-tuning, but continual adaptation often leads to catastrophic forgetting. We propose CRAFT, a continual learning framework that avoids updating model weights by instead learning low-rank interventions on hidden representations. CRAFT proceeds in three stages: it first routes each task to a group of similar tasks based on output-distribution divergence; it then fine-tunes the model using a Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence against the group's prior state, which directly controls forgetting and determines convergence; finally, it merges interventions for the updated task into the shared representation using the same KL signal. This design unifies routing, regularization, and merging through a single KL-based objective. CRAFT improves overall performance and reduces forgetting compared to strong LoRA-based approaches across multiple benchmarks and model scales, while remaining robust to task ordering. These results suggest that controlling adaptation in representation space, guided by output-space divergence, provides a scalable and principled approach to continual learning in LLMs.

</details>


### [212] [CoMemNet: Contrastive Sampling with Memory Replay Network for Continual Traffic Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05738)
*Mei Wu,Wenchao Weng,Wenxin Su,Wenjie Tang,Wei Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoMemNet presents a dual-branch continual learning framework for traffic prediction on evolving graphs. An online branch handles primary predictions while a momentum-updated target branch derives Wasserstein-distance features to form a Dynamic Contrastive Sampler that focuses training on dynamically changing nodes, mitigating catastrophic forgetting. A lightweight Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer enables memory replay to curb memory explosion. Open benchmarks are provided, and SOTA results are reported on three real datasets with code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To handle streaming, evolving traffic networks where underlying graphs continually change, and to mitigate catastrophic forgetting and memory explosion in continual learning approaches for spatio-temporal graph modeling.

Method: A dual-branch architecture: (1) Online branch for fast-converging predictions; (2) Momentum-updated Target branch that uses Wasserstein Distance features to construct a Dynamic Contrastive Sampler (DC Sampler) selecting nodes with significant dynamic changes; A Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) for memory replay and preventing memory explosion.

Result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance across three large-scale real-world datasets; two open-source datasets are curated; code released at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: CoMemNet provides an effective and efficient approach for continual learning in streaming traffic graphs, addressing catastrophic forgetting and memory growth with a simple, modular design and strong empirical performance; it contributes datasets and code for reproducibility.

Abstract: In recent years, the integration of non-topological space modeling with temporal learning methods has emerged as an effective approach for capturing spatio-temporal information in non-Euclidean graphs. However, most existing methods rely on static underlying graph structures, which are inadequate for capturing the continuously expanding and evolving patterns in streaming traffic networks. To address this challenge, we propose a simple yet efficient dual-branch continual learning framework for traffic prediction, named CoMemNet. The fast-converging Online branch undertakes the primary prediction tasks, while the momentum-updated Target branch extracts historical information using Wasserstein Distance features to create a Dynamic Contrastive Sampler (DC Sampler). This sampler selects a node set with significant dynamic network feature changes for training, effectively mitigating the issue of catastrophic forgetting. Additionally, the backbone incorporates a lightweight Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) to consolidate old knowledge through memory replay and address the risk of memory explosion. Finally, we provide two newly curated open-source datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that CoMemNet achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance across all three large-scale real-world datasets. The code is available at: https://github.com/meiwu5/CoMemNet.

</details>


### [213] [Multi-Dimensional Behavioral Evaluation of Agentic Stock Prediction Systems Using LLM Judges with Closed-Loop Reinforcement Learning Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05739)
*Mohammad Al Ridhawi,Mahtab Haj Ali,Hussein Al Osman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A behavioral evaluation framework for agentic stock-prediction systems using LLM judges to diagnose per-decision quality; perturbation tests reveal dimension-specific weaknesses; penalizing deficient dimensions improves offline metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aggregate metrics like MAPE and directional accuracy hide per-decision quality. A granular, interpretable behavioral framework is needed to diagnose and align decisions with downstream risk-adjusted performance.

Method: Trace autonomous decisions into five-day episodes; score on six domain-specific dimensions (regime detection, routing, adaptation, risk calibration, strategy coherence, error recovery) by an ensemble of three LLM judges (GPT 5.4, Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro); perturbation-based validation on 420 episodes; compute a composite behavioral score; convert per-dimension deficits into a penalty term added to Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reward; three validation-period-only fine-tuning cycles; offline backtesting on 2017-2025 period.

Result: Perturbation tests yield targeted score drops of -1.6 to -2.4 on intended dimensions vs -0.32 on the other five; cross-model agreement up to Krippendorff's α = 0.85. Composite score correlates with 20-day Sharpe ratio at ρ = 0.72. Post-fine-tuning on held-out data: one-day MAPE from 0.61% to 0.54% (11.5% relative, p<0.001, d=0.31); directional accuracy 71%→74%; Sharpe ratio up 18% (CI 8.2%–27.4%), especially in high-volatility episodes.

Conclusion: The framework provides interpretable behavioral diagnostics and can improve offline risk-adjusted performance via reward shaping. However, claims are limited to offline backtesting; live-deployment effects remain unaddressed.

Abstract: Agentic stock prediction systems make sequences of interdependent decisions (regime detection, pathway routing, reinforcement learning control) whose individual quality is hidden by aggregate metrics such as mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) or directional accuracy. We present a behavioral evaluation framework that addresses this gap. Behavioral traces logged at every autonomous decision point are grouped into five-day episodes and scored along six domain-specific dimensions (regime detection, routing, adaptation, risk calibration, strategy coherence, error recovery) by an ensemble of three large language model (LLM) judges (GPT 5.4, Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro). Perturbation-based validation on 420 episodes yields targeted score drops of $-1.6$ to $-2.4$ on intended dimensions versus an average of $-0.32$ on the remaining five, with cross-model agreement up to Krippendorff's $α= 0.85$. The composite behavioral score, used here only for cross-episode reporting, correlates at $ρ= 0.72$ with realized 20-day Sharpe ratio from offline backtesting. Closing the loop, the framework converts deficient per-dimension scores into a credit-assigned penalty term added to the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reward. Three short fine-tuning cycles, all confined to the validation period, produce on the held-out 2017-2025 test period a one-day MAPE reduction from 0.61% to 0.54% (an 11.5% relative reduction; $p<0.001$, Cohen's $d=0.31$), a directional accuracy increase from 71% to 74%, and an 18% Sharpe ratio improvement (95% bootstrap CI [8.2%, 27.4%]), with gains concentrated in high-volatility episodes where the original system was most behaviorally deficient. Results are from offline backtesting and do not address effects specific to live deployment.

</details>


### [214] [Weak-to-Strong Generalization is Nearly Inevitable (in Linear Models)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05742)
*Scott Geng,Dutch Hansen,Jerry Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Weak-to-strong generalization also arises in simple linear logistic regression; it does not require a student with greater capacity and appears almost inevitable under mild data-distribution assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether weak-to-strong generalization is a phenomenon unique to frontier models or a general property of post-training, by analyzing a simple, well-understood model class.

Method: Theoretical analysis of post-training with teacher feedback in standard linear logistic regression under mild distributional assumptions. The study examines many student–teacher pairs and shows that finetuning with weaker-teacher feedback can push the student beyond the teacher’s performance in most cases, even when capacities are equal.

Result: Weak-to-strong generalization is demonstrated for most student–teacher pairs in this basic setting; the phenomenon is nearly inevitable under the stated assumptions and does not require the student to be more expressive than the teacher.

Conclusion: The findings challenge the belief that capacity mismatch is central to weak-to-strong generalization, showing the effect is a generic property of post-training dynamics even in simple models; calls for broader theoretical and empirical exploration beyond complex frontier models.

Abstract: Weak-to-strong generalization is a phenomenon in post-training whereby a strong student model, when finetuned solely with feedback from a weaker teacher, can not only surpass the teacher, but can improve upon its own capabilities. Recent work of Burns et al. (2023) demonstrated that this can occur in the setting of frontier language models, and subsequently there has been a flurry of both empirical work trying to exploit this phenomenon, as well as theoretical work attempting to understand it. In this work, we demonstrate that weak-to-strong generalization occurs in standard linear logistic regression, under mild distributional assumptions on the data. In fact, we show that this happens for most student-teacher pairs, suggesting that weak-to-strong generalization is in fact \emph{almost inevitable}, even in this basic setting. Notably, our setting does not require the student to be more expressive or have more model capacity in any way compared to the teacher, which runs contrary to the prevailing theoretical belief that a mismatch in model capacity is a central mechanism to weak-to-strong generalization.

</details>


### [215] [RVPO: Risk-Sensitive Alignment via Variance Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05750)
*Ivan Montero,Tomasz Jurczyk,Bhuwan Dhingra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RVPO is a variance-regularized multi-objective RLHF method that penalizes reward variance to prevent constraint neglect, showing improvements on HealthBench and competitive GPQA-Diamond results across multiple model scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Arithmetic-mean aggregation of multiple RL rewards hides bottleneck failures and hard constraints (e.g., safety/formatting); a risk-sensitive objective that emphasizes consistency is needed for reliable multi-objective alignment.

Method: Introduce a variance penalty via a LogSumExp (SoftMin) operator to replace simple sum in advantage aggregation; provide a Taylor-expansion justification; apply to multi-reward RLHF across rubric-based medical/scientific reasoning and tool-calling with constraints, tested on Qwen 2.5 models (3B/7B/14B) and smaller tool-using variants.

Result: HealthBench score improves (0.261 with RVPO vs. 0.215 with GDPO at 14B, p < 0.001); maintains competitive accuracy on GPQA-Diamond with no late-stage degradation observed in other multi-reward methods; effective across model scales (Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B and 1.5B/3B for tool-calling).

Conclusion: Variance regularization mitigates constraint neglect in multi-reward RLHF and preserves general capabilities while improving reliability across model scales.

Abstract: Current critic-less RLHF methods aggregate multi-objective rewards via an arithmetic mean, leaving them vulnerable to constraint neglect: high-magnitude success in one objective can numerically offset critical failures in others (e.g., safety or formatting), masking low-performing "bottleneck" rewards vital for reliable multi-objective alignment. We propose Reward-Variance Policy Optimization (RVPO), a risk-sensitive framework that penalizes inter-reward variance during advantage aggregation, shifting the objective from "maximize sum" to "maximize consistency." We show via Taylor expansion that a LogSumExp (SoftMin) operator effectively acts as a smooth variance penalty. We evaluate RVPO on rubric-based medical and scientific reasoning with up to 17 concurrent LLM-judged reward signals (Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B) and on tool-calling with rule-based constraints (Qwen2.5-1.5B/3B). By preventing the model from neglecting difficult constraints to exploit easier objectives, RVPO improves overall scores on HealthBench (0.261 vs. 0.215 for GDPO at 14B, $p < 0.001$) and maintains competitive accuracy on GPQA-Diamond without the late-stage degradation observed in other multi-reward methods, demonstrating that variance regularization mitigates constraint neglect across model scales without sacrificing general capabilities.

</details>


### [216] [Full-Spectrum Graph Neural Network: Expressive and Scalable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05759)
*Xiaohan Wang,Deyu Bo,Longlong Li,Kelin Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A second-order spectral GNN that operates on node-pair signals with bivariate eigenvalue filters; universal for node-pair signals, bounded by Local 2-GNN, with scalable, low-rank implementations and strong heterophily performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To surpass the expressivity bound of 1-WL-limited spectral GNNs and enable higher-order signal processing, improving learning on heterophilic graphs.

Method: Lifts node signals to the node-pair domain and generalizes spectral filtering to bivariate eigenvalue filters. Classical spectral GNNs appear as diagonal special cases. Establishes that FSpecGNN is at most as expressive as Local 2-GNN while universally approximating node-pair signals. Proposes scalable implementations that avoid explicit node-pair computations and uses a low-rank approximation to reduce full-spectrum convolution to polynomial filters.

Result: Theoretically, FSpecGNN cannot exceed Local 2-GNN in expressivity but can universally approximate node-pair signals, which is advantageous for heterophily. Empirically, it demonstrates strong performance on heterophilic benchmarks and validates the predicted expressivity.

Conclusion: FSpecGNN offers a scalable, more expressive framework for node-pair signal processing in spectral GNNs, bridging the gap beyond 1-WL limitations and improving heterophilic graph learning.

Abstract: It is well established that spectral graph neural networks (GNNs) can universally approximate node signals; however, their expressive power remains bounded by the 1-dimensional Weisfeiler-Lehman test, which is mirrored in their lack of universality for higher-order signals. To go beyond this bound, we propose the Full-Spectrum GNN (FSpecGNN), a second-order generalization of classical spectral GNNs. FSpecGNN advances spectral filtering in two perspectives: (1) it lifts the signal from the node domain to the node-pair domain; and (2) it extends the univariate spectral filter over eigenvalues to a bivariate filter over eigenvalue pairs. We show that classical spectral GNNs arise as a diagonal special case of FSpecGNN, and prove that FSpecGNN can be at most as expressive as Local 2-GNN while universally approximating node-pair signals, the latter being particularly beneficial for heterophilic graph learning. Moreover, FSpecGNN admits scalable implementations that avoid explicit node-pair-level computations; combined with a low-rank approximation that reduces full-spectrum convolution to a combination of polynomial spectral filters, it enables learning on large graphs. Empirically, FSpecGNN validates the predicted expressivity and delivers strong performance on heterophilic benchmarks.

</details>


### [217] [Adaptive Selection of LoRA Components in Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05769)
*Myoungjun Kim,Sangwoo Park,Yoseob Han,Jin-Hyun Ahn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AS-LoRA is an adaptive, curvature-aware framework for DP federated fine-tuning of large models with LoRA. It introduces layer-wise freedom and round-wise adaptivity, guided by a second-order loss-based score, to mitigate aggregation errors from LoRA’s multiplicative structure, yielding faster convergence and flatter minima without extra privacy cost. It achieves up to +7.5 pp on GLUE, +12.5 pp on MNLI-mm, while matching/exceeding SVD-based methods at 33–180× lower aggregation cost and negligible communication overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DP federated fine-tuning with LoRA suffers aggregation errors due to LoRA's multiplicative structure, exacerbated by DP noise. Existing methods apply uniform update modes across layers and rounds, ignoring structural asymmetry and round dynamics, causing instability and accuracy loss. A method that adapts per layer and per round, with a curvature-aware objective, could reduce reconstruction error and improve convergence under DP constraints.

Method: AS-LoRA proposes a three-axis adaptive framework: (i) layer-wise freedom, allowing each layer to independently select its active LoRA component; (ii) round-wise adaptivity, updating the selected components across communication rounds; (iii) a curvature-aware score derived from a second-order (e.g., Hessian-based) approximation of the loss to guide updates. Theoretical analysis claims elimination of the reconstruction-error floor in layer-tied schedules, faster convergence, implicit bias toward flatter minima, and no additional privacy cost.

Result: Empirically validated on GLUE, SQuAD, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under strict DP budgets and non-IID partitions. AS-LoRA outperforms federated LoRA baselines by up to 7.5 percentage points on GLUE and 12.5 points on MNLI-mm. It matches or exceeds SVD-based aggregation methods while achieving 33–180× lower aggregation cost and negligible communication overhead.

Conclusion: AS-LoRA provides a principled, adaptive approach to DP federated fine-tuning with LoRA, addressing the multiplicative-structure aggregation error via layer- and round-level adaptivity and a curvature-aware objective. It yields faster convergence, bias toward flatter minima, no privacy overhead, and strong empirical gains with substantial efficiency advantages.

Abstract: Differentially private federated fine-tuning of large models with LoRA suffers from aggregation error caused by LoRA's multiplicative structure, which is further amplified by DP noise and degrades both stability and accuracy. Existing remedies apply a single update mode uniformly across all layers and all communication rounds (or alternate them on a fixed schedule), ignoring both the structural asymmetry between the two LoRA factors and the round-wise dynamics of training. We propose AS-LoRA, an adaptive framework defined by three axes (i) layer-wise freedom, in which each layer independently selects its active component, (ii) round-wise adaptivity, in which the selection updates over communication rounds, and (iii) a curvature-aware score derived from a second-order approximation of the loss. Theoretically, AS-LoRA eliminates the reconstruction-error floor of layer-tied schedules, accelerates convergence, implicitly biases solutions toward flatter minima, and incurs no additional privacy cost. Across GLUE, SQuAD, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under strict DP budgets and non-IID partitions, AS-LoRA improves over the federated LoRA baselines by up to $+7.5$ pp on GLUE and $+12.5$ pp on MNLI-mm for example, while matching or exceeding SVD-based aggregation methods at $33\text{--}180 \times$ lower aggregation cost and with negligible communication overhead. Code for the proposed method is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/as_lora-F75F/.

</details>


### [218] [A Measure-Theoretic Finite-Sample Theory for Adaptive-Data Fitted Q-Iteration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05791)
*Manuel Haussmann,Mustafa Mert Çelikok,Melih Kandemir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified finite-sample analysis for fitted Q-iteration (FQI) on general measurable spaces, establishing a pathwise online regret and sequential Rademacher complexity-based generalization bound, bridging measure-theoretic MDP foundations, error propagation, and PAC-style results to support deep RL theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between empirical success of model-free off-policy deep RL and theoretical analysis that is largely confined to tabular or linearizable settings. Combine measure-theoretic MDP foundations, error-propagation analysis, and PAC generalization bounds into a single framework for FQI in continuous spaces.

Method: Develop a unified framework for FQI on general measurable Borel spaces by coupling measure-theoretic probability with Bellman-operator contraction in Banach spaces. Derive finite-sample adaptive-data performance bounds via chaining arguments. Show that sequential Rademacher complexity controls Bellman-regression generalization under policy-dependent data. Extend the analysis to obtain a cumulative, pathwise online regret guarantee for FQI in continuous spaces.

Result: Finite-sample adaptive-data bound for FQI; generalization bounds for Bellman regression via sequential Rademacher complexity under policy-dependent data; first cumulative online regret guarantee for FQI in continuous spaces.

Conclusion: Lays foundational theory for the formal analysis of modern deep RL algorithms, enabling rigorous treatment of FQI and serving as a stepping stone toward comprehensive deep RL guarantees in continuous settings.

Abstract: While reinforcement learning (RL) promises to revolutionize the control of complex nonlinear robotic systems, a profound gap persists between the heuristic success of model-free off-policy deep RL and the underlying theory, which remains largely confined to tabular or linearizable settings. We identify the cause of this gap as an emergent isolation of three traditions: (i) measure-theoretic MDP foundations on general spaces limit their analysis to exact dynamic programming and ignore all error sources of a learning process; (ii) deterministic error propagation analysis addresses the approximation error via concentrability coefficients without a finite-sample analysis of the estimation error; and (iii) PAC generalization bounds characterize the estimation errors of simplified topologies. We bridge these traditions with a unified theoretical framework for fitted Q-iteration (FQI) on general measurable Borel spaces. Our main result provides a finite-sample, adaptive-data performance bound by chaining measure-theoretic probability with Bellman-operator contraction in Banach spaces. We prove that sequential Rademacher complexity controls Bellman-regression generalization under policy-dependent data collection. We further extend this analysis to provide the first cumulative, pathwise online regret guarantee for FQI in continuous spaces. These results lay the necessary foundations for the formal analysis of many modern deep RL algorithms.

</details>


### [219] [Revealing Modular Gradient Noise Imbalance in LLMs: Calibrating Adam via Signal-to-Noise Ratio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05794)
*Ziqing Wen,Zhouyang Liu,Jiahuan Wang,Ping Luo,Li Shen,Dongsheng Li,Tao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoLS automatically scales Adam updates across modules by estimating module-level SNRs, addressing gradient heterogeneity in large models without manual tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large models exhibit module-wise gradient heterogeneity; per-parameter optimizers lack cross-module adaptivity; manual module-specific LRs are costly and non-generalizable.

Method: Estimate module-level signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) to scale Adam updates, enabling automated module-wise LR allocation; compatible with memory-efficient training; validated on multiple LLM benchmarks.

Result: Faster convergence and better generalization; performance comparable to carefully tuned module-specific LRs; fewer tuning requirements.

Conclusion: Module-wise learning-rate scaling via SNR (MoLS) provides a principled, automated approach to handle module heterogeneity, improving optimization for LLMs while maintaining compatibility with memory-efficient methods.

Abstract: The impressive performance of large language models (LLMs) arises from their massive scale and heterogeneous module composition. However, this structural heterogeneity introduces additional optimization challenges. While adaptive optimizers such as Adam(W) provide per-parameter adaptivity, they do not explicitly account for module-level gradient heterogeneity, resulting in slower convergence, suboptimal performance, or training instability. Existing approaches typically rely on manually tuned module-specific learning rates or specific optimization strategies, which are computationally costly and difficult to generalize across tasks or models. To establish a more principled approach, we first analyze the noise-damping behavior of Adam in high-noise modules and introduce \textbf{Module-wise Learning Rate Scaling via SNR (MoLS)}. MoLS estimates module-level SNRs to scale Adam updates, allowing automated module-wise learning rate allocation without manual tuning. Empirical results through multiple LLM training benchmarks demonstrate that MoLS improves convergence speed and generalization, achieving performance comparable to carefully tuned module-specific learning rates, while remaining compatible with memory-efficient training algorithms.

</details>


### [220] [Selective Rollout: Mid-Trajectory Termination for Multi-Sample Agent RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05802)
*Zhiyuan Zhai,Xin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a one-parameter early-stop gate for GRPO that halts groups when partial trajectories converge (small mean pairwise prefix edit distance), reducing wasted rollouts and yielding faster training with modest gain in unseen-task success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Group-relative RL training incurs substantial compute waste when groups finish with identical rewards (zero-variance). Existing prompt-level filtering cannot exploit information developing during rollouts.

Method: Define a gate based on the mean pairwise prefix edit distance among partial action sequences within a group; stop a group when the mean distance falls below a threshold. Evaluate on a 60-iteration on-policy GRPO run on ALFWorld with Qwen2.5-7B, averaged over four seeds.

Result: Gate yields 10.7% faster wall-clock training; held-out success on 50 unseen tasks improves by 2.5 percentage points. The gain is attributed to reduced zero-advantage gradient-batch dilution. Code available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: Partial-trajectory-based gating effectively reduces wasted compute in GRPO and improves generalization on unseen tasks by enabling early stopping based on intra-group trajectory information.

Abstract: Group-relative RL training (GRPO) samples a small group of parallel rollouts for every training prompt and uses their within-group reward spread to compute per-trajectory advantages. In agentic environments each rollout is a long multi-turn dialogue with one LLM call per step, so this multi-sample multiplier dominates the total training cost. When every rollout of a prompt ends with the same reward, the group has zero reward variance and contributes no gradient, so the extra rollouts add no information; such groups are common in practice (typically around 40% of all groups), so the wasted-compute fraction is substantial rather than marginal. Existing methods filter such groups at the prompt level, either after their rollouts are paid for or before any rollout begins, but both decide without using information that becomes available during the rollout itself. We instead ask whether the in-group divergence between the partial trajectories at an intermediate step can already predict that the group will be zero-variance: when the parallel rollouts have already converged on the same action prefix, the group is on track to produce a single reward, and we can stop early. We propose a one-parameter gate that stops a group when the mean pairwise prefix edit distance between its partial action sequences falls below a threshold. On a 60-iteration on-policy GRPO run on ALFWorld with Qwen2.5-7B, averaged over four random seeds, the gated arm finishes 10.7% faster in wall-clock (bootstrap 95% CI excludes 0) and shifts held-out success rate on 50 unseen tasks by +2.5 pp, with the held-out gain tracing to a measurable reduction in zero-advantage gradient-batch dilution. Code is available at https://github.com/zhiyuanZhai20/selective-rollout.

</details>


### [221] [Retrieval from Within: An Intrinsic Capability of Attention-Based Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05806)
*Elad Hoffer,Yochai Blau,Ron Banner,Daniel Soudry,Boris Ginsburg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: INTRA unifies retrieval with generation by using decoder attention to fetch pre-encoded evidence chunks from its own internal representations, eliminating external retrievers and improving QA performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve the retriever–generator mismatch in RAG and reduce context-encoding overhead by leveraging attention over internally stored evidence.

Method: Introduce INTRA: the decoder's attention queries score pre-encoded evidence chunks and reuse them as generation context, reusing encoder states across queries to amortize encoding cost; end-to-end attention-based retrieval from internal representations.

Result: INTRA outperforms strong engineered retrieval pipelines on evidence recall and end-to-end QA quality on QA benchmarks, demonstrating that attention-based models can retrieval-elicit internal representations effectively.

Conclusion: Attention-based models inherently possess an elicitable retrieval mechanism; retrieval can be integrated rather than added as an external module, enabling more seamless RAG-like systems.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) typically treats retrieval and generation as separate systems. We ask whether an attention-based encoder-decoder can instead retrieve directly from its own internal representations. We introduce INTRA (INTrinsic Retrieval via Attention), a framework where decoder attention queries score pre-encoded evidence chunks that are then directly reused as context for generation. By construction, INTRA unifies retrieval and generation, eliminating the retriever-generator mismatch typical of RAG pipelines. This design also amortizes context encoding by reusing precomputed encoder states across queries. On question-answering benchmarks, INTRA outperforms strong engineered retrieval pipelines on both evidence recall and end-to-end answer quality. Our results demonstrate that attention-based models already possess a retrieval mechanism that can be elicited, rather than added as an external module.

</details>


### [222] [A Testable Certificate for Constant Collapse in Teacher-Guided VAEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05813)
*Zegu Zhang,Jianhua Peng,Jian Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Derives an exact threshold for input-independent constant collapse in VAEs: for any fixed nonconstant teacher T, the best constant student is the dataset-average teacher; alignment cost equals the teacher mutual information I_T(X;T). If an observed alignment loss is below this bound (with margin), the witness cannot be constant in input. Empirically validated on CIFAR-100 and Tiny-ImageNet-200 with per-seed teacher search, showing a collapse–rescue pattern; the guarantee is narrow and only certifies a nonconstant teacher-relative variation passes through the latent pathway.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To convert qualitative post-Collapse symptoms into a measurable, certifiable boundary for a concrete posterior-collapse failure mode (input-independent constant collapse).

Method: Derive an exact threshold by analyzing the alignment cost for a constant student versus a nonconstant teacher. Show that the minimal alignment cost for a constant student equals the mutual information between X and T. Use a raw witness with alignment loss to test whether it can be constant; validate with CIFAR-100 and Tiny-ImageNet-200 experiments, including per-seed teacher search and restart-from-collapse with alignment enabled.

Result: An exact boundary (threshold) that separates constant from nonconstant behavior in this collapse mode. Empirical results show staying below the threshold prevents constant behavior and that removing alignment pushes the witness into a constant regime; alignment-enabled rescues collapsed checkpoints. Baselines do not satisfy the certificate.

Conclusion: The guarantee is narrow: it certifies that a matched nonconstant teacher-relative variation passes through the latent pathway, not that all forms of posterior collapse are ruled out.

Abstract: Posterior collapse in variational autoencoders is often diagnosed by its symptoms: a small KL term, a strong decoder, or weak use of the latent code. These signals are useful, but they do not define a collapse boundary. We study a concrete failure mode, input-independent constant collapse, and show that this case admits an exact threshold. For any fixed nonconstant teacher distribution \(T(\cdot\mid x)\), the best constant student is the dataset-average teacher distribution, and its alignment cost is the teacher mutual information \(I_T(X;T)\). Therefore, if a strictly latent-only raw witness achieves alignment loss below this value, with a safety margin, the witness cannot be constant in the input.
  This identity turns a qualitative failure mode into a measurable one. In CIFAR-100 experiments with per-seed teacher search, full training stays on the certified side of the boundary, removing alignment drives the raw witness into the constant-student regime, and restarting from a collapsed checkpoint with alignment enabled restores the certificate. Tiny-ImageNet-200 fixed-target runs show the same prevention--collapse--rescue pattern across three independently searched teachers. Standard VAE-style baselines, including methods that preserve reconstruction quality or post-hoc predictability, remain negative under the raw certificate. The guarantee is intentionally narrow: it certifies that the matched nonconstant teacher-relative variation passes through the latent pathway, rather than claiming that all forms of posterior collapse have been ruled out.

</details>


### [223] [HCInfer: An Efficient Inference System via Error Compensation for Resource-Constrained Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05819)
*Shen Xu,Xiangwen Zhuge,Zhe Xu,Yingkun Hu,Zheng Yang,Yunhao Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HCInfer offloads residual compensation to CPU, leveraging asynchronous processing and sensitivity-aware dynamic rank allocation to hide overhead. It runs a compressed backbone on GPU, achieving up to 5.2% accuracy gain and 10.4x speedup over full-precision baselines on downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying large language models on consumer hardware is hindered by massive parameter counts and memory constraints. While model compression and offloading help, they often cause accuracy loss or throughput bottlenecks. Error-compensation methods using LoRA-style branches offer a path to recovery, since these branches store many parameters but only require a subset during each inference step, making them amenable to CPU/offload strategies.

Method: Propose HCInfer, a heterogeneous inference system that runs the compressed backbone on the GPU while offloading residual compensation to the CPU. It introduces an asynchronous compensation pipeline and sensitivity-aware dynamic rank allocation to hide compensation overhead and maximize accuracy recovery.

Result: Experimental results show HCInfer achieves up to 5.2% accuracy improvement on downstream tasks compared to the compression model, and up to 10.4x speedup relative to the full-precision model.

Conclusion: HCInfer demonstrates that strategically offloading compensation components and employing asynchronous processing with adaptive rank allocation can reclaim accuracy while delivering substantial throughput gains in memory-constrained LLM deployment.

Abstract: LLMs often struggle with memory-constrained deployment on consumer-grade hardware due to their massive parameter sizes. While existing solutions such as model compression and offloading improve deployment feasibility, they often suffer from substantial accuracy degradation or severe throughput bottlenecks. Recent error compensation methods recover accuracy through auxiliary LoRA-style branches, and we observe that these branches are inherently amenable to offloading: they require substantial parameter storage but access only a small subset of compensation parameters during each inference step. Motivated by this opportunity, we propose HCInfer, a heterogeneous inference system that offloads residual compensation to the CPU while executing the compressed backbone on the GPU, and further introduces an asynchronous compensation pipeline and sensitivity-aware dynamic rank allocation to hide compensation overhead and maximize accuracy recovery. Experimental results show that HCInfer achieves a maximum accuracy improvement of 5.2% on downstream tasks compared to compression model and sustaining a maximum speedup of 10.4x compared to full-precision model.

</details>


### [224] [MDN: Parallelizing Stepwise Momentum for Delta Linear Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05838)
*Yulong Huang,Xiang Liu,Hongxiang Huang,Xiaopeng Lin,Zunchang Liu,Xiaowen Chu,Zeke Xie,Bojun Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MDN introduces a momentum-based, chunkwise parallel linear attention approach for long-sequence LLMs, using a stepwise momentum rule with geometrically reordered update coefficients and gating constraints derived from a second-order dynamical system. It achieves stable training and competitive throughput, showing improved performance over Transformers, Mamba2, and GDN on 400M and 1.3B parameter models; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address optimization instability and information decay in linear attention using naive SGD and to balance training efficiency with effectiveness by incorporating momentum in a geometrically structured update and stabilizing dynamics.

Method: Develop a chunkwise parallel algorithm for linear attention with a stepwise momentum rule achieved by geometrically reordering the update coefficients. Analyze momentum recurrence as a second-order system with complex conjugate eigenvalues to design stable gating constraints. Implement the model, Momentum DeltaNet (MDN), with Triton kernels to achieve competitive throughput.

Result: MDN demonstrates comparable training throughput to strong linear models like Mamba2 and KDA and shows consistent performance gains over baselines such as Transformers, Mamba2, and GDN across 400M and 1.3B parameter experiments on diverse downstream benchmarks. Code is released at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Momentum DeltaNet provides a stable, efficient momentum-based linear attention framework for long-sequence LLMs, offering performance benefits over existing linear and Transformer baselines and validating the utility of dynamical-systems-guided gating constraints in LA.

Abstract: Linear Attention (LA) offers a promising paradigm for scaling large language models (LLMs) to long sequences by avoiding the quadratic complexity of self-attention. Recent LA models such as Mamba2 and GDN interpret linear recurrences as closed-form online stochastic gradient descent (SGD), but naive SGD updates suffer from rapid information decay and suboptimal convergence in optimization. While momentum-based optimizers provide a natural remedy, they pose challenges in simultaneously achieving training efficiency and effectiveness. To address this, we develop a chunkwise parallel algorithm for LA with a stepwise momentum rule by geometrically reordering the update coefficients. Further, from a dynamical systems perspective, we analyze the momentum-based recurrence as a second-order system that introduces complex conjugate eigenvalues. This analysis guides the design of stable gating constraints. The resulting model, Momentum DeltaNet (MDN), leverages Triton kernels to achieve comparable training throughput with competitive linear models such as Mamba2 and KDA. Extensive experiments on the 400M and 1.3B parameter models demonstrate consistent performance improvements over strong baselines, including Transformers, Mamba2 and GDN, across diverse downstream evaluation benchmarks. Code: https://github.com/HuuYuLong/MomentumDeltaNet .

</details>


### [225] [Hypothesis generation and updating in large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05851)
*Hua-Dong Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs approximate Bayesian inference on a number-reasoning task but with systematic biases and a gap between evaluation and generation, suggesting limited generalizability for scientific inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify how LLMs perform inference, compare to an optimal Bayesian model and humans, and assess consistency across inference probes.

Method: Use the number game where a learner infers rules (e.g., powers of two, intervals) from a few positive integers. Compute posterior over hypotheses via three probes: posterior prediction, hypothesis evaluation, hypothesis generation. Compare LLMs to Bayes-optimal and humans. Assess whether the same posterior is expressed across probes.

Result: LLMs are largely described by a two-parameter Bayesian fit but with systematic biases: strong-sampling leading to implicit Occam's razor (favoring narrower hypotheses), and 'thinking mode' pushing toward higher prior reliance. There is a robust evaluation-generation gap: LLMs select more correct hypotheses during evaluation but generate simpler, rule-like hypotheses. The Bayesian-with-bias pattern fails to extrapolate to parts of the hypothesis space not covered by data; LLMs can act as if holding rule-like hypotheses yet generalize poorly.

Conclusion: This limits LLMs as general problem solvers for scientific inference, where hypotheses must go beyond the observed data.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly help people solve problems, from debugging code to repairing machinery. This process requires generating plausible hypotheses from partial descriptions, then updating them as more information arrives. Yet how LLMs perform this form of inference, and how close it is to optimal, remains unclear. We study this question in the number game, a controlled setting in which a learner infers the hypothesis supported by a few positive integers, such as $\{16, 8, 2, 64\}$: a rule like powers of 2 or an interval like numbers near 20. We measure the posterior over hypotheses using three complementary probes: posterior prediction, hypothesis evaluation, and hypothesis generation. We then compare LLM behavior with an optimal Bayesian model and human behavior, and test whether the same posterior is expressed across probes. LLMs are often well described by a two-parameter Bayesian fit, but with systematic offsets: by default they show a strong-sampling assumption that creates an implicit Occam's razor, favoring narrower hypotheses, while thinking mode shifts them toward greater prior reliance. We also find a robust evaluation--generation gap: LLMs select more correct hypotheses during hypothesis evaluation but generate simpler, more rule-like hypotheses. Finally, this Bayesian-with-bias pattern does not extrapolate. Models can behave as if they hold rule-like hypotheses over observed examples, yet generalize poorly to parts of the hypothesis domain not covered by those examples. Our results highlight a limitation of LLMs as general problem solvers, especially for scientific inference, where hypotheses must go beyond the data.

</details>


### [226] [Measuring Learning Progress via Gradient-Momentum Coupling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05856)
*Samuel Blad,Martin Längkvist,Amy Loutfi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A gradient-momentum coupling signal quantifies each sample's usefulness for ongoing learning, improving robustness to noise and yielding emergent curriculum in curiosity-driven RL; can outperform prediction-error baselines and benefits MiniGrid experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prediction error-based signals often conflate random noise with learnable structure, hindering curiosity-driven exploration. A signal that reflects the optimization dynamics of learning could better identify informative samples.

Method: Introduce Gradient-Momentum Coupling (GMC): compute per-parameter normalized absolute product of the current gradient with the previous gradients' momentum. This couples instantaneous gradient information with momentum, filtering noise. GMC serves as a sample-level learning-progress signal integrated into existing curiosity-driven architectures. Evaluations include controlled experiments on noise robustness, emergent curriculum, and MiniGrid with observation noise.

Result: GMC robustly filters noise via momentum, leads to emergent curriculum where samples are prioritized by learning speed rather than difficulty, and improves robustness to observation noise when substituted for prediction error in existing curiosity frameworks on MiniGrid.

Conclusion: GMC provides a theoretically and empirically effective learning-progress signal for curiosity-driven RL, enabling robust learning and more efficient curricula by leveraging optimization dynamics. It can replace prediction error in current architectures and generalizes to noisy observation settings.

Abstract: Measuring learning progress is essential for curiosity-driven exploration in reinforcement learning, but widely used signals such as prediction error often fail to distinguish meaningful, learnable patterns from random noise. This paper proposes Gradient-Momentum Coupling (GMC), a signal derived from optimization dynamics that quantifies how useful each sample's gradient is for ongoing learning by measuring its per-parameter normalized absolute product with the momentum from previous gradients. By leveraging momentum's natural filtering of noise and oscillations, GMC identifies samples that contribute to ongoing parameter updates. Controlled experiments demonstrate noise robustness and emergent curriculum learning, with the signal prioritizing tasks by learning speed rather than difficulty. Experiments on MiniGrid suggest that replacing prediction error with GMC within existing curiosity-driven architectures can improve robustness to observation noise.

</details>


### [227] [Offline Reinforcement Learning for Rotation Profile Control in Tokamaks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05857)
*Rohit Sonker,Hiro Josep Farre Kaga,Jiayu Chen,Andrew Rothstein,Ian Char,Ricardo Shousha,Egemen Kolemen,Jeff Schneider*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Offline RL and offline model-based RL for rotation-profile control in a tokamak, trained solely on historical DIII-D data. Uses probabilistic plasma dynamics models to generate rollouts for training. Policy deployed on DIII-D with promising real-world results. Highlights challenges and insights when training/deploying RL on a complex physical device with limited data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Control of the plasma rotation profile in tokamaks is crucial for stability, confinement, and transport. The high dimensionality and multi-actuator coupling make traditional control challenging. Offline RL (and offline model-based RL) leverages historical data when simulators are unavailable, offering data-efficient policy learning.

Method: Train offline RL and offline model-based RL policies using historical DIII-D data. Build probabilistic models of plasma dynamics to generate simulated rollouts for RL training. Deploy the resulting policy on the DIII-D tokamak for real-world evaluation.

Result: The approach yields promising real-world results on DIII-D, indicating the learned policy can effectively influence the rotation profile under actual device conditions.

Conclusion: Uncover key challenges and insights from training and deploying an RL policy on a complex physical system with limited past data, including data quality, model bias, safety considerations, and deployment robustness; outline directions for improving offline RL applicability to tokamak control.

Abstract: Tokamaks remain leading candidates for achieving practical fusion energy, yet many important control problems inside these devices are still difficult or unsolved. One such challenge is controlling the plasma rotation profile, which strongly influences stability, confinement, and transport. While the average rotation can be controlled, controlling the full profile is challenging due to high dimensionality, response to multiple actuators and dependence on plasma condition. Learning-based control methods, such as reinforcement learning (RL), provide a potential solution to this challenging problem with ability to model complex interactions leading to effective multi-input multi-output control. However, learning such policies is challenging due to the lack of accurate simulators that can model the rotation profile dynamics. In this work, we investigate the use of offline RL and offline model-based RL algorithms for rotation profile control, training them solely on historical data from the DIII-D tokamak. Our final method uses probabilistic models of plasma dynamics to generate rollouts for RL training. We deploy this policy on the DIII-D Tokamak and observe promising real-world results. We conclude by highlighting key challenges and insights from training and deploying an RL policy on a complex physical device while using only limited past data.

</details>


### [228] [Do Neural Operators Forget Geometry? The Forgetting Hypothesis in Deep Operator Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05862)
*Yanming Xia,Angelica I. Aviles-Rivero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural operators progressively lose geometric information with depth (geometric forgetting), impacting performance on irregular domains; a lightweight geometry memory injection mitigates this and suggests geometry retention is a structural constraint, not optional.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why neural operators struggle on irregular geometries and whether depth inherently erodes domain geometry, formalizing the Geometric Forgetting Hypothesis.

Method: Layer-wise geometric probing across spectral and attention-based neural operators; analyze Markovian layer structure and global mixing; introduce a lightweight geometry memory injection to enforce geometric constraints at intermediate depths; assess its effect on forgetting, accuracy, stability, and generalization.

Result: Geometric forgetting occurs across both spectral and attention-based operators, with depth causing loss of geometry fidelity leading to degraded performance and instability; the memory injection consistently mitigates forgetting and reveals a geometric shortcut instability in transformer-based operators; geometry retention appears to be a structural requirement.

Conclusion: Preserving geometric information is essential for reliable operator performance on irregular domains. The proposed geometry memory injection provides a simple, effective remedy and underscores geometry retention as a design principle for neural operators, especially transformers.

Abstract: Neural operators perform well on structured domains, yet their behaviour on irregular geometries remains poorly understood. We show that this limitation is not merely an encoding issue, but a depth-wise failure mode inherent to deep operator architectures.
  We formalise the Geometric Forgetting Hypothesis: due to the Markovian structure of operator layers and their reliance on global mixing mechanisms, neural operators progressively lose access to domain geometry as depth increases.
