Using layer-wise geometric probing, we demonstrate that both spectral and attention-based operators systematically lose geometric fidelity.
  We show that this geometric forgetting degrades accuracy, stability, and generalisation. To counteract it, we introduce a lightweight geometry memory injection mechanism that restores geometric constraints at intermediate depths with minimal architectural overhead. This simple intervention consistently mitigates forgetting and exposes a geometric shortcut instability in transformer-based operators, revealing that geometric retention is a structural requirement rather than a design choice.

</details>


### [229] [SOPE: Stabilizing Off-Policy Evaluation for Online RL with Prior Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05863)
*Carlo Romeo,Girolamo Macaluso,Alessandro Sestini,Andrew D. Bagdanov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOPE introduces an actor-aligned Off-Policy Policy Evaluation signal as an automated early-stopping mechanism to dynamically determine offline training phase length in online RL, achieving substantial performance gains and large reductions in computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Incorporating prior data into online RL often creates a trade-off between lengthy training pipelines and high computational costs; automatic, adaptive scheduling is needed to preserve prior knowledge without overfitting or manual tuning.

Method: SOPE evaluates a critic on a held-out validation split under the current policy's action distribution to determine when gradient updates should stop. This actor-aligned OPE signal halts updates exactly when out-of-distribution benefits saturate, eliminating manual schedule tuning and enabling adaptive offline training phases.

Result: Evaluated on 25 continuous-control tasks from the Minari benchmark; SOPE yields up to 45.6% improvement over baselines and up to 22x reduction in TFLOPs, demonstrating a favorable trade-off between sample efficiency and computational cost.

Conclusion: Adaptive, evaluation-driven update schedules can outperform static, exhaustive schedules in online RL by automatically regulating the length of offline training phases.

Abstract: Incorporating prior data into online reinforcement learning accelerates training but typically forces a difficult trade-off between high computational costs and long, multi-stage training pipelines. While fixed-length stabilization phases are significantly more computationally efficient than static update schedules, they require task-dependent manual tuning, risking either the waste of prior knowledge or severe overfitting. To address this, we propose SOPE, an algorithm that uses an actor-aligned Off-Policy Policy Evaluation (OPE) signal as an automated early-stopping mechanism to dynamically control the length of offline training phases. By evaluating the critic on a held-out validation split under the current policy's action distribution, SOPE halts gradient updates exactly when out-of-distribution benefits saturate, eliminating the need for manual schedule tuning. Evaluated on 25 continuous control tasks from the Minari benchmark suite, SOPE improves baseline performance by up to 45.6% while reducing the required TFLOPs by up to 22x, thus balancing the tradeoff between sample and computational efficiency. These findings demonstrate that adaptive, evaluation-driven update schedules are more effective than relying on static, exhaustive update schedules.

</details>


### [230] [QuadraSHAP: Stable and Scalable Shapley Values for Product Games via Gauss-Legendre Quadrature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05870)
*Majid Mohammadi,Grigory Reznikov,Pavel Sinitcyn,Krikamol Muandet,Siu Lun Chau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Shapley values for product games can be computed exactly via a 1D integral; Gauss-Legendre quadrature with m_q nodes is exact if m_q >= ceil(d/2) and near-exact with geometric error otherwise; complexity O(d m_q) and log d parallel time; log-space stable; QuadraSHAP achieves fast, numerically stable performance across configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Alleviate the combinatorial blowup in Shapley value computation for multiplicative (product) value functions, which arise in ML explainability with product kernels or tree-based models.

Method: Derive an exact 1D integral representation of the Shapley value as the integral of a degree-(d-1) polynomial over [0,1]. Use Gauss-Legendre quadrature to approximate the integral. Prove exactness criteria (m_q ≥ ceil(d/2)) and establish geometric decay of error for smaller m_q. Implement numerically stably via log-space evaluation and a parallel associative-scan-based algorithm achieving O(d m_q) work and O(log d) parallel time.

Result: Theoretical guarantees: exact Shapley values for product games with m_q ≥ ceil(d/2). Near-exact with provable geometric error decay when m_q < ceil(d/2). Practically, a few hundred nodes suffice for thousands of features. Empirical results show QuadraSHAP is the fastest numerically stable method across tested configurations.

Conclusion: Provides a scalable, provably accurate method (QuadraSHAP) for Shapley value computation in product games, enabling efficient ML explainability for models with multiplicative structure; methods leverage a 1D integral reformulation, stable log-space evaluation, and parallel scan techniques.

Abstract: We study the efficient computation of Shapley values for \emph{product games} -- cooperative games in which the coalition value factorizes as a product of per-player terms. Such games arise in machine learning explainability whenever the value function inherits a multiplicative structure from the underlying model, as in kernel methods with product kernels and tree-based models. Our key result is that the Shapley value of each player in a product game admits an exact one-dimensional integral representation: the weighted sum over exponentially many feature coalitions collapses to the integral of a degree-$(d-1)$ polynomial over $[0,1]$, where $d$ is the total number of features. This yields a Gauss--Legendre quadrature scheme that is \emph{provably exact} whenever the number of nodes satisfies $m_q \geq \lceil d/2 \rceil$, and otherwise provides a \emph{near-exact} approximation with error provably decaying geometrically in $m_q$. In practice, a few hundred nodes can achieve highly precise estimates even with thousands of features. Building on this formulation, we derive a numerically stable implementation via log-space evaluation, together with an efficient parallel implementation based on associative scan primitives that achieves $O(d\,m_q)$ total work and $O(\log d)$ parallel time. Experiments show that \textsc{QuadraSHAP} is the fastest numerically stable method across all tested configurations.

</details>


### [231] [Retain-Neutral Surrogates for Min-Max Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05871)
*Junhao Cai,Dohun Kim,Dowon Kim,Sung Il Choi,Chengjun Jin,Juhyun Park,Changhee Joo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ROSU introduces a retain-orthogonal surrogate unlearning mechanism that yields a closed-form perturbation, enabling efficient and controlled unlearning. It improves surrogate retain loss in high-coupling regimes and remains competitive otherwise across standard vision and language benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unlearning aims to remove the influence of designated training data while preserving performance on the remaining data. Min–max unlearning can fail when forget and retain gradients align, allowing unconstrained perturbations to increase retain loss. A constrained surrogate aiming for retain-orthogonality seeks stability and efficiency.

Method: Maximize first-order forget gain under a fixed perturbation budget while enforcing zero first-order retain change, yielding a closed-form retain-orthogonal perturbation. This enables a lightweight transported outer update and amplification along the retain-neutral direction. The authors provide a curvature-controlled second-order bound on retain damage, identify a positive-alignment regime where ROSU beats standard min-max perturbations, and show near-equivalence when gradients are nearly orthogonal.

Result: Empirically, across CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, TOFU, and WMDP, ROSU’s gains are clearest in high-coupling regimes and competitive in other settings, aligning with the proposed geometric intuition.

Conclusion: ROSU offers a principled, efficient approach to approximate unlearning with theoretical guarantees and empirically favorable performance, particularly when forget and retain gradients exhibit strong coupling.

Abstract: Machine unlearning seeks to remove the influence of designated training data while preserving performance on the remaining data. Approximate unlearning can be viewed as a local editing problem; in min-max unlearning, the key local object is the surrogate point at which the retain objective is evaluated. When forget and retain gradients are strongly aligned, an unconstrained forget-maximizing perturbation can move to a surrogate point that increases retain loss. We propose Retain-Orthogonal Surrogate Unlearning (ROSU), which constrains the inner surrogate construction by maximizing first-order forget gain subject to zero first-order retain change under a fixed perturbation budget. This yields a closed-form retain-orthogonal perturbation, a lightweight transported outer update, and amplification along the retain-neutral direction. Our analysis establishes (i) a curvature-controlled second-order bound on retain damage, (ii) a positive-alignment regime in which ROSU strictly reduces surrogate retain loss relative to standard min-max perturbations, and (iii) near-equivalence when the two gradients are nearly orthogonal. Across vision and language benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, TOFU, WMDP), the empirical pattern follows this geometry: ROSU gives its clearest gains in high-coupling regimes while remaining competitive elsewhere.

</details>


### [232] [RepFlow: Representation Enhanced Flow Matching for Causal Effect Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05890)
*Yifei Xie,Jian Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RepFlow formulates causal effect estimation from observational data as a joint optimization of representation learning and Conditional Flow Matching, using entropically regularized Wasserstein distance to align treated/control representations and L2 normalization for stability, enabling both point and distributional causal effect estimation with strong empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need to estimate distributional causal effects from observational data while mitigating selection bias; existing methods focus on point estimates and lack distribution modeling.

Method: Joint optimization of representation learning and Conditional Flow Matching; minimize entropically regularized Wasserstein distance between treated and control representations; apply L2 normalization on latent space for numerical stability; use a flow model to capture distribution of potential outcomes conditioned on treatment.

Result: Extensive experiments across benchmarks show RepFlow consistently outperforms existing methods for both point and distributional causal effect estimation.

Conclusion: RepFlow provides balanced representations that enable accurate distributional causal inference in observational data, improving both point estimates and the learning of outcome distributions; potential applicability across healthcare, economics, and policy.

Abstract: Estimating causal effects from observational data has become increasingly critical in diverse fields including healthcare, economics, and social policy. The fundamental challenge in causal inference arises from the missing counterfactuals and the selection bias. Existing methods are largely limited to point estimates and lack the capacity for distribution modeling. In this work, we propose RepFlow, a novel framework that formulates causal effect estimation as a joint optimization problem integrating representation learning with Conditional Flow Matching (CFM).
  RepFlow mitigates selection bias by minimizing the entropically regularized Wasserstein distance between treated and control representations.
  To enhance numerical stability, we further introduce an $L_2$ normalization constraint on latent representations.
  This balanced representation enables the flow model to accurately capture the distribution of potential outcomes. Extensive experiments across a wide range of benchmarks demonstrate that RepFlow consistently outperforms existing methods in both point and distributional causal effect estimation.

</details>


### [233] [VARS-FL: Validation-Aligned Client Selection for Non-IID Federated Learning in IoT Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05896)
*Mohamed Lakas,Mohamed Amine Ferrag*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VARS-FL introduces a reputation-based client selection mechanism for federated learning that aligns client contributions with global validation loss reductions. It aggregates per-round contributions into a history-aware score with a participation term, requiring no changes to local training or aggregation and improving convergence on a non-IID IoT intrusion task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stateless client selection using local proxies (e.g., local loss) can misalign with the global objective in non-IID FL, causing slow convergence and instability, especially in heterogeneous IoT/IIoT settings. A robust, history-aware contribution signal is needed to guide selection.

Method: Per-round client contribution is measured as the reduction in server-side validation loss attributable to the client's update. These contributions are accumulated into a Reputation score via a sliding-window average plus a logarithmically scaled participation term, enabling exploration-exploitation in client selection. No changes to local training or aggregation; compatible with FedAvg.

Result: Empirical evaluation on a 15-class non-IID IoT intrusion detection task using Edge-IIoTset with 100 clients across several seeds. VARS-FL outperforms FedAvg, Oort, and Power-of-Choice in accuracy, F1-Macro, and loss, and converges up to 36% faster to reach 80% accuracy.

Conclusion: Validation-aligned, history-aware client selection improves the reliability and efficiency of FL in heterogeneous IoT environments and can be adopted without modifying training or aggregation steps, enhancing convergence and performance.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) systems typically employ stateless client selection, treating each communication round independently and ignoring accumulated evidence of client contribution quality. Under non-IID data, this leads to slow convergence and unstable training, particularly when selection relies on local proxies (e.g., training loss) that are misaligned with the global optimization objective. These challenges are especially pronounced in Internet of Things (IoT) and Industrial IoT (IIoT) environments, where data is highly heterogeneous and distributed across devices observing different traffic patterns. In this paper, we propose VARS-FL (Validation-Aligned Reputation Scoring for Federated Learning), a client selection framework that quantifies each client's contribution using the reduction in server-side validation loss induced by its update. These per-round signals are aggregated into a Reputation score that combines a sliding-window average of recent contributions with a logarithmically scaled participation term, enabling robust exploration-exploitation selection. VARS-FL requires no changes to local training or aggregation and remains fully compatible with standard FedAvg. We evaluate VARS-FL on a 15-class non-IID IoT intrusion detection task using the Edge-IIoTset dataset, with 100 clients across multiple seeds, and compare it against FedAvg, Oort, and Power-of-Choice. VARS-FL consistently improves accuracy, F1-Macro, and loss, while accelerating convergence (up to 36% fewer rounds to reach 80% accuracy). These results demonstrate that validation-aligned, history-aware client selection provides a more reliable and efficient training process for federated learning in heterogeneous IoT environments.

</details>


### [234] [VisMMOE: Exploiting Visual-Expert Affinity for Efficient Visual-Language MoE Offloading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05899)
*Cheng Xu,Xiaofeng Hou,Jiacheng Liu,Chao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VisMMoE introduces visual-expert affinity via selective visual token pruning to concentrate MoE expert usage, improving memory-bounded VL-MoE offloading performance with preserved accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VL-MoE models are memory- and compute-intensive. Traditional text-centric offloading assumes stable, small expert sets; however, visual-heavy inputs produce many tokens and unpredictable expert accesses, reducing offloading efficiency.

Method: Propose visual-expert affinity: prune redundant visual tokens to reduce computation and reshape expert demand; combine affinity-aware token compression, lookahead expert prediction, and cache/pipeline orchestration; implement VisMMoE across frameworks; evaluate on representative VL-MoE models/benchmarks.

Result: End-to-end inference speedups of up to 2.68x and 1.61x over strong baselines on multiple VL-MoE deployments, with competitive accuracy.

Conclusion: Token pruning that shifts expert demand toward concentrated, stable working sets improves offloading efficiency under tight memory budgets; VisMMoE is effective across frameworks and datasets.

Abstract: Large-scale vision-language mixture-of-experts (VL-MoE) models provide strong multimodal capability, but efficient deployment on memory-constrained platforms remains difficult. Existing MoE offloading systems are largely designed for text-centric workloads and become much less effective for visual-heavy inputs, where large numbers of visual tokens induce broader and less predictable expert accesses. We present VisMMoE, a VL-MoE offloading system built on a single systems insight: pruning redundant visual tokens can improve offloading not only by reducing computation, but also by reshaping expert demand. We refer to this effect as \textit{visual-expert affinity}: token pruning makes expert accesses more concentrated within layers and more stable across layers, producing a smaller and more predictable expert working set. Guided by this insight, VisMMoE combines affinity-aware token compression, lookahead expert prediction, and cache/pipeline orchestration to improve expert locality and prefetch effectiveness under tight memory budgets. We implement VisMMoE on multiple frameworks and evaluate it on representative VL-MoE models and benchmarks. VisMMoE improves end-to-end inference performance by up to 2.68x and 1.61x, respectively, over strong baselines for today's VL-MoE deployments while maintaining competitive accuracy.

</details>


### [235] [Quadratic Objective Perturbation: Curvature-Based Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05905)
*Daniel Cortild,Coralia Cartis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QOP perturbs ERM by a random quadratic term to guarantee DP without requiring gradient-bounded losses, unlike LOP. It leverages curvature (spectral properties) to control sensitivity, works in the interpolation regime, tolerates inexact solves, provides utility/empirical excess risk bounds, and can be solved efficiently with splitting methods; offers advantages over LOP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LOP relies on bounded gradients to ensure stability and privacy, which excludes many modern models. There is a need for DP-ERM methods that work under weaker assumptions and with unbounded gradients. The interpolation regime offers a natural setting where stability can be achieved via curvature rather than gradient bounds.

Method: Introduce a random quadratic form to perturb the objective. This deterministic curvature induces strong convexity, ensuring stability and DP via spectral control of the perturbation. Analyze DP guarantees in (ε,δ) under weaker assumptions, extend to approximate (inexact) solvers, derive empirical excess risk bounds, and compare theoretically and numerically to LOP. Discuss implementation via modern splitting schemes for efficient optimization.

Result: Provides (ε,δ)-DP under weaker assumptions (interpolation), preserves privacy for inexact solves, and yields meaningful utility guarantees in terms of empirical excess risk. The paper includes both theoretical guarantees and numerical comparisons showing curvature-based perturbations can outperform gradient-based LOP in relevant settings.

Conclusion: Curvature-based perturbations (QOP) extend DP-ERM applicability by removing the stringent gradient-boundedness requirement of LOP. They ensure stability through objective curvature, remain robust to inexact optimization, and can be efficiently solved with splitting methods, offering a theoretical and practical advantage over LOP.

Abstract: Objective perturbation is a standard mechanism in differentially private empirical risk minimization. In particular, Linear Objective Perturbation (LOP) enforces privacy by adding a random linear term, while strong convexity and stability are ensured by an additional deterministic quadratic term. However, this approach requires the strong assumption of bounded gradients of the loss function, which excludes many modern machine learning models. In this work, we introduce Quadratic Objective Perturbation (QOP), which perturbs the objective with a random quadratic form. This perturbation induces strong convexity and enforces stability of the problem through curvature, thereby enabling privacy and allowing sensitivity to be controlled through spectral properties of the perturbation rather than assumptions on the gradients. As a result, we obtain $(\varepsilon, δ)$-differential privacy under weaker assumptions, in the interpolation regime. Furthermore, we extend the analysis to account for approximate solutions, showing that privacy guarantees are preserved under inexact solves. Additionally, we derive utility guarantees in terms of empirical excess risk, and provide a theoretical and numerical comparison to LOP, highlighting the advantages of curvature-based perturbations. Finally, we discuss algorithmic aspects and show that the resulting problems can be solved efficiently using modern splitting schemes.

</details>


### [236] [From Drops to Grid: Noise-Aware Spatio-Temporal Neural Process for Rainfall Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05912)
*Rafael Pablos Sarabia,Joachim Nyborg,Morten Birk,Ira Assent*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DropsToGrid is a Neural Process-based framework that densifies sparse, noisy rainfall observations by fusing irregular temporal station sequences with radar context to produce stochastic, high-resolution rainfall maps with calibrated uncertainty; it outperforms baselines, even with few stations and across regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate high-resolution rainfall fields are essential for forecasting and hazard mitigation, but traditional sensors are biased/low-res and DL densification struggles with skewed, localized rainfall and noisy data; there is a need for a method that can fuse temporal station data with spatial radar context and quantify uncertainty.

Method: Neural Process-based model leveraging multi-scale feature extraction, temporal attention, and multi-modal fusion to integrate temporal sequences from noisy, irregular stations with radar-derived spatial context. Produces stochastic rainfall fields with explicit uncertainty estimates.

Result: On real-world datasets, DropsToGrid outperforms operational and DL baselines, yielding accurate high-resolution rainfall maps with well-calibrated uncertainty, effective even with few stations and in cross-regional scenarios.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the effectiveness of neural-process-based, multi-modal densification for rainfall; provides uncertainty estimates and robustness to data sparsity and cross-region transfer, suggesting potential utility for forecasting and hazard management.

Abstract: High-resolution rainfall observations are crucial for weather forecasting, water management, and hazard mitigation. Traditional operational measurements are often biased and low-resolution, limiting their ability to capture local rainfall. Accurate high-resolution rainfall maps require integrating sparse surface observations, yet existing deep learning densification methods are hindered by rainfall's skewed, localized nature, noise, and limited spatio-temporal fusion. We present DropsToGrid, a Neural Process-based method that generates dense rainfall fields by fusing temporal sequences from noisy, irregularly distributed private weather stations with spatial context from radar. Leveraging multi-scale feature extraction, temporal attention, and multi-modal fusion, the model produces stochastic, continuous rainfall estimates and explicitly quantifies uncertainty. Evaluations on real-world datasets demonstrate that DropsToGrid outperforms both operational and deep learning baselines, generating accurate high-resolution rainfall maps with well-calibrated uncertainty, even when only few stations are available and in cross-regional scenarios.

</details>


### [237] [Near-Policy: Accelerating On-Policy Distillation via Asynchronous Generation and Selective Packing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05940)
*Miao Rang,Zhenni Bi,Hang Zhou,Kai Han,Xuechun Wang,An Xiao,Xinghao Chen,Yunhe Wang,Hanting Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Asynchronous near-policy distillation (NPD) decouples student generation from training using sparse updates and Δ-IFD filtering to stabilize training, achieving substantial speedups and improved SFT scores over on-policy baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address distribution mismatch and inefficiency of standard knowledge distillation for autoregressive models; on-policy RL methods are accurate but costly, so a more efficient learning paradigm is desired.

Method: Near-Policy Distillation (NPD): asynchronous framework that decouples generation from training, uses sequence packing, sparse updates, and Δ-IFD heuristic filtering to stabilize updates, enabling supervised fine-tuning (SFT) instead of RL.

Result: Empirically shows 8.1x speedup over on-policy baselines and an 8.09% improvement over standard SFT; openPangu-Embedded-1B reaches 68.73% state-of-the-art, outperforming Qwen3-1.7B; code will be released.

Conclusion: NPD effectively narrows the exploration space, mitigates policy lag and sample noise through sparse updates and Δ-IFD filtering, enabling efficient, near-policy distillation that outperforms larger baselines on autoregressive tasks.

Abstract: Standard knowledge distillation for autoregressive models often suffers from distribution mismatch. While on-policy methods mitigate this by leveraging student-generated outputs, they rely on computationally expensive Reinforcement Learning (RL) frameworks. To improve efficiency, we propose Near-Policy Distillation (NPD), an asynchronous approach that decouples student generation from training. This reformulation enables Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with sequence packing. However, asynchronous updates inevitably introduce policy lag and sample noise, which can cause the behavior to drift from near-policy toward off-policy. To counteract this without sacrificing efficiency, NPD integrates sparse student updates and the $Δ$-IFD filtering mechanism, a heuristic sample selection mechanism that empirically stabilizes the optimization trajectory. By filtering extreme out-of-distribution samples, $Δ$-IFD prevents noise from dominating the gradients, ensuring updates remain within a safe proximal learning zone. Empirically, the NPD framework achieves a 8.1x speedup over on-policy baselines and outperforms SFT by 8.09%. Crucially, by effectively narrowing the exploration space for subsequent RL, our method enables openPangu-Embedded-1B to reach a state-of-the-art score of 68.73%, outperforming the substantially larger Qwen3-1.7B. Codes will be released soon.

</details>


### [238] [Knowing but Not Correcting: Routine Task Requests Suppress Factual Correction in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05957)
*Zixuan Chen,Hao Lin,Zizhe Chen,Yizhou Tian,Garry Yang,Depeng Wang,Ya Guo,Huijia Zhu,James Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper names 'correction suppression' where LLMs fail to correct false premises within task contexts, builds a 300-premise benchmark across eight models, reveals widespread suppression, analyzes the mechanism (knowing but not correcting), and proposes two training-free interventions (CDS and DPA) that improve factual strictness without harming reasoning, introducing a new reliability dimension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize and mitigate a prevalent failure mode in LLMs where factual corrections are overridden by task-context pressures, risking misinformation in applied use.

Method: Construct a benchmark of 300 false premises and evaluate eight models to quantify correction suppression; perform mechanistic analysis of information flow across model layers to distinguish knowledge encoding from response selection; develop two training-free interventions—Correction Direction Steering (CDS) and Dynamic Payload Amplification (DPA); test on Qwen3.5-9B and LLaMA3.1-8B to assess efficacy and effects on reasoning.

Result: Suppression rates range from 19% to 90%, with four models exceeding 80%. Mechanistic findings show 'knowing but not correcting' occurs at the response selection stage due to task context diverting attention; CDS increased correction rate on Qwen3.5-9B from 0% to 58.2%; DPA improved final-layer representations and preserved or enhanced reasoning; both interventions substantially improved factual strictness across models.

Conclusion: Correction suppression is a prevalent, tractable reliability problem. The proposed training-free interventions can substantially boost factual strictness, with DPA offering better preservation of reasoning; the work introduces 'factual strictness' as a new reliability dimension and motivates further study on generalization and trade-offs across tasks and models.

Abstract: LLMs reliably correct false claims when presented in isolation, yet when the same claims are embedded in task-oriented requests, they often comply rather than correct. We term this failure mode \emph{correction suppression} and construct a benchmark of 300 false premises to systematically evaluate it across eight models. Suppression rates range from 19\% to 90\%, with four models exceeding 80\%, establishing correction suppression as a prevalent and severe phenomenon. Mechanistic analysis reveals that suppression is not a knowledge failure: the model registers the error internally but task context diverts early-layer attention from the false claim as output intent crystallizes toward compliance at middle layers. We characterize this as \emph{knowing but not correcting} -- suppression occurs at response selection rather than knowledge encoding. Guided by this mechanism, we propose two training-free interventions. Correction Direction Steering (CDS) estimates a correction-compliance direction from matched pairs and injects it at middle layers before output intent crystallizes. Dynamic Payload Amplification (DPA) localizes payload tokens via attention divergence between early and late layers and amplifies their representation at the final layer, requiring no calibration data. Experiments on Qwen3.5-9B and LLaMA3.1-8B show both methods substantially improve factual strictness. CDS achieves the highest correction rate on Qwen3.5-9B (0\%$\to$58.2\%). DPA is the only method that preserves or improves reasoning capability on both models. These findings introduce \emph{factual strictness} -- the willingness to uphold accuracy against contextual pressures -- as a new dimension of model reliability.

</details>


### [239] [Uncertainty Estimation via Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05964)
*Eunseo Choi,Ho-Yeon Kim,Jaewon Lee,Taeyong jo,Myungjun lee,Heejin Ahn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HCM offers a sampling-free, distribution-free uncertainty estimation by decomposing outputs into a magnitude and a unit-direction; uncertainty is the violation of the hyperspherical constraint, enabling deterministic, interpretable estimates for regression and classification, with competitive accuracy and lower inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the high cost and restrictive assumptions of existing uncertainty methods (ensembles, evidential networks) in safety-critical domains; need scalable, interpretable uncertainty estimates.

Method: Decompose model outputs into a magnitude and a direction constrained to the unit hypersphere; uncertainty arises from violation of this geometric constraint; yields a deterministic uncertainty measure applicable to regression and classification; sampling-free and distribution-free.

Result: Empirical results across diverse benchmarks and real-world industrial tasks show HCM matches or surpasses ensemble and evidential approaches, with substantially lower inference cost and stronger confidence–error alignment.

Conclusion: Leverages geometric structure to deliver a versatile, interpretable uncertainty estimator that serves as a competitive alternative to conventional sampling-based or distributional approaches.

Abstract: Quantifying uncertainty in neural network predictions is essential for high-stakes domains such as autonomous driving, healthcare, and manufacturing. While existing approaches often depend on costly sampling or restrictive distributional assumptions, we propose Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping (HCM), a simple yet principled framework for sampling-free and distribution-free uncertainty estimation. HCM decomposes outputs into a magnitude and a normalized direction vector constrained to lie on the unit hypersphere, enabling a novel interpretation of uncertainty as the degree of violation of this geometric constraint. This yields deterministic and interpretable estimates applicable to both regression and classification. Experiments across diverse benchmarks and real-world industrial tasks demonstrate that HCM matches or surpasses ensemble and evidential approaches, with far lower inference cost and stronger confidence-error alignment. Our results highlight the power of geometric structure in uncertainty estimation and position HCM as a versatile alternative to conventional techniques.

</details>


### [240] [Beyond Uniform Credit Assignment: Selective Eligibility Traces for RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05965)
*Chaoli Mou,Zhan Zhuang,Xinning Chen,Yu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes S-trace, a selective eligibility traces method, to achieve fine-grained credit assignment in critic-free RL for LLM reasoning, building on P-trace and reframing GSPO as uniform credit assignment; demonstrates superior performance and efficiency over GRPO on Qwen models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve credit assignment in critic-free RL methods like GRPO by moving beyond uniform trajectory-level credit assignment, reducing variance and improving sample and token efficiency.

Method: Introduce P-trace (a sample-efficient critic-free eligibility traces method) and S-trace (sparse eligibility traces with low-entropy token masking). The paper also contextualizes GSPO as a special case within the eligibility traces framework, i.e., uniform credit assignment.

Result: Empirical gains over GRPO: +0.49% on Qwen3-1.7B, +3.16% on Qwen3-4B, and +2.98% on Qwen3-8B (average pass@16); improved sample and token efficiency.

Conclusion: S-trace achieves finer-grained credit assignment and improved performance/efficiency compared to GRPO, by leveraging selective eligibility traces and framing GSPO within the eligibility traces framework.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a key approach for improving the reasoning abilities of large language models. However, widely used critic-free algorithms such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) necessitate a ``uniform credit assignment'' assumption that indiscriminately broadcast trajectory-level advantages, hindering learning efficiency by failing to distinguish critical reasoning steps. To address this limitation, we propose Selective Eligibility Traces (S-trace). Grounded in the intuition of partial trust region preservation, we initially introduce P-trace as a sample-efficient, critic-free eligibility traces method, upon which we build S-trace, implementing a sparse eligibility traces mechanism to further mitigate variance and achieve fine-grained credit assignment by selectively masking low-entropy tokens. Theoretically, we contextualize the recent Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) method within the critic-free eligibility traces framework, identifying it as a special instance of the eligibility traces method operating under uniform credit assignment. Experiments demonstrate that S-trace not only outperforms GRPO, showing gains of 0.49\% on Qwen3-1.7B and 3.16\% on Qwen3-4B, and maintaining a robust 2.98\% improvement when scaled further to Qwen3-8B in average pass@16, but notably achieves this with simultaneously higher sample and token efficiency.

</details>


### [241] [Sharper Guarantees for Misspecified Kernelized Bandit Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05967)
*Davide Maran,Csaba Szepesvári*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Misspecification amplification in kernelized bandits can be dramatically reduced: from scales with kernel complexity (sqrt(d_eff) offline; sqrt(γ_n) n online) to polylogarithmic factors under spectral localization. Offline: high-probability simple-regret bounds governed by a spectral Lebesgue constant; online: a domain-splitting approach yields regret ~ sqrt(γ_n n) + nε, removing the extra sqrt(γ_n) factor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Misspecified kernels degrade bandit performance. Existing guarantees scale with kernel-induced complexity (d_eff or γ_n), potentially causing large penalties for even small misspecification ε. The work aims to tighten these bounds by exploiting spectral localization to limit how misspecification propagates.

Method: Offline: derive high-probability simple-regret bounds using a spectral Lebesgue constant that captures localization; establish results for 1D monotone spectra and multivariate Fourier-diagonal product kernels. Online: adapt a domain-splitting algorithm and prove a cumulative regret bound under mild localized eigendecay assumptions, achieving O~(√(γ_n n) + nε) and removing the extra √γ_n factor.

Result: Misspecification amplification reduced to logarithmic or polylogarithmic growth for the considered kernels. Offline bounds exhibit logarithmic or polylogarithmic amplification via the spectral Lebesgue constant; online bound achieves near-optimal rate with a localized misspecification term, no longer amplified by √γ_n.

Conclusion: Localization is the unifying mechanism: spectral localization governs offline Lebesgue constants, while online domain splitting implements a spatial analogue to prevent local misspecification from being globally amplified. This yields substantially tighter guarantees for misspecified kernelized bandits across both offline and online settings.

Abstract: Existing guarantees for misspecified kernelized bandit optimization pay for misspecification through kernel complexity: in generic offline bounds, the misspecification level $\varepsilon$ is multiplied by $\sqrt{d_\mathrm{eff}}$, where $d_\mathrm{eff}$ is the kernel effective dimension, while in online regret bounds, the corresponding penalty is $\sqrt{γ_n}\,n\varepsilon$, where $γ_n$ is the maximum information gain after $n$ rounds of interaction.
  In this work, we show that, for a large class of kernels, the misspecification amplification can be reduced to logarithmic or polylogarithmic growth. In the offline setting, we first prove high-probability simple-regret bounds whose misspecification term is governed by a spectral Lebesgue constant. This yields logarithmic amplification for one-dimensional monotone spectra and polylogarithmic amplification for multivariate Fourier-diagonal product kernels. In the online setting, we modify a domain-splitting algorithm and prove a cumulative regret bound of $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(\sqrt{γ_n n}+n\varepsilon)$ under mild localized eigendecay assumptions, removing the extra $\sqrt{γ_n}$ factor from the misspecification term. The common principle is localization: spectral localization controls the Lebesgue constant of the offline approximation operator, while domain splitting implements the spatial analogue of this mechanism in the online setting, preventing local misspecification errors from being amplified globally.

</details>


### [242] [Training Transformers for KV Cache Compressibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05971)
*Yoav Gelberg,Yam Eitan,Michael Bronstein,Yarin Gal,Haggai Maron*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KV compressibility is a property of learned representations; KV-CAT training with train-time KV-slot masking induces compressible representations, improving downstream compression performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context language models with KV caches incur memory and decode-time costs that scale with prefix length. Post-hoc KV compression on fixed pretrained models is limited by the representations’ compressibility. Therefore, representations should be guided to be compressible during training.

Method: KV-CAT (KV-Compression Aware Training): continued pretraining with a train-time KV sparsification policy that masks KV slots during training, forcing the model to use fewer KV slots and learn representations amenable to post-hoc compression.

Result: Empirically, KV-CAT improves the quality-budget tradeoff of downstream compression methods across tasks including retrieval, long-context question answering, and perplexity-based evaluation of compressed-prefix continuation.

Conclusion: Compressibility is a property of learned representations; targeted training (KV-CAT) can steer transformers toward compressible representations, yielding better downstream compression performance.

Abstract: Long-context language modeling is increasingly constrained by the Key-Value (KV) cache, whose memory and decode-time access costs scale linearly with the prefix length. This bottleneck has motivated a range of context-compression methods, from token-level summarization to recent optimization-based KV compression methods. These post-hoc methods operate on the KV cache of a fixed pretrained model, so their effectiveness is fundamentally limited by how well the model's internal representations can be compressed. In this work, we formalize the notion of KV compressibility and show that it is a property of the learned representations, rather than of the context alone. We prove that almost any sequence-to-vector function admits both highly compressible and inherently non-compressible transformer implementations, highlighting the need to guide transformers toward compressible representations during training. Motivated by this, we propose KV-Compression Aware Training (KV-CAT), a continued pretraining procedure that incentivizes the emergence of compressible representations. We introduce a train-time KV sparsification policy that masks KV slots during training. This forces the model to use fewer KV slots and encourages it to learn representations amenable to post-hoc compression. Empirically, we show that KV-CAT improves the quality-budget tradeoff of downstream compression methods across retrieval, long-context question answering, and perplexity-based evaluation of compressed-prefix continuation.

</details>


### [243] [Physical Fidelity Reconstruction via Improved Consistency-Distilled Flow Matching for Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05975)
*Sicheng Ma,Tianyue Yang,Xiuzhe Wu,Xiao Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Distills a high-fidelity, diffusion-based flow-matching model into a one-step consistency model for fast, conditional reconstruction from noisy low-fidelity observations; achieves large speedups with minimal performance loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconstruct high-fidelity flow fields from low-fidelity observations in latency-sensitive settings (ensemble forecasting, real-time visualization, simulation-in-the-loop inference) where iterative sampling is too costly.

Method: Train a one-step student by distilling an optimal-transport flow-matching teacher. At inference, initialize the trajectory from a noised version of the observation along the transport path to enable conditional reconstruction with the unconditional student. Evaluate on Smoke Buoyancy, Turbulent Channel Flow, and Kolmogorov Flow at up to 256×256, comparing against the teacher and a scratch-trained one-step model.

Result: The distilled student preserves teacher-like spectrum metrics with roughly 50% fewer parameters and ~12× faster inference than the teacher. With the same training budget, it also beats a one-step model trained from scratch by 23.1% in SSIM, indicating improved training efficiency due to distillation.

Conclusion: Teacher distillation can convert large, high-capacity scientific generative models into compact, fast-to-train reconstruction models suitable for real-time or latency-sensitive applications without substantial loss in fidelity.

Abstract: Reconstructing high-fidelity flow fields from low-fidelity observations is a central problem in scientific machine learning, yet recent diffusion and flow-matching models typically rely on iterative sampling, making them costly for latency-sensitive workflows such as ensemble forecasting, real-time visualization, and simulation-in-the-loop inference. We study whether a high-fidelity flow-matching generative model can be compressed into a compact one-step model for fast scientific flow reconstruction. Our approach distills an optimal-transport flow-matching teacher into a one-step consistency model. Low-fidelity observations are incorporated at inference by initializing the generative trajectory from a noised observation along the transport path, allowing an unconditional high-fidelity flow model to perform conditional reconstruction without retraining the teacher. We evaluate this distillation strategy on three fluid benchmarks, Smoke Buoyancy, Turbulent Channel Flow, and Kolmogorov Flow, using coarse-to-fine reconstruction as a controlled testbed at field sizes up to $256 \times 256$. Across these settings, the distilled student retains similar performance of the teacher's model on spectrum metrics, while using roughly half as many parameters and achieving a $12\times$ inference speedup over the flow-matching teacher. Under the same training budget, the distilled student also outperforms a one-step consistency model trained directly from scratch by $23.1\%$ in SSIM, showing that teacher distillation improves training efficiency rather than merely accelerating sampling. These results suggest a promising route for turning future high-capacity scientific generative models into compact reconstruction models that are faster to train, cheaper to run, and easier to deploy.

</details>


### [244] [Towards Steering without Sacrifice: Principled Training of Steering Vectors for Prompt-only Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05983)
*Yuntai Bao,Qinfeng Li,Xinyan Yu,Xuhong Zhang,Ge Su,Wenqi Zhang,Liu Yan,Haiqin Weng,Jianwei Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Jointly train steering directions and factors to remove post-hoc factor selection, and introduce Prompt-only SV (PrOSV) that intervenes on a limited set of prompt tokens; PrOSV outperforms full-sequence SVs on AxBench under joint training and offers a better utility–robustness tradeoff.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two main issues with fine-tuned steering vectors: (1) the need to carefully select steering factors for each SV to balance steering strength and generation quality; (2) full-sequence SVs that intervene across the entire generation can degrade quality. The paper aims to enable stable, efficient joint training and to reduce intervention scope without sacrificing performance.

Method: 1) Jointly train steering factors and directions using a neural-network scaling perspective, finding that moderately large initialization sizes and learning rates are crucial for stability and efficiency. 2) Propose Prompt-only SV (PrOSV) that intervenes only on a few prompt tokens, inspired by representation fine-tuning. 3) Evaluate on AxBench, comparing against traditional full-sequence SVs (FSSVs) under the joint-training scheme, and analyze the utility–robustness tradeoff.

Result: Joint training eliminates the need for post-hoc factor selection and yields effective steering; PrOSV outperforms FSSVs on AxBench with the proposed joint-training setup; PrOSV achieves a better balance between general model utility and adversarial robustness than FSSV.

Conclusion: A joint-training framework for steering vectors combined with Prompt-only SV (PrOSV) provides a practical, efficient, and robust approach to steering LLMs, reducing manual tuning and preserving generation quality while enhancing robustness against adversarial inputs.

Abstract: Recently, steering vectors (SVs) have emerged as an effective and lightweight approach to steer behaviors of large language models (LLMs), among which fine-tuned SVs are more effective than optimization-free ones. However, current approaches to fine-tuned SVs suffer from two limitations. First, they require careful selection of steering factors on a per-SV basis to balance steering effectiveness and generation quality at inference time. Second, they operate as full-sequence SVs (FSSVs), which can sacrifice generation quality regardless of factor selection due to excessive intervention on the model generation process. To address the first limitation, we propose joint training of steering factors and directions, such that post-hoc factor selection is no longer required. Using neural network scaling theory, we find that moderately large initialization sizes and learning rates for steering factors are essential for stability and efficiency of joint training. To tackle the second limitation, we draw inspiration from representation fine-tuning and introduce Prompt-only SV (PrOSV), an SV that intervenes only on a few prompt tokens. Our empirical results show that PrOSV outperforms traditional FSSVs on AxBench when using our joint training scheme. We also find that PrOSV achieves a better tradeoff between general model utility and adversarial robustness than FSSV.

</details>


### [245] [DiBA: Diagonal and Binary Matrix Approximation for Neural Network Weight Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05994)
*Nobutaka Ono*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiBA proposes a compact matrix factorization for neural network weights using a product of three diagonals and two binary matrices, enabling reduced storage and FLOPs. It introduces DiBA-Greedy for optimization and DiBARD for retraining only diagonal factors, showing improved SNR and task accuracy in pretrained-model experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce storage and compute of dense weight matrices (linear layers, 1x1 convs, attention, embeddings) in modern networks while preserving accuracy, enabling efficient deployment.

Method: A in R^{m×n} factorization: A ≈ D1 B1 D2 B2 D3, with Diagonal D1,D2,D3 and binary B1,B2. Intermediate rank k controls storage/accuracy. For mat-vec, replace multiplies with three element-wise scales and two binary mixings (cost m+k+n multiplies). Optimization via DiBA-Greedy: alternating updates with closed-form LS for diagonals and exact one-bit improvement tests for binaries. DiBARD replaces dense layers with DiBA factors, freezes binaries, retunes only diagonals downstream. Empirical evaluation on 40 dense matrices from public pretrained models.

Result: DiBA-Greedy yields consistent SNR improvements as the storage ratio increases. In two component-replacement studies, DiBARD improves DistilBERT/WikiText masked-token accuracy from 0.4447 to 0.5210 and Speech Commands accuracy for an Audio Spectrogram Transformer from 0.7684 to 0.9781 without reoptimizing binary factors.

Conclusion: DiBA enables compact, efficient neural network weight representations with controllable storage-accuracy trade-offs. The proposed DiBA-Greedy and DiBARD variants offer practical pathways for deploying compressed models with limited retraining, maintaining or even enhancing task performance.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose DiBA (Diagonal and Binary Matrix Approximation), a compact matrix factorization for neural network weight compression. Many components of modern networks, including linear layers, $1\times1$ convolutions, attention projections, and embedding layers, have dense matrix weights. DiBA approximates $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ by $\widehat A=D_1B_1D_2B_2D_3$, where $D_1,D_2,D_3$ are diagonal matrices and $B_1,B_2$ are $0/1$ binary matrices. The intermediate dimension $k$ controls the trade-off between theoretical storage and approximation accuracy. For matrix-vector products, DiBA decomposes dense multiplication into three element-wise scaling operations and two binary mixing operations, reducing the floating-point multiplication count from $mn$ to $m+k+n$. For optimization, we introduce DiBA-Greedy, an alternating solver that combines closed-form least-squares updates for the diagonal factors with exact one-bit improvement tests for the binary factors. We also introduce DiBARD (DiBA with Retuning only Diagonal factors), which replaces dense-matrix layers by DiBA factors, freezes the binary matrices, and retunes only the diagonal entries on downstream data. This preserves compact binary mixing without discrete search during adaptation. On 40 dense weight matrices extracted from public pretrained models, DiBA-Greedy yields consistent SNR improvements as the theoretical storage ratio increases. After DiBA replacement in two component-replacement studies, DiBARD improves DistilBERT/WikiText masked-token accuracy from 0.4447 to 0.5210 and Speech Commands test accuracy for an Audio Spectrogram Transformer from 0.7684 to 0.9781 without reoptimizing the binary factors.

</details>


### [246] [A Fine-Grained Understanding of Uniform Convergence for Halfspaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06004)
*Aryeh Kontorovich,Kasper Green Larsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The abstract presents a fine-grained uniform convergence analysis for halfspaces, distinguishing inhomogeneous halfspaces (d≥2) from homogeneous ones in R^2, and deriving tight, dimension- and structure-dependent bounds that go beyond standard VC bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the precise uniform convergence behavior of halfspaces beyond worst-case VC bounds, identifying dimensional and structural thresholds that separate qualitatively different regimes.

Method: For inhomogeneous halfspaces (d≥2): leverage standard VC-based arguments to show tightness of bounds, yielding population error Θ(d ln(n/d)/n) for consistent hypotheses and agnostic deviation √(τ ln(1/τ)) at true error τ. For homogeneous halfspaces in R^2: in the realizable case, show that every consistent hypothesis has error O(1/n). In the agnostic case, develop a bandwise, log-free deviation bound on each dyadic risk band using a critical-wedge localization argument; union over bands adds an unavoidable ln ln n overhead. Complement with a matching lower bound.

Result: A precise, nearly complete picture: (i) Inhomogeneous halfspaces: first-order VC bounds are essentially tight; population error scales as Θ(d ln(n/d)/n), agnostic deviation scales as √(τ ln(1/τ)). (ii) Homogeneous halfspaces in R^2: realizable case yields O(1/n) error for any consistent hypothesis; agnostic case yields a bandwise, log-free deviation bound per dyadic band with an unavoidable ln ln n overhead, and a matching lower bound. These results reveal sharp thresholds by dimension and by symmetry (homogeneous vs inhomogeneous).

Conclusion: There is a sharp, dimension- and structure-dependent break in uniform convergence behavior for halfspaces. Inhomogeneous halfspaces exhibit near-V C bounds with dimensional dependence, while homogeneous halfspaces in the plane admit substantially stronger, bandwise deviation control in the agnostic setting, up to an only mildly super-logarithmic overhead. This yields a near-complete characterization of uniform convergence regimes for halfspaces.

Abstract: We study the fine-grained uniform convergence behavior of halfspaces beyond worst-case VC bounds. For inhomogeneous halfspaces in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with $d\ge 2$, we show that standard first-order VC bounds are essentially tight: even consistent hypotheses can incur population error $Θ(d\ln(n/d)/n)$, and in the agnostic setting the deviation scales as $\sqrt{τ\ln(1/τ)}$ at true error $τ$. In contrast, homogeneous halfspaces in $\mathbb{R}^2$ exhibit a markedly different behavior. In the realizable case, every hypothesis consistent with the sample has error $O(1/n)$. In the agnostic case, we prove a bandwise, log-free deviation bound on each dyadic risk band via a critical-wedge localization argument. Unioning over bands incurs only a $\ln\ln n$ overhead, and we establish a matching lower bound showing this overhead is unavoidable. Together, these results give a fine-grained and nearly complete picture of uniform convergence for halfspaces, revealing sharp dimensional and structural thresholds.

</details>


### [247] [Quantizing With Randomized Hadamard Transforms: Efficient Heuristic Now Proven](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06014)
*Ran Ben-Basat,William Kuszmaul,Michael Mitzenmacher,Amit Portnoy,Shay Vargaftik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs) approximate uniform random rotations (URRs) for many modern quantization/inference tasks, with Gaussian-like coordinate marginals and asymptotic performance matching URRs. However, vector quantization (VQ) benefits from three RHTs due to cross-block covariance decay. An O(d) moment-based check enables runtime adaptivity of the number of RHTs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess worst-case performance of randomized Hadamard transforms relative to URRs, quantify coordinate distributions after composition, and evaluate implications for DRIVE, QUIC-FL, and Vector Quantization. Propose practical adaptivity to improve efficiency.

Method: Theoretically analyze the effect of composing RHTs on a d-dimensional input: show that after two RHTs, each fixed coordinate of the normalized rotated vector is within O(d^{-1/2}) of a standard Gaussian in both Kolmogorov and 1-Wasserstein distances. Apply these bounds to analyses of DRIVE and QUIC-FL to show asymptotic URR-matching performance; show that three RHTs induce decaying coordinate covariance, enabling VQ to retain URR-like error; propose a linear-time moment-based check to adapt the number of RHTs at run time based on input moments.

Result: Two RHTs yield near-Gaussian marginals for each coordinate and achieve asymptotic performance close to URRs in DRIVE and QUIC-FL. However, two RHTs are insufficient for Vector Quantization, which requires three RHTs to achieve decaying cross-coordinate covariance. A linear-time O(d) moment check allows adaptive selection of the number of RHTs in practice, improving performance on non-adversarial inputs.

Conclusion: Two RHTs suffice for many modern compression/inference tasks, but VQ needs three for covariance decay. Practical, non-adversarial inputs benefit from adaptive RHT counts via simple moment checks, balancing performance and cost.

Abstract: Uniform random rotations (URRs) are a common preprocessing step in modern quantization approaches used for gradient compression, inference acceleration, KV-cache compression, model weight quantization, and approximate nearest-neighbor search in vector databases. In practice, URRs are often replaced by randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs), which preserve orthogonality while admitting fast implementations. The remaining issue is the performance for worst-case inputs. With a URR, each coordinate is individually distributed as a shifted beta distribution, which converges to a Gaussian distribution in high dimensions. Generally, one RHT is not suitable in the worst case, as individual coordinates can be far from these distributions. We show that after composing two RHTs on any $d$-sized input vector, the marginal distribution of every fixed coordinate of the normalized rotated vector is within $O(d^{-1/2})$ of a standard Gaussian both in Kolmogorov distance and in $1$-Wasserstein distance. We then plug these bounds into the analyses of modern compression schemes, namely DRIVE and QUIC-FL, and show that two RHTs achieve performance that asymptotically matches URRs.
  However, we show that two RHTs may not be sufficient for Vector Quantization (VQ), which often requires weak correlation across fixed-size blocks of coordinates (as opposed to only marginal distribution convergence for single coordinates). We prove that a composition of three RHTs leads to decaying coordinate covariance. This ensures that any fixed, bounded, multi-dimensional VQ codebook optimized for URRs has the same expected error when using three RHTs, up to an additive term that vanishes with the dimension.
  Finally, because practical inputs are rarely adversarial, we propose a linear-time ${O}(d)$ check on the input's moments to dynamically adapt the number of RHTs used at runtime to improve performance.

</details>


### [248] [Matrix-Decoupled Concentration for Autoregressive Sequences: Dimension-Free Guarantees for Sparse Long-Context Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06017)
*Pei-Sen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC), a sharp McDiarmid-type inequality for dependent sequences in autoregressive LLMs. It preserves coordinate sparsity to avoid variance blow-up, yielding a dimension-free O(1) variance proxy and optimal constants for Markov chains, with order-optimal bounds for causal trees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tight, dimension-free concentration bounds for sequence-level evaluations in autoregressive models are hindered by dependencies and the loss of causal structure in existing methods. A framework that preserves sparsity and strictly causal filtration is needed.

Method: Derives a matrix-vector formulation of the dependency resolvent, yielding a Matrix-Decoupled Concentration inequality. Utilizes directed d-separation to exploit causal structure (Markov chains, causal trees) and preserves coordinate-wise reward sparsity within a strictly causal setting.

Result: Provides a sharp, dimension-free O(1) variance proxy for dependent sequences in autoregressive settings. Recovers optimal constants for Markov chains and yields order-optimal bounds for causal trees, offering a rigorous justification for stability in long-context reasoning.

Conclusion: MDC resolves two bottlenecks in existing analyses by coupling dependency with target sensitivity in a matrix-driven framework, enabling tight, causal-constrained concentration bounds suitable for sequence-level evaluations in autoregressive LLMs.

Abstract: Sequence-level evaluations in autoregressive Large Language Models (LLMs) rely on highly dependent token generation. Establishing tight concentration bounds for these processes remains a challenge due to two fundamental bottlenecks in existing frameworks: (i) classical inequalities typically separate dependency structures from target sensitivities, leading to a scalar collapse that inflates the variance proxy to a suboptimal $\mathcal{O}(N)$ for sparse terminal rewards; (ii) conversely, while certain spatial methods achieve tighter bounds, they lack the strictly causal filtration required by sequential generation, rendering them inapplicable to the autoregressive setting. To resolve both bottlenecks, we establish a sharp McDiarmid-type inequality for dependent sequences, governed strictly by the exact matrix-vector multiplication of the causal dependency resolvent and the target sensitivity vector. This Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC) framework natively recovers optimal constants for Markov chains and exploits directed $d$-separation to yield order-optimal bounds for causal trees. Crucially, by exactly preserving the coordinate-wise sparsity of rewards within a strictly causal framework, MDC mathematically prevents scalar collapse, guaranteeing a dimension-free $\mathcal{O}(1)$ variance proxy and providing a rigorous mathematical justification for the stability of long-context reasoning.

</details>


### [249] [Multi-agent decision making: A Blackwell's informativeness approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06028)
*Zheng Zhang,Cuong C. Nguyen,Kevin Wells,Gustavo Carneiro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A principled Blackwell-info analysis of multi-LLM decisions shows voting/debate are not more informative than pooling all agents' private info; Bayesian pooled posterior maximisation is the info-theoretic upper bound. A practical QA method estimates individual posteriors and uses a product-of-posteriors to approximate the pooled posterior, yielding state-of-the-art results on six QA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide formal guarantees on the informativeness of multi-agent LLM decision rules and address the gap where existing voting/debate methods are ad-hoc and lack guarantees.

Method: Theoretical analysis within Blackwell's information-structure framework demonstrating that voting and debate yield information structures no more informative than the pooled private information of all agents; identify Bayesian pooled posterior maximisation as an upper-bound under Blackwell ordering. Propose a practical QA method that (i) estimates each agent's posterior, and (ii) approximates the pooled posterior via a product-of-posteriors estimator.

Result: Establishes an information-theoretic upper bound via Bayesian pooled posterior maximisation; empirically, the proposed product-of-posteriors method for multi-LLM QA outperforms state-of-the-art voting/debate methods across six QA benchmarks.

Conclusion: Bayesian pooled posterior maximisation serves as a principled upper bound for decision informativeness in multi-LLM systems; the proposed product-of-posteriors approach provides a practical, superior QA method that aligns with the theory.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has motivated research on decision-making in multi-agent systems, where multiple agents collaborate to achieve shared objectives. Existing aggregation approaches, such as voting and debate, are largely ad-hoc and lack formal guarantees regarding the informativeness of the resulting decisions. In this paper, we provide a principled approach to analyse decisions made in the multi-LLM setting using Blackwell's informativeness framework. Within the Blackwell information-structure abstraction, we show that voting and debate induce information structures that are no more informative than the pooled private information of all agents. This result identifies Bayesian pooled posterior maximisation as an information-theoretic upper-bound decision rule under the Blackwell ordering. Motivated by this theoretical analysis, we introduce a practical method for LLM-based question-answering (QA) tasks that estimates each agent's posterior and approximates the pooled posterior using a product-of-posteriors estimator. Extensive experiments on six QA benchmarks demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art multi-LLM debate and voting methods.

</details>


### [250] [Does Synthetic Data Help? Empirical Evidence from Deep Learning Time Series Forecasters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06032)
*Hugo Cazaux,Eyjólfur Ingi Ásgeirsson,Hlynur Stefánsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A large, architecture-aware study of synthetic data augmentation for time-series forecasting shows mixed benefits: channel-mixing models often gain, channel-independent models suffer; gains can be dramatic in low-resource cases, but overall augmentation hurts in two-thirds of trials. The Seasonal-Trend generator is the only consistently beneficial synthetic signal, while hard curriculum switching degrades performance. The work provides concrete guidelines and releases code for replication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify when and how synthetic data can help time-series forecasting, given conflicting results in existing literature, by conducting a large-scale, controlled empirical evaluation across architectures, synthetic signals, datasets, and resource regimes.

Method: Systematic empirical study with 9 experiment groups and 4,218 runs evaluating synthetic augmentation across 5 architectures, 4 synthetic signals, and 7 datasets. Analyses include architecture-dependence (channel mixing vs. channel independent), low-resource settings, and curriculum strategies (soft vs. hard scheduling).

Result: Overall augmentation hurts in 67% of trials. Channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) benefit in the majority of trials; channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST) are consistently degraded. In low-resource settings, gains can be substantial (e.g., TimesNet with 10% Weather data plus synthetic augmentation surpassing full-data baseline in 4 of 16 sparsity-dataset combos). The Seasonal-Trend generator is the only synthetic signal that reliably helps across benchmarks. Hard curriculum switching causes substantial degradation (+24% MSE).

Conclusion: Guidelines: use synthetic augmentation with channel-mixing architectures; adopt gradual annealing schedules; treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent. Code is available for replication.

Abstract: Synthetic data has transformed language model training, yet its role in time series forecasting remains poorly understood. We present a large-scale empirical study: nine experiment groups, 4,218 runs systematically evaluating synthetic time series augmentation across five architectures, four synthetic signals and seven datasets. The effect is sharply architecture-conditional: channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) benefit in the majority of trials, while channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST) are consistently degraded. In selected low-resource settings the gains are striking: TimesNet trained on only 10\% of Weather data with synthetic augmentation surpasses the full-data baseline (4 of 16 sparsity-dataset combinations). Averaged across all architectures, augmentation hurts in 67\% of trials. We further find that only the Seasonal-Trend generator reliably helps across the tested benchmarks, and that hard curriculum switching is actively harmful (+24\% MSE degradation). These results provide concrete, actionable guidelines on how to use synthetic data: use synthetic augmentation with channel-mixing architectures, use gradual annealing schedules, and treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/hugoiscracked/synthetic-ts/tree/main}

</details>


### [251] [Optimal Transport for LLM Reward Modeling from Noisy Preference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06036)
*Licheng Pan,Haochen Yang,Haoxuan Li,Yunsheng Lu,Yongqi Tong,Yinuo Wang,Shijian Wang,Zhixuan Chu,Lei Shen,Yuan Lu,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SelectiveRM uses optimal transport to robustly align model predictions with noisy human preferences; it introduces Joint Consistency Discrepancy and Mass Relaxation via partial transport to exclude outliers, yielding a tighter bound on clean risk and superior performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Noisy preferential data in RLHF hinders learning; homogeneous noise assumptions fail to capture linguistic preference complexity; a selective, transport-based denoising approach is needed.

Method: Introduce Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align model-prediction and preference distributions; apply Mass Relaxation via partial transport to relax strict mass conservation and automatically exclude conflicting samples; prove tighter upper bound on unobserved clean risk; empirically validate.

Result: Significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks; robust to noisy preferences; scalable with partial transport.

Conclusion: SelectiveRM provides a principled, optimal-transport-based solution for denoising preferences in RLHF, improving robustness and performance, with potential applicability to other preference-based learning tasks.

Abstract: Reward models are fundamental to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), yet real-world datasets are inevitably corrupted by noisy preference. Conventional training objectives tend to overfit these errors, while existing denoising approaches often rely on homogeneous noise assumptions that fail to capture the complexity of linguistic preferences. To handle these challenges, we propose SelectiveRM, a framework grounded in optimal transport. We first devise a Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align the distribution of model predictions with preference data. Furthermore, to address the limitation of strict mass conservation which compels the model to fit outliers, we incorporate a Mass Relaxation mechanism via partial transport. This enables the autonomous exclusion of samples with noisy preference that contradict semantic consistency. Theoretically, we demonstrate that SelectiveRM optimizes a tighter upper bound on the unobserved clean risk. Extensive experiments validate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.

</details>


### [252] [Requests of a Feather Must Flock Together: Batch Size vs. Prefix Homogeneity in LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06046)
*Saksham Rathi,Preeti,Mythili Vutukuru*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Feather is a prefix-aware RL-based scheduler (with a lightweight Chunked Hash Tree, CHT) that optimizes batch formation for autoregressive LLM decoding by balancing batch size and prefix homogeneity. It reduces KV cache accesses and improves end-to-end throughput, especially for workloads with prefix sharing, while remaining neutral when prefix sharing is weak.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive decoding is memory-bound due to KV cache attendance for all prior tokens. While batching can boost throughput, larger heterogeneous batches underperform compared to smaller, prefix-homogeneous batches because of locality in KV cache accesses. Existing schedulers reuse prefixes to save memory but do not stop batching at smaller, homogeneous batches that could yield higher throughput. Prefix detection via radix-tree traversals imposes substantial CPU overhead, comparable to GPU time, motivating a low-overhead solution.

Method: Introduce Feather, a reinforcement-learning-based scheduler that learns the optimal tradeoff between batch size and prefix homogeneity for throughput. Develop Chunked Hash Tree (CHT) to enable fast, low-overhead prefix detection and request selection for the RL agent. Integrate Feather into vLLM and SGLang and evaluate across workloads with varying prefix sharing.

Result: Feather achieves 2–10× higher end-to-end throughput than existing schedulers, by reducing the total KV cache accesses and outperforming prefix-aware attention kernels with the same goal. When workload prefix sharing is insufficient, Feather performs no worse than the status quo.

Conclusion: Prefix-aware RL scheduling combined with a lightweight prefix-detection structure yields substantial throughput gains for autoregressive decoding in LLMs, especially under prefix-sharing workloads, while maintaining performance on workloads with limited prefix reuse.

Abstract: Auto-regressive token generation in large language models is memory-bound because it requires "attending to" key and value tensors (KV cache) of all previous tokens. Prior work aims to improve the efficiency of this decode process by batching multiple requests together, and maximizing batch size subject to GPU memory constraints. The key observation of our work is that with prefix-sharing workloads, smaller, prefix-homogeneous batches -- where all requests share a common prefix -- can achieve higher decode throughput than larger, heterogeneous batches, due to better spatial and temporal locality during KV cache accesses. However, prefix-aware schedulers in state-of-the-art inference engines maximize prefix reuse within a batch only to reduce KV cache memory footprint, but do not stop batch formation at smaller homogeneous batches that could have performed better. Further, we show that shared prefix detection in existing schedulers relies on radix-tree traversals, incurring substantial CPU overhead that is often comparable to GPU execution time. This paper presents Feather, a prefix-aware scheduler that uses reinforcement learning (RL) to learn the optimal tradeoff between batch size and prefix homogeneity. We also introduce Chunked Hash Tree (CHT), a lightweight data structure that enables fast prefix detection and efficient request selection for the RL scheduler, avoiding expensive tree traversals. We integrate Feather into vLLM and SGLang, and our evaluation shows that Feather achieves 2--10$\times$ higher end-to-end throughput as compared to existing schedulers, while doing no worse than the status quo when the workload does not have enough prefix sharing. Feather achieves these gains by reducing the total number of KV cache accesses, surpassing the performance of prefix-aware attention kernels that have the same goal.

</details>


### [253] [TFM-Retouche: A Lightweight Input-Space Adapter for Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06047)
*Duong Nguyen,Mohammed Jawhar,Nicolas Chesneau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TFM-Retouche introduces an architecture-agnostic input-space residual adapter for tabular foundation models (TFMs) to enable lightweight per-task adaptation without full fine-tuning. It achieves state-of-the-art results on TabArena-Lite with TabICLv2-Retouche, boosting Elo and offering a favorable accuracy/time trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frozen inductive biases in TFMs limit post-hoc adaptation; weight-space fine-tuning is costly and its benefits are mixed. There is a need for a lightweight, architecture-agnostic, input-space adaptation that can align data with a pretrained TFM’s biases without extensive fine-tuning.

Method: Propose TFM-Retouche: a small input-space residual adapter that learns a residual correction to inputs. Trained end-to-end while freezing the TFM backbone; includes a post-training identity guard to revert to the unmodified TFM if adaptation harms performance on held-out validation. Evaluated on TabArena-Lite with TabICLv2-Retouche.
