Result: On TabArena-Lite (51 datasets spanning binary, multiclass, and regression), TabICLv2-Retouche achieved top-ranked performance with light per-task tuning and ensembling, increasing aggregate Elo by +56 over the frozen TabICLv2 base and lying on the Pareto front for predictive quality vs training/inference time.

Conclusion: An architecture-agnostic, input-space residual adapter can effectively adapt TFMs to new tabular tasks with minimal tuning, delivering strong performance and favorable efficiency characteristics.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models (TFMs), such as TabPFN-2.6, TabICLv2, ConTextTab, Mitra, LimiX, and TabDPT, achieve strong zero-shot performance through in-context learning, but their inductive biases remain fixed at inference time. Adapting a pretrained TFM to a specific dataset or task typically requires either full fine-tuning, which is computationally expensive, or parameter-efficient tuning methods (PEFT) such as LoRA, which must be tailored to the internal architecture of each TFM. Furthermore, the evidence on whether weight-space fine-tuning improves accuracy or calibration is mixed \citep{tanna_exploring_2026,rubachev_finetuning_2025}. We introduce TFM-Retouche, a lightweight input-space residual adapter that is architecture-agnostic by design with respect to the frozen TFM backbone. TFM-Retouche learns a small residual correction in the input space to align the input data with the inductive biases of the pretrained model. The adapter is trained end-to-end through the frozen TFM, with a post-training identity guard that falls back to the unmodified TFM whenever adaptation does not help on held-out validation. On TabArena-Lite (51 datasets spanning binary classification, multiclass classification, and regression), TabICLv2-Retouche -- the framework instantiated on TabICLv2 -- is the top-ranked method on the leaderboard with light per-task tuning and ensembling, lifting aggregate Elo by +56 over the frozen TabICLv2 base and sitting on the Pareto front of predictive quality versus both training and inference time.

</details>


### [254] [When Brain Networks Travel: Learning Beyond Site](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06050)
*Yingxu Wang,Kunyu Zhang,Yanwu Yang,Thomas Wolfers,Yujie Wu,Siyang Gao,Nan Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CORE is a cross-site OOD-robust brain-network learning framework that decouples site confounds, models transient dynamics on a cross-site connectivity scaffold, and uses a prior-guided gating mechanism to adapt to individuals, achieving robust cross-site performance and atlas-robustness across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-site out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization in fMRI-based brain network analysis is hampered by site-specific confounders and the loss of transient neurodynamics due to temporal averaging. A method that (i) mitigates site bias, (ii) preserves/transduces transient dynamics, and (iii) adapts to subject variability is needed for unseen sites.

Method: CORE comprises three stages: (1) site-aware confounder decoupling to extract reproducible cross-site diagnostic connectivity edges; (2) profiling transient pathway dynamics on this scaffold via lightweight temporal descriptors and constructing a line graph for transferable pathway-level modeling; (3) a prior-guided subject-adaptive gating mechanism that uses scaffold-derived population priors while preserving individual connectivity variability.

Result: In leave-one-site-out evaluations on real-world datasets (ABIDE, REST-meta-MDD, SRPBS, ABCD), CORE achieves consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines, with up to 6.7% relative gain, and maintains performance gains across different brain parcellations (atlas variations).

Conclusion: CORE offers a unified, robust approach for cross-site brain network learning that effectively mitigates site bias, captures transient dynamics on a transferable scaffold, and adapts to subject variability, yielding strong cross-site generalization and atlas robustness.

Abstract: Graph-based learning on functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has shown strong potential for brain network analysis. However, existing methods degrade under cross-site out-of-distribution (OOD) settings because site-conditioned confounders induce non-pathological shortcuts, while functional connectivity constructed by temporal averaging obscures transient neurodynamics, limiting generalization to unseen sites. In this paper, we propose Cross-site OOD Robust brain nEtwork (CORE), a unified framework for brain network learning across unseen sites. CORE first performs site-aware confounder decoupling to mitigate site-conditioned bias and extract a cross-site population scaffold of reproducible diagnostic connectivity edges. It then profiles transient pathway dynamics over this scaffold using lightweight temporal descriptors and organizes scaffold edges into a line graph for transferable pathway-level modeling. Finally, CORE introduces a prior-guided subject-adaptive gating mechanism that leverages scaffold-derived population priors while preserving subject-specific connectivity variability. Extensive experiments under leave-one-site-out evaluation on real-world datasets (ABIDE, REST-meta-MDD, SRPBS, and ABCD) show that CORE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with up to 6.7% relative gain. Furthermore, CORE remains robust to atlas variations, maintaining performance gains across different brain parcellation schemes.

</details>


### [255] [Towards Generation-Efficient Uncertainty Estimation in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06053)
*Mingcheng Zhu,Yu Liu,Tingting Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Uncertainty estimation for LLMs can be performed with partial generation or input-only signals, reducing inference cost while maintaining reliability; two methods—Logit Magnitude (top-M logit evidence from early prefixes) and MetaUE (input-only estimator distilled from generation-based uncertainty)—achieve competitive performance, enabling earlier detection of unreliable responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Healthcare, finance, and other high-stakes domains require reliable uncertainty estimates for LLM outputs, but existing methods rely on full autoregressive generations, incurring high costs and delays.

Method: 1) Propose a unified framework treating uncertainty estimation as an early estimation problem along the autoregressive generation process; 2) introduce Logit Magnitude, using top-M logit evidence from an early-stopped prefix; 3) introduce MetaUE, a distillation approach that learns an input-only estimator using generation-based uncertainty scores; 4) extensive experiments across general and domain-specific benchmarks.

Result: Logit Magnitude delivers strong performance with evidence that partial generation often suffices for effective uncertainty estimation; MetaUE provides a competitive input-only approximation in several settings.

Conclusion: Effective uncertainty estimation in LLMs can be achieved with less generation than commonly assumed, enabling earlier identification of unreliable outputs and faster decision-making in high-stakes applications.

Abstract: Uncertainty estimation is important for deploying LLMs in high-stakes applications such as healthcare and finance, where hallucinations can appear fluent and plausible while being factually incorrect, making it difficult for users to judge whether an output should be trusted. Existing methods require one or more full autoregressive generations to estimate uncertainty, which introduces substantial inference cost and often delays uncertainty assessment. In this paper, we investigate whether effective uncertainty estimation can be achieved with partial generation or even input-only information. Specifically, we first develop a unified framework that formulates uncertainty estimation as an early estimation problem over the autoregressive generation process of LLMs. This framework organises existing and proposed estimators by the information they observe, ranging from multi-generation to input-only prediction, and clarifies the performance-cost trade-off underlying different uncertainty estimation methods. Building on this view, we study two largely underexplored low-cost settings: estimating uncertainty with part of the generation, and predicting uncertainty from the input prompt. We propose Logit Magnitude, which uses top-M logit evidence to estimate uncertainty from an early-stopped generation prefix, and MetaUE, which distils generation-based uncertainty into a lightweight input-only estimator trained with uncertainty scores. Extensive experiments on general and domain-specific benchmarks show that Logit Magnitude achieves strong performance, and partial generations of LLMs are often sufficient for effective uncertainty estimation. MetaUE further provides a competitive input-only approximation in several settings. These findings suggest that effective uncertainty estimation requires less generation than commonly assumed, enabling unreliable responses to be identified earlier.

</details>


### [256] [Towards Self-Explainable Document Visual Question Answering with Chain-of-Explanation Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06058)
*Kjetil Indrehus,Adrian Duric,Changkyu Choi,Ali Ramezani-Kebrya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces CoExVQA, a self-explainable DocVQA framework that grounds reasoning in identified evidence and localized answer regions, enabling transparent prediction by decoding only from grounded regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DocVQA models struggle to separate evidence localization from answer decoding, yielding black-box predictions that are hard to verify against visual evidence; there is a need for explainable, verifiable reasoning across text and image modalities.

Method: Chain-of-explanation: (1) identify question-relevant evidence, (2) explicitly localize the answer region, (3) decode the answer exclusively from the grounded region.

Result: On PFL-DocVQA, constraining decoding to grounded evidence yields state-of-the-art explainable DocVQA performance, with ANLS improvement of 12% over the best explainable baselines; predictions are more transparent and verifiable.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables direct inspection of multimodal reasoning, improves explainability without sacrificing performance, and provides verifiable predictions grounded in evidence.

Abstract: Document Visual Question Answering (DocVQA) requires vision-language models to reason not only about what information in a document is relevant to a question, but also where the answer is grounded on the page. Existing DocVQA models entangle question-relevant evidence and answer localization and operate largely as black boxes, offering limited means to verify how predictions depend on visual evidence. We propose CoExVQA, a self-explainable DocVQA framework with a grounded reasoning process through a chain-of-explanation design. CoExVQA first identifies question-relevant evidence, then explicitly localizes the answer region, and finally decodes the answer exclusively from the grounded region. Prediction via CoExVQA's chain-of-explanation enables direct inspection and verification of the reasoning process across modalities. Empirical results show that restricting decoding to grounded evidence achieves SotA explainable DocVQA performance on PFL-DocVQA, improving ANLS by 12% over the current explainable baselines while providing transparent and verifiable predictions.

</details>


### [257] [Causal Reinforcement Learning for Complex Card Games: A Magic The Gathering Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06066)
*Cristiano da Costa Cunha,Ajmal Mian,Tim French,Wei Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: New benchmark MTG-Causal-RL for causal RL in complex, partially observed domains with explicit causal structure; combines MTG game data, large masked action space, and a hand-specified SCM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of benchmarks capturing causal credit assignment, transfer across archetypes, and policy auditability in high-dimensional, partially observable RL with explicit causal structure.

Method: A Gymnasium-based MTG environment with 3,077-dim partial observations, 478-action masked discrete space, five archetypes, three reward schemes, and an SCM over strategic variables. Evaluation uses baseline agents (random, heuristic, masked PPO, causal-world-model PPO variant, scalar control) and introduces CGFA-PPO, which uses SCM parents of win probability as factor-aligned critic targets plus an intervention-calibration loss. Metrics include per-factor credit traces, leave-one-out transfer, and policy auditability via SCM-predicted interventions.

Result: Masked PPO and CGFA-PPO achieve competitive in-distribution win rates, outperforming random baselines. Per-factor calibration trajectories and leave-one-out transfer gaps reveal diagnostic structure not captured by scalar win rate alone. The authors release the benchmark, baselines, and evaluation protocol.

Conclusion: MTG-Causal-RL provides a shared testbed for causal-RL, world-model, and LLM-agent research to study causal credit assignment under masked actions, structural transfer across archetypes, and SCM-grounded policy auditability; enabling analyses beyond aggregate win rates.

Abstract: Causal reinforcement learning (RL) lacks benchmarks for complex systems that combine sequential decision making, hidden information, large masked action spaces, and explicit causal structure. We introduce MTG-Causal-RL, a Gymnasium benchmark built on Magic: The Gathering with a 3,077-dimensional partial observation, a 478-action masked discrete action space, five competitive Standard archetypes, three reward schemes, and a hand-specified Structural Causal Model (SCM) over strategic variables. Every episode exposes causal variables, SCM-predicted intervention effects, and per-factor credit traces, making causal credit assignment, leave-one-out cross-archetype transfer, and policy auditability first-class metrics. We adapt a panel of reference baselines: random, heuristic, masked PPO, a causal-world-model PPO variant, and an architecture-matched scalar control. We propose Causal Graph-Factored Advantage PPO (CGFA-PPO) as a reference causal agent that uses SCM parents of win probability as factor-aligned critic targets with an intervention-calibration loss. All comparisons use paired seeds, paired-bootstrap confidence intervals, and Holm-Bonferroni correction within pre-registered families. Masked PPO and CGFA-PPO reach competitive in-distribution win rates and exceed the random baseline; per-factor calibration trajectories and leave-one-out transfer gaps expose diagnostic structure that scalar win rate alone cannot. We release the benchmark, reference-baseline results, and full evaluation protocol openly. By coupling a strategically rich, partially observed domain with an explicit causal interface and statistical protocol, MTG-Causal-RL gives causal-RL, world-model, and LLM-agent research a shared testbed for questions current benchmarks cannot pose together: causal credit assignment under masked action spaces, structural transfer across archetypes, and SCM-grounded policy auditability.

</details>


### [258] [Normalized Architectures are Natively 4-Bit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06067)
*Maxim Fishman,Brian Chmiel,Ron Banner,Daniel Soudry,Boris Ginsburg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: nGPT with a unit hypersphere constraint on weights/hidden representations is inherently more robust to 4-bit quantization, enabling stable end-to-end NVFP4 training without extra pre/post-processing. Robustness arises from constructive signal accumulation in dot products, yielding higher SNR and a flatter loss landscape that strengthens with hidden dimension and scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: reduce compute and memory costs of training large language models by using very low-precision arithmetic while preserving model quality, addressing quantization noise and stability concerns.

Method: Constrain model parameters and activations to lie on the unit hypersphere (nGPT). Evaluate on a 1.2B dense model and on hybrid Mamba-Transformer MoE models up to 3B/30B params. Compare against standard architectures, analyze dot-product noise correlations, and show constructive signal accumulation. Provide a reference implementation.

Result: Demonstrates robust training at NVFP4 precision across dense and MoE models, with higher effective SNR, a flatter loss landscape, and increased advantages as hidden dimension grows. Robustness attributed to enhanced positive correlations in element-wise products under the hypersphere constraint, which aids signal accumulation while noise averages out.

Conclusion: Hypersphere-constrained nGPT enables stable, low-precision training at scale, removing the need for corrections like random transforms or per-tensor scaling and suggesting stronger advantages as model size grows; supports efficient procurement of low-precision LLMs.

Abstract: Training large language models at 4-bit precision is critical for efficiency. We show that nGPT, an architecture that constrains weights and hidden representations to the unit hypersphere, is inherently more robust to low-precision arithmetic. This removes the need for interventions-such as applying random Hadamard transforms and performing per-tensor scaling calculations-to preserve model quality, and it enables stable end-to-end NVFP4 training. We validate this approach on both a 1.2B dense model and hybrid (Mamba-Transformer) MoE models of up to 3B/30B parameters. We trace this robustness to the dot product: while quantization noise remains largely uncorrelated in both standard and normalized architectures, the signal behaves differently. In nGPT, the hypersphere constraint enhances weak positive correlations among the element-wise products, leading to a constructive accumulation of the signal across the hidden dimension while the noise continues to average out. This yields a higher effective signal-to-noise ratio and a flatter loss landscape, with the effect strengthening as the hidden dimension grows, suggesting increasing advantages at scale. A reference implementation is available at https://github.com/anonymous452026/ngpt-nvfp4

</details>


### [259] [Understanding diffusion models requires rethinking (again) generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06077)
*Pierre Marion,Yu-Han Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A position paper argues diffusion-model generalization needs new theory beyond classical learning and benign overfitting; memorization conflicts with novelty; reviews explanations and proposes studying pre-memorization learning; includes a CIFAR-10 study and open questions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve the lack of a coherent theoretical framework for diffusion-model generalization and why practical models generalize despite non-memorization evidence; propose a shift to pre-memorization dynamics.

Method: Theoretical argumentation complemented by an empirical CIFAR-10 study; distillation of findings into concrete open questions.

Result: Advocates a pivot from memorization-focused explanations to understanding pre-memorization learning dynamics; provides a set of open questions and an empirical study to motivate them.

Conclusion: New theoretical frameworks and empirical directions are required to understand what diffusion models actually learn before memorization to explain generalization.

Abstract: This position paper argues that understanding generalization in diffusion models requires fundamentally new theoretical frameworks that go beyond both classical statistical learning theory and the benign overfitting paradigm developed for supervised learning. In diffusion models, unlike in supervised learning, memorization of training data and generalization to novel samples are incompatible: a model that has fully memorized its training set generates copies rather than novel data. Several theoretical explanations for why practical diffusion models nevertheless generalize have been proposed, based on capacity limitations, implicit regularization from optimization, or architectural inductive biases, but their interactions remain unclear. We argue that the field should pivot from explaining why the diffusion models do not memorize to investigating what the model actually learns during pre-memorization phase. To highlight our stance, we conduct empirical study of diffusion models trained on CIFAR-10, and we distill the findings into concrete open questions that we believe are key to improve understanding of generalization in diffusion models.

</details>


### [260] [Fast Gauss-Newton for Multiclass Cross-Entropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06081)
*Mikalai Korbit,Mario Zanon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decomposition of full multiclass GGN into true-vs-rest plus within-competitor covariance; Fast Gauss-Newton (FGN) keeps the former, drops the latter to yield a PSD under-approximation exact for binary problems; efficient matrix-free solver via a whitened row-space representation and CG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As multiclass softmax cross-entropy couples all outputs in the curvature, full GGN becomes computationally expensive for many classes. A scalable, stable second-order approximation is sought.

Method: 1) Derive an exact true-vs-rest decomposition of the full multiclass GGN; 2) represent loss/gradient via a scalar-margin form; 3) define FGN by dropping the within-competitor covariance; 4) express damped update as a whitened row-space system with one row per mini-batch; 5) solve with conjugate gradient using JVP/VJP of the scalar-margin map.

Result: FGN remains closest to full GGN when competitor mass is concentrated or damping is large; deviation grows as the dropped within-competitor covariance increases. Targeted mechanism experiments and a fixed-feature multiclass head support the predicted behavior.

Conclusion: FGN provides a scalable, PSD under-approximation of multiclass GGN that is exact for binary tasks. The decomposition clarifies when the approximation is accurate and enables efficient, matrix-free optimization for large-class problems.

Abstract: In multiclass softmax cross-entropy, the full generalized Gauss-Newton (GGN) curvature couples all output logits through the softmax covariance, making curvature-vector products harder to scale as the number of classes grows. We show that the standard multiclass GGN can be decomposed exactly into a true-vs-rest term and a positive semidefinite within-competitor covariance term. Fast Gauss-Newton (FGN) retains the first term and drops the second, yielding a positive semidefinite under-approximation of the multiclass GGN that is exact for binary classification. The derivation uses an exact true-vs-rest scalar-margin representation of softmax cross-entropy: the loss and gradient are unchanged, and the approximation enters only at the curvature level. Exploiting the FGN curvature structure, the damped update can be written as an equivalent whitened row-space system with one row per mini-batch example. We solve this system matrix-free by conjugate gradient using Jacobian-vector and vector-Jacobian products of the scalar margin map. Targeted mechanism experiments and an evaluation on a fixed-feature multiclass head support the predictions from the decomposition: FGN stays closest to the full softmax GGN when competitor mass is concentrated or damping is large, and deviates as the dropped within-competitor covariance grows.

</details>


### [261] [Beyond Autoregressive RTG: Conditioning via Injection Outside Sequential Modeling in Decision Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06104)
*Yongyi Wang,Hanyu Liu,Lingfeng Li,Bozhou Chen,Ang Li,Qirui Zheng,Xionghui Yang,Chucai Wang,Wenxin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SlimDT removes the Return-to-Go (RTG) token from the autoregressive sequence in Decision Transformer, injecting RTG into state representations prior to modeling. This shortens the token sequence by one-third, reduces self-attention cost, and yields better or comparable performance on D4RL compared to standard DT and SOTA methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the inefficiency of RTG as a scalar token and the quadratic cost of self-attention by decoupling conditioning signals from the information-rich sequence, thereby improving both inference efficiency and task performance.

Method: Modify Decision Transformer by removing the RTG token from the sequence and injecting RTG information into the state representations before the transformer processes the (state, action) tokens, enabling a shorter (state, action) sequence.

Result: SlimDT outperforms standard Decision Transformer on the D4RL benchmark across tasks and achieves performance comparable to existing state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Decoupling a sparse conditioning signal (RTG) from an information-rich sequence yields computational gains and improved task performance.

Abstract: Decision Transformer (DT) formulates offline reinforcement learning as autoregressive sequence modeling, achieving promising results by predicting actions from a sequence of Return-to-Go (RTG), state, and action tokens. However, RTG is a scalar that summarizes future rewards, containing far less information than typical state or action vectors, yet it consumes the same computational budget per token. Worse, the self-attention cost of Transformers grows quadratically with sequence length, so including RTG as a separate token adds unnecessary overhead. We propose SlimDT, which removes RTG from the autoregressive sequence. Instead, we inject RTG information into the state representations before the sequential modeling step, allowing the Transformer to process only a compact (state, action) sequence. This reduces the sequence length by one-third, directly improving inference efficiency. On the D4RL benchmark, SlimDT surpasses standard DT across various tasks and achieves performance comparable to existing state-of-the-art methods. Decoupling a sparse conditioning signal from an information-rich sequence thus yields both computational gains and higher task performance.

</details>


### [262] [BoostLLM: Boosting-inspired LLM Fine-tuning for Few-shot Tabular Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06117)
*Yi-Siang Wang,Kuan-Yu Chen,Yu-Chen Den,Darby Tien-Hao Chang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BoostLLM reframes PEFT fine-tuning as sequential residual boosting, using a dual-view input (raw features + decision-tree paths) to inject tabular inductive bias and improve data-efficient tabular prediction with LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limited performance of LLMs on tabular data in low-data regimes by leveraging boosting as a general fine-tuning principle and by injecting structured tabular bias via tree-path views.

Method: Train sequential parameter-efficient fine-tuning adapters as weak learners in a multi-round residual optimization loop (boosting). Augment inputs with a second view—the decision-tree paths—so the model can learn from structured, path-based features early on, acting as a structured teacher before feature-driven representations dominate.

Result: BoostLLM yields consistent improvements over standard fine-tuning across multiple LLM backbones and datasets, matching or surpassing XGBoost across various small-shot settings, and outperforming GPT-4o-based methods with a 4B model. Scaling experiments show further gains when pairing with stronger tree models and extending the boosting horizon, with stabilization needed for long horizons.

Conclusion: Boosting can serve as a general training principle for LLM fine-tuning, especially for structured data in low-data regimes, by combining sequential PEFT adapters with tabular-induced inductive bias via tree-path views.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently been adapted to tabular prediction by serializing structured features into natural language, but their performance in low-data regimes remains limited compared to gradient-boosted decision trees (GBDTs). In this work, we revisit the boosting paradigm, traditionally associated with tree ensembles, and ask whether it can be applied as a general training principle for LLM fine-tuning. We propose BoostLLM, a framework that transforms parameter-efficient fine-tuning into a multi-round residual optimization process by training sequential PEFT adapters as weak learners. To incorporate tabular inductive bias, BoostLLM integrates decision-tree paths as a second input view alongside raw features; analysis reveals that the path view acts as a structured teacher in early training steps before the model shifts toward feature-driven representations. Empirically, BoostLLM achieves consistent improvements over standard fine-tuning across multiple LLM backbones and datasets, matching or surpassing XGBoost across a wide range of shot counts and outperforming GPT-4o-based methods with a 4B model. We further show that the framework scales: pairing with stronger tree models and extended boosting horizons yields additional gains under appropriate stabilization. These results suggest that boosting can serve as a general training principle for LLM fine-tuning, particularly in low-data regimes for structured data.

</details>


### [263] [Listwise Policy Optimization: Group-based RLVR as Target-Projection on the LLM Response Simplex](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06139)
*Yun Qu,Qi Wang,Yixiu Mao,Heming Zou,Yuhang Jiang,Yingyue Li,Wutong Xu,Lizhou Cai,Weijie Liu,Clive Bai,Kai Yang,Yangkun Chen,Saiyong Yang,Xiangyang Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Listwise Policy Optimization (LPO) explicitly performs target-projection on the response simplex for RL with verifiable rewards, yielding monotonic improvement, stability, and diverse outputs, outperforming group-based policy gradient baselines in LLM reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR uses group-based policy gradients that implicitly target a distribution on the response simplex; this implicit projection motivates an explicit, principled approach to target projection to improve stability and performance.

Method: Restrict the proximal RL objective to the response simplex and perform exact divergence minimization to project the policy onto the target distribution; decouple the projection step to allow flexible divergences; derive monotonic improvement guarantees and bounded, self-correcting projection gradients.

Result: Empirically, LPO improves training performance over standard policy gradient baselines under matched targets across diverse reasoning tasks and LLM backbones, while maintaining optimization stability and response diversity.

Conclusion: LPO provides a unified, interpretable framework for target-projection in RLVR that combines explicit projection with flexible divergence choices, yielding stable training and better performance in LLM reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a standard approach for large language models (LLMs) post-training to incentivize reasoning capacity. Among existing recipes, group-based policy gradient is prevalent, which samples a group of responses per prompt and updates the policy via group-relative advantage signals. This work reveals that these optimization strategies share a common geometric structure: each implicitly defines a target distribution on the response simplex and projects toward it via first-order approximation. Building on this insight, we propose Listwise Policy Optimization (LPO) to explicitly conduct the target-projection, which demystifies the implicit target by restricting the proximal RL objective to the response simplex, and then projects the policy via exact divergence minimization. This framework provides (i) monotonic improvement on the listwise objective with bounded, zero-sum, and self-correcting projection gradients, and (ii) flexibility in divergence selection with distinct structural properties through the decoupled projection step. On diverse reasoning tasks and LLM backbones, LPO consistently improves training performance over typical policy gradient baselines under matched targets, while intrinsically preserving optimization stability and response diversity.

</details>


### [264] [SymDrift: One-Shot Generative Modeling under Symmetries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06140)
*Samir Darouich,Vinh Tong,Lluís Pastor-Pérez,Tanja Bien,Loay Mualem,Mathias Niepert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SymDrift introduces symmetry-aware drifting models for 3D physical systems to enable single-shot generation by making the drift field invariant to global rotations, through symmetrized coordinate-space drift and a G-invariant embedding, achieving strong performance with large speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning distributions invariant under global symmetries (rotations) is crucial for 3D physical systems like molecules. Equivariant diffusion/flow can handle invariances but rely on costly multi-step sampling; drifting models enable single-shot generation but face a symmetry-specific challenge since an equivariant generator’s drift may not align with the symmetrized target; symmetrization of empirical data is expensive.

Method: Two complementary strategies: (i) a symmetrized drift in coordinate space based on optimal alignment; (ii) a G-invariant embedding that removes symmetry ambiguity by construction; results in a symmetry-aware drift that supports one-shot inference.

Result: Empirically, SymDrift outperforms existing one-shot methods on standard benchmarks for conformer and transition state generation, and remains competitive with significantly more expensive multi-step approaches.

Conclusion: SymDrift enables one-shot inference with up to ~40x computational overhead reductions compared to baselines, making it promising for high-throughput applications like virtual drug screening and large-scale reaction network exploration; highlight that symmetry-aware drift fields are effective for molecular generative modeling.

Abstract: Generative modeling of physical systems, such as molecules, requires learning distributions that are invariant under global symmetries, such as rotations in three-dimensional space. Equivariant diffusion and flow matching models can incorporate such invariances effectively, even when trained on a non-invariant empirical distribution, but they typically rely on costly multi-step sampling. Recently, drifting models have emerged as an efficient alternative, enabling single-step generation and achieving state-of-the-art performance in generative modeling tasks. However, we show that drifting models face a symmetry-specific challenge, since an equivariant generator does not generally produce the same drifting field as the one obtained from the symmetrized target distribution. Addressing this issue would require expensive symmetrization of the empirical distribution. To avoid this cost, we propose SymDrift, a framework that makes the drifting field itself symmetry-aware. We introduce two complementary strategies: (i) a symmetrized drift in coordinate space based on optimal alignment, and (ii) a $G$-invariant embedding that removes symmetry ambiguity by construction. Empirically, SymDrift outperforms existing one-shot methods on standard benchmarks for conformer and transition state generation, while remaining competitive with significantly more expensive multi-step approaches. By enabling one-shot inference, SymDrift reduces computational overhead by up to 40$\times$ compared to existing baselines, making it promising for high-throughput applications such as virtual drug screening and large-scale reaction network exploration.

</details>


### [265] [Matrix-Valued Optimism is Matrix-Valued Augmentation: Additive Hybrid Designs for Constrained Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06141)
*Jiayi Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An additive matrix-correction framework unifies augmented Lagrangian and optimistic primal-dual methods for equality-constrained optimization, extending their equivalence from scalars to matrices; proposes a closed-form hybrid rule that optimally splits a symmetric matrix correction between channels, with step sizes tuned by local spectral weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the non-uniqueness and design freedom in matrix corrections for primal-dual methods, understand how correction allocation affects curvature and memory, and improve finite-step feasibility under ill-conditioned Jacobians.

Method: Prove an additivity principle for symmetric matrix corrections: ideal primal trajectory depends only on the sum of corrections; analyze how split between augmented and optimistic channels affects primal curvature and dual memory, formulate a step-size-limited design problem, derive a closed-form hybrid rule to choose correction, its split, and step sizes via local spectral weights; validate with experiments on nonlinear equality-constrained problems with controlled Jacobian conditioning.

Result: Hybrid rule improves over pure endpoints, tracks a grid-search oracle, competitive with first-order baselines under mild-to-moderate ill-conditioning; reveals limitation: exact cancellation needs increasingly large corrections as Jacobian ill-conditioning grows.

Conclusion: There is a design freedom in matrix corrections that preserves the ideal trajectory; the equivalence extends to matrix corrections; an explicit, practically implementable hybrid rule bridges augmented and optimistic channels, subject to conditioning.

Abstract: Augmented Lagrangian and optimistic primal--dual methods stabilize equality-constrained optimization through seemingly different mechanisms: the former adds constraint-dependent primal curvature, while the latter adds dual memory. Recent work has shown that these mechanisms are equivalent for scalar parameters. We extend this equivalence to matrix-valued correction. We prove an additivity principle: for symmetric matrix parameters, the ideal primal trajectory depends only on the summed correction matrix, not on how it is split between augmented and optimistic channels. This exposes a design freedom: algebraically equivalent decompositions can have different finite-step feasibility because augmented correction affects primal curvature, whereas optimistic correction affects the scale of the dual memory correction. We formulate the resulting step-size-limited design problem and derive a closed-form hybrid rule that selects a matrix correction, splits it between the two channels, and chooses primal and dual steps using local spectral weights. Experiments on nonlinear equality-constrained problems with controlled constraint-Jacobian conditioning show that the hybrid design improves over pure augmented and pure optimistic endpoints, closely tracks a grid-search hybrid oracle, and is competitive with first-order primal--dual baselines under mild-to-moderate ill-conditioning. The experiments also identify the expected limitation: exact cancellation requires increasingly large matrix corrections as the constraint Jacobian becomes ill-conditioned.

</details>


### [266] [Unifying Goal-Conditioned RL and Unsupervised Skill Learning via Control-Maximization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06145)
*Alireza Modirshanechi,Benjamin Eysenbach,Peter Dayan,Eric Schulz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MISL and GCRL can be viewed as control-maximization; despite inequivalence among canonical GCRL formulations, MISL objectives bound and align with downstream goal-sensitivity, enabling matched pretraining for specific downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify MISL and GCRL to explain why unsupervised pretraining helps goal-conditioned RL and provide a theoretical basis for selecting pretraining objectives.

Method: Theoretical analysis: prove inequivalence of three canonical GCRL formulations; interpret good GCRL policy as high sensitivity of future trajectories to the goal; show MISL objectives measure skill-sensitivity akin to goal-sensitivity; derive bounds linking MISL objectives with downstream goal-sensitivity; establish a correspondence and matching MISL for each GCRL formulation.

Result: Foundational equivalences/bounds: inequivalence of canonical GCRL formulations; MISL objectives bounded by downstream goal-sensitivities; for each GCRL form there exists a MISL objective whose diversity enhances downstream sensitivity.

Conclusion: Provides a theoretical basis for RL pretraining and practical guidance on choosing pretraining objectives to match specific downstream task classes.

Abstract: Unsupervised pretraining has driven empirical advances in goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL), but its theoretical foundations remain poorly understood. In particular, an influential class of methods, mutual information skill learning (MISL), discovers behaviorally diverse skills that can later be used for downstream goal-reaching. However, it remains a theoretical mystery why skills learned through MISL should support goal-reaching. A subtle challenge is that both GCRL and MISL are umbrella terms: different GCRL tasks use distinct criteria for measuring goal-reaching performance, while different MISL methods optimize distinct notions of behavioral diversity. We address this challenge and unify GCRL and MISL as instances of control maximization. We identify three canonical GCRL formulations and prove that they are fundamentally inequivalent: they can induce incompatible optimal policies even in the same environment. Nevertheless, they all share a common interpretation: a well-performing goal-conditioned policy is one whose future trajectory is highly sensitive to the commanded goal, with the precise notion of sensitivity determined by the GCRL formulation. Noting that MISL objectives can be understood as measures of skill-sensitivity akin to goal-sensitivity, we show that MISL objectives are bounded by formulation-specific downstream goal-sensitivities. These bounds establish a precise correspondence between MISL methods and downstream GCRL tasks: for every GCRL formulation, there exists a matching MISL objective for which more diverse skills afford greater downstream goal sensitivity. Our results thus lay a theoretical foundation for RL pretraining and have important practical implications, such as suggesting which pretraining objectives to use when a user cares about a specific class of downstream tasks.

</details>


### [267] [AdaGamma: State-Dependent Discounting for Temporal Adaptation in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06149)
*Yaomin Wang,Jianting Pan,Ran Tian,Xiaoyang Li,Yu Zhang,Hengle Qin,Tianshu YU*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdaGamma enables state-dependent discounting in deep actor-critic methods by coupling a discount function with a return-consistency regularizer, improving SAC/PPO performance and showing online gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed discount factors constrain planning horizons and bootstrapping; naive state-dependent discounting can destabilize learning (TD-error collapse); a regularizer is needed to preserve reliable backup structures.

Method: Introduce AdaGamma, a practical algorithm learning a state-dependent discount together with a return-consistency objective; integrate with SAC and PPO; provide theoretical analysis of the induced Bellman operator.

Result: Theoretical analysis establishes well-posedness properties of the Bellman operator under state-dependent discounting; empirical results show consistent improvements on continuous-control benchmarks and statistically significant gains in an online A/B test on JD Logistics.

Conclusion: State-dependent discounting can be effective in deep RL when paired with a return-consistency objective that prevents degenerate target manipulation and stabilizes learning.

Abstract: The discount factor in reinforcement learning controls both the effective planning horizon and the strength of bootstrapping, yet most deep RL methods use a single fixed value across all states. While state-dependent discounting is conceptually appealing, naive deep actor--critic implementations can become unstable and degenerate toward TD-error collapse. We propose AdaGamma, a practical deep actor--critic method for state-dependent discounting that learns a state-dependent discount function together with a return-consistency objective to regularize the induced backup structure. On the theory side, we analyze the Bellman operator induced by state-dependent discounting and establish its basic well-posedness properties under suitable conditions. Empirically, AdaGamma integrates into both SAC and PPO, yielding consistent improvements on continuous-control benchmarks, and achieves statistically significant gains in an online A/B test on the JD Logistics platform. These results suggest that state-dependent discounting can be made effective in deep RL when coupled with a return-consistency objective that prevents degenerate target manipulation.

</details>


### [268] [Grokking or Glitching? How Low-Precision Drives Slingshot Loss Spikes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06152)
*Liu Hanqing,Jianjun Cao,Yuanze Li,Zijian Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Slingshot is explained by finite-precision rounding: a Numerical Feature Inflation (NFI) mechanism where the correct-class gradient rounds to zero, breaking gradient zero-sum and driving exponential growth of classifier and feature means, accounting for loss spikes and norm growth in late-stage training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain unexplained long-term training loss spikes and norm growth; provide a reproducible, testable finite-precision mechanism applicable to real-world deep nets.

Method: Theoretical/ mathematical analysis of backpropagation under finite-precision arithmetic; show that when logits become highly separated, the correct-class gradient can be absorbed (rounded to zero) while other gradients remain nonzero, breaking the zero-sum constraint; derive a positive feedback loop that inflates feature and classifier means (Numerical Feature Inflation, NFI); argue that NFI can occur with partial absorption and still drive parameter growth; connect to Slingshot dynamics.

Result: NFI provides a causal explanation for Slingshot: rapid norm growth preceding spikes, reappearance of gradients, and eventual loss spike; also predicts logit divergence and abnormal parameter growth even when spikes are not visually evident.

Conclusion: Slingshot can be interpreted as a finite-precision numerical dynamic; NFI offers a testable mechanism for abnormal late-stage training behavior, with implications for precision choices and potential mitigation strategies.

Abstract: Deep neural networks exhibit periodic loss spikes during unregularized long-term training, a phenomenon known as the "Slingshot Mechanism." Existing work usually attributes this to intrinsic optimization dynamics, but its triggering mechanism remains unclear. This paper proves that this phenomenon is a result of floating-point arithmetic precision limits. As training enters a high-confidence stage, the difference between the correct-class logit and the other logits may exceed the absorption-error threshold. Then during backpropagation, the gradient of the correct class is rounded exactly to zero, while the gradients of the incorrect classes remain nonzero. This breaks the zero-sum constraint of gradients across classes and introduces a systematic drift in the parameter update of the classifier layer. We prove that this drift forms a positive feedback loop with the feature, causing the global classifier mean and the global feature mean to grow exponentially. We call this mechanism Numerical Feature Inflation (NFI). This mechanism explains the rapid norm growth before a Slingshot spike, the subsequent reappearance of gradients, and the resulting loss spike. We further show that NFI is not equivalent to an observed loss spike: in more practical tasks, partial absorption may not produce visible spikes, but it can still break the zero-sum constraint and drive rapid growth of parameter norms. Our results reinterpret Slingshot as a numerical dynamic of finite-precision training, and provide a testable explanation for abnormal parameter growth and logit divergence in late-stage training.

</details>


### [269] [Entropy-Regularized Adjoint Matching for Offline RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06156)
*Abdelghani Ghanem,Mounir Ghogho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ME-AM integrates maximum entropy with adjoint matching in offline reinforcement learning using flow-based policies to overcome popularity bias and expand support via a mixture behavior prior. It introduces two mechanisms: Mirror Descent entropy maximization and a Mixture Behavior Prior, maintaining absolute continuity of the generative vector field. Empirically, it achieves competitive or superior results on sparse-reward continuous control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the fixed-behavior limitation, popularity bias, and restricted off-manifold exploration in Q-learning with Adjoint Matching (QAM) when using expressive flow-based policies in offline RL.

Method: Propose Maximum Entropy Adjoint Matching (ME-AM) within continuous flow formulation, incorporating (1) Mirror Descent entropy maximization to reduce popularity bias and improve data-utilization, (2) a Mixture Behavior Prior to widen geometric support to include out-of-distribution high-reward regions, while preserving absolute continuity of the vector field.

Result: Empirically demonstrates competitive or superior performance compared to prior SOTA methods across diverse sparse-reward continuous control environments.

Conclusion: ME-AM jointly addresses the expressivity-accuracy trade-offs in offline RL with flow-based policies by expanding geometry and regularizing policy entropy, achieving robust off-policy performance without sacrificing the continuity of the flow dynamics.

Abstract: Integrating expressive generative policies, such as flow-matching models, into offline reinforcement learning (RL) allows agents to capture complex, multi-modal behaviors. While Q-learning with Adjoint Matching (QAM) stabilizes policy optimization via the continuous adjoint method, it remains inherently bound to the fixed behavior distribution. This dependence induces a \textit{popularity bias} that can suppress high-reward actions in low-density regions, and creates a \textit{support binding} that restricts off-manifold exploration. Existing workarounds, such as appending \textit{residual} Gaussian policies, often re-introduce the expressivity bottlenecks associated with unimodal distributions. In this work, we propose \textit{Maximum Entropy Adjoint Matching} (ME-AM), a unified framework that addresses these limitations within the continuous flow formulation. ME-AM incorporates two mechanisms: (1) a Mirror Descent entropy maximization objective that mitigates the popularity bias to facilitate the extraction of optimal policies from offline datasets, and (2) a \textit{Mixture Behavior Prior} that mathematically broadens the geometric support to encompass out-of-distribution high-reward regions. By exploring this extended geometry, ME-AM identifies robust actions while preserving the absolute continuity of the generative vector field. Empirically, ME-AM demonstrates competitive or superior performance compared to prior state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods across a diverse suite of sparse-reward continuous control environments.

</details>


### [270] [One Algorithm, Two Goals: Dual Scoring for Parameter and Data Selection in LLM Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06166)
*Xinrui Chen,Liu Yang,Ou Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes DualSFT, a unified framework for jointly selecting parameters (via masking) and data subsets for fine-tuning LLMs, using a shared gradient interaction matrix to derive a one-shot dual-scoring rule that improves joint trade-offs and efficiency versus baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parameter masking and data selection are often treated separately in fine-tuning, causing redundant overhead and suboptimal coordination. A unified approach aims to reduce cost while maintaining or improving performance by jointly optimizing both signals.

Method: Formulates two bilevel selection problems under a common validation objective. Derives a local response-surrogate scoring rule from first- and second-order validation-improvement approximations, showing parameter importance (columns) and data utility (rows) as aggregations of a gradient interaction matrix. Proposes DualSFT to compute a parameter mask and data subset from shared gradient statistics in one shot; includes single-axis variants and a full dual-scoring approach.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 3B–9B LLMs shows that single-axis DualSFT variants improve target-task performance and stability-plasticity trade-offs within their comparison groups. The full DualSFT achieves a more favorable joint-constrained trade-off than sequential hybrid baselines under matched budgets.

Conclusion: A unified, gradient-based scoring framework enables effective joint parameter and data selection for fine-tuning. The one-shot DualSFT approach reduces overhead and yields improved joint performance under budget constraints, with strong empirical results and a clear theoretical link between parameter importance and data utility via the gradient interaction matrix.

Abstract: In Large Language Model (LLM) fine-tuning, parameter and data selection are common strategies for reducing fine-tuning cost, yet they are typically driven by separate scoring mechanisms. When a parameter mask and data subset jointly determine restricted fine-tuning, this separation incurs redundant overhead and makes coordinated selection difficult. We cast parameter and data selection as two bilevel selection problems under a common validation objective and derive a shared local response-surrogate scoring rule. Under first- and second-order validation-improvement approximations, parameter importance and data utility emerge as column-wise and row-wise aggregations of a single gradient interaction matrix, yielding a closed-form row-column correspondence for co-extracting both signals. Building on this structure, we propose DualSFT (Dual-Selection Fine-Tuning), a one-shot dual-scoring algorithm that produces a parameter mask and data subset from shared gradient statistics. On 3B-9B LLMs, single-axis DualSFT variants strengthen target-task performance and stability-plasticity trade-offs within their comparison groups, while full DualSFT yields a more favorable joint-constrained trade-off than sequential hybrid baselines under matched budgets.

</details>


### [271] [Mean Mode Screaming: Mean--Variance Split Residuals for 1000-Layer Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06169)
*Pengqi Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identifies MMS-driven collapse in ultra-deep Diffusion Transformers, analyzes gradient decomposition and Softmax Jacobian null-space effects, and proposes MV-Split residuals to stabilize training up to 1000 layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling diffusion transformers to hundreds of layers induces a silent mean-dominated collapse that homogenizes token representations. Understanding the mechanism and mitigating it is essential for stable ultra-deep models.

Method: Mechanistic auditing of gradient dynamics revealing a decomposition into mean-coherent and centered components, and analysis of attention-logit gradient suppression via the Softmax Jacobian null-space. Proposes MV-Split Residuals that pair a centered residual update with a leaky trunk-mean replacement. Empirical validation on a 400-layer single-stream DiT and a 1000-layer scale-validation run.

Result: MV-Split prevents the divergent collapse observed in the un-stabilized baseline at 400 layers, maintaining behavior close to the pre-crash trajectory and outperforming token‑isotropic gating methods such as LayerScale. The architecture remains stably trainable at 1000 layers.

Conclusion: Mean-Variance Split residuals provide a robust stabilization mechanism for ultra-deep diffusion transformers, enabling stable training at extreme depths and highlighting the importance of decomposing gradient dynamics and leveraging residual design to mitigate mean-dominated collapse.

Abstract: Scaling Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) to hundreds of layers introduces a structural vulnerability: networks can enter a silent, mean-dominated collapse state that homogenizes token representations and suppresses centered variation. Through mechanistic auditing, we isolate the trigger event of this collapse as Mean Mode Screaming (MMS). MMS can occur even when training appears stable, with a mean-coherent backward shock on residual writers that opens deep residual branches and drives the network into a mean-dominated state. We show this behavior is driven by an exact decomposition of these gradients into mean-coherent and centered components, compounded by the structural suppression of attention-logit gradients through the null space of the Softmax Jacobian once values homogenize.
  To address this, we propose Mean-Variance Split (MV-Split) Residuals, which combine a separately gained centered residual update with a leaky trunk-mean replacement. On a 400-layer single-stream DiT, MV-Split prevents the divergent collapse that crashes the un-stabilized baseline; it tracks close to the baseline's pre-crash trajectory while remaining substantially better than token-isotropic gating methods such as LayerScale across the full schedule. Finally, we present a 1000-layer DiT as a scale-validation run at boundary scales, establishing that the architecture remains stably trainable at extreme depth.

</details>


### [272] [In-Context Black-Box Optimization with Unreliable Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06187)
*Nicolas Samuel Blumer,Julien Martinelli,Samuel Kaski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A feedback-informed in-context black-box optimizer (FICBO) that uses a structured feedback prior and a feedback-aware transformer to combine history with cheap auxiliary feedback from multiple sources, improving robustness and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Black-box optimization often has auxiliary signals (experts, simulators, predictors) that can speed search but may be biased or noisy. Existing feedback-aware BO methods typically handle one task or rely only on past history; there is a need for cross-source generalization and robust use of multiple feedback signals.

Method: Introduce a structured feedback prior that encodes access, relevance, and distortion of different feedback sources; pretrain a feedback-aware transformer conditioned on observed history and available auxiliary feedback. At test time, estimate source reliability in-context by comparing observed objective values with auxiliary signals, then reweight sources to guide query selection.

Result: Empirical results on synthetic and real-world tasks show that FICBO effectively exploits informative feedback while remaining robust to weak or misleading sources, achieving improvements over baselines.

Conclusion: Demonstrates cross-task adaptation with multi-source feedback, provides interpretability by revealing how test-time signals are weighed, and offers a general framework for robust feedback-aware in-context optimization.

Abstract: Black-box optimization in science and engineering often comes with side information: experts, simulators, pretrained predictors, or heuristics can suggest which candidates look promising. This information can accelerate search, but it can also be biased, input-dependent, or misleading. Feedback-aware BO methods typically handle one task at a time, limiting their ability to generalize over multiple sources of feedback. In-context optimizers address cross-task adaptation, but usually assume that optimization history is the only available signal at test time. We study feedback-informed in-context black-box optimization (FICBO), where a pretrained optimizer conditions on both the observed history and cheap auxiliary feedback for the current candidate set. We introduce a structured feedback prior that models how feedback sources vary in their access, relevance, and distortion relative to the true objective, and use it to pretrain a feedback-aware transformer. At test time, the model estimates source reliability in context by comparing observed objective values with auxiliary signals, improving query selection. On synthetic and real-world tasks, FICBO effectively exploits informative feedback while remaining robust to weak or misleading sources, improving over other baselines. Empirical investigations further illustrate how the model perceives test-time sources, offering insights into its interpretability and decision-making process.

</details>


### [273] [Constrained Contextual Bandits with Adversarial Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06190)
*Dhruv Sarkar,Abhishek Sinha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A modular reduction framework for budget-constrained contextual bandits with adversarial contexts, leveraging online regression oracles to map the constrained problem to an unconstrained contextual bandit using surrogate rewards, yielding joint regret and budget-violation guarantees, and extending SquareCB.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Budgeted decision-making under adversarial contexts is challenging and existing stochastic-context methods may underperform; the continuing setting requires sustained performance even after budget depletion. A reduction-based approach promises general guarantees and practical algorithms.

Method: Extend the SquareCB framework by incorporating online regression oracles to construct adaptive surrogate reward functions, thereby reducing the constrained problem to a standard unconstrained contextual bandit problem. The approach is modular and relies on a simple offline/online regression interface, enabling a compact analysis.

Result: The proposed reduction yields improved guarantees for adversarial contexts, providing simultaneous control of regret and budget violation in the continuing setting, with an efficient algorithm and transparent analysis.

Conclusion: A simple, modular reduction of budget-constrained contextual bandits to unconstrained bandits extends SquareCB to adversarial contexts, delivering robust joint guarantees and practical, transparent methodology.

Abstract: We study budget-constrained contextual bandits with adversarial contexts, where each action yields a random reward and incurs a random cost. We adopt the standard realizability assumption: conditioned on the observed context, rewards and costs are drawn independently from fixed distributions whose expectations belong to known function classes. We focus on the continuing setting, in which the algorithm operates over the entire horizon even after the budget for cumulative cost is exhausted. In this setting, the objective is to simultaneously control regret and the violation of the budget constraint. Building on the seminal $\mathsf{SquareCB}$ framework of Foster et al. [2018], we propose a simple and modular framework that leverages online regression oracles to reduce the constrained problem to a standard unconstrained contextual bandit problem with adaptively defined surrogate reward functions. In contrast to prior works, which focus on stochastic contexts, our reduction yields improved guarantees for more general adversarial contexts, together with an efficient algorithm with a compact and transparent analysis.

</details>


### [274] [Playing the network backward: A Game Theoretic Attribution Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06212)
*Jakob Paul Zimmermann,Jim Berend,Georg Loho,Sebastian Lapuschkin,Wojciech Samek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Recasts backward attribution as a two-player game on an extended network graph; gradients and LRP arise as integrals over game trajectories; attribution maps are trajectory projections; game-theoretic concepts specify explanation properties; an alpha-beta-LRP adaptation improves localisation on ViT-B/16 compared to prior transformer-specific methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify diverse backward attribution methods (gradients, LRP, transformer-specific rules) under a single framework to enable principled comparisons and adaptable explanations.

Method: Extend the ReLU Net Game to backward attribution, formulating it as a two-player game on an expanded network graph. Gradients and the full alpha-beta-LRP family correspond to integrals over game trajectories under specific equilibria. Attribution maps become projections of trajectory distributions. Incorporate game-theoretic concepts (policy regularization, risk aversion, extended action sets) to encode desired properties and yield novel backward-rule adaptations.

Result: Empirically, a selected alpha-beta-LRP adaptation outperforms prior transformer-specific backward methods across localisation metrics on ViT-B/16.

Conclusion: The framework provides a flexible, principled way to specify and realize explanation properties via game-theoretic constructs, translating into effective backward attribution rules and improved localisation in transformers.

Abstract: Attribution methods explain which input features drive a model's prediction, making them central to model debugging and mechanistic interpretability. Yet backward attribution methods, including gradients, LRP, and transformer-specific rules, lack a shared framework in which to compare the underlying backward calculations. We introduce such a framework by recasting backward attribution as a two-player game on an extended network graph, building on Gaubert and Vlassopoulos' ReLU Net Game. Gradients and the full alpha-beta-LRP family arise as integrals over game trajectories under specific equilibria, so attribution maps become projections of trajectory distributions rather than the primary object. Desired explanation properties, such as localisation focus, robustness to input noise, or stable attention routing, can be specified as game-theoretic concepts, including policy regularization, risk aversion, and extended action sets, and translate directly into novel adaptations of the well-known backward rules. On ViT-B/16, one such selected adaptation of alpha-beta-LRP outperforms prior transformer-specific backward methods across all considered localisation metrics.

</details>


### [275] [Bandit Learning in General Open Multi-agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06202)
*Mengfan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified open-system bandit framework with general dynamics, introducing pre-training degree, stability, and global dynamic regret, and providing certified global-UCB algorithms with tight bounds and matching lower bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open systems with arrivals/departures cause endogenous non-stationarity and information dynamics that existing bandit models cannot capture; a general framework is needed to understand regret and design algorithms.

Method: Formalize an open-system bandit model with heterogeneous rewards and agent patterns; define pre-training degree, stability, and global dynamic regret; develop certified global-UCB learning algorithms; derive upper bounds and prove lower bounds to show tightness.

Result: Regret bounds separate the impact of entry uncertainty (linear in pre-training degree) from the time to identify a persistent optimal arm and agent-pattern effects; in stable regimes, these factors govern regret; lower bounds validate tightness of bounds; algorithms come with provable guarantees.

Conclusion: The framework successfully characterizes open-system dynamics and provides algorithmic solutions with provable guarantees, illustrating how entry, stability, and agent patterns shape regret and informing design of robust open-system learning policies.

Abstract: Recent developments in digital platforms have highlighted the prevalence of open systems, where agents can arrive and depart over time. While bandit learning in open systems has recently received initial attention, existing work imposes structural assumptions that are frequently violated in practice. A learning paradigm for general open systems creates fresh challenges: newly arriving agents induce endogenous non-stationarity; agent patterns determine how quickly information accumulates; and new agents make regret scale further with the time horizon. To this end, we formulate a unified open-system bandit problem with general dynamics, including heterogeneous rewards and general agent patterns. We introduce new concepts to capture the inherent complexities: the \emph{pre-training degree} of new agents quantifies how much information an agent carries upon entry, \emph{stability} measures the impact of new agents on the system, and \emph{global dynamic regret} compares the cumulative expected reward of all active agents with that of the varying optimal arms. We develop certified global-UCB learning methodologies with provable guarantees. Our regret bounds reveal that entry uncertainty enters linearly via the pre-training degree, while in stable regimes, regret is governed by the time needed to identify a persistent optimal arm, as well as by the agent patterns. We further show that these dependencies are tight via lower bounds in hard instances.

</details>


### [276] [When Labels Have Structure: Improving Image Classification with Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06274)
*April Chan,Davide D'Ascenzo,Sebastiano Cultrera di Montesano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy (HACE) adds hierarchy-aware components to cross-entropy, improving classification by propagating probabilities up the class tree and smoothing labels along root paths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard cross-entropy ignores semantic distances encoded by class hierarchies, potentially wasting information and misaligning predictions with hierarchical structure.

Method: Introduce two components: (1) prediction aggregation that upwardly propagates probability mass in the hierarchy to accumulate parent confidence from child predictions; (2) ancestral label smoothing that distributes the ground-truth signal along the path from the true class to the root. HACE is a drop-in replacement for cross-entropy and evaluated in end-to-end training and linear probing across CIFAR-100, FGVC Aircraft, NABirds, six architectures, and DINOv2-Large features.

Result: End-to-end: 15/18 architecture-dataset pairs show accuracy gains, mean +4.66%. Linear probing with frozen features: HACE outperforms all competing methods on all three datasets, mean +2.18% over the next best baseline.

Conclusion: HACE effectively leverages class hierarchy to improve performance across architectures and settings, demonstrating the utility of hierarchy-aware loss for both supervised and self-supervised feature representations.

Abstract: Standard cross-entropy is the default classification loss across virtually all of machine learning, yet it treats all misclassifications equally, ignoring the semantic distances that a class hierarchy encodes. We propose Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy (HACE), a drop-in replacement for standard cross-entropy that incorporates a known class hierarchy directly into the loss. HACE combines two components: prediction aggregation, which propagates the model's probability mass upward through the class hierarchy to ensure that parent nodes accumulate the confidence of their children; and ancestral label smoothing, which distributes the ground-truth signal along the path from the true class to the root. We evaluate HACE on CIFAR-100, FGVC Aircraft, and NABirds in two regimes: end-to-end training across six architectures spanning convolutional and attention-based designs, and linear probing on frozen DINOv2-Large features. In end-to-end training, HACE improves accuracy over standard cross-entropy in 15 out of 18 architecture--dataset pairs, with a mean gain of 4.66\%. In linear probing on frozen DINOv2-Large features, HACE outperforms all competing methods on all three datasets, with a mean improvement of 2.18\% over the next best baseline.

</details>


### [277] [Memory Efficient Full-gradient Attacks (MEFA) Framework for Adversarial Defense Evaluations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06357)
*Yuan Du,Mitchel Hill,HanQin Cai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memory-efficient full-gradient evaluation for stochastic purification defenses using gradient checkpointing enables exact white-box attacks, revealing vulnerabilities missed by approximate gradients and strengthening robustness benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Approximate backpropagation due to memory constraints can understate attack strength and robustness, leading to unreliable benchmarking of iterative stochastic defenses.

Method: Introduce a memory-efficient framework that combines gradient checkpointing with controlled evaluation across stochastic purification trajectories; apply it to diffusion-based purification and Langevin sampling with Energy-Based Models (EBMs) to perform exact end-to-end gradient attacks under white-box settings.

Result: Full-gradient attacks uncover vulnerabilities not seen with approximate gradients, yielding stronger l∞ and l2 white-box attacks and enabling more reliable probing of out-of-distribution robustness for iterative stochastic defenses.

Conclusion: Exact-gradient evaluation is essential for reliable benchmarking of iterative stochastic defenses; gradient checkpointing makes practical end-to-end gradients feasible and improves assessment of robustness and OOD resilience.

Abstract: This work studies the robust evaluation of iterative stochastic purification defenses under white-box adversarial attacks. Our key technical insight is that gradient checkpointing makes exact end-to-end gradient computation through long purification trajectories practical by trading additional recomputation for substantially lower memory usage. This enables full-gradient adaptive attacks against diffusion- and Langevin-based purification defenses, where prior evaluations often resort to approximate backpropagation due to memory constraints. These approximations can weaken the attack signal and risk overestimating robustness. In parallel, stochasticity in iterative purification is frequently under-controlled, even though different purification trajectories can substantially change reported robustness metrics. Building on this insight, we introduce a memory-efficient full-gradient evaluation framework for stochastic purification defenses. The framework combines checkpointed backpropagation with evaluation protocols that control stochastic variability, thereby reducing memory bottlenecks while preserving exact gradients. We evaluate diffusion-based purification and Langevin sampling with Energy-Based Models (EBMs), demonstrating that full-gradient attacks uncover vulnerabilities missed by approximate-gradient evaluations. Our framework yields stronger state-of-the-art $\ell_{\infty}$ and $\ell_{2}$ white-box attacks and further supports probing out-of-distribution robustness. Overall, our results show that exact-gradient evaluation is essential for reliable benchmarking of iterative stochastic defenses.

</details>


### [278] [Contrastive Identification and Generation in the Limit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06211)
*Xiaoyu Li,Andi Han,Jiaojiao Jiang,Junbin Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces contrastive identification and generation in the limit with relational supervision; provides exact characterization of identifiable classes, a new contrastive closure dimension, and tight sample complexity for uniform contrastive generation; establishes a crossing-graph framework and a sharp reversal under finite adversarial corruption, plus a hierarchy showing incomparability with positive-data notions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend identification in the limit from singleton labels to relational (contrastive) supervision, addressing generation in the limit for data presented as pairs with opposite labels.

Method: Theoretical analysis in the noiseless setting. Defines contrastive identifiable classes via a refinement of Angluin's tell-tale condition, introduces the contrastive closure dimension, and derives tight sample complexity for uniform contrastive generation. Proves a strict hierarchy showing incomparability with text identification, and establishes a budget-independent algorithm that can succeed under finite corruption. Unifies results via the crossing graph, which encodes ambiguity, generation obstructions, and corruption defects.

Result: Exact characterization of contrastive identifiable classes; introduction of the contrastive closure dimension; tight sample complexity for uniform contrastive generation; a strict hierarchy (contrastive generation and text identification are incomparable); a reversal result: under finite corruption, some classes are identifiable from contrastive pairs with any budget, yet not identifiable from positive examples with any corruption; crossing graph as a unifying tool.

Conclusion: The crossing graph provides a unified language for ambiguity, generation obstacles, and corruption effects in contrastive identification/generation. This framework opens avenues for noisy settings, algorithmic development, and empirical validation for relational supervision tasks.

Abstract: In the classical identification in the limit model of Gold [1967], a stream of positive examples is presented round by round, and the learner must eventually recover the target hypothesis. Recently, Kleinberg and Mullainathan [2024] introduced generation in the limit, where the learner instead must eventually output novel elements of the target's support. Both lines of work focus on positive-only or fully labeled data. Yet many natural supervision signals are inherently relational rather than singleton, which encode relationships between examples rather than labels of individual ones. We initiate the study of contrastive identification and generation in the limit, where the learner observes a contrastive presentation of data: a stream of unordered pairs $\{x,y\}$ satisfying $h(x)\ne h(y)$ for an unknown target binary hypothesis $h$, but which element is positive is hidden from the learner. We first present three results in the noiseless setting: an exact characterization of contrastive identifiable classes (a one-line geometric refinement of Angluin [1980]'s tell-tale condition), a combinatorial dimension called contrastive closure dimension (a contrasitive analogue of the closure dimension in Raman et al. [2025]) and exactly characterizing uniform contrastive generation with tight sample complexity, and a strict hierarchy in which contrastive generation and text identification are mutually incomparable. We then prove a sharp reversal under finite adversarial corruption: there exist classes identifiable from contrastive pairs under any finite corruption budget by a single budget-independent algorithm, yet not identifiable from positive examples under even one corrupted observation.
