TL;DR: Pro-KLShampoo exploits a spike-and-flat eigenvalue structure in KL-Shampoo's Kronecker preconditioners by restricting one Kronecker factor to a subspace with full spectral structure and a shared eigenvalue across the rest, then orthogonalizing on the remaining directions. This yields a memory- and time-efficient preconditioner that matches full KL-Shampoo in principle, and empirically improves validation loss, memory, and wallclock time across GPT-2 and LLaMA scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KL-Shampoo's Kronecker preconditioners exhibit a spike-and-flat eigenvalue spectrum across layers and training stages, suggesting potential efficiency gains from exploiting this structure. A more parameter-efficient preconditioning strategy could reduce memory and compute while preserving effectiveness.

Method: Introduce Pro-KLShampoo by constraining one Kronecker factor to a parametric spike-and-flat family: compute the full spectral structure on an r-dimensional tracked subspace and assign a single shared eigenvalue for the remaining directions; apply orthogonalization on those remaining directions. An identity shows this recovers the algebraic form of the full KL-Shampoo preconditioner. Validate on four pre-training scales (GPT-2 124M/350M, LLaMA 134M/450M).

Result: Across tested subspace ranks, Pro-KLShampoo consistently outperforms KL-Shampoo in validation loss, peak per-GPU memory, and wallclock time to reach each loss level.

Conclusion: Exploiting the spike-and-flat spectral structure in gradient Kronecker factors via a restricted subspace preconditioner and orthogonalization yields improved efficiency without sacrificing performance, suggesting a general design principle for structured gradient preconditioning.

Abstract: Optimizers that exploit the matrix structure of gradients are central to modern LLM pre-training, with two distinct frontiers: explicit Kronecker-factored preconditioning -- most recently KL-Shampoo, which estimates the preconditioner via KL divergence minimization -- and orthogonalization of the gradient momentum, exemplified by Muon and analyzed as steepest descent under the spectral norm. The two routes are typically developed in isolation. We make a structural observation about KL-Shampoo's Kronecker preconditioners: their eigenvalue spectra exhibit a \emph{spike-and-flat} shape -- a few dominant eigenvalues followed by an approximately uniform tail -- across layers and training stages, holding exactly under a rank-$ρ$ signal-plus-noise gradient model. We exploit this structure by restricting one of KL-Shampoo's Kronecker factors to a parametric family aligned with the spike-and-flat shape: full spectral structure on a tracked $r$-dimensional subspace, single shared eigenvalue across the remaining $n-r$ directions. On these directions, we apply orthogonalization. An identity shows that this orthogonalization recovers the algebraic form of full KL-Shampoo's preconditioner. On four pre-training scales (GPT-2 124M / 350M, LLaMA 134M / 450M), Pro-KLShampoo consistently outperforms KL-Shampoo at every subspace rank we test in validation loss, peak per-GPU memory, and wallclock time to reach each loss level.

</details>


### [305] [LINC: Decoupling Local Consequence Scoring from Hidden Matching in Constructive Neural Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06332)
*Shaofeng Qin,Li Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LINC (Local Inference via Normed Comparison) is a decoder-side architecture that explicitly models deterministic one-step routing consequences to improve constructive neural routing solvers across CVRPTW, CVRP, and TSP, achieving substantial gap reductions on benchmark metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing decoder-only scoring often misses explicit travel, waiting, slack, and capacity changes; explicit local inference can preserve global matching and reduce the burden on hidden states, enabling better planning accuracy.

Method: LINC computes centered relative consequences via a shared linear local scorer and uses feasible-set summaries to modulate the decoder context, preserving standard global matching while incorporating deterministic consequences.

Result: On CVRPTW, LINC reduces PolyNet's Solomon/Homberger gaps from 13.83% to 7.26% and 38.15% to 14.71%; improvements also extend to TSP and CVRP on external benchmarks.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling deterministic one-step effects at the decoder side enhances constructive routing solvers, preserving global matching and generalizing across CVRPTW, CVRP, and TSP.

Abstract: Constructive neural routing solvers usually score the next action by matching a decoder context to candidate embeddings, hiding deterministic one-step consequences such as travel, waiting, slack, and capacity changes. We propose LINC (Local Inference via Normed Comparison), a decoder-side candidate decision architecture that computes these consequences explicitly. LINC uses them according to their decision role: centered relative consequences are compared by a shared linear local scorer, while feasible-set summaries modulate the decoder context. This preserves standard global matching and relieves the hidden state from rediscovering transition arithmetic. The Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (CVRPTW) serves as the main constrained-routing stress test; the same interface extends to the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) and Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP). In particular, for CVRPTW, LINC reduces PolyNet's Solomon/Homberger gaps from 13.83\%/38.15\% to 7.26\%/14.71\%; for TSP and CVRP, it also improves external-benchmark gaps.

</details>


### [306] [Eliciting associations between clinical variables from LLMs via comparison questions across populations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06335)
*Fabian Kabus,Kian Kordtomeikel,Thomas Brox,Heinz Wiendl,Daiana Stolz,Harald Binder*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes triplet-based indirect elicitation to infer correlations and invariant causal links from LLMs, using ICP; demonstrated in COPD and MS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To recover correlation and causal structure from LLMs without accessing model internals, by indirect elicitation through structured triplet questions and environment shifts.

Method: Use structured patient comparison triplets; model LLM representations to estimate correlations without activations; induce prompt-level environment shifts to obtain subpopulation-specific correlations; apply invariant causal prediction (ICP) to identify conserved parent links; validate in COPD and MS.

Result: Correlations elicited by triplet comparisons are smooth, stable, and clinically interpretable across environments, with significant variation enabling ICP to produce a small set of candidate invariant parent links.

Conclusion: Indirect elicitation via triplet comparisons can recover meaningful association structure from LLMs and enable cautious, invariant causal statements aligned with LLM answering patterns.

Abstract: The training data of large language models (LLMs) comprises a wide range of biomedical literature, reflecting data from many different patient populations. We investigate how it might be possible to recover information on correlation and causal links between patient characteristics, as a key building block for medical decision making. To avoid the pitfalls of direct elicitation, we propose an approach based on structured comparison questions, specifically patient comparison triplet questions. This is combined with a statistical model for the LLM representation that provides estimates of correlations without access to activations or model internals. Intuitively, we consider how similarity decisions of LLMs based on a first variable are affected by providing information on a second variable for one of the patients being assessed. We then induce prompt-level environment shifts to obtain correlation estimates for different subpopulations, which enables an invariant causal prediction (ICP) approach to obtain conservative candidate parent links. We demonstrate the method in two clinical domains, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and multiple sclerosis (MS). Across prompted environments, the elicited correlations are smooth, stable, and clinically interpretable, yet vary in a statistically significant way that supports downstream invariance testing, such that ICP provides a small set of candidate invariant parent links. These results show that indirect elicitation via triplet comparisons can recover meaningful association structure from LLMs and offer a cautious route from implicit correlations to causal statements that are congruent with LLM answering patterns.

</details>


### [307] [Is Escalation Worth It? A Decision-Theoretic Characterization of LLM Cascades](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06350)
*Dylan Bouchard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A decision-theoretic analysis of multi-model cascades reveals the cost-quality frontier is piecewise concave and given by the envelope of pairwise cascades; shadow prices link budget and quality; full deterministic chains underperform the envelope; lightweight routing can beat cascade policies, underscoring structural cost limits rather than model count in cascade gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide theoretical guidance for deferral thresholds in model cascades and the geometry of the cost–quality frontier across a pool of models, to optimize deployment cost and accuracy in a principled way.

Method: Constrained optimization with duality. For two-model cascades, prove piecewise concavity of the frontier on decreasing-benefit regions of the confidence support and relate budget- and quality-constrained solutions via reciprocal shadow prices. For k models, show the frontier is the envelope of all pairwise cascades (k choose 2), with switching points where the optimal pair changes. Derive first-order conditions for k-model cascades where a single shadow price equalizes marginal quality-per-cost across stage boundaries. Empirically validate on five benchmarks (MATH, MMLU, TriviaQA, SimpleQA, LiveCodeBench) using eight models from five providers, comparing deterministic threshold cascades, pairwise envelopes, subsequences, and a lightweight router.

Result: Frontier geometry is tractable: piecewise-concave for two-model cascades; the envelope across all pairs governs the k-model frontier with identifiable switching points; shadow prices connect budget- and quality-constrained optima; optimized pairwise cascades outperform full fixed chains; subsequence cascades offer limited extra gains; a lightweight pre-generation router often outperforms the best cascade policy by avoiding cheap-model costs for queries better handled by a larger model; overall, gains are limited more by structural cost than by adding more stages.

Conclusion: Cascade performance is constrained by structural costs. Adding more stages yields diminishing returns; efficient routing that bypasses cheap models for certain queries yields the most practical gains. This suggests focusing on cost-aware routing and threshold design informed by duality, rather than merely expanding the model pool or enforcing longer fixed chains.

Abstract: Model cascades, in which a cheap LLM defers to an expensive one on low-confidence queries, are widely used to navigate the cost-quality tradeoff at deployment. Existing approaches largely treat the deferral threshold as an empirical hyperparameter, with limited guidance on the geometry of the resulting cost-quality frontier over a model pool. We develop a decision-theoretic framework grounded in constrained optimization and duality. For a two-model cascade, we establish piecewise concavity of the cost-quality frontier on decreasing-benefit regions of the confidence support, with reciprocal shadow prices linking the budget- and quality-constrained formulations. Given a pool of $k$ models, we characterize the frontier achievable by deterministic two-model threshold cascades as the pointwise envelope over $\binom{k}{2}$ pairwise cascades, with switching points where the optimal pair changes. For $k$-model cascades, we derive first-order conditions in which a single shadow price equalizes marginal quality-per-cost across stage boundaries. We validate the framework on five benchmarks (MATH, MMLU, TriviaQA, SimpleQA, LiveCodeBench) across eight models from five providers. Within the deterministic threshold-cascade class, full fixed chains underperform the pairwise envelope, and optimized subsequence cascades do not deliver practically meaningful held-out gains over it. A lightweight pre-generation router exceeds the best cascade policy on four of five datasets, mainly because it avoids the cheap model's generation cost on queries sent directly to a larger model rather than because of a stronger routing signal. These results suggest that cascade performance is limited primarily by structural cost, since cascades pay the cheap model before any escalation decision, rather than by a shortage of intermediate stages.

</details>


### [308] [Topological Signatures of Grokking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06352)
*Yifan Tang,Qiquan Wang,Inés García-Redondo,Anthea Monod*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Persistent homology reveals grokking in neural nets; high H1 persistence signals a topological transition aligned with generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain grokking through topology and provide a unified, interpretable geometric signature that complements spectral and geometric diagnostics.

Method: Compute persistent homology on embedding-derived point clouds from models trained on modular arithmetic with varying primes; track maximum and total H1 persistence; analyze persistence diagrams for a dominant long-lived feature and structured secondary features; compare against Fourier analysis and local intrinsic dimension; perform ablations across data regimes and controls.

Result: A sharp rise in both maximum and total H1 persistence coincides with grokking; persistence diagrams show a dominant long-lived H1 feature with increasingly structured secondary features, reflecting the task's cyclic structure; topological transitions correlate with generalization rather than memorization; persistent homology provides a unified geometric/topological characterization of representation learning.

Conclusion: Persistent homology offers a principled framework to analyze how neural networks internalize latent structure during training, capturing local and global multi-scale structure and complementing existing diagnostics; potential applicability to broader representation learning scenarios.

Abstract: We study the grokking phenomenon through the lens of topology. Using persistent homology on point clouds derived from the embedding matrices of a range of models trained on modular arithmetic with varying primes, we identify a clear and consistent topological signature of grokking: a sharp increase in both the maximum and total persistence of first homology ($H_1$). Persistence diagrams reveal the emergence of a dominant long-lived topological feature together with increasingly structured secondary features, reflecting the underlying cyclic structure of the task. Compared to existing spectral and geometric diagnostics -- specifically, Fourier analysis and local intrinsic dimension -- persistent homology provides a unified geometric and topological characterization of representation learning, capturing both local and global multi-scale structure. Ablations across data regimes and control settings show that these topological transitions are tied to generalization rather than memorization. Our results suggest that persistent homology offers a principled and interpretable framework for analyzing how neural networks internalize latent structure during training.

</details>


### [309] [Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Modelling with Missing Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06355)
*Ignacio Peis,Pablo M. Olmos,Jes Frellsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MO-ARM extends order-agnostic autoregressive models to incomplete data by training under missingness and enabling active information acquisition, achieving robust imputation and improved downstream inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Incomplete data is pervasive in real-world datasets. Existing order-agnostic autoregressive models trained on fully observed data implicitly perform imputation under MCAR, but principled training on incomplete data under general missingness mechanisms is lacking. The work also addresses how to actively acquire information to improve predictions.

Method: 1) Reinterpret standard OA-AR training as imputation under a missing-at-random mechanism; 2) Introduce Missingness-Aware Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Model (MO-ARM), a principled framework for training on incomplete data under general missingness; 3) Exploit amortized conditional density estimation to perform active information acquisition by sequentially selecting the most informative missing variables for downstream tasks.

Result: Empirically, MO-ARM consistently outperforms established imputation baselines across real-world benchmarks, demonstrating robust out-of-sample imputation and effective active information acquisition.

Conclusion: MO-ARM provides a principled approach to handling incomplete data with order-agnostic autoregressive models, yielding strong imputation performance and enabling informative sequential data gathering for downstream tasks.

Abstract: Order-Agnostic autoregressive models have demonstrated strong performance in deep generative modeling, yet their use in settings with incomplete data remains largely unexplored. In this work, we reinterpret them through the lens of missing data. First, we show that their standard training procedure on fully observed data implicitly performs imputation under a missing completely at random mechanism, resulting in robust out-of-sample imputation performance in settings with high missingness. Second, we introduce the first principled framework for training them directly on incomplete datasets under general missingness mechanisms. Third, we leverage their amortized conditional density estimation to perform active information acquisition, i.e., sequentially selecting the most informative missing variables for downstream prediction or inference. Across a suite of real-world benchmarks, our Missingness-Aware Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Model (MO-ARM) consistently outperforms established imputation baselines.

</details>


### [310] [Preliminary Insights in Chronos Frequency Data Understanding and Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06361)
*Alessandro Pagani,Marco Cominelli,Liying Han,Gaofeng Dong,Sergio Benini,Francesco Gringoli,Mattia Savardi,Mani B. Srivastava,Trevor Bihl,Erik P. Blasch,Daniel O. Brigham,Kara Combs,Lance M. Kaplan,Federico Cerutti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chronos encodes and separates frequency information in internal representations, with reliable capture across frequencies in some regimes and degradation in others, as revealed by online MDL probes on the decoder.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how foundation models for temporal data encode fundamental signal properties (frequency content) and to inform interpretability and evaluation.

Method: Analyze Chronos using controlled, simple sinusoidal inputs across frequencies and apply lightweight online minimum description length (MDL) probes to the decoder to test presence and separability of frequency information.

Result: Frequency content is captured in Chronos’ internal representations across the spectrum, with identifiable regimes where representation quality degrades or requires special handling; MDL probes reveal separability of frequency information in certain regimes.

Conclusion: These findings yield practical guidance for signal processing and information fusion with Chronos and advance interpretability/evaluation of temporal foundation models.

Abstract: This paper presents a preliminary analysis of the ability of Chronos foundation model to process and internally represent frequency domain information. Foundation models that process time-series data offer practitioners a unified architecture capable of learning generic temporal representations across diverse tasks and domains, reducing the need for task-specific feature engineering and enabling transfer across signal modalities. Despite their growing adoption, the extent to which such models encode fundamental signal properties remains insufficiently characterised. We address this gap by analysing Chronos under controlled conditions, starting from the simplest class of signals: discrete sinusoids generated at fixed frequencies. Using lightweight online minimum description length probes applied to the decoder architecture, we test for the presence and separability of frequency information in the model's internal representations. The results provide insight into how frequential content is captured across the frequency spectrum and highlight regimes in which representation quality may degrade or require particular care. These findings offer practical guidance for users of Chronos in signal processing and information fusion contexts, and contribute to ongoing efforts to improve the interpretability and evaluation of foundation models for temporal data.

</details>


### [311] [Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06364)
*Xin Peng,Ang Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AuxPath-FM generalizes conditional flow matching by allowing an auxiliary variable η drawn from arbitrary distributions to shape probability paths X_t = a(t)X_1 + b(t)X_0 + c(t)η, preserving the continuity equation and marginal objective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limitations of Gaussian-only auxiliary noise in existing flow-matching frameworks; seek richer priors and controllable geometry for probability paths and potential label guidance.

Method: Introduce Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths. Define X_t as a linear combination of endpoints plus a time-dependent scaled auxiliary η with η drawn from any distribution (Gaussian, Uniform, Laplace, discrete, etc.). Prove preservation of the continuity equation and that the training objective remains consistent with the marginal formulation.

Result: Theoretical validation that the generalized probability paths preserve the key equations and training objective; practical flexibility to use diverse priors enabling varied geometric properties; supports tasks such as label-guided generation via structured auxiliary distributions.

Conclusion: AuxPath-FM provides a principled, general foundation for probability-path design in generative modeling, offering theoretical generality and practical flexibility across diverse tasks.

Abstract: We introduce a new generative modeling framework, \textbf{Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths (AuxPath-FM)}, which generalizes conditional flow matching by incorporating an auxiliary variable drawn from an arbitrary distribution into the probability path. Unlike prior methods that restrict auxiliary components to Gaussian noise, AuxPath-FM allows the variable $η$ to follow any distribution, producing trajectories of the form $X_t = a(t)X_1 + b(t)X_0 + c(t)η$. We theoretically demonstrate that this construction preserves the continuity equation and maintains a training objective consistent with the marginal formulation. This flexibility enables the design of diverse probability paths using various priors, including Gaussian, Uniform, Laplace, and discrete Rademacher distributions, each offering unique geometric properties for generative flows. Furthermore, our framework allows for specialized tasks such as label-guided generation by encoding structured semantic information into the auxiliary distribution. Overall, AuxPath-FM provides a principled and general foundation for probability path design, offering both theoretical generality and practical flexibility for diverse generative modeling tasks.

</details>


### [312] [A Unified Pair-GRPO Family: From Implicit to Explicit Preference Constraints for Stable and General RL Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06375)
*Hao Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Pair-GRPO, a unified family for preference-based RL optimization in LLM alignment, with Soft-Pair-GRPO and Hard-Pair-GRPO variants. It shows a gradient equivalence to GRPO under first-order expansion, adds local probability constraints for the hard variant, and delivers monotonic improvement, variance reduction, and dynamic convergence guarantees. Empirically, Pair-GRPO outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on HH-RLHF, UltraFeedback, and HalfCheetah-v4, with ablations confirming component contributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM alignment via RLHF suffers from unstable policy updates, ambiguous gradient directions, poor interpretability, and high gradient variance in pairwise preference learning. There is a need for a unified theoretical framework for preference-based RL optimization to improve stability, interpretability, and performance.

Method: Soft-Pair-GRPO: a minimal modification of GRPO replacing group-normalized scalar rewards with binary pairwise preference rewards, retaining GRPO's clipped surrogate and KL-regularized structure; proves a gradient equivalence theorem: under a first-order Taylor expansion around the current policy, Soft-Pair-GRPO's gradient is a positive scalar multiple of GRPO's gradient. Hard-Pair-GRPO: adds explicit local probability constraints and constrained KL-fitting optimization to further suppress gradient noise and policy drift.

Result: Theoretically, both variants provide monotonic policy improvement, deterministic gradient direction, gradient-variance reduction, and dynamic step-size convergence guarantees. Empirically, they outperform state-of-the-art baselines on standard LLM alignment benchmarks (HH-RLHF, UltraFeedback) and MuJoCo HalfCheetah-v4 in alignment quality, human preference win rate, training stability, and generalization to general RL. Ablation studies validate the contributions of core components.

Conclusion: Pair-GRPO offers a unified, theoretically grounded, and empirically strong framework for preference-based RL optimization in LLM alignment, delivering stable, low-variance updates and improved alignment quality, with potential applicability to broader RL settings.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) alignment via reinforcement learning from human preferences (RLHF) suffers from unstable policy updates, ambiguous gradient directions, poor interpretability, and high gradient variance in mainstream pairwise preference learning paradigms. To systematically address these limitations, we establish a unified theoretical framework for preference-based RL optimization centered on the Pair-GRPO family, comprising two tightly coupled variants: Soft-Pair-GRPO and Hard-Pair-GRPO. Soft-Pair-GRPO is a minimal modification of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) that replaces group-normalized scalar rewards with binary pairwise preference rewards, retaining GRPO's clipped surrogate and KL-regularized structure. We prove a critical gradient equivalence theorem: under first-order Taylor expansion around the current policy, Soft-Pair-GRPO's gradient is a positive scalar multiple of standard GRPO's gradient, explaining its empirical stability despite discarding continuous reward magnitudes. Building on this foundation, we propose Hard-Pair-GRPO, an advanced variant introducing explicit local probability constraints and constrained KL-fitting optimization to further suppress gradient noise and global policy drift. We provide comprehensive theoretical guarantees for both variants--including monotonic policy improvement, deterministic gradient direction, gradient-variance reduction, and dynamic step-size convergence. Extensive experiments on standard LLM alignment benchmarks (HH-RLHF,UltraFeedback) and the MuJoCo continuous control task HalfCheetah-v4 demonstrate that our Pair-GRPO family consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in alignment quality, human preference win rate, training stability, and generalization to general reinforcement learning. Ablation studies validate the critical contributions of each core component.

</details>


### [313] [MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06384)
*Alessandro Ronca*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs) are a recurrence-based architecture that achieves strong expressivity, efficient and parallelizable evaluation, uniform boundedness of state/activations, and gradient stability, with empirical success on synthetic tasks and promising performance on next-token prediction for large models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome vanishing/exploding gradients in recurrent models while maximizing expressivity and enabling efficient evaluation, both in parallel and sequential modes, within a finite-memory framework.

Method: Introduce MinMax algebra-based recurrence and cascade several neuron layers to form MinMax RNCs. The paper provides theoretical analyses showing: (i) expressivity includes all regular languages, (ii) parallelizable evaluation with logarithmic runtime given sufficient parallelism (and also sequential evaluation), (iii) uniform boundedness of state/activations across input lengths, (iv) almost-sure existence and boundedness of the loss gradient, (v) non-vanishing state gradients (state-to-past-state gradients can remain constant at one irrespective of time distance), and (vi) empirical validation on synthetic tasks and a 127M-parameter model on next-token prediction demonstrating competitive performance.

Result: The framework achieves strong theoretical guarantees: maximal finite-memory expressivity (regular languages), parallel-evaluation with logarithmic runtime, uniform boundedness of internal quantities, robust gradient properties including non-vanishing state gradients, and practical efficacy evidenced by solving synthetic tasks and competitive performance on a large-scale language modeling task.

Conclusion: MinMax RNCs combine favorable theoretical properties with practical capabilities, indicating strong potential for real-world sequential tasks. The results motivate further exploration of MinMax algebra in neural recurrence and scaling to larger datasets and tasks.

Abstract: We show that the MinMax algebra provides a form of recurrence that is expressively powerful, efficiently implementable, and most importantly it is not affected by vanishing or exploding gradient. We call MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs) the models obtained by cascading several layers of neurons that employ such recurrence. We show that MinMax RNCs enjoy many favourable theoretical properties. First, their formal expressivity includes all regular languages, arguably the maximal expressivity for a finite-memory system. Second, they can be evaluated in parallel with a runtime that is logarithmic in the input length given enough processors; and they can also be evaluated sequentially. Third, their state and activations are bounded uniformly for all input lengths. Fourth, at almost all points, their loss gradient exists and it is bounded. Fifth, they do not exhibit a vanishing state gradient: the gradient of a state w.r.t. a past state can have constant value one regardless of the time distance between the two states. Finally, we find empirical evidence that the favourable theoretical properties of MinMax RNCs are matched by their practical capabilities: they are able to perfectly solve a number of synthetic tasks, showing superior performance compared to the considered state-of-the-art recurrent neural networks; also, we train a MinMax RNC of 127M parameters on next-token prediction, and the obtained model shows competitive performance for its size, providing evidence of the potential of MinMax RNCs on real-world tasks.

</details>


### [314] [Data-Driven Covariate Selection for Nonparametric and Cycle-Agnostic Causal Effect Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06385)
*Ana Leticia Garcez Vicente,Gijs van Seeventer,Saber Salehkaleybar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A local, data-driven covariate selection method based on conditional independence can identify valid adjustment sets in both cyclic and acyclic causal models, extending soundness and completeness via σ-acyclification invariance; empirically validated on synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal effect estimation from observational data is hampered by latent confounding and feedback loops. Existing methods assume acyclicity or require global structure learning, limiting applicability and efficiency. A cycle-agnostic, data-driven covariate selection method is desirable.

Method: Proposes a local, data-driven covariate selection procedure that relies on conditional independence testing. The key theoretical tool is the invariance of CI statements under σ-acyclification, which preserves validity when cycles are present. It yields a cycle-agnostic framework for selecting covariates for causal effect estimation.

Result: The theoretical guarantees (soundness and completeness) of the CI-based covariate selection extend to cyclic causal models. This provides a unified approach applicable to both cyclic and acyclic settings without modification. Empirically, the method performs reliably on synthetic data with cyclic causal structures.

Conclusion: The study delivers a unified, cycle-agnostic framework for covariate selection and causal effect estimation, valid for both cyclic and acyclic graphs, supported by empirical validation on synthetic datasets.

Abstract: Estimating causal effects from observational data requires identifying valid adjustment sets. This task is especially challenging in realistic settings where latent confounding and feedback loops are present. Existing approaches typically assume acyclicity or rely on global causal structure learning, limiting applicability and computational efficiency. In this work, we study a local, data-driven method for covariate selection based on conditional independence information. While this method is known to be sound and complete in acyclic causal models, its validity in the presence of cycles has remained unclear. Our main contribution is to show that these guarantees extend to cyclic causal models. In particular, our result relies on the invariance of conditional independence assertions under $σ$-acyclification. These findings establish a unified, cycle-agnostic perspective on covariate selection and causal effect estimation, showing that the method applies across cyclic and acyclic settings without modification. Empirically, we validate this on extensive synthetic data, showing reliable performance in cyclic causal models.

</details>


### [315] [Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation: Bridging Exploitation and Imitation at the Token Level](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06387)
*Nan Jia,Haojin Yang,Xing Ma,Jiesong Lian,Shuailiang Zhang,Weipeng Zhang,Ke Zeng,Xunliang Cai,Zequn Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation (AOPD) mitigates OPD weaknesses by replacing negative reinforcement in non-positive advantage regions with localized divergence minimization, yielding improved performance, entropy, and tool-use retention on mathematical reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OPD's advantage-weighted policy gradient suffers high variance, vanishing gradients in zero-advantage regions, and exploration bottlenecks, hindering learning efficiency and robustness.

Method: Introduce asymmetric updates: preserve positive reinforcement while replacing ineffective negative reinforcement with localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions; maintain on-policy distillation framework; apply to mathematical reasoning benchmarks; analyze policy entropy and sequential tool-use adaptation.

Result: AOPD consistently outperforms standard OPD on mathematical reasoning benchmarks; average gains of 4.09 (strong initialization) and 8.34 (weak initialization); higher policy entropy during training; better capability retention during sequential tool-use adaptation.

Conclusion: AOPD alleviates core defects of OPD, enabling more stable, diverse, and transferable policies in on-policy distillation settings.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student on its own trajectories with token-level teacher feedback and often outperforms off-policy distillation and standard reinforcement learning. However, we find that its standard advantage weighted policy gradient suffers from three structural weaknesses, including high variance updates, vanishing gradients in zero-advantage regions, and exploration bottlenecks when corrective signals are insufficient.We therefore propose Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation (AOPD), which replaces ineffective negative reinforcement with localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions while preserving positive reinforcement learning. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that AOPD consistently outperforms standard OPD, with average gains of 4.09 / 8.34 under strong / weak initialization, respectively. AOPD also maintains higher policy entropy during training and better capability retention during sequential tool-use adaptation.

</details>


### [316] [Consistent Geometric Deep Learning via Hilbert Bundles and Cellular Sheaves](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06395)
*Kartik Tandon,Julian Gould,Tanishq Bhatia,Francesca Dominici,Alejandro Ribeiro,Claudio Battiloro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces HilbNets: a convolutional framework for infinite-dimensional signals on manifolds via the connection Laplacian of a Hilbert bundle. Provides two-stage sampling, convergence theorems (sheaf Laplacian to connection Laplacian; discretized networks converge and transfer across samplings), and empirical validation, extending geometric learning to fiber-wise Hilbert spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify learning theory for signals that are infinite-dimensional and defined over irregular domains; extend Laplacian-based geometric learning to manifold-valued signals with fiberwise Hilbert spaces.

Method: Define convolution via connection Laplacian on a Hilbert bundle; construct Hilbert Cellular Sheaf from manifold sampling; prove probabilistic convergence of sheaf Laplacian to connection Laplacian; discretize signals to obtain implementable HilbNets and prove convergence and transferability across samplings; build neural networks using these operators.

Result: Theoretical results: convergence in probability of the sheaf Laplacian to the connection Laplacian; discretized networks converge to continuous architectures; transferability across samplings. Empirical validation on synthetic and real-world tasks.

Conclusion: Broadens geometric learning by lifting Laplacian-based methods to infinite-dimensional fiber-valued signals, enabling learning on manifolds with Hilbert-space-valued data.

Abstract: Modern deep learning architectures increasingly contend with sophisticated signals that are natively infinite-dimensional, such as time series, probability distributions, or operators, and are defined over irregular domains. Yet, a unified learning theory for these settings has been lacking. To start addressing this gap, we introduce a novel convolutional learning framework for possibly infinite-dimensional signals supported on a manifold. Namely, we use the connection Laplacian associated with a Hilbert bundle as a convolutional operator, and we derive filters and neural networks, dubbed as \textit{HilbNets}. We make HilbNets and, more generally, the convolution operation, implementable via a two-stage sampling procedure. First, we show that sampling the manifold induces a Hilbert Cellular Sheaf, a generalized graph structure with Hilbert feature spaces and edge-wise coupling rules, and we prove that its sheaf Laplacian converges in probability to the underlying connection Laplacian as the sampling density increases. Notably, this result is a generalization to the infinite-dimensional bundle setting of the Belkin \& Niyogi \cite{BELKIN20081289} convergence result for the graph Laplacian to the manifold Laplacian, a theoretical cornerstone of geometric learning methods. Second, we discretize the signals and prove that the discretized (implementable) HilbNets converge to the underlying continuous architectures and are transferable across different samplings of the same bundle, providing consistency for learning. Finally, we validate our framework on synthetic and real-world tasks. Overall, our results broaden the scope of geometric learning as a whole by lifting classical Laplacian-based frameworks to settings where the signal at each point lives in its own Hilbert space.

</details>


### [317] [SparseForge: Efficient Semi-Structured LLM Sparsification via Annealing of Hessian-Guided Soft-Mask](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06402)
*Liu Hanzuo,Chaofan Lin,Weixuan Sun,Yulong Wang,Key,Rayying,Mingyu Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SparseForge is a post-training sparsity framework that directly optimizes the sparsity mask using Hessian-aware importance estimation and progressive annealing of soft masks into hardware-friendly structured sparsity, enabling efficient recovery of accuracy with vastly fewer retraining tokens on LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training semi-structured pruning suffers substantial quality degradation due to strong structural coupling, often requiring large-scale sparse retraining to recover accuracy, which is computationally expensive.

Method: Hessian-aware importance estimation combined with progressive annealing of soft masks into hardware-executable structured sparsity.

Result: On LLaMA-2-7B under 2:4 sparsity, SparseForge achieves 57.27% average zero-shot accuracy with only 5B retraining tokens, surpassing the dense model's 56.43% and approaching the 57.52% of a state-of-the-art method that uses 40B tokens; improvements hold across model families.

Conclusion: SparseForge improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for post-training semi-structured sparsity and generalizes across model families.

Abstract: Semi-structured sparsity provides a practical path to accelerate large language models (LLMs) with native hardware support, but post-training semi-structured pruning often suffers from substantial quality degradation due to strong structural coupling. Existing methods rely on large-scale sparse retraining to recover accuracy, resulting in high computational cost.
  We propose SparseForge, a post-training framework that improves recovery efficiency by directly optimizing the sparsity mask rather than scaling up retraining tokens. SparseForge combines Hessian-aware importance estimation with progressive annealing of soft masks into hardware-executable structured sparsity, enabling stable and efficient sparse recovery. On LLaMA-2-7B under 2:4 sparsity, SparseForge achieves 57.27% average zero-shot accuracy with only $\textbf{5B}$ retraining tokens, surpassing the dense model's 56.43% accuracy and approaching the 57.52% result of a state-of-the-art method using $\textbf{40B}$ tokens. Such improvements on the accuracy-efficiency trade-off from SparseForge are shown to be consistent across model families.

</details>


### [318] [FRInGe: Distribution-Space Integrated Gradients with Fisher--Rao Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06404)
*Gabriele Martino,Sebastian Tschiatschek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FRInGe reframes IG in predictive distribution space using Fisher-Rao geodesics, replacing baselines with a max-entropy reference and pullback geometry to enable better calibration-oriented attributions while remaining competitive on other metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address brittleness of Integrated Gradients due to heuristic baselines, interpolation paths, discretization, and saturation; pursue more calibration-aligned attributions without sacrificing perturbation robustness or fidelity.

Method: Define reference and interpolation in predictive distribution space. Use a maximum-entropy predictive reference and follow a Fisher-Rao geodesic on the probability simplex. Realize the input-space trajectory via the pullback Fisher metric and stabilize with KL and Euclidean trust regions. Compute attributions by integrating input gradients along this trajectory.

Result: On six ImageNet architectures, FRInGe yields clear improvements in calibration-oriented attribution metrics, notably MAS scores, while remaining competitive on perturbation AUC and infidelity.

Conclusion: FRInGe provides a principled, geometry-based attribution method that enhances calibration-oriented explanations by leveraging Fisher-Rao geometry, with strong empirical gains on MAS and solid performance on other attribution metrics.

Abstract: Gradient-based attribution methods are model-faithful and scalable, but Integrated Gradients (IG) can be brittle because explanations depend on heuristic baselines, straight-line paths, discretization, and saturation. We propose Fisher--Rao Integrated Gradients (FRInGe), which defines both the reference and interpolation schedule in predictive distribution space. FRInGe replaces input baselines with a maximum-entropy predictive reference and follows a Fisher-Rao geodesic on the probability simplex. The corresponding input-space trajectory is realized through the pullback Fisher metric and stabilized by KL and Euclidean trust regions; attributions are obtained by integrating input gradients along this trajectory. Across six ImageNet architectures, FRInGe most clearly improves calibration-oriented attribution metrics, especially MAS scores, while remaining competitive on perturbation AUC and infidelity.

</details>


### [319] [Federated Cross-Client Subgraph Pattern Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06433)
*Selin Ceydeli,Rui Wang,Kubilay Atasu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A federated, per-step embedding-exchange framework is proposed to align distributed GNNs with a centralized model for subgraph pattern detection, under an extended-subgraph assumption; fresh per-step embeddings are essential, and embedding exchange complements federated parameter aggregation to recover the representation gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized GNNs assume access to the full graph, but distributed graphs across parties cause a representation-equivalence gap due to lost cross-partition observability. The goal is to preserve privacy while achieving representations equivalent to a centralized model for subgraph pattern detection.

Method: Clients perform local GNN forward passes and exchange intermediate node embeddings at each layer (per-step, layer-wise), with shared model parameters and extended-subgraph assumptions. No raw features or labels are exposed. Federated parameter aggregation can be used in tandem. Embeddings must be refreshed per-step to be effective.

Result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic directed multigraphs shows that embedding exchange and federated parameter aggregation are complementary; their combination recovers most of the representation gap. Using fresh per-step embeddings is crucial; stale per-epoch embeddings degrade performance.

Conclusion: The framework can bridge the representational gap between federated and centralized GNNs for subgraph pattern detection under the stated assumptions, enabling privacy-preserving cross-partition learning, with practical guidance that per-step embedding freshness is essential for effectiveness.

Abstract: Subgraph pattern detection aims to uncover complex interaction structures in graphs. However, state-of-the-art graph neural network (GNN)-based solutions assume centralized access to the entire graph. When graphs are instead distributed across multiple parties, client-local GNN computations diverge from those of a centralized model, resulting in a representation-equivalence gap. We formalize this as a structural observability problem, where subgraph patterns crossing partition boundaries become locally unidentifiable. To bridge this gap, we propose a per-step, layer-wise embedding exchange framework in which clients synchronize intermediate node representations at each layer of the forward pass, without exposing raw features or labels. Under an extended-subgraph assumption and shared model parameters across clients, this framework recovers the same node representations as a centralized GNN over the full graph. Experiments on synthetic directed multigraphs with cycles, bicliques, and scatter-gather patterns show that embedding exchange and federated parameter aggregation are complementary rather than interchangeable: their combination recovers most of the representation gap, provided exchanged embeddings are fresh per-step rather than stale per-epoch.

</details>


### [320] [FedFrozen: Two-Stage Federated Optimization via Attention Kernel Freezing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06446)
*Junye Du,Zhenghao Li,Yushi Feng,Long Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedFrozen is a two-stage federated optimization for Transformer models under data heterogeneity: full-model warm-up followed by freezing the query/key (attention kernel) and continuing to train the value block, under a linear-attention assumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate client drift in heterogeneous FL and understand how different attention components affect optimization robustness, leveraging the inherent stability properties of Transformer architectures.

Method: Decompose attention into query/key and value blocks; perform full-model warm-up; freeze Q/K block; optimize V block with a fixed kernel; analyze under linear-attention; interpret warm-up as inexact descent on a regularized kernel-profile objective; derive a trade-off for warm-up length.

Result: Theoretical analysis reveals a bias-drift trade-off governed by warm-up duration; simulations confirm predicted bias and drift behavior; experiments on real data show improved stability and effectiveness of Transformer models in heterogeneous FL with FedFrozen.

Conclusion: FedFrozen provides a principled, component-wise freezing strategy that enhances stability and performance in heterogeneous FL for Transformers; the warm-up length plays a critical role via a kernel-profile objective framework.

Abstract: Federated learning with heterogeneous clients remains a significant challenge for deep learning, primarily due to client drift arising from inconsistent local updates. Existing federated optimization methods typically address this issue through objective-level regularization or update-correction mechanisms. Recent studies, however, suggest that Transformer-based architectures may be inherently more robust than conventional models under heterogeneous federated training. Motivated by this observation, we investigate how different parameter components within the attention mechanism influence federated optimization. Specifically, we decompose the attention module into a query/key block, which determines the attention kernel, and a value block, which performs semantic transformation under the induced kernel. Based on this perspective, we propose FedFrozen, a two-stage federated optimization framework that first performs full-model warm-up training and then freezes the query/key block while continuing to optimize the value block. Under a linear-attention formulation, we show that the warm-up stage can be interpreted as an inexact descent procedure on a regularized kernel-profile objective, while the frozen stage reduces to a restricted value-block optimization problem under a fixed attention kernel. Our analysis further reveals an explicit trade-off that governs the choice of warm-up length. Simulations validate the predicted bias-drift behavior, and real-data experiments demonstrate that FedFrozen improves both the stability and effectiveness of Transformer models in heterogeneous federated learning.

</details>


### [321] [Scene-Adaptive Continual Learning for CSI-based Human Activity Recognition with Mixture of Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06447)
*Wenhan Zheng,Yuyi Mao,Ivan Wang-Hei Ho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable continual-learning framework for CSI-based HAR using a scene-adaptive mixture-of-experts with a semantic router and a tiny replay buffer; achieves competitive accuracy with lower inference cost on a four-scene dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CSI-based HAR suffers performance degradation under domain shifts; existing continual-learning methods either scale poorly with accumulating domains, require large replay buffers, or incur linearly growing inference costs. There is a need for scalable, efficient cross-domain adaptation in real-world HAR systems.

Method: A scene-adaptive mixture of experts (SAMoE) with clustered specialists (SAMoE-C) where an attention-based semantic router activates only a subset of experts per input, enabling scene-specific adaptation. A lightweight training protocol uses only a tiny replay buffer to stabilize domain discrimination of the router.

Result: On a four-scene CSI dataset, SAMoE-C achieves near state-of-the-art accuracy while significantly reducing inference cost, demonstrating scalable cross-domain HAR with low training overhead.

Conclusion: SAMoE-C provides a scalable, efficient approach for cross-domain CSI-based HAR by combining modular experts, selective router activation, and a lightweight training protocol. It enables deployment with lower inference cost and training overhead, though performance on unseen scenes and broader scalability warrants further study.

Abstract: Channel state information (CSI)-based human activity recognition (HAR) is vulnerable to performance degradation under domain shifts across varying physical environments. Continual learning (CL) offers a principled way to learn new domains sequentially while preserving past knowledge, but existing CL solutions for CSI-based HAR scale poorly with accumulating domains, rely on a large replay buffer, or incur linearly growing inference cost. In this letter, we propose Scene-Adaptive Mixture of Experts with Clustered Specialists (SAMoE-C), which formulates cross-domain CSI-based HAR as a mixture-of-experts system that enables scene-specific adaptation, via an attention-based semantic router that activates only selected experts for each input. Moreover, we develop a novel training protocol, which requires only a tiny replay buffer for stabilizing domain discrimination of the router. Experimental results on a four-scene CSI dataset demonstrate that SAMoE-C approaches the state-of-the-art accuracy, while maintaining a significantly lower inference cost. By jointly combining modular experts, selective activation with router and a lightweight training protocol, SAMoE-C enables scalable cross-domain CSI-based HAR deployment with low training overhead and high computational efficiency in real-world settings.

</details>


### [322] [ORTHOBO: Orthogonal Bayesian Hyperparameter Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06454)
*Maresa Schröder,Pascal Janetzky,Michael Klar,Stefan Feuerriegel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Orthogonal acquisition estimator (OrthoBO) reduces Monte Carlo variance in Bayesian optimization by using a score-function control variate to create an acquisition residual orthogonal to posterior score directions, combined with ensemble surrogates and an outer log transform; theory ensures variance reduction and preserved target, with strong empirical results in hyperparameter optimization for neural networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finite-sample Monte Carlo error in acquisition estimates can flip candidate rankings, causing unstable BO decisions even when the surrogate model and acquisition target are correctly specified; this failure mode has been underappreciated.

Method: Introduce an orthogonal acquisition estimator that subtracts an optimally weighted score-function control variate from the acquisition estimate, yielding a residual orthogonal to posterior score directions. Combine this with ensemble surrogates and an outer log transformation to form OrthoBO; provide theoretical guarantees that the estimator preserves the acquisition target and reduces variance, and demonstrate variance reduction and improved ranking stability.

Result: Theoretical results show variance reduction and preserved target; numerical experiments demonstrate reduced acquisition-variance, stabilized candidate rankings, and strong BO performance; empirical validation includes hyperparameter optimization and neural network fine-tuning.

Conclusion: OrthoBO provides a principled variance-reduction framework for acquisition estimation in Bayesian optimization, improving ranking stability and overall performance in expensive-evaluation settings.

Abstract: Bayesian optimization is widely used for hyperparameter optimization when model evaluations are expensive; however, noisy acquisition estimates can lead to unstable decisions. We identify acquisition estimation noise as a failure mode that was previously overlooked: even when the surrogate model and acquisition target are correctly specified, finite-sample Monte Carlo error can perturb acquisition values. This can, in turn, flip candidate rankings and lead to suboptimal BO decisions. As a remedy, we aim at variance reduction and propose an orthogonal acquisition estimator that subtracts an optimally weighted score-function control variate, which yields an acquisition residual orthogonal to posterior score directions and which thus reduces Monte Carlo variance. We further introduce OrthoBO: a Bayesian optimization framework that combines our orthogonal acquisition estimator with ensemble surrogates and an outer log transformation. We show theoretically that our estimator preserves the target, leads to variance reduction, and improves pairwise ranking stability. We further verify the theoretical properties of OrthoBO through numerical experiments where our framework reduces acquisition estimation variance, stabilizes candidate rankings, and achieves strong performance. We also demonstrate the downstream utility of OrthoBO in hyperparameter optimization for neural network training and fine-tuning.

</details>


### [323] [Invariant Features in Language Models: Geometric Characterization and Model Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06458)
*Agnibh Dasgupta,Abdullah Tanvir,Xin Zhong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A local geometric framework explains semantic invariance in language models: semantically equivalent inputs cluster in invariant subspaces within latent space, with paraphrase variation along nuisance directions; a contrastive subspace discovery method separates semantic-changing from semantic-preserving variation, and invariant representations enable zero-shot model attribution across layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the origin and structure of semantic invariances (robust paraphrase behavior) in neural language models by grounding it in the geometry of latent representations.

Method: Introduce a local geometric framework for latent spaces; define invariant subspaces and nuisance directions; develop a contrastive subspace discovery algorithm to separate semantic-preserving vs semantic-changing variation; conduct causal interventions and zero-shot attribution experiments across models and layers.

Result: Invariant structure emerges in specific depth regions; semantic displacement largely lies outside the nuisance subspace; interventions reveal a causal role for invariant components in model outputs; invariant representations exhibit model-specific geometric patterns enabling accurate attribution across models.

Conclusion: Semantic invariance can be understood as a local geometric property of latent representations, offering a principled perspective on how language models organize meaning and enabling applications such as attribution and robust semantic analysis.

Abstract: Language models exhibit strong robustness to paraphrasing, suggesting that semantic information may be encoded through stable internal representations, yet the structure and origin of such invariance remain unclear. We propose a local geometric framework in which semantically equivalent inputs occupy structured regions in latent space, with paraphrastic variation along nuisance directions and semantic identity preserved in invariant subspaces. Building on this view, we make three contributions: (1) a geometric characterization of invariant latent features, (2) a contrastive subspace discovery method that separates semantic-changing from semantic-preserving variation, and (3) an application of invariant representations to zero-shot model attribution. Across models and layers, empirical results support these contributions. Invariant structure emerges in specific depth regions, semantic displacement lies largely outside the nuisance subspace, and representation-level interventions indicate a causal role of invariant components in model outputs. Invariant representations also capture model-specific geometric patterns, enabling accurate attribution. These findings suggest that semantic invariance can be viewed as a local geometric property of latent representations, offering a principled perspective on how language models organize meaning.

</details>


### [324] [MINER: Mining Multimodal Internal Representation for Efficient Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06460)
*Weien Li,Rui Song,Zeyu Li,Haochen Liu,Gonghao Zhang,Difan Jiao,Zhenwei Tang,Bowei He,Haolun Wu,Xue Liu,Ye Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MINER is a plug-in for dense single-vector retrievers that mines retrieval-relevant signals across transformer layers via layerwise probing and adaptive sparse fusion, achieving strong gains over backbone and narrowing gaps to late-interaction methods while preserving efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense single-vector retrievers compress all information into a final embedding, often lagging late-interaction methods in retrieval quality. Internal layer representations may contain richer, layer-specific signals that are relevant for retrieval but are discarded by single-vector encoders. Exploiting these signals without changing the backbone could close the performance gap while retaining efficiency.

Method: 1) Retrieval-Aligned Layer Probing: attach lightweight probes at each transformer layer to surface dimensions carrying retrieval-relevant information. 2) Adaptive Sparse Multi-Layer Fusion: apply performance-adaptive neuron-level masking on the selected layers and fuse the surviving signals into the final compact dense vector. The approach is a plug-in that does not modify the backbone or incur additional inference cost beyond the fusion.

Result: MINER outperforms existing dense single-vector retrievers on ViDoRe V1/V2/V3 benchmarks, with up to 4.5% nDCG@5 improvement over the backbone. Compared to strong late-interaction baselines, it narrows the nDCG@5 gap in some settings to as little as 0.2, while maintaining dense-vector storage and serving efficiency.

Conclusion: Layer-wise probing reveals that retrieval-relevant signals are present in internal representations, and an adaptive multi-layer fusion scheme can leverage these signals to enhance single-vector retrieval without backbone changes, offering a practical balance of accuracy and efficiency.

Abstract: Visual document retrieval has become essential for accessing information in visually rich documents. Existing approaches fall into two camps. Late-interaction retrievers achieve strong quality through fine-grained token-level matching but store hundreds of vectors per page, incurring large index footprints and high serving costs. By contrast, dense single-vector retrievers retain storage and latency advantages but consistently lag in quality because they compress all information into a single final-layer embedding. In this work, we first conduct a layerwise diagnostic on single-vector retrievers, revealing that retrieval-relevant signal resides in internal representations. Motivated by these findings, we propose MINER (Mining Multimodal Internal RepreseNtation for Efficient Retrieval), a lightweight plug-in module that probes and fuses internal signals across transformer layers into a single compact embedding without modifying the backbone or sacrificing single-vector efficiency. The first Retrieval-Aligned Layer Probing stage attaches a lightweight probe at each layer, surfacing which dimensions carry retrieval-relevant information. The subsequent Adaptive Sparse Multi-Layer Fusion stage applies performance-adaptive neuron-level masking to the selected layers and fuses the surviving signals into the final dense vector. Across ViDoRe V1/V2/V3, MINER outperforms existing dense single-vector retrievers on the majority of benchmarks, with up to 4.5% nDCG@5 improvement over its corresponding backbone. Compared to strong late-interaction baselines, in some settings MINER substantially narrows the nDCG@$5$ gap to $0.2$ while preserving the storage and serving advantages of dense retrieval.

</details>


### [325] [Invariant-Based Diagnostics for Graph Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06462)
*Richard von Moos,Mathieu Alain,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph invariants provide a diagnostic framework to separate structure from features in graph benchmarks. Invariants can be more expressive than standard GNNs, reveal structural heterogeneity, predict multi-task performance, and enable simple, competitive baselines across many datasets, suggesting structure-aware evaluation should become standard in graph learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark practices conflate node features and graph structure, obscuring whether models truly leverage connectivity. A diagnostic, task-agnostic, permutation-invariant descriptor of graph structure is needed to isolate the role of topology in learning.

Method: Introduce permutation-invariant, task-agnostic graph invariants as a diagnostic framework; evaluate their expressivity relative to standard GNNs; analyze their ability to characterize heterogeneity across benchmarks; assess multi-task predictive power; compare simple invariant-based models against transformer/MPNN baselines on 26 datasets.

Result: Invariants are more expressive than standard GNNs; they capture structural heterogeneity within and across benchmark datasets; invariants predict multi-task performance; simple invariant-based models are competitive with, and sometimes superior to, transformer and message-passing baselines across 26 datasets.

Conclusion: Expressivity is not the sole predictor of performance; for structure-sensitive tasks, a non-trainable structural proxy can match trained models. Invariant baselines should become a standard tool to assess whether structure is required and whether a model uses it, facilitating progress toward graph foundation models.

Abstract: Progress on graph foundation models is hindered by benchmark practices that conflate the contributions of node features and graph structure, making it hard to tell whether a model actually learns from connectivity, or whether it even needs to. We propose addressing this using graph invariants, i.e., permutation-invariant, task-agnostic structural descriptors that serve as a diagnostic framework for graph benchmarks. We show that (i) invariants are more expressive than standard GNNs, (ii) invariants characterize structural heterogeneity within and across benchmark datasets, (iii) invariants predict multi-task performance, and (iv) simple invariant-based models are competitive with, and sometimes exceed, transformer and message-passing baselines across 26 datasets. Our results suggest that expressivity is not the main driver of predictive performance, and that on tasks where structure matters, a non-trainable structural proxy often matches trained message-passing models. We thus posit that invariant baselines should become a standard for evaluating whether structure is required for a task and whether a model picks up on it, serving as a stepping stone towards graph foundation models.

</details>


### [326] [Diversity Curves for Graph Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06466)
*Katharina Limbeck,Nadja Häusermann,Martin Carrasco,Guy Wolf,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes diversity curves, size-aware graph embeddings built via edge contraction coarsening, capturing metric geometry with a novel spread invariant for unsupervised graph analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph comparisons across different cardinalities are challenging; there is a need for interpretable, scalable representations for unsupervised tasks.

Method: Compute coarsening hierarchies via edge contraction; define 'spread' as an isometry-invariant measure of geometric diversity; track diversity across coarsening levels to form diversity curves; demonstrate expressivity gains over structural descriptors.

Result: Diversity curves enable clustering/visualization across sizes, distinguish geometry of single-cell graphs, compare molecular dataset structures, and characterize geometric shapes.

Conclusion: Diversity curves provide interpretable, efficient, and directly comparable size-aware graph representations suitable for diverse unsupervised tasks.

Abstract: Graph-level representations are crucial tools for characterising structural differences between graphs. However, comparing graphs with different cardinalities, even when sampled from the same underlying distribution, remains challenging. Unsupervised tasks in particular require interpretable, scalable, and reliable size-aware graph representations. Our work addresses these issues by tracking the structural diversity of a graph across coarsening levels. The resulting graph embeddings, which we denote diversity curves, are interpretable by construction, efficient, and directly comparable across coarsening hierarchies. Specifically, we track the spread of graphs, a novel isometry invariant that is inherently well-suited for encoding the metric diversity and geometry of graphs. We utilise edge contraction coarsening and prove that this improves expressivity, thus leading to more powerful graph-level representations than structural descriptors alone. Demonstrating their utility over a range of baseline methods in practice, we use diversity curves to (i) cluster and visualise simulated graphs across varying sizes, (ii) distinguish the geometry of single-cell graphs, (iii) compare the structure of molecular graph datasets, and (iv) characterise geometric shapes.

</details>


### [327] [No Triangulation Without Representation: Generalization in Topological Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06467)
*Johannes S. Schmidt,Martin Carrasco,Ernst Röell,Guy Wolf,Nello Blaser,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends the MANTRA benchmark to a broader set of manifolds, showing GNNs and HOMP can saturate the benchmark with appropriate representations; introduces a representation- and refinement-aware evaluation protocol; finds no evidence of generalization beyond combinatorial structure; highlights the need for topology-aware inductive biases and scale-invariant understanding of topology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of consensus on evaluating topology-focused deep learning models and the risk that current benchmarks rely on combinatorial structure rather than true topological understanding; extend benchmarks to diverse homeomorphism types to probe model capabilities.

Method: Extend MANTRA to a larger class of manifolds with diverse homeomorphism types; evaluate GNNs and higher-order message passing (HOMP) methods under varied representations and feature assignments; propose an evaluation protocol based on representational diversity and triangulation refinement.

Result: Both GNNs and HOMP can saturate the benchmark when provided with suitable representations and features; performance is contingent on representation choices; no evidence that models generalize beyond the combinatorial data; triangulation refinement offers a new dimension for evaluation.

Conclusion: A robust scaffolding is provided to evaluate future topology-aware models, emphasizing representational choices and triangulation refinement; underscores a gap in developing models that understand topological structure independently of scale and motivates topology-aware inductive biases.

Abstract: Despite an ever-increasing interest in topological deep learning models that target higher-order datasets, there is no consensus on how to evaluate such models. This is exacerbated by the fact that topological objects permit operations, such as structural refinements, that are not appropriate for graph data. In this work, we extend MANTRA, a benchmark dataset containing manifold triangulations, to a larger class of manifolds with more diverse homeomorphism types. We show that, unlike prior claims, both graph neural networks (GNNs) and higher-order message passing (HOMP) methods can saturate the benchmark. However, we find that this is contingent on the right representation and feature assignment, emphasizing their importance in baseline models. We thus provide a novel evaluation protocol based on representational diversity and triangulation refinement. Surprisingly, we find no indication that existing models are capable of generalizing beyond the combinatorial structure of the data. This points towards a research gap in developing models that understand topological structure independent of scale. Our work thus provides the necessary scaffolding to evaluate future models and enable the development of topology-aware inductive biases.

</details>


### [328] [Hitting Time Isomorphism for Multi-Stage Planning with Foundation Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06470)
*Magnus Victor Boock,Abdullah Akgül,Mustafa Mert Çelikok,Melih Kandemir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Isomorphic Embedding Learning (IEL) introduces an operator-theoretic, Hilbert-space embedding that encodes directed temporal geometry of controlled Markov processes via hitting-time functionals, with identifiability results, finite-sample guarantees, and a practical offline-RL foundation policy learner for long-horizon planning in maze tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional distance notions in representation learning often yield symmetric, triangle-inequality-lacking metrics that fail to capture decision-time progress in directed trajectories. There is a need for an asymmetric, compositional geometry that reflects hitting times and supports robust, long-horizon planning in offline RL.

Method: Develop a Hilbert-space displacement framework where expected hitting times are linear functionals of latent displacements. Prove existence under latent linear closure and unique identifiability up to a bounded linear isomorphism. In finite dimensions, bound global hitting-time error by one-step transition error times the environment's transient spectral radius. Provide finite-sample guarantees incorporating approximation, statistical complexity, and trajectory-label mismatch. Derive Isomorphic Embedding Learning (IEL), a goal-agnostic foundation-policy learner that combines a HILP-style consistency objective with explicit hitting-time regression to align learned geometry with decision-time progress.

Result: The theory yields an identifiable, directed temporal geometry; IEL offers a practical algorithm for offline RL and long-horizon planning. Empirical results on offline maze locomotion data show IEL improves state-of-the-art foundation-policy learning. The authors release code at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: IEL furnishes an asymmetric, compositional geometry for controlled processes, enabling robust graph-based multi-stage planning and improved foundation-policy learning in offline RL. It provides theoretical guarantees and a scalable, goal-agnostic framework, with an open-source implementation.

Abstract: We present a new operator-theoretic representation learning framework for offline reinforcement learning that recovers the directed temporal geometry of a controlled Markov process from hitting time observations. While prior art often produces symmetric distances or fails to satisfy the triangle inequality, our framework learns a Hilbert-space displacement geometry where expected hitting times are realized as linear functionals of latent displacements. We prove that this representation exists under latent linear closure and is uniquely identifiable up to a bounded linear isomorphism. For finite-dimensional implementations, we show that global hitting-time error is bounded by one-step transition error amplified by the environment's transient spectral radius. Furthermore, we provide finite-sample guarantees accounting for approximation, statistical complexity, and trajectory-label mismatch. Derived from this theory, we curate Isomorphic Embedding Learning (IEL) as a new goal-agnostic foundation policy learning algorithm that anchors a HILP-style consistency objective with explicit hitting-time regression to ensure that the learned geometry reflects actual decision-time progress. This asymmetric and compositional structure enables robust graph-based multi-stage planning for long-horizon navigation. Our experiments demonstrate that IEL improves the state of the art of learning foundation policy policies from offline maze locomotion data. Our code can be found on https://github.com/MagnusBoock/IEL

</details>


### [329] [Efficient Serving for Dynamic Agent Workflows with Prediction-based KV-Cache Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06472)
*Haoyu Zheng,Fangcheng Fu,Jia Wu,Binhang Yuan,Yongqiang Zhang,Hao Wang,Yuanyuan Zhu,Xiao Yan,Jiawei Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PBKV predicts future agent invocations in dynamic LLM-based workflows to manage KV-Cache efficiently, combining historical guidance with current context to select high-potential cache entries; robust eviction/prefetching, yielding notable speedups over LRU and KVFlow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing cache strategies either operate at the agent level and miss intra-workflow reuse opportunities, or assume static, fixed agent sequences in workflows. Dynamic, context-dependent task flows can reveal untapped KV-Cache reuse opportunities, but are hard to exploit efficiently.

Method: PBKV predicts upcoming agent invocations for several future steps by fusing historical workflow data with current workflow context. It estimates the reuse potential of cache entries and retains high-potential ones in GPU memory. The system uses conservative strategies for eviction and prefetching to mitigate prediction errors, aiming to be robust.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three workflow benchmarks shows PBKV achieving up to 1.85× speedup over LRU on dynamic workflows and up to 1.26× speedup over the state-of-the-art KVFlow on static workflows.
