Conclusion: PBKV provides a prediction-based cache management framework that improves efficiency for both dynamic and static LLM-based workflows by exploiting cross-step KV-Cache reuse under uncertainty.

Abstract: LLM-based workflows compose specialized agents to execute complex tasks, and these agents usually share substantial context, allowing KV-Cache reuse to save computation. Existing approaches either manage KV-Cache at agent level and fail to exploit the reuse opportunities within workflows, or manage cache at the workflow level but assume that each workflow calls a static sequence of agents. However, practical workflows are typically dynamic, where the sequence of invoked agents and thus induced cache reuse opportunities depend on the context of each task. To serve such dynamic workflows efficiently, we build a system dubbed PBKV (\textbf{P}rediction-\textbf{B}ased \textbf{KV}-Cache Management). For each workflow, PBKV predicts the agent invocations in several future steps by fusing the guidance from historical workflows and context of the target workflow. Based on the predictions, PBKV estimates the reuse potential of cache entries and keeps the high-potential entries in GPU memory. To be robust to prediction errors, PBKV utilizes the predictions conservatively during both cache eviction and prefetching. Experiments on three workflow benchmarks show that PBKV achieves up to $1.85\times$ speedup over LRU on dynamic workflows, and up to $1.26\times$ speedup over the SOTA baseline KVFlow on the static workflow.

</details>


### [330] [Q-MMR: Off-Policy Evaluation via Recursive Reweighting and Moment Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06474)
*Xiang Li,Nan Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Q-MMR: a data-driven weight-based off-policy evaluation method for finite-horizon MDPs with dimension-free, realizability-based guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address off-policy evaluation in finite-horizon MDPs using a principled moment-matching objective to reweight data and approximate the target-return; provide finite-sample guarantees under realizability of Q^c0.

Method: Learn per-data-point weights via a moment-matching objective against a value-function discriminator class; top-down inductive learning; connects to importance sampling and linear FQE; yields a dimension-free finite-sample bound, independent of the function class complexity; includes analysis of coverage in offline RL.

Result: Introduces Q-MMR with a data-dependent, dimension-free finite-sample guarantee under realizability; links to existing methods (IS, linear FQE); provides theoretical insights into coverage in offline RL.

Conclusion: Presents a theoretically grounded framework for off-policy evaluation in finite-horizon MDPs with strong guarantees under realizability; clarifies connections to established methods and implications for coverage, with avenues for practical application and future work.

Abstract: We present a novel theoretical framework, Q-MMR, for off-policy evaluation in finite-horizon MDPs. Q-MMR learns a set of scalar weights, one for each data point, such that the reweighted rewards approximate the expected return under the target policy. The weights are learned inductively in a top-down manner via a moment matching objective against a value-function discriminator class. Notably, and perhaps surprisingly, a data-dependent finite-sample guarantee for general function approximation can be established under only the realizability of $Q^π$, with a dimension-free bound -- that is, the error does not depend on the statistical complexity of the function class. We also establish connections to several existing methods, such as importance sampling and linear FQE. Further theoretical analyses shed new light on the nature of coverage, a concept of fundamental importance to offline RL.

</details>


### [331] [Operator-Guided Invariance Learning for Continuous Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06500)
*Zuyuan Zhang,Fei Xu Yu,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes VPSD-RL, a framework to discover value-preserving structures in continuous-time RL using Lie-group actions and pullback/pushforward operators; exact structures occur when pullback of value function and pushforward of actions commute with the controlled generator and reward; approximate structures exist when HJB mismatch is small; learns infinitesimal generators, builds finite transformations via ODE flows, and uses augmentation and regularization to improve data efficiency and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning in continuous time/space is data-intensive and brittle to nuisance variability and distribution shift. Existing methods rely on fixed symmetries or exact equivariance; thus there is a need to discover more general, nonlinear, value-preserving structures to stabilize learning.

Method: Model continuous RL as a controlled diffusion. Define value-preserving mappings via Lie-group actions and pullback operators. Existence of exact structures is tied to commutation: pulling back the value function and pushing forward actions must commute with the controlled generator and reward. Approximate structures are found by minimizing the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman mismatch. Learn infinitesimal generators by solving determining equations residuals, exponentiate via ODE flows to obtain finite transformations, apply as transition augmentation and transform-consistency regularization in RL.

Result: Theoretical result: bounded generator/reward mismatch yields quantitative stability of the optimal value function along approximate orbits, with sensitivity governed by an effective horizon. Empirically, the method improves data efficiency and robustness on continuous-control benchmarks.

Conclusion: VPSD-RL provides a general framework to discover exact or approximate value-preserving structures in continuous RL. By leveraging Lie-group operators and diffusion models, it enables transfer of value functions across transformed states/actions and offers theoretical guarantees tied to mismatch and horizon, with practical gains in data efficiency and robustness.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) with continuous time and state/action spaces is often data-intensive and brittle under nuisance variability and shift, motivating methods that exploit value-preserving structures to stabilize and improve learning. Most existing approaches focus on special cases, such as prescribed symmetries and exact equivariance, without addressing how to discover more general structures that require nonlinear operators to transform and map between continuous state/action systems with isomorphic value functions. We propose \textbf{VPSD-RL} (Value-Preserving Structure Discovery for Reinforcement Learning). It models continuous RL as a controlled diffusion with value-preserving mappings defined through Lie-group actions and associated pullback operators. We show that a value-preserving structure exists exactly when pulling back the value function and pushing forward actions commute with the controlled generator and reward functional. Further, approximate value-preserving structures with rigorous guarantees can be found when the Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman mismatch is small. This framework discovers exact and approximate value-preserving structures by searching for the associated Lie group operators. VPSD-RL fits differentiable drift, diffusion, and reward models; learns infinitesimal generators via determining-equation residual minimization; exponentiates them with ODE flows to obtain finite transformations; and integrates them into continuous RL through transition augmentation and transformation-consistency regularization. We show that bounded generator/reward mismatch implies quantitative stability of the optimal value function along approximate orbits, with sensitivity governed by the effective horizon, and observe improved data efficiency and robustness on continuous-control benchmarks.

</details>


### [332] [Cubit: Token Mixer with Kernel Ridge Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06501)
*Chuanyang Zheng,Jiankai Sun,Yihang Gao,Yuehao Wang,Liangchen Tan,Mac Schwager,Anderson Schneider,Yuriy Nevmyvaka,Xiaodong Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Interprets Transformer attention as Nadaraya-Watson regression and introduces Cubit, a Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR)–based architecture, with Limited-Range Rescale (LRR) for stability; claims stronger mathematical foundation and improved long-sequence modeling, validated by experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the core token-mixing mechanism in Transformers by providing a stronger mathematical grounding than attention alone and exploring a potential next-generation architecture that can handle long sequences better.

Method: Recast attention as Nadaraya-Watson regression and propose Cubit that uses the closed-form solution of Kernel Ridge Regression to compute value aggregation with kernel-based normalization. Introduce Limited-Range Rescale (LRR) to stabilize training.

Result: Experiments indicate Cubit achieves superior performance to the Transformer, with the performance gap enlarging for longer training sequences, suggesting improved long-sequence modeling capability.

Conclusion: Cubit offers a more solid mathematical foundation than the vanilla Transformer and may serve as a promising next-generation architecture, particularly for long-sequence tasks.

Abstract: Since its introduction in 2017, the Transformer has become one of the most widely adopted architectures in modern deep learning. Despite extensive efforts to improve positional encoding, attention mechanisms, and feed-forward networks, the core token-mixing mechanism in Transformers remains attention. In this work, we show that the attention module in Transformers can be interpreted as performing Nadaraya-Watson regression, where it computes similarities between tokens and aggregates the corresponding values accordingly. Motivated by this perspective, we propose Cubit, a potential next-generation architecture that leverages Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR), while the vanilla Transformer relies on Nadaraya-Watson regression. Specifically, Cubit modifies the classical attention computation by incorporating the closed-form solution of KRR, combining value aggregation through kernel similarities with normalization via the inverse of the kernel matrix. To improve the training stability, we further propose the Limited-Range Rescale (LRR), which rescales the value layer within a controlled range. We argue that Cubit, as a KRR-based architecture, provides a stronger mathematical foundation than the vanilla Transformer, whose attention mechanism corresponds to Nadaraya-Watson regression. We validate this claim through comprehensive experiments. The experimental results suggest that Cubit may exhibit stronger long-sequence modeling capability. In particular, its performance gain over the Transformer appears to increase as the training sequence length grows.

</details>


### [333] [PACZero: PAC-Private Fine-Tuning of Language Models via Sign Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06505)
*Murat Bilgehan Ertan,Xiaochen Zhu,Phuong Ha Nguyen,Marten van Dijk,Srinivas Devadas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PACZero provides PAC-private zeroth-order updates for fine-tuning LLMs with mutual-information leakage set to zero on certain unanimity steps, achieving near non-private performance even at zero-information release; two variants offer MI-bounded and zero-information options.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable usable private fine-tuning of large language models under a privacy notion stronger and more practically meaningful than conventional DP, by controlling information leakage via mutual information rather than a single epsilon bound.

Method: Sign-quantized subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients are released; unanimity steps where all candidate subset updates agree incur zero conditional mutual information. Two variants: PACZero-MI (calibrated MI budget on binary release) and PACZero-ZPL (I=0 via a coin flip on disagreement steps). Evaluated on SST-2 and SQuAD with OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B, in LoRA and full-parameter settings.

Result: On SST-2 with OPT-1.3B in full fine-tuning at I=0, PACZero-ZPL attains 88.99±0.91, within 2.1 percentage points of the non-private MeZO baseline (91.1). In the high-privacy regime ε<1, no prior method yields usable utility; PACZero-ZPL yields competitive SST-2 and nontrivial SQuAD F1 across OPT sizes.

Conclusion: PACZero demonstrates practical utility under PAC privacy for zeroth-order fine-tuning of LLMs, achieving near-private performance and extending the privacy-utility frontier. The two variants provide flexible trade-offs between MI budget and zero-information leakage.

Abstract: We introduce PACZero, a family of PAC-private zeroth-order mechanisms for fine-tuning large language models that delivers usable utility at $I(S^*; Y_{1:T})=0$. This privacy regime bounds the membership-inference attack (MIA) posterior success rate at the prior, an MIA-resistance level the DP framework matches only at $\varepsilon=0$ and infinite noise. All DP-ZO comparisons below are matched at the MIA posterior level. The key insight is that PAC Privacy charges mutual information only when the release depends on which candidate subset is the secret. Sign-quantizing subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients creates frequent unanimity, steps at which every candidate subset agrees on the update direction; at these steps the released sign costs zero conditional mutual information. We propose two variants that span the privacy-utility trade-off: PACZero-MI (budgeted MI via exact calibration on the binary release) and PACZero-ZPL ($I=0$ via a uniform coin flip on disagreement steps). We evaluate on SST-2 and SQuAD with OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B in both LoRA and full-parameter tracks. On SST-2 OPT-1.3B full fine-tuning at $I=0$, PACZero-ZPL reaches ${88.99\pm0.91}$, within $2.1$pp of the non-private MeZO baseline ($91.1$ FT). No prior method produces usable utility in the high-privacy regime $\varepsilon<1$, and PACZero-ZPL obtains competitive SST-2 accuracy and nontrivial SQuAD F1 across OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B at $I=0$.

</details>


### [334] [Is One Layer Enough? Understanding Inference Dynamics in Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06510)
*Amir Rezaei Balef,Mykhailo Koshil,Katharina Eggensperger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Six state-of-the-art transformer-based tabular models exhibit deep layerwise redundancy; predictions emerge in stages; a looped single-layer model with ~20% of parameters matches performance, highlighting potential for efficient inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to elucidate the internal mechanism of TFMs on tabular tasks and understand how depth contributes to inference.

Method: perform a large-scale mechanistic study across six tabular in-context learning models, analyzing layerwise dynamics, inference stages, and latent-space evolution; then design a minimal looped single-layer model.

Result: revealed substantial depthwise redundancy and iterative refinement with overlapping computations; the 20%-parameter single-layer looped model achieved comparable performance to the larger models.

Conclusion: deep transformers for tabular data may be over-parameterized; there is potential for efficient, depth-reduced architectures; code available.

Abstract: Transformer-based tabular foundation models (TFMs) dominate small to medium tabular predictive benchmark tasks, yet their inference mechanisms remain largely unexplored. We present the first large-scale mechanistic study of layerwise dynamics in 6 state-of-the-art tabular in-context learning models. We explore how predictions emerge across depth, identify distinct stages of inference and reveal latent-space dynamics that differ from those of language models. Our findings indicate substantial depthwise redundancy across multiple models, suggesting iterative refinement with overlapping computations during inference stages. Guided by these insights, we design a proof-of-concept, looped single-layer model that uses only 20% of the original model's parameters while achieving comparable performance. The code is available at https://github.com/amirbalef/is_one_layer_enough.

</details>


### [335] [Efficient Techniques for Data Reconstruction, with Finite-Width Recovery Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06519)
*Edward Tansley,Roy Makhlouf,Estelle Massart,Coralia Cartis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified optimization framework for data reconstruction in trained neural networks is proposed, with finite-width recovery guarantees in the random feature regime and subspace-aware methods that relax width constraints; an efficient practical algorithm leverages early-layer weight changes to identify a low-dimensional data subspace and reconstructs from the last layer, yielding better performance than full-space techniques on synthetic data and CIFAR-10.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data reconstruction attacks threaten privacy by recovering training data. The work unifies competing proposals into a single optimization formulation, provides finite-width recovery guarantees, and tackles practical reconstruction under low-dimensional subspaces.

Method: Formulates data reconstruction as a unified optimization problem using both initial and trained parameters. Theoretical analysis in the random feature model yields high-probability reconstruction with sufficiently large network width via PAC-style bounds. When data lies in a low-dimensional subspace, width requirements are reduced, with bounds tied to subspace dimension. For general networks, an efficient algorithm approximates the data subspace by tracking changes in the first-layer weights during training and uses only last-layer weights for reconstruction, thereby shrinking the search space.

Result: In the random feature model, the formulation provably enables training data reconstruction with high probability for sufficiently wide networks. Subspace structure relaxes width requirements, with bounds depending on subspace dimension. The proposed subspace-aware algorithm reduces search space and network width needed for high-quality reconstructions. Empirical results on synthetic data and CIFAR-10 show the subspace-aware method outperforms standard full-space techniques.

Conclusion: The work bridges theory and practice by establishing finite-width reconstruction prospects and delivering a practical, subspace-aware algorithm that improves reconstruction quality, as validated by experiments on synthetic data and CIFAR-10.

Abstract: Data reconstruction attacks on trained neural networks aim to recover the data on which the network has been trained and pose a significant threat to privacy, especially if the training dataset contains sensitive information. Here, we propose a unified optimization formulation of the data reconstruction problem based on initial and trained parameter values, incorporating state-of-the-art proposals. We show that in the random feature model, this formulation provably leads to training data reconstruction with high probability, provided the network width is sufficiently large; this unprecedented finite-width result uses PAC-style bounds. Furthermore, when the data lies in a low-dimensional subspace, we show that the network width requirement for successful reconstruction can be relaxed, with bounds depending on the subspace dimension rather than the ambient dimension. For general neural network models and unknown data orientations, we propose an efficient reconstruction algorithm that approximates the low-dimensional data subspace through the change in the first-layer weights during training and uses only the last-layer weights for reconstruction, thus reducing the search space dimension and the required network width for high-quality reconstructions. Our numerical experiments on synthetic datasets and CIFAR-10 confirm that our subspace-aware reconstruction approach outperforms standard full-space techniques.

</details>


### [336] [On the Implicit Reward Overfitting and the Low-rank Dynamics in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06523)
*Hao Ye,Jisheng Dang,Junfeng Fang,Bimei Wang,Yizhou Zhang,Ning Lv,Wencan Zhang,Hong Peng,Bin Hu,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR gains are concentrated in rank-1 components; periodic rank-1 substitution reveals implicit reward overfitting to training data; RLVR optimizes a specific singular spectrum with heavy-tailed layer spectra; left singular vectors of rank-1 components align more during training, indicating optimized sampling efficiency. These findings illuminate how RLVR shapes parameters and suggest spectral-aware approaches for continual learning and RL improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover the spectral and representational mechanisms by which RLVR enhances reasoning, particularly the role of rank-1 components, reward dynamics, and singular-spectrum structure, and to assess implications for robustness and continual learning.

Method: Apply Periodic Rank-1 Substitution to isolate and analyze rank-1 components; examine the singular value spectrum and left singular vectors across almost all linear layers in RLVR-trained models; correlate training rewards with test performance; characterize the dynamics of alignment and spectrum distribution (heavy-tailed behavior).

Result: Rank-1 components predominantly encode mathematical reasoning while other knowledge remains bounded in those components; RLVR appears to optimize a specific singular spectrum; the majority of linear layers exhibit heavy-tailed singular-value distributions; left singular vectors associated with rank-1 components show stronger alignment during training, consistent with sampling-efficiency optimization; test performance can be high even when training rewards are relatively low, indicating implicit reward overfitting.

Conclusion: RLVR shapes model parameters through targeted spectral optimization and alignment of rank-1 components, offering insights for improving RL and continual-learning paradigms by leveraging spectral properties and component-wise analysis.

Abstract: Recent extensive research has demonstrated that the enhanced reasoning capabilities acquired by models through Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) are primarily concentrated within the rank-1 components. Predicated on this observation, we employed Periodic Rank-1 Substitution and identified a counterintuitive phenomenon: RLVR may exhibit implicit reward overfitting to the training dataset. Specifically, the model can achieve satisfactory performance on the test set even when its rewards remain relatively low during the training process. Furthermore, we characterize three distinct properties of RL training: (1) The effective rank-1 component in RLVR don't maintain other model knowledge except mathematical reasoning capability. (2) RLVR fundamentally functions by optimizing a specific singular spectrum. The distribution of singular values of almost all linear layers in RLVR-trained model behaves like heavy-tailed distribution. (3) the left singular vectors associated with rank-1 components demonstrate a stronger alignment tendency during training, which echoes the discovery that RLVR is optimizing sampling efficiency in essence. Taken together, our findings and analysis further reveal how RLVR shapes model parameters and offer potential insights for improving existing RL paradigms or other training paradigms to implement continual learning.

</details>


### [337] [Diffusion-Based Posterior Sampling: A Feynman-Kac Analysis of Bias and Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06538)
*Matias G. Delgadino,Sebastien Motsch,Advait Parulekar,William Porteous,Sanjay Shakkottai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes bias in diffusion-based posterior samplers (DPS, STSL) and provides a principled PDE- and Feynman-Kac-based framework to quantify Radon-Nikodym corrections, revealing when samplers oversample or undersample; it shows how STSL and early stopping mitigate instabilities and guides stable designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite practical success, the theoretical behavior of diffusion priors in inverse problems is poorly understood: outputs are biased even with exact priors, and low-temperature discretizations can be unstable. A tractable characterization is needed to diagnose bias and guide corrections.

Method: Introduce a surrogate path linking the true posterior to a standard Gaussian; derive a parabolic PDE for the density ratio with a reaction term encoding accumulated bias; use a Feynman-Kac representation to express the Radon-Nikodym correction as an explicit path expectation; apply to DPS and STSL; derive an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck path expectation that couples data covariance with reward curvature; reinterpret STSL as an auxiliary drift that concentrates trajectories into low-uncertainty regions; analyze how early guidance-stopping mitigates forward-Euler instability.

Result: The framework identifies where DPS under- or over-samples the posterior; quantifies the influence of data-conditioned covariance and reward curvature on sampling bias; STSL is shown to flatten parts of the DPS reaction term, reducing bias; early stopping is characterized as a practical mitigation for numerical instabilities; the work provides actionable understanding of existing corrections and how to design more stable samplers.

Conclusion: A unified PDE/ stochastic- calculus view clarifies sampler bias sources, explains existing corrective strategies, and guides the design of stable diffusion-based posterior samplers (for DPS, STSL) with concrete guidance on when and how to apply corrections such as Radon-Nikodym terms and early stopping.

Abstract: Diffusion-based posterior samplers use pretrained diffusion priors to sample from measurement- or reward-conditioned posteriors, and are widely used for inverse problems. Yet their theoretical behavior remains poorly understood: even with exact prior scores, their outputs are biased, and in low-temperature regimes their discretizations can become unstable. We characterize this bias by introducing a tractable surrogate path connecting the true posterior to a standard Gaussian and comparing it to the sampler's path. Their density ratio satisfies a parabolic PDE whose reaction term measures the accumulated bias. A Feynman-Kac representation then expresses the Radon-Nikodym correction as an explicit path expectation, identifying which posterior regions are over- or under-sampled.
  We apply this framework to DPS and STSL, a related sampler. For DPS, the correction is an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck path expectation coupling the data conditional covariance with the reward curvature, revealing where DPS over- or under-samples. Next, we reinterpret STSL as an auxiliary drift that steers trajectories toward low-uncertainty regions, flattening the spatially varying part of the DPS reaction term. Finally, we characterize early guidance-stopping, a common mitigation for low-temperature instabilities caused by forward-Euler integration of the vector field. Together, these results clarify sampler bias, explain existing correctives, and guide stable variant designs.

</details>


### [338] [Hedging Memory Horizons for Non-Stationary Prediction via Online Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06541)
*Yutong Wang,Yannig Goude,Qiwei Yao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MELO is a model-agnostic online prediction method for distribution-shift scenarios that hedges across adaptation scales using EWLS with multiple forgetting factors and aggregates raw predictions with MLpol. It achieves strong theoretical guarantees and improved RMSE on COVID-period electricity-load data with lightweight, no-retrain updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In sequential prediction, inputs arrive over time and regime shifts can occur unpredictably; the memory length for adaptation is unknown and preserving stability in quiet periods while adapting during shifts is essential.

Method: MELO wraps a pool of non-anticipating base predictors with EWLS adaptation experts at multiple forgetting factors, and then aggregates raw and adapted forecasts using MLpol, a parameter-free online aggregation rule; the approach is model-agnostic and requires no regime indicators.

Result: Under boundedness, deterministic oracle inequalities show competitiveness with the best raw predictor and with the best time-varying affine combination of base predictors, up to a path-length dependent tracking cost and sublinear aggregation overhead. Empirically, MELO reduces RMSE by 34.7% vs base-only MLpol on French national electricity-load forecasting during the COVID lockdown and outperforms a TabICL reference with external covariates; updates are lightweight and do not require model retraining.

Conclusion: MELO provides robust, model-agnostic online adaptation to distribution shift with theoretical guarantees and practical efficacy, delivering significant RMSE improvements while maintaining low per-step complexity.

Abstract: We study online prediction under distribution shift, where inputs arrive chronologically and outcomes are revealed only after prediction. In this setting, predictors must remain stable in quiet regimes yet adapt when regimes shift, and the right adaptation memory is unknown in advance. We propose MELO (Memory-hedged Exponentially Weighted Least-Squares Online aggregation), a model-agnostic method that hedges across adaptation scales: it wraps any non-anticipating base-predictor pool with exponentially weighted least-squares (EWLS) adaptation experts at multiple forgetting factors, and aggregates raw and EWLS-adapted forecasts with MLpol, a parameter-free online aggregation rule. Under boundedness conditions, we establish deterministic oracle inequalities showing that it competes with both the best raw predictor and the best bounded, time-varying affine combinations of the base predictions, up to a path-length-dependent tracking cost and a sublinear aggregation overhead. We evaluate MELO on French national electricity-load forecasting through the COVID-19 lockdown using no regime indicators, lockdown dates, or policy covariates. MELO reduces overall RMSE by 34.7\% relative to base-only MLpol and achieves lower overall RMSE than a TabICL reference supplied with an external COVID policy-response covariate. Moreover, MELO requires only lightweight per-step recursive updates without model retraining.

</details>


### [339] [Sequential Design of Genetic Circuits Under Uncertainty With Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06552)
*Michal Kobiela,Diego A. Oyarzún,Michael U. Gutmann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Amortized RL-based framework for optimizing genetic circuits under intrinsic noise and cross-lab variability; uses simulators (ODEs/Markov) and policy-based RL to suggest experiments without per-round inference, enabling immediate adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty from stochastic biomolecular reactions and lab-to-lab variability hampers genetic circuit design; need efficient, adaptive optimization that avoids repeated inference.


Method: Train a policy-based RL agent across a distribution of uncertain parameters (amortized) using simulator models (ODEs or Markov jump processes). The agent proposes experiments adaptively based on observations, eliminating explicit parameter inference during the design cycle.

Result: Demonstrates on heterologous gene expression and a repressilator that the framework efficiently handles molecular noise and cross-lab variability, enabling faster, observation-driven design decisions.

Conclusion: An amortized, RL-driven design framework reduces computational bottlenecks of Bayesian iterative methods and provides immediate adaptation under stochasticity and inter-lab variability in genetic circuit design.

Abstract: The design of biological systems is hindered by uncertainty arising from both intrinsic stochasticity of biomolecular reactions and variability across laboratory or experimental conditions. In this work, we present a sequential framework to optimize genetic circuits under both forms of uncertainty. By employing simulator models based on differential equations or Markov jump processes alongside a reinforcement learning (RL) policy-based approach, our method suggests experiments that adapt to unknown laboratory conditions while accounting for inherent stochasticity. While previous Bayesian methods address uncertainty through iterative experiment-inference-optimization cycles, they typically require computationally expensive inference and optimization steps after each experimental round, leading to delays. To overcome this bottleneck, we propose an amortized approach trained up-front across a distribution of possible uncertain parameters. This strategy sidesteps the need for explicit parameter inference during the design cycle, enabling immediate, observation-based adaptation. We demonstrate our framework on models for heterologous gene expression and a repressilator circuit, showing that it efficiently handles both molecular noise and cross-laboratory variability.

</details>


### [340] [Diverse Sampling in Diffusion Models with Marginal Preserving Particle Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06553)
*Gal Vinograd,Idan Achituve,Ethan Fetaya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EDDY: Exact-marginal Diversification via Divergence-free dynamics


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Promote sample diversity in diffusion/flow models without sacrificing marginal fidelity; address limited diversity in generated samples.

Method: Drift perturbations arranged as kernel-based anti-symmetric pairwise fields (divergence-free) that reconfigure particle trajectories while preserving evolving marginals; constructed from repulsive directions; no additional training; practical approximations for efficiency.

Result: In experiments with synthetic distributions and text-to-image generation, EDDY achieves higher diversity with strong distributional fidelity compared to common baselines.

Conclusion: Divergence-free, exact-marginal perturbations enable diversity without altering marginals; scalable via practical approximations; applicable to diffusion and flow-matching models.

Abstract: We present EDDY (Exact-marginal Diversification via Divergence-free dYnamics), a guidance mechanism for diffusion and flow matching models that promotes diversity among samples generated while maintaining quality. EDDY exploits symmetries of the Fokker-Planck equation, using drift perturbations that change particle trajectories while preserving the evolving marginal distribution. We instantiate this principle through kernel-based anti-symmetric pairwise matrix fields, constructed from the repulsive directions. The resulting divergence-free dynamics promote diversity at the joint particle level while preserving each particle's marginal distribution without any additional training. As computing the guidance can be computationally expensive in cases such as text-to-image generation with perceptual embeddings, we propose practical approximations as an effective and efficient solution. Experiments on synthetic distributions and text-to-image generation show that EDDY improves diversity while maintaining strong distributional fidelity compared to common baselines.

</details>


### [341] [Optimal Counterfactual Search in Tree Ensembles: A Study Across Modeling and Solution Paradigms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06561)
*Awa Khouna,Youssouf Emine,Julien Ferry,Thibaut Vidal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CPCF uses constraint programming to compute optimal counterfactual explanations for tree ensembles under plausibility and actionability constraints, showing CP generally outperforms MaxSAT and MILP across datasets, with regime-specific strengths for each paradigm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Trust in counterfactual explanations requires truly minimal and feasible recourses; suboptimal or unevenly distributed alternatives undermine their utility. The work investigates optimal counterfactuals for tree ensembles under practical constraints.

Method: Introduce CPCF: a CP formulation encoding numerical features as interval domains defined by split thresholds and discrete features as finite domains; supports multiple distance objectives. Compare CP with a MaxSAT extension for soft-voting ensembles and with MILP on three ensemble types across ten datasets, evaluating scalability, anytime performance, and distance metrics.

Result: CP achieved best overall performance across datasets and ensemble types. MaxSAT excels for hard-voting ensembles; MILP remains competitive for amortized inference with a moderate number of splits. The study delineates regimes where each paradigm is advantageous.

Conclusion: Constraint programming is a versatile and effective framework for producing optimal, plausible, and actionable counterfactual explanations for tree ensembles, with a nuanced landscape of when alternative paradigms may outperform. 

Abstract: Trust in counterfactual explanations depends critically on whether their recommended changes are truly minimal: suboptimal explanations may vastly overshoot the actual changes needed to alter a decision, and heuristic errors can affect individuals unevenly, giving some users relevant recourse while assigning others unnecessarily costly recommendations. Consequently, we study the problem of computing optimal counterfactual explanations for tree ensembles under plausibility and actionability constraints. This is a combinatorial problem: for a fixed model, counterfactual search boils down to selecting consistent branching decisions and threshold-defined regions under a distance objective. We exploit this structure through CPCF, a constraint programming (CP) formulation in which numerical features are encoded as interval domains induced by split thresholds, while discrete features retain native finite-domain representations. This yields a compact finite-domain formulation that supports multiple distance objectives without continuous split-boundary search. We then place CPCF in a broader comparison across mathematical programming paradigms: we extend a maximum Boolean satisfiability (MaxSAT) formulation, originally designed for hard-voting random forests, to soft-voting ensembles, and compare against the current state-of-the-art mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) optimal approach. Across ten datasets and three types of tree ensembles, we analyze scalability, anytime performance, and sensitivity to distance metrics. We observe that CP achieves the best overall performance. More importantly, our results identify regimes in which the specific strengths of each paradigm make it best suited: CP is most versatile overall, MaxSAT handles hard-voting ensembles particularly well, and MILP remains competitive in amortized inference settings with a moderate number of split levels.

</details>


### [342] [Feature Dimensionality Outweighs Model Complexity in Breast Cancer Subtype Classification Using TCGA-BRCA Gene Expression Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06562)
*Meena Al Hasani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Comparative analysis of logistic regression, random forest, and SVM for TCGA-BRCA gene expression-based breast cancer subtype classification across varying numbers of highly variable genes. All models reach high accuracy, but macro F1 reveals that logistic regression provides the most balanced, stable performance across subtypes (including rare classes), random forest underperforms on minority subtypes, and SVM performance is highly sensitive to feature dimensionality; highlights importance of model simplicity, proper evaluation metrics, and feature selection in high-dimensional data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenges of high dimensionality and limited samples in cancer subtype classification, and determine how model complexity and feature selection affect subtype-level performance.

Method: Train logistic regression, random forest, and SVM on TCGA-BRCA gene expression data using varying numbers of highly variable genes (50 to 20,518). Evaluate with stratified 5-fold cross-validation using accuracy and macro F1; analyze subtype-level performance and stability across feature counts.

Result: All models achieve high accuracy; macro F1 reveals differences in subtype-level performance. Logistic regression is the most stable and balanced across subtypes, including rare classes. Random forest underperforms on minority subtypes despite strong overall accuracy. SVM is sensitive to feature dimensionality.

Conclusion: Model simplicity, appropriate evaluation metrics (macro F1), and careful feature selection are crucial for reliable high-dimensional biological classification; LR offers robust subtype balance, RF may miss minority classes, and SVM requires careful dimensionality management.

Abstract: Accurate classification of breast cancer subtypes from gene expression data is critical for diagnosis and treatment selection. However, such datasets are characterized by high dimensionality and limited sample size, posing challenges for machine learning models.
  In this study, we evaluate the impact of model complexity and feature selection on subtype classification performance using TCGA-BRCA gene expression data. Logistic regression, random forest, and support vector machine (SVM) models were trained using varying numbers of highly variable genes (50 to 20,518). Performance was evaluated using stratified 5-fold cross-validation and assessed with accuracy and macro F1 score. While all models achieved high accuracy, macro F1 analysis revealed substantial differences in subtype-level performance. Logistic regression demonstrated the most stable and balanced performance across subtypes, including improved detection of rare classes. Random forest underperformed on minority subtypes despite strong overall accuracy, while SVM showed sensitivity to feature dimensionality. These findings highlight the importance of model simplicity, evaluation metrics, and feature selection in high-dimensional biological classification tasks.

</details>


### [343] [Criticality and Saturation in Orthogonal Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06563)
*Max Guillen,Jan E. Gerken*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Derives explicit layer-wise recursions for finite-width corrections in orthogonally initialized networks, extends diagrammatic methods to all orders in 1/width, and proves stability of finite-width tensors with depth, matching Monte Carlo estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why orthogonal initialization yields stable finite-width statistics and improved training, filling a gap in the literature and extending Day et al.'s empirical observations.

Method: Analytical derivation of layer-wise recursions for finite-width tensor statistics under orthogonal initialization; extension of Feynman-diagrammatic recursions to all orders in 1/width; analysis of fixed-point stability for activations; numerical validation by solving recursions and large-depth expansions and comparing with Monte Carlo ensembles.

Result: Explicit recursions reproduce the observed stability of finite-width tensors with depth; provide conditions for stability related to activation fixed points; strong agreement between numerical solution of recursions and Monte Carlo estimates.

Conclusion: Provides a theoretical explanation for why orthogonal initialization yields stable finite-width nonlinear networks, closing a long-standing gap; supported by numerical and analytical evidence.

Abstract: It has been known for a long time that initializing weight matrices to be orthogonal instead of having i.i.d. Gaussian components can improve training performance. This phenomenon can be analyzed using finite-width corrections, where the infinite-width statistics are supplemented by a power series in $1/\mathrm{width}$. In particular, recent empirical results by Day et al. show that the tensors appearing in this treatment stabilize for large depth, as opposed to the tensors of i.i.d.-initialized networks. In this article, we derive explicit layer-wise recursion relations for the tensors appearing in the finite-width expansion of the network statistics in the case of orthogonal initializations. We also provide an extension of recently-introduced Feynman diagrams for the corresponding recursions in the i.i.d.-case which are valid to all orders in $1/\mathrm{width}$. Finally, we show explicitly that the recursions we derive reproduce the stability of the finite-width tensors which was observed for activation functions with vanishing fixed point. This work therefore provides a theoretical explanation for the stability of nonlinear networks of finite width initialized with orthogonal weights, closing a long-standing gap in the literature. We validate our theoretical results experimentally by showing that numerical solutions of our recursion relations and their analytical large-depth expansions agree excellently with Monte-Carlo estimates from network ensembles.

</details>


### [344] [SNAPO: Smooth Neural Adjoint Policy Optimization for Optimal Control via Differentiable Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06570)
*Dmitri Goloubentsev,Natalija Karpichina*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SNAPO (Smooth Neural Adjoint Policy Optimization) embeds a neural policy inside a differentiable simulator, uses smooth constraint approximations, and leverages a single adjoint pass to obtain exact gradients for all policy parameters and inputs, enabling fast training and broad sensitivity analyses across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the trade-off between dynamic programming (exact but intractable for high dimensions) and black-box RL (scalable but slow and provides few sensitivities). Provide exact, comprehensive gradients efficiently.

Method: Integrate a neural policy into a known differentiable simulator, replace hard constraints with smooth approximations, and compute exact objective gradients with respect to all policy parameters and all inputs via an adjoint (backward) pass—training and sensitivity computation occur in a single pass.

Result: Validated on three domains: natural gas storage (training < 1 minute; 365 forward-curve sensitivities at no extra cost), pension fund asset-liability management (6.5x–200x sensitivity-speedup vs bump-and-revalue; scales with risk factors), pharmaceutical manufacturing (cross-unit 4-unit chain; 20 regulatory sensitivities from 5 adjoint passes in 74.5 ms). All sensitivities produced in the same backward pass, cost proportional to one reverse pass regardless of the number of sensitivities.

Conclusion: SNAPO delivers efficient, exact gradient-based policy optimization with broad, scalable sensitivity analysis by leveraging differentiable simulators and adjoint methods, combining strengths of DP and RL across real-world sequential decision problems.

Abstract: Many real-world problems require sequential decisions under uncertainty: when to inject or withdraw gas from storage, how to rebalance a pension portfolio each month, what temperature profile to run through a pharmaceutical reactor chain. Dynamic programming solves small instances exactly but scales exponentially in state dimensions. Black-box reinforcement learning handles high-dimensional states but trains slowly and produces no sensitivities. We introduce SNAPO (Smooth Neural Adjoint Policy Optimization), a framework that embeds a neural policy inside a known, differentiable simulator, replaces hard constraints with smooth approximations, and computes exact gradients of the objective with respect to all policy parameters and all inputs in a single adjoint pass. We demonstrate SNAPO on three domains: natural gas storage (training in under a minute, 365 forward curve sensitivities at no additional cost per sensitivity), pension fund asset-liability management (6.5x-200x sensitivity speedup over bump-and-revalue, scaling with the number of risk factors), and pharmaceutical manufacturing (cross-unit sensitivities through a 4-unit process chain, with 20 ICH Q8 regulatory sensitivities from 5 adjoint passes in 74.5 milliseconds). All sensitivities are produced by the same backward pass that trains the policy, at a cost proportional to one reverse pass regardless of how many sensitivities are computed.

</details>


### [345] [CLAD: A Clustered Label-Agnostic Federated Learning Framework for Joint Anomaly Detection and Attack Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06571)
*Iason Ofeidis,Nikos Papadis,Randeep Bhatia,Leandros Tassiulas,TV Lakshman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CLAD is a federated learning framework for IoT security that combines Clustered Federated Learning (CFL) with a Dual-Mode Micro-Architecture (DM^2A) to handle device heterogeneity and label scarcity. It uses a shared encoder with dual branches for unsupervised anomaly detection and supervised attack classification, and dynamically clusters devices by traffic patterns to prevent global model divergence. It achieves ~30% relative detection improvement in scenarios with 80% unlabeled clients at ~50% communication cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT/IIoT expand attack surfaces and are heterogeneous; traditional centralized IDS raises privacy concerns and fails to generalize across devices. There is abundant unlabeled data at edge, which is underutilized. A privacy-preserving, scalable IDS must handle heterogeneity and label scarcity.

Method: Introduce CLAD framework that integrates CFL with a Dual-Mode Micro-Architecture (DM^2A). DM^2A includes a shared encoder and two branches enabling both unsupervised anomaly detection and supervised attack classification on labeled/unlabeled clients. The clustering component groups devices with similar traffic patterns to maintain specialized local models and prevent global divergence. The framework preserves all data and leverages both labeled and unlabeled information.

Result: Extensive evaluations show the approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 30% relative improvement in detection performance with 80% unlabeled clients and only half the communication cost.

Conclusion: CLAD effectively addresses IoT device heterogeneity and label scarcity by unifying CFL with a dual-mode architecture for joint unsupervised and supervised learning, yielding substantial detection gains with reduced communication overhead.

Abstract: The rapid expansion of the Internet of Things (IoT) and Industrial IoT (IIoT) has created a massive, heterogeneous attack surface that challenges traditional network security mechanisms. While Federated Learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving alternative to centralized Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), standard approaches struggle to generalize across diverse device behaviors and typically fail to utilize the vast amounts of unlabeled data present in realistic edge environments. To bridge these gaps, we propose CLAD, a holistic framework that seamlessly incorporates Clustered Federated Learning (CFL) with a novel Dual-Mode Micro-Architecture ($\text{DM}^2\text{A}$). This unified approach simultaneously tackles the two primary bottlenecks of IoT security: device heterogeneity and label scarcity. The $\text{DM}^2\text{A}$ component features a shared encoder followed by two branches, enabling joint unsupervised anomaly detection and supervised attack classification; this allows the framework to harvest intelligence from both labeled and unlabeled clients. Concurrently, the clustering component dynamically groups devices with congruent traffic patterns, preventing global model divergence. By carefully combining these elements, CLAD ensures that no data is discarded and distinct operational patterns are preserved. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that this integrated approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 30% relative improvement in detection performance in scenarios with 80% unlabeled clients, with only half the communication cost.

</details>


### [346] [Directional Consistency as a Complementary Optimization Signal: The GONO Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06575)
*Victor Daniel Gera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GONO adapts Adam's momentum using directional coherence cc_t to exploit temporal gradient consistency, decoupling direction from loss; theory shows O(1/√T) convergence; empirically competitive; reduces to Adam when signal is uninformative.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the mismatch in existing optimizers that rely on gradient magnitude rather than temporal directional consistency; directional alignment and loss convergence can be decoupled, so exploiting cc_t could improve optimization.

Method: Define cc_t as cosine similarity of consecutive gradients. Adapt beta1_t based on cc_t: boost momentum during directional consistency, dampen during oscillations. Prove convergence rate O(1/√T). Show it reduces to Adam when cc_t carries no information. Compare empirically on benchmarks; report oscillation detection accuracy (F1) and accuracy metrics; provide code.

Result: Theoretical convergence rate matches Adam's O(1/√T). cc_t detects oscillations with F1=1.00 (vs 0.45 for gradient norm). Empirical performance: MNIST 98.15% accuracy; CIFAR-10 43.14%; ResNet-18 75.44%, competitive with AdamW.

Conclusion: Directional alignment is a grounded, actionable optimization signal; GONO offers a principled method to exploit it and can be a drop-in alternative to Adam when directional signal is informative; code available.

Abstract: We identify and formalize an underexplored phenomenon in deep learning optimization: directional alignment and loss convergence can be decoupled. An optimizer can exhibit near-perfect directional consistency (cc_t -> 1, measured via consecutive gradient cosine similarity) while the loss remains high or decreases slowly. This observation reveals that existing optimizers such as Adam, SGD, and RMSprop lack explicit mechanisms to exploit temporal consistency in gradient directions, relying instead on magnitude-based signals that fail to distinguish plateaus, saddle points, and genuine convergence. Motivated by this, we introduce GONO (Gradient-Oriented Norm-Adaptive Optimizer), which adapts Adam's momentum coefficient beta_1 based on cc_t: amplifying momentum under directional consistency and suppressing it during oscillation. We prove GONO matches Adam's O(1/sqrt(T)) convergence rate and reduces exactly to Adam when the signal is uninformative. Empirically, cc_t achieves oscillation detection with F1=1.00 (vs. 0.45 for gradient norm), and GONO remains competitive with AdamW on MNIST (98.15%), CIFAR-10 (43.14%), and ResNet-18 (75.44%), establishing directional alignment as a theoretically grounded, practically actionable optimization signal. Code: https://github.com/victordaniel/gono-optimizer

</details>


### [347] [On the Safety of Graph Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06576)
*Xiaoguang Guo,Zehong Wang,Ziming Li,Shawn Spitzel,Soonwoo Kwon,Tianyi Ma,Yanfang Ye,Chuxu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRL-Safety provides a multi-axis safety evaluation benchmark for graph representation learning, assessing 12 methods across 25 datasets on 5 axes (corruption robustness, OOD generalization, class imbalance, fairness, interpretation) with per-axis reporting to reveal cross-axis insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current GRL evaluations emphasize clean transfer and task coverage, but fail to reveal reliability under stressed graph signals, contexts, label support, and structural groups. A multi-axis safety benchmark is needed to diagnose robustness and deployment gaps.

Method: Evaluate 12 representative methods spanning topology-only embeddings, supervised GNNs, self-supervised graph models, and GFMs on 25 graph datasets under standardized conditions, measuring five safety axes with per-axis and sub-condition reports rather than a single aggregate score.

Result: Three cross-axis insights emerge: (1) safety behavior depends on the interaction between representation design and stressed graph factors, not solely on method family; (2) foundation-era methods show axis-specific strengths rather than universal safety dominance; (3) deployment regimes remain challenging even for best methods, signaling gaps requiring new robustness, adaptation, or training objectives.

Conclusion: GRL-Safety provides a practical, axis-aware benchmarking framework, with protocols and code available, to spur targeted improvements in GRL robustness and deployment reliability.

Abstract: Graph representation learning (GRL) has evolved from topology-only graph embeddings to task-specific supervised GNNs, and more recently to reusable representations and graph foundation models (GFMs). However, existing evaluations mainly measure clean transfer, adaptation, and task coverage. It remains unclear whether GRL methods stay reliable when deployment stresses affect graph signals, graph contexts, label support, structural groups, or predictive evidence. We introduce GRL-Safety, a multi-axis safety evaluation benchmark for GRL. GRL-Safety evaluates twelve representative methods, spanning topology-only embedding methods, supervised GNNs, self-supervised graph models, and GFMs, on twenty-five graph datasets under standardized evaluation conditions while preserving method-native adaptation. The evaluation covers five safety axes: corruption robustness, OOD generalization, class imbalance, fairness, and interpretation, with per-axis and sub-condition reporting rather than a single aggregate score. Our analysis yields three cross-axis insights that can inspire future research. First, safety behavior is shaped by the interaction between representation design and the stressed graph factor, rather than by method family alone. Second, foundation-era methods show axis-specific strengths rather than broad safety dominance. Third, several deployment regimes remain difficult even for the best evaluated method, revealing capability gaps that require new robustness, adaptation, or training objectives beyond model selection. The benchmark, evaluation protocols, and code are available at: https://github.com/GXG-CS/GRL-Safety.

</details>


### [348] [PairAlign: A Framework for Sequence Tokenization via Self-Alignment with Applications to Audio Tokenization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06582)
*Adhiraj Banerjee,Vipul Arora*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PairAlign introduces a self-aligned, sequence-level tokenizer for audio that learns compact, cross-view-consistent symbolic sequences by treating tokenization as conditional sequence generation and optimizing for edit-distance preservation without collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current audio tokenizers rely on local quantization or reconstruction, which does not directly optimize sequence-level properties like edit distance, length control, or termination. A discrete, cross-view-consistent tokenization promises better downstream reasoning, retrieval, and memory over sensory data.

Method: PairAlign maps speech to a continuous condition with an encoder and uses an autoregressive decoder to generate tokens from a BOS. It trains two content-preserving views so that each view's sequence is likely under the other's representation, using competing sequences from unrelated examples. Starting from VQ-style tokenization, it refines with EMA-teacher targets, cross-paired teacher forcing, prefix corruption, likelihood contrast, and explicit length control.

Result: On 3-second speech, PairAlign produces compact, non-degenerate token sequences with broad vocabulary usage and strong cross-view consistency. In TIMIT retrieval, it preserves edit-distance search while reducing archive token count by 55%. A continuous-sweep probe shows lower local overlap than a dense geometric tokenizer, with improved length control and bounded edit trajectories under 100 ms shifts.

Conclusion: PairAlign frames tokenization as a sequence-symbolic predictive learner akin to JEPA objectives, predicting a variable-length symbolic sequence from another view rather than a continuous latent, enabling scalable, edit-distance-aware audio tokens.

Abstract: Many operations on sensory data -- comparison, memory, retrieval, and reasoning -- are naturally expressed over discrete symbolic structures. In language this interface is given by tokens; in audio, it must be learned. Existing audio tokenizers rely on quantization, clustering, or codec reconstruction, assigning tokens locally, so sequence consistency, compactness, length control, termination, and edit similarity are rarely optimized directly.
  We introduce PairAlign, a framework for compact audio tokenization through sequence-level self-alignment. PairAlign treats tokenization as conditional sequence generation: an encoder maps speech to a continuous condition, and an autoregressive decoder generates tokens from BOS, learning token identity, order, length, and EOS placement. Given two content-preserving views, each view's sequence is trained to be likely under the other's representation, while unrelated examples provide competing sequences. This gives a scalable surrogate for edit-distance preservation while discouraging many-to-one collapse.
  PairAlign starts from VQ-style tokenization and refines it with EMA-teacher targets, cross-paired teacher forcing, prefix corruption, likelihood contrast, and length control.
  On 3-second speech, PairAlign learns compact, non-degenerate sequences with broad vocabulary usage and strong cross-view consistency. On TIMIT retrieval, it preserves edit-distance search while reducing archive token count by 55%. A continuous-sweep probe shows lower local overlap than a dense geometric tokenizer, but stronger length control and bounded edit trajectories under 100 ms shifts. PairAlign is a sequence-symbolic predictive learner: like JEPA-style objectives, it predicts an abstract target from another view as a learned variable-length symbolic sequence, not a continuous latent.

</details>


### [349] [Distributionally-Robust Learning to Optimize](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06585)
*Vinit Ranjan,Jisun Park,Bartolomeo Stellato*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Wasserstein distributionally robust learning-to-optimize (L2O) framework for first-order methods that interpolates between classical L2O and worst-case PEP; uses SGD to optimize algorithm parameters by differentiating through an inner SDP; yields high-probability risk bounds and robust out-of-sample performance on quadratic, LASSO, and LP, outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To learn problem-dependent hyperparameters of first-order methods with generalization guarantees by bridging L2O and worst-case optimal design, addressing robustness to distributional shift across problem instances.

Method: Minimize a Wasserstein distributionally robust version of the performance estimation problem (PEP) over algorithm parameters (e.g., step sizes). Inner PEP is an SDP; gradient is computed by differentiating through the SDP solution; SGD optimizes the parameters; robustness radius tunes between L2O and worst-case PEP.

Result: High-probability bounds show true risk ≤ in-sample L2O optimum + shrinking slack and ≤ worst-case PEP bound; empirical benchmarks on unconstrained quadratic, LASSO, and linear programming show strong out-of-sample performance and certifiable robustness, outperforming both worst-case optimal and vanilla L2O baselines.

Conclusion: The framework unifies L2O and worst-case PEP, provides provable robustness guarantees, and delivers improved out-of-sample performance across several convex optimization tasks.

Abstract: We propose a distributionally robust approach to learning hyperparameters for first-order methods in convex optimization. Given a dataset of problem instances, we minimize a Wasserstein distributionally robust version of the performance estimation problem (PEP) over algorithm parameters such as step sizes. Our framework unifies two extremes: as the robustness radius vanishes, we recover classical learning to optimize (L2O); as it grows, we recover worst-case optimal algorithm design via PEP. We solve the resulting problem with stochastic gradient descent, differentiating through the solution of an inner semidefinite program at each step. We prove high-probability bounds showing that the true risk of the learned algorithm is at most the in-sample L2O optimum plus a slack that shrinks with the sample size, and is no worse than the worst-case PEP bound. On unconstrained quadratic minimization, LASSO, and linear programming benchmarks, our learned algorithms achieve strong out-of-sample performance with certifiable robustness, outperforming both worst-case optimal and vanilla L2O baselines.

</details>


### [350] [Towards Metric-Faithful Neural Graph Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06588)
*Jyotirmaya Shivottam,Subhankar Mishra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical framework links encoder geometry (bi-Lipschitz properties) to GED estimation quality for two neural GED paradigms, showing geometry-aware encoders (e.g., FSW-GNN) improve GED prediction, ranking stability, and alignment-based objectives; supported by ablations and transfer tests, advocating encoder geometry as a design principle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph Edit Distance (GED) is NP-hard; neural GED methods rely on GNN encodings whose geometric properties influence estimation quality. Understanding how encoder geometry affects performance can unify and improve both similarity-predictor and alignment-based neural GED methods.

Method: Develop a theoretical framework connecting encoder geometry to GED estimation for two estimator classes (graph similarity predictors and alignment-based methods) using doubly-stochastic metric d_DS. Prove that graph-level bi-Lipschitz encoders yield better surrogate GED predictions and ranking stability; show that node-level bi-Lipschitz geometry propagates to alignment costs in matching-based estimators. Instantiate the framework with FSW-GNN, a bi-Lipschitz WL-equivalent encoder, as a drop-in replacement in representative neural GED architectures. Validate via experiments across baselines/datasets, plus ablations, transfer experiments, and a faithfulness case study on untrained encoders.

Result: Geometry-aware variants consistently improve GED prediction accuracy and ranking metrics across multiple baselines and benchmarks.

Conclusion: Encoder geometry is a practical design principle for neural graph matching, enabling more faithful GED estimation and improved stability; untrained-encoder analyses and transfers support the central role of representation geometry in neural GED methods.

Abstract: Graph Edit Distance (GED) is a fundamental, albeit NP-hard, metric for structural graph similarity. Recent neural graph matching architectures approximate GED by first encoding graphs with a Graph Neural Network (GNN) and then applying either a graph-level regression head or a matching-based alignment module. Despite substantial architectural progress, the role of encoder geometry in neural GED estimation remains poorly understood. In this paper, we develop a theoretical framework that connects encoder geometry to GED estimation quality for two broad classes of neural GED estimators: graph similarity predictors and alignment-based methods. On fixed graph collections, where the doubly-stochastic metric $d_{\mathrm{DS}}$ is comparable to GED, we show that graph-level bi-Lipschitz encoders yield controlled GED surrogates and improved ranking stability; for matching-based estimators, node-level bi-Lipschitz geometry propagates to encoder-induced alignment costs and the resulting optimized alignment objective. We instantiate this perspective using FSW-GNN, a bi-Lipschitz WL-equivalent encoder, as a drop-in replacement in representative neural GED architectures. Across representative baselines and benchmark datasets, the resulting geometry-aware variants significantly improve GED prediction and ranking metrics. A faithfulness case study of untrained encoders, together with ablations and transfer experiments, supports the view that these gains arise from improved representation geometry, positioning encoder geometry as a useful design principle for neural graph matching.

</details>


### [351] [BRICKS: Compositional Neural Markov Kernels for Zero-Shot Radiation-Matter Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06591)
*Richard Hildebrandt,Evangelos Kourlitis,Baran Hashemi,Manuel Bünstorf,Thierry Meyer,Nikola Boskov,Michael Kagan,Dan Rosenbaum,Sanmay Ganguly,Lukas Heinrich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A differentiable, compositional neural surrogate for radiation-matter interactions that learns a next-particle prediction kernel via hybrid discrete-continuous transformers and Riemannian Flow Matching, enabling zero-shot composition to unseen materials with GPU speedups and a new 20M-event dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create scalable, differentiable surrogates that can replace costly mechanistic simulators for radiation-matter interactions across domains, enabling tractable likelihoods and downstream optimization.

Method: Develop a next-particle prediction kernel using hybrid discrete-continuous transformer architectures trained with Riemannian Flow Matching on product manifolds. The model handles variable-sized, typed particle sets and radiation side effects, enabling composition to simulate large-scale material distributions in a zero-shot manner. The approach emphasizes differentiability and tractable likelihoods, with autoregressive multi-round rollouts for stability.

Result: Demonstrates substantial GPU speed-ups over CPU-bound mechanistic simulators for single-kernel execution. Kernel-level evaluation shows predictive stability across multi-round autoregressive rollouts. Introduces and releases a 20M-event radiation-matter interaction dataset for research.

Conclusion: The strategy offers a differentiable, scalable, and composable surrogate for radiation-matter interactions, capable of zero-shot extrapolation to new materials and distributions, with practical GPU acceleration and a useful dataset to foster further research.

Abstract: We introduce a new strategy for compositional neural surrogates for radiation-matter interactions, a key task spanning domains from particle physics through nuclear and space engineering to medical physics. Exploiting the locality and the Markov nature of particle interactions, we create a \emph{next-particle prediction} kernel using hybrid discrete-continuous transformer models based on Riemannian Flow Matching on product manifolds. The model generates variable-sized typed sets of particles and radiation side effects that are the result of the interaction of an incident particle with a material volume. The resulting kernel can be composed to simulate unseen large-scale material distributions in a zero-shot manner. Unlike mechanistic simulators, our model is designed to be differentiable, provides tractable likelihoods for future downstream applications. A significant computational speed-up on GPU compared to CPU-bound mechanistic simulation is observed for single-kernel execution. We evaluate the model at the kernel level and demonstrate predictive stability over multi-round autoregressive rollouts. We additionally release a novel 20M-event radiation-matter interaction dataset for further research.

</details>


### [352] [Weight-Decay Turns Transformer Loss Landscapes Villani: Functional-Analytic Foundations for Optimization and Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06599)
*Abhijit Das,Sayantan Dutta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Characterizes the regularized Transformer objective as a coercive energy satisfying Villani’s criteria, derives log-Sobolev and Poincaré constants tied to regularization and model size, and validates predictions with large-scale experiments, linking weight decay to fast Langevin mixing and improved generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the theoretical role of weight decay in Transformer loss landscapes and provide rigorous convergence and generalization guarantees for regularized deep learning objectives, a gap in the mathematical understanding of L2 regularization in large models.

Method: Functional-analytic analysis of cross-entropy with L2 regularization; proves coercivity, C∞ smoothness, quadratic growth, Gaussian tails; establishes the differential growth condition -ΔF + (1/s)||∇F||^2 → ∞; derives explicit log-Sobolev and Poincaré constants; introduces a scalable Villani diagnostic Ψ_s and estimates via Hutchinson probes; empirical validation on GPT-Neo-125M with PTB and WikiText-103.

Result: Demonstrates that the regularized loss satisfies Villani’s criteria; provides finite-time convergence bounds for noisy SGD and PAC-Bayes generalization bounds that improve with larger λ; Ψ_s exhibits predicted quadratic growth; Hessian spectral inflation; experimental evidence of exponential convergence consistent with log-Sobolev analysis.

Conclusion: Weight decay enforces mathematical conditions enabling fast Langevin mixing and curvature-aware optimization, thereby reinforcing both empirical generalization benefits and theoretical foundations for regularized deep learning in Transformers.

Abstract: Weight decay is widely used as a regularizer in large language models, yet its precise role in shaping Transformer loss landscapes remains theoretically underexplored. This paper provides the first rigorous functional-analytic characterization of the standard Transformer objective--cross-entropy loss with $L^2$ regularization--by proving it satisfies Villani's criteria for coercive energy functions. Specifically, we show that the regularized loss $\mathcal{F}$ is infinitely differentiable, grows at least quadratically, has Gaussian-integrable tails, and satisfies the differential growth condition $-Δ\mathcal{F} + \tfrac{1}{s}\|\nabla\mathcal{F}\|^{2} \to \infty$ as $\|θ\| \to \infty$ for all $s>0$. From this structure, we derive explicit log-Sobolev and Poincaré constants $C_{\mathrm{LS}} \leq λ^{-1} + d/λ^{2}$, linking the regularization strength $λ$ and model dimension $d$ to finite-time convergence guarantees for noisy stochastic gradient descent and PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds that tighten with increasing $λ$. To validate our theory, we introduce a scalable Villani diagnostic $Ψ_s(θ) = -Δ\mathcal{F} + s^{-1}\|\nabla \mathcal{F}\|^2$ and estimate it efficiently using Hutchinson trace probes in models with over 100M parameters. Experiments on GPT-Neo-125M across Penn Treebank and WikiText-103 confirm the predicted quadratic growth of $Ψ_s$, spectral inflation of the Hessian, and exponential convergence behavior consistent with our log-Sobolev analysis. These results demonstrate that weight decay not only improves generalization empirically but also establishes the mathematical conditions required for fast Langevin mixing and theoretically grounded curvature-aware optimization in deep learning.

</details>


### [353] [How Many Iterations to Jailbreak? Dynamic Budget Allocation for Multi-Turn LLM Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06605)
*Shai Feldman,Yaniv Romano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DAPRO introduces a theoretically valid dynamic budget allocation framework for bounding time-to-event in multi-turn LLM interactions. It provides distribution-free, finite-sample coverage guarantees without requiring conditional independence, achieving tighter bounds via a novel mean-censoring weight scaling. Empirically, it yields near-nominal coverage with lower variance than static baselines and enables unbiased, low-variance population estimates for metrics like jailbreak rate under limited compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating LLM performance in multi-turn conversations is computationally expensive due to rare events (e.g., jailbreaks). Static budgeting in prior conformal survival methods is inefficient and often relies on assumptions like conditional independence between censoring and event times. A dynamic budget allocation method is needed to efficiently bound time-to-event with reliable guarantees under limited resources.

Method: Propose Dynamic Allocation via PRojected Optimization (DAPRO). Formulate dynamic budget allocation for time-to-event bounds, ensuring budget feasibility. Derive a distribution-free, finite-sample coverage bound that scales with the square root of the mean censoring weight instead of the worst-case weight, yielding tighter guarantees. Do not require conditional independence between censoring and event times. Use DAPRO to obtain unbiased, low-variance estimates of population metrics under resource constraints.

Result: Across tasks such as agentic task success, adversarial jailbreaks, toxic content generation, and RAG hallucinations, using LLMs like Llama 3.1 and Qwen 2.5, DAPRO achieves coverage closer to the nominal level with lower variance than static baselines and adheres to the budget constraint.

Conclusion: DAPRO is the first theoretically valid dynamic budget allocation framework for bounding time-to-event in multi-turn LLM interactions. It provides distribution-free, finite-sample guarantees, avoids restrictive independence assumptions, and enables unbiased, low-variance population estimates under limited computing resources.

Abstract: Evaluating and predicting the performance of large language models (LLMs) in multi-turn conversational settings is critical yet computationally expensive; key events -- e.g., jailbreaks or successful task completion by an agent -- often emerge only after repeated interactions. These events might be rare, and under any feasible computational budget, remain unobserved.
  Recent conformal survival frameworks construct reliable lower predictive bounds (LPBs) on the number of iterations to trigger the event of interest, but rely on static budget allocation that is inefficient in multi-turn setups. To address this, we introduce \emph{Dynamic Allocation via PRojected Optimization} (DAPRO), the first theoretically valid dynamic budget allocation framework for bounding the time-to-event in multi-turn LLM interactions.
  We prove that DAPRO satisfies the budget constraint and provides distribution-free, finite-sample coverage guarantees without requiring the conditional independence between censoring and event times assumed by prior conformal survival approaches.
  A key theoretical contribution is a novel coverage bound that scales with the square root of the mean censoring weight rather than the worst-case weight, yielding provably tighter guarantees than prior work. Furthermore, DAPRO can be employed to obtain unbiased, low-variance estimates of population-level evaluation metrics, such as the jailbreak rate, under limited computing resources.
