Conclusion: FIT enables proportionate oversight and deliberate calibration of autonomy in agentic systems by translating intentionality into interpretable levels.

Abstract: As AI systems increasingly exhibit autonomous, goal-directed, and long-horizon behavior, users lack a standardized way to detect the degree to which a system functions like an intentional actor for governance and accountability purposes. This position paper defines intentionality not as consciousness, but as a behavioral profile characterized by purpose, foresight, volition, temporal commitment, and coherence - criteria long used in legal and philosophical contexts to infer intent. These properties are design-contingent: architectural choices such as memory persistence, planning depth, and tool autonomy shape the degree to which systems exhibit organized goal pursuit. If intentionality is design-contingent, it is in principle controllable. Yet control requires measurement.
  We introduce the Functional Intentionality Test (FIT), a multidimensional framework that quantifies intentional-like behavior across five observable dimensions, and propose FIT-Eval, a structured evaluation protocol for eliciting and scoring them. While reduced human agency can increase efficiency, rising intentional capacity heightens accountability risks. By translating intentionality into interpretable levels, FIT enables proportionate oversight and deliberate autonomy calibration in increasingly agentic systems.

</details>


### [380] [LANTERN: LLM-Augmented Neurosymbolic Transfer with Experience-Gated Reasoning Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05478)
*Mahyar Alinejad,Yue Wang,Amrit Singh Bedi,George Atia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LANTERN enables multi-source neurosymbolic transfer in reinforcement learning using LLM-generated DFAs, semantic embedding-based policy aggregation, and adaptive teacher-student gating, achieving 40-60% sample-efficiency gains across multiple domains and robust performance against poorly aligned sources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current neurosymbolic transfer methods rely on manually specified task automata, assume a single source task, and use fixed knowledge-integration mechanisms, limiting scalability and robustness. There is a need for a unified, adaptive framework that can leverage multiple sources with varying relevance in symbolic RL.

Method: Three components: (i) deterministic finite automata derived from natural-language task descriptions using large language models; (ii) semantic embedding-based aggregation of multiple source policies, weighted by cross-task similarity; (iii) adaptive teacher-student gating driven by temporal-difference error and semantic uncertainty.

Result: Empirically, LANTERN achieves 40-60% improvements in sample efficiency over baselines across domains such as resource management, navigation, and control, and shows robustness to poorly aligned sources.

Conclusion: Multi-source, adaptively weighted neurosymbolic transfer improves scalability and robustness in symbolic RL; LANTERN provides a unified framework that can generalize across domains and tasks, with potential for broader applicability and extension.

Abstract: Transfer learning in reinforcement learning (RL) seeks to accelerate learning in new tasks by leveraging knowledge from related sources. Existing neurosymbolic transfer methods, however, typically rely on manually specified task automata, assume a single source task, and use fixed knowledge-integration mechanisms that cannot adapt to varying source relevance. We propose LANTERN, a unified framework for multi-source neurosymbolic transfer that addresses these limitations through three components: (i) deterministic finite automata generated from natural language task descriptions using large language models, (ii) semantic embedding-based aggregation of multiple source policies weighted by cross-task similarity, and (iii) adaptive teacher-student gating based on temporal-difference error and semantic uncertainty. Across domains spanning resource management, navigation, and control, LANTERN achieves 40-60% improvements in sample efficiency over existing baselines while remaining robust to poorly aligned sources. These results demonstrate that multi-source, adaptively weighted neurosymbolic transfer can improve scalability and robustness in symbolic RL settings.

</details>


### [381] [FinRAG-12B: A Production-Validated Recipe for Grounded Question Answering in Banking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05482)
*Denys Katerenchuk,Pablo Duboue,Keelan Evanini,David Gondek,Nithin Govindugari,Olivier Allauzen,Joshua Baptiste,David J More,Joshua Schechter*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A data-efficient framework for grounded, domain-specific LLMs in banking, training a 12B model with 143M tokens to optimize answer quality, citation grounding, and calibrated refusal; includes data generation, calibration, and end-to-end deployment, achieving strong grounding, safer refusals, and large-cost savings vs GPT-4.1 across 40+ institutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Banks require high accuracy, regulatory compliance, verifiable grounding, and reliable refusal mechanisms. Off-the-shelf LLMs often hallucinate or fail to cite sources, making them risky for finance. The work aims to deliver a production-ready, data-efficient approach to grounded LLMs for banking.

Method: A data generation pipeline combining LLM-as-a-Judge filtering, citation annotation, and curriculum learning using only 143M tokens. Train a 12B-parameter model. Develop a calibrated refusal mechanism by training on 22% unanswerable examples. End-to-end approach from data curation to quantized serving, deployed at 40+ financial institutions.

Result: The 12B model achieves high answer quality with improved citation grounding, with a tradeoff of slightly reduced grounding accuracy compared to the base untuned model. Calibrated refusal yields 12% I don’t know rate, reducing unsafe responses from 4.3% (base) and avoiding GPT-4.1’s over-refusal at 20.2%. In deployment, yields a 7.1 percentage point improvement in query resolution (p < 0.001) and 3–5x faster responses at 20–50x lower cost than GPT-4.1.

Conclusion: A practical, scalable pipeline for producing grounded, compliant banking LLMs that can be deployed broadly with strong performance, safety controls, and substantial cost savings.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly being adopted across various domains. However, their adoption in banking industry faces resistance due to demands for high accuracy, regulatory compliance, and the need for verifiable and grounded responses. We present a unified, data-efficient framework for training grounded domain-specific LLMs that optimizes answer quality, citation grounding, and calibrated refusal under real-world deployment constraints. First, we describe a data generation pipeline that combines LLM-as-a-Judge filtering, citation annotation, and curriculum learning with only 143M tokens. The resulting 12B model achieves high answer quality outperforming GPT-4.1 on citation grounding, with a modest citation tradeoff versus the untuned base. Second, we propose a calibrated refusal mechanism: training on 22% unanswerable examples yield a 12% "I don't know" rate, substantially improving over the base model's unsafe 4.3% rate while avoiding GPT-4.1's over-refusal (20.2%). Third, we present an end-to-end methodology spanning from data curation to quantized serving. The system is deployed at 40+ financial institutions, achieving a 7.1 percentage point improvement in query resolution (p < 0.001). Additionally, the model delivers 3-5x faster responses at 20-50x lower cost compared to GPT-4.1.

</details>


### [382] [FoodCHA: Multi-Modal LLM Agent for Fine-Grained Food Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05499)
*Woojin Lee,Pranav Mekkoth,Ye Tian,Onat Gungor,Tajana Rosing*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A hierarchical multimodal framework (FoodCHA) reframes food recognition as a hierarchical decision process to achieve fine-grained categorization, subcategory labeling, and cooking-style attributes using a compact Moondream-2B VLM, outperforming a baseline on FoodNExTDB.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world food imagery has high intra-class similarity and multiple items per image. Fine-grained attributes (e.g., cooking style) and reliable label generation are challenging; open-ended VLMs may produce non-canonical labels, hindering deployment. A compact, semantically consistent approach is needed for practical use.

Method: FoodCHA uses a hierarchical decision-making pipeline that anchors high-level category predictions to guide subcategory identification and subsequently guide cooking-style recognition, enabling attribute-level discrimination. It leverages the compact Moondream-2B vision-language model for reasoning with lower compute/memory requirements.

Result: On FoodNExTDB, FoodCHA achieves 13.8% higher category precision and 38.2% higher subcategory precision than Food-Llama-3.2-11B, and a striking 153.2% improvement in cooking-style classification precision.

Conclusion: A hierarchical, agentic multimodal approach with a compact VLM improves semantic consistency and fine-grained attribute recognition in real-world food images, offering a practical, deployable solution for dietary monitoring with strong performance gains over a strong baseline.

Abstract: The widespread adoption of camera-equipped mobile devices and wearables has enabled convenient capture of meal images, making food recognition a key component for real time dietary monitoring. However, real-world food images present challenges due to high intra-class similarity and the frequent presence of multiple food items within a single image. While deep learning models achieve strong performance in coarse grained classification, they often struggle to capture fine-grained attributes such as cooking style. Moreover, open-ended generation in modern vision-language models can produce non-canonical labels, limiting their practical deployment. We propose FoodCHA, a multimodal agentic framework that reformulates food recognition as a hierarchical decision-making process. By progressively anchoring predictions, FoodCHA guides subcategory identification using high-level categories and guides cooking style recognition using subcategories, improving semantic consistency and attribute-level discrimination. To ensure practical deployability, FoodCHA utilizes the compact Moondream-2B vision language model, which provides strong reasoning capability while maintaining lower computational and memory overhead. Experiments on FoodNExTDB show that FoodCHA outperforms Food-Llama-3.2-11B by 13.8% and 38.2% in category and subcategory recognition precision, respectively, and achieves a striking 153.2% improvement in cooking style classification precision.

</details>


### [383] [Housing Potential Common Data Model and City Digital Twin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05535)
*Megan Katsumi,Mark Fox,Anderson Wong,Divnoor Chatha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A standardized common data model (HPCDM) is proposed to break data silos and enable interoperable housing-potential analysis, demonstrated via a City Digital Twin and a pilot dashboard, with identified adoption barriers and mitigation strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fragmented housing-related data across zoning, demographics, and services hampers comprehensive analysis; need a standard to support integration, interoperability, and practical decision-support tools.

Method: Develop HPCDM; evaluate the model; construct a City Digital Twin for housing; build a pilot dashboard; analyze barriers and propose mitigation strategies.

Result: Successful evaluation of HPCDM; creation of a City Digital Twin; demonstration via a pilot dashboard; identification of adoption barriers with actionable mitigations.

Conclusion: A standardized data model and digital twin framework can support housing-potential analysis; practical adoption requires addressing governance, data quality, and interoperability challenges.

Abstract: The evaluation of housing potential requires consideration of a location from multiple perspectives, ranging from zoning and land use to population characteristics and access to services. This research introduces the Housing Potential Common Data Model (HPCDM) to overcome existing data silos, serving as a standard to support integration and interoperability across the diverse range of datasets that are required for housing potential analysis. This report details the evaluation of the model along with the creation of a City Digital Twin for housing and a pilot dashboard application to demonstrate a practical implementation. Beyond the technical framework, this work identifies critical barriers to adoption and provides actionable mitigation strategies for urban planners and stakeholders.

</details>


### [384] [AgenticRAG: Agentic Retrieval for Enterprise Knowledge Bases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05538)
*Susheel Suresh,Hazel Mak,Shangpo Chou,Fred Kroon,Sahil Bhatnagar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgenticRAG provides a practical agentic harness on top of enterprise search enabling a reasoning LLM to use tools (search, find, open, summarize) for iterative retrieval and evidence analysis, yielding strong gains on multiple benchmarks and showing production-readiness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on fixed candidate sets and rigid retrieval pipelines in standard RAG; empower autonomous, tool-enabled reasoning over enterprise knowledge bases.

Method: Layered harness atop existing search, enabling tools for the LLM: search, find, open, summarize; supports iterative retrieval, in-document navigation, and evidence analysis; includes ablation studies and pre-production deployment learnings.

Result: Substantial improvements: BRIGHT recall@1 49.6% (+21.8 pp); WixQA factuality 0.96 (+13% relative); FinanceBench 92% answer correctness, near oracle. Agentic tool use drives most gains (≈5.9x), multi-query search and in-document navigation aid quality and efficiency.

Conclusion: Shows practical viability and strong performance benefits of agentic tool-enabled reasoning for enterprise RAG, suitable for production deployment.

Abstract: We present AgenticRAG, a practical agentic harness for retrieval and analysis over enterprise knowledge bases. Standard RAG pipelines place significant burden of grounding on the search stack, constraining the language model to a fixed candidate set chosen deep in the retrieval process. Our approach reduces this overdependence by layering a lightweight harness on top of existing enterprise search infrastructure, equipping a reasoning LLM with search, find, open, and summarize tools enabling the model to iteratively retrieve information, navigate within documents, and analyze evidence autonomously. On three open benchmarks we observe substantial gains: $49.6\%$ recall@1 on BRIGHT (+21.8 pp over the best embedding baseline), 0.96 factuality on WixQA ($+13\%$ relative improvement), and $92\%$ answer correctness on FinanceBench--within 2 pp of oracle access to true evidence. Ablation studies show that the most significant factor is the shift from single-shot retrieval to agentic tool use ($5.9\times$ improvement), while multi-query search and in-document navigation contribute to both quality and efficiency. We present various design choices in our agentic harness that were informed by pre-production deployments. Our results demonstrate its suitability for real-world enterprise production environments.

</details>


### [385] [SPARK: Self-Play with Asymmetric Reward from Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05546)
*Hyobin Park,Taeseop Kim,Dong-Geol Choi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPARK uses knowledge-graph grounded self-play to generate relational reasoning questions and verifiable rewards from scientific literature, improving multi-hop QA across documents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome challenges of self-play in unstructured scientific text by grounding reasoning and rewards in a unified knowledge graph.

Method: Construct a unified KG from multi-document science papers; use KG paths over multimodal nodes to generate relational questions; structured facts stored in the KG provide verifiable rewards; a small vision-language model alternates between Proposer and Solver under information asymmetry against a fixed KG.

Result: Outperforms flat-corpus baselines on public benchmarks and a self-constructed cross-document multi-hop QA dataset; the performance gap widens as hop count increases.

Conclusion: KG-grounding enhances relational multi-hop reasoning in science QA; the framework shows potential for online adaptation and broader KG-based self-play.

Abstract: Self-play reinforcement learning has shown strong performance in domains with formally verifiable structure, such as mathematics and coding, where both problem generation and reward computation can be grounded in explicit rules. Extending this paradigm to scientific literature is more challenging: the relationships among multi-modal elements within and across documents are rarely made explicit in text, which makes automatic generation of relational reasoning questions difficult and weakens the reliability of reward signals. We propose SPARK (Self-Play with Asymmetric Reward from Knowledge Graphs), a framework that automatically constructs a unified knowledge graph (KG) from multi-document scientific literature and uses it as the structural basis for self-play. KG paths over multimodal nodes serve as a source for generating relational reasoning questions, and structured facts stored in the KG provide a basis for verifiable reward computation. A single small vision-language model (sVLM) alternates between Proposer and Solver roles under information asymmetry against a fixed KG, a design that we believe can be naturally extended toward online adaptation in future work. We evaluate SPARK on public benchmarks and a self-constructed cross-document multi-hop QA dataset. Results show that SPARK consistently outperforms flat-corpus-based self-play baselines, and the performance gap widens as hop count increases, suggesting that KG-structure grounding contributes to relational multi-hop reasoning beyond what unstructured corpus grounding can provide.

</details>


### [386] [Who Prices Cognitive Labor in the Age of Agents? A Position on Compute-Anchored Wages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05558)
*Siqi Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Compute-anchored wage (CAW): AI agents are a production technology that converts compute capital into cognitive labor; the labor market is no longer the sole price-setter; the wage for cognitive labor is bounded by the compute rental rate and agent intensity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Re-examine the canonical view that replicable AI agents with near-zero marginal cost drive cognitive-labor wages to zero; identify the correct mechanism and its policy implications.

Method: Derive a CAW bound using the textbook factor-pricing framework: w ≤ λ k r_c on tasks where human and agent cognitive labor are substitutes; define k as compute intensity, r_c as rental rate of compute capital, and λ as relative human-to-agent productivity; extend with CES aggregation, separate substitutable from complementary tasks, and discuss factor-share consequences.

Result: Establishes a Compute-Anchored Wage bound and shows the elasticity of substitution shifts the price-setting locus from the labor market to the compute capital market; yields a directional inversion of skill-biased technical change and implications for factor shares.

Conclusion: Cognitive labor pricing is governed by the compute capital market rather than the labor market; price-setter for cognitive labor is the compute capital market.

Abstract: A natural intuition about the economics of AI agents is that, because agents can be replicated at near-zero marginal cost, they constitute a labor input in infinitely elastic supply, and therefore drive cognitive-labor wages to zero. We argue this framing is wrong in mechanism but partially correct in conclusion, and that the correction matters for both theory and policy. \textbf{Agents are not labor; they are a production technology that converts compute capital $K_c$ into effective units of cognitive labor $L_A$.} Once this is recognized, the elastic-supply margin that anchors the equilibrium wage migrates from the labor market to the compute capital market. Building on the textbook factor-pricing framework \citep{mankiw2020}, we derive a \emph{Compute-Anchored Wage} (CAW) bound stating that, on tasks where human and agent cognitive labor are substitutes, the competitive human wage is bounded above by $λ\cdot k \cdot r_c$, where $r_c$ is the rental rate of compute capital, $k$ is the compute intensity of one effective agent-labor unit, and $λ$ is the relative human-to-agent productivity. We generalize the result through CES aggregation, separate substitutable from complementary tasks (yielding a directional inversion of skill-biased technical change), and discuss factor-share consequences. The position is concise: \emph{the price-setter for cognitive labor is no longer the labor market.}

</details>


### [387] [Nonsense Helps: Prompt Space Perturbation Broadens Reasoning Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05566)
*Langlin Huang,Chengsong Huang,Jinyuan Li,Donghong Cai,Yuyi Yang,Jiaxin Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LoPE introduces prompt perturbations using Lorem Ipsum sequences to overcome the zero-advantage problem in GRPO, improving exploration and training efficiency across large LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), if all sampled rollouts fail for a query, the relative advantage collapses to zero, wasting data and computation. Static sampling budgets limit exploration for hard questions. A lightweight perturbation strategy could unlock alternative reasoning paths.

Method: Prepend stochastically assembled Lorem Ipsum sequences to prompts before resampling, effectively perturbing the prompt-space. The study also notes effectiveness of other Latin-based sequences with low perplexity. Evaluations on 1.7B, 4B, and 7B models.

Result: LoPE significantly outperforms resampling with original prompts across the tested model scales; perturbations with Latin-based sequences of low perplexity are also effective.

Conclusion: LoPE provides a simple, effective exploration augmentation for LLM reinforcement learning and establishes a strong baseline for broadening exploration in GRPO.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, particularly Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), has significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, in complex tasks, GRPO frequently suffers from the ``zero-advantage problem'': when all sampled rollouts for a query fail, the relative advantage collapses to zero. Consequently, the model loses effective training signals for these questions, wasting the training data and computational budget. While simply increasing the sampling budget for these questions is a common remedy, the static sampling policy inherently constrains reasoning exploration, limiting the success rate. In this paper, we propose Lorem Perturbation for Exploration (LoPE), a simple yet effective training framework to break this exploration bottleneck. We posit that task-irrelevant prompt-space perturbations can shift the model's output distribution enough to unlock orthogonal reasoning pathways for hard questions. Specifically, LoPE prepends sequences stochastically assembled from Lorem Ipsum vocabulary (a pseudo-Latin placeholder text) to the prompts before resampling. Experiments across 1.7B, 4B, and 7B models demonstrate that LoPE significantly outperforms resampling with the original prompts. Further analysis reveals that other Latin-based random sequences with low perplexity are also effective perturbations. Our results establish LoPE as a strong baseline for broadening exploration in LLM reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [388] [Locality-aware Private Class Identification for Domain Adaptation with Extreme Label Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05567)
*Chuan-Xian Ren,Cheng-Jun Guo,Hong Yan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces ReOT, an OT-based domain adaptation method robust to severe label shift by identifying private classes via a locality-aware transport-mass score, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Under partial overlap of label spaces (private classes), standard DA methods struggle due to large intra-class variance and the difficulty of distinguishing private from shared samples; existing outlier-based assumptions are insufficient.

Method: Define a locality-aware score on OT transport mass to identify private samples. Propose ReOT, which minimizes classification risk while enforcing a separated cluster structure between shared and private classes and avoids mismatches between shared-private sample pairs, enabling reliable intra-class transport. Derives a generalization upper bound for extreme label shift.

Result: The score function is theoretically proven to distinguish shared vs private samples. ReOT achieves effective target risk minimization and mitigates cross-domain class-conditional discrepancy. Extensive experiments on benchmarks validate its effectiveness.

Conclusion: ReOT provides a reliable OT-based solution for domain adaptation under severe label shift, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance; the generalization bound informs performance under extreme shift.

Abstract: Domain adaptation aims to transfer knowledge from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain with different distributions. In real-world scenarios, the label spaces of the two domains often have an inclusion relationship, where some classes exist only in one domain but not the other. These non-overlapping classes are referred to as private classes. Identifying private class samples and mitigating their adverse effects is critical in the literature. Existing methods rely on the assumption that shifts in private classes are large enough to be considered outliers. However, the variance within a single shared class can be significantly larger than the difference between a private class and another shared class, challenging this assumption. Consequently, private classes substantially increase the difficulty of cross-domain classification. To address these issues, based on local transportation and metric properties of optimal transport (OT), a locality-aware private class identification approach is proposed in the form of a score function on transport mass. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is theoretically proven, highlighting the score function's strong ability to distinguish between shared and private class samples. Building on this, we introduce a reliable OT-based method (ReOT) for domain adaptation under severe label shift. ReOT minimizes classification risk while learning the separated cluster structure between the identified shared classes and private classes, effectively avoiding mismatch between shared-private sample pairs, thus ensuring that important knowledge is reliably transported intra-class to mitigate class-conditional discrepancy. Furthermore, a generalization upper bound of the target risk is provided for extreme label shift scenarios, which can be minimized by ReOT. Extensive experiments on benchmarks validate the effectiveness of ReOT.

</details>


### [389] [AlphaCrafter: A Full-Stack Multi-Agent Framework for Cross-Sectional Quantitative Trading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05580)
*Yishuo Yuan,Jiayi Sheng,Sirui Zeng,Jiaqi Wang,Jiaheng Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A full-stack, adaptive multi-agent framework (AlphaCrafter) that continuously discovers factors, screens by market regime, and executes with risk constraints, forming a closed-loop pipeline for cross-sectional trading with superior risk-adjusted performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationarity in financial markets and fragmentation between factor discovery and execution. Need a unified, automated pipeline that adapts to evolving regimes without manual intervention.

Method: Three agents: Miner (LLM-guided factor search to expand factor pool), Screener (assesses market conditions to construct regime-conditioned factor ensembles), Trader (translates ensembles into quantitative strategies under explicit risk constraints). The agents operate in a closed loop to form a cross-sectional trading system, evaluated on CSI 300 and S&P 500.

Result: AlphaCrafter consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in risk-adjusted returns and shows the lowest cross-trial variance across experiments, indicating robustness and stability.

Conclusion: A cohesive, adaptive factor-to-execution framework can improve quantitative trading performance by integrating discovery, regime adaptation, and execution under risk constraints.

Abstract: Financial markets are inherently non-stationary, driven by complex interactions among macroeconomic regimes, microstructural frictions, and behavioral dynamics. Building quantitative strategies that remain profitable demands the continuous coupling of factor discovery, regime-adaptive selection, and risk-constrained execution. Prevailing approaches, however, optimize these components under static or isolated assumptions. Factor mining frameworks typically treat alpha discovery as a one-time search process, implicitly assuming that factor efficacy persists across market regimes. Execution-oriented systems often adopt role-playing agent architectures that simulate anthropomorphic trading committees, introducing behavioral noise rather than systematic rationality. Consequently, a fully automated, rationality-driven framework unifying a coherent quantitative pipeline remains absent. We introduce AlphaCrafter, a full-stack multi-agent framework that closes this gap through a continuously adaptive factor-to-execution pipeline, designed to track and respond to evolving market conditions without manual intervention. AlphaCrafter operates via three specialized agents: a Miner that continuously expands the factor pool via LLM-guided search, a Screener that assesses prevailing market conditions to construct regime-conditioned factor ensembles, and a Trader that translates these ensembles into quantitative strategies under explicit risk constraints. Together, these three agents form a closed-loop cross-sectional trading system that adapts holistically to evolving market dynamics. Extensive experiments on CSI 300 and S&P 500 demonstrate that AlphaCrafter consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in risk-adjusted returns while exhibiting the lowest cross-trial variance, confirming that integrated and adaptive factor-to-execution design yields robust trading performance.

</details>


### [390] [Belief Memory: Agent Memory Under Partial Observability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05583)
*Junfeng Liao,Qizhou Wang,Jianing Zhu,Bo Du,Rui Yan,Xiuying Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BeliefMem stores multiple candidate conclusions with probabilities in memory, updated by Noisy-OR, enabling uncertainty-aware operation of LLM agents with long context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To avoid the self-reinforcing errors of deterministic single-conclusion memory, by preserving uncertainty and alternative beliefs for partially observable environments.

Method: Maintain separate memory entries for each candidate conclusion, each with its probability; update probabilities with Noisy-OR as new observations arrive; retrieve all candidates with their probabilities to surface alternatives to the agent; allows confident action on strong evidence while remaining revisable.

Result: Empirical evaluations on LoCoMo and ALFWorld show BeliefMem achieves the best average performance with limited data, outperforming strong baselines.

Conclusion: Probabilistic, multi-hypothesis memory improves robustness and adaptability of LLM-based agents in partially observable tasks and opens a new direction for agent memory design.

Abstract: LLM agents that operate over long context depend on external memory to accumulate knowledge over time. However, existing methods typically store each observation as a single deterministic conclusion (e.g., inferring "API~X failed" from temporary errors), even though such observations are inherently partial and potentially ambiguous. By committing to one conclusion and discarding uncertainty, these methods introduce self-reinforcing error: the agent acts on the stored conclusion, never revisits alternatives, and reinforces the conclusion over time. To address this issue, we propose BeliefMem, which shifts the memory paradigm from committing to a single conclusion per observation to retaining multiple candidate conclusions with their probabilities. Concretely, BeliefMem stores the candidate conclusions as separate memory entries, each carrying a probability that is updated via Noisy-OR rules as new observations arrive. At retrieval, all candidates surface together with their probabilities, keeping alternatives visible to the agent. Since each conclusion in memory retains its probability, BeliefMem preserves the uncertainty that the deterministic paradigm discards, enabling the agent to act with high confidence on well-evidenced knowledge while retaining the capacity to update its confidence when new evidence arrives. Empirical evaluations on LoCoMo and ALFWorld benchmarks show that, even with limited data, BeliefMem achieves the best average performance, remarkably outperforming well-known baselines. More broadly, such probabilistic memory produces substantial gains and explores a new direction for agent memory in partially observable environments.

</details>


### [391] [Causal Probing for Internal Visual Representations in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05593)
*Zehao Deng,Tianjie Ju,Zheng Wu,Liangbo He,Jun Lan,Huijia Zhu,Weiqiang Wang,Zhuosheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Activation-steering causally probes MLLMs to reveal how visual concepts are encoded; entities localize, abstract concepts distribute; model depth scales distributed concepts; blocking outputs triggers latent activations; perception vs reasoning decoupled; geometric relations recognized but not procedural for abstract tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify internal representations and grounding mechanisms in Multimodal Large Language Models, linking encoding patterns to scaling and reasoning capabilities.

Method: Introduce a causal activation-steering framework with systematic interventions across four visual concept categories; examine localization vs distribution; analyze effects of model depth; perform reverse steering by blocking explicit outputs; extend analysis to visual reasoning tasks.

Result: Entities show localized memorization; abstract concepts are globally distributed; depth is necessary to encode distributed/abstract concepts; entity encoding invariant to scale; reversing trigger increases latent activations; perception and generation show compensatory dynamics; MLLMs recognize geometric relations but treat them as static features, hindering procedural execution for abstract problems.

Conclusion: Understanding interior encodings informs model scaling and grounding strategies; suggests architectural or training adjustments to better couple perception and reasoning; highlights current limits in abstract problem-solving despite perceptual competence.

Abstract: Despite the remarkable success of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) across diverse tasks, the internal mechanisms governing how they encode and ground distinct visual concepts remain poorly understood. To bridge this gap, we propose a causal framework based on activation steering to actively probe and manipulate internal visual representations. Through systematic intervention across four visual concept categories, our results reveal a divergence in concept encoding: entities exhibit distinct localized memorization, whereas abstract concepts are globally distributed across the network. Critically, this divergence uncovers a mechanistic driver of scaling laws: increasing model depth is indispensable for encoding distributed and complex abstract concepts, whereas entity localization remains remarkably invariant to scale. Furthermore, reverse steering uncovers that blocking explicit output triggers a surge in latent activations, exposing a compensatory mechanism between perception and generation. Finally, extending our analysis to visual reasoning, we expose a disconnect between perception and reasoning although MLLMs successfully recognize geometric relations, they treat them merely as static visual features, failing to trigger the procedural execution necessary for abstract problem-solving.

</details>


### [392] [Text-Graph Synergy: A Bidirectional Verification and Completion Framework for RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05643)
*Jiarui Zhong,Hong Cai Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A unified Text-Graph Synergistic RAG framework (TGS-RAG) that bidirectionally enhances retrieval via Graph-to-Text Global Voting and Text-to-Graph Memory-based Orphan Entity Bridging, improving multi-hop reasoning and efficiency over standard RAG and baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address Information Island due to asymmetric reasoning between unstructured text and structured graphs, and limitations of existing RAG approaches that either misalign evidence or prune valid reasoning paths.

Method: Introduce TGS-RAG with two channels: Graph-to-Text using Global Voting over visited graph nodes to re-rank/refine textual evidence; Text-to-Graph using Memory-based Orphan Entity Bridging to resurrect pruned reasoning paths from search history without extra DB overhead.

Result: Empirical results on multiple multi-hop benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance, with a better trade-off between retrieval precision and computational cost.

Conclusion: Bidirectional text-graph synergy mitigates the Information Island problem and yields effective, efficient RAG; TGS-RAG demonstrates strong potential for robust multi-hop reasoning.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become a core paradigm for enhancing factual grounding and multi-hop reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). Traditional text-based RAG often retrieves logically irrelevant pseudo-evidence, while graph-based RAG is frequently hindered by search-time pruning, which may discard potentially valid reasoning paths. Existing hybrid approaches primarily adopt simple evidence concatenation or unidirectional enhancement, which fails to address the fundamental "Information Island" problem caused by asymmetric reasoning flows between unstructured text and structured graphs. We propose \textbf{TGS-RAG}, a unified framework for \textbf{T}ext-\textbf{G}raph \textbf{S}ynergistic enhancement. TGS-RAG introduces a bidirectional mechanism: (i) a \textbf{Graph-to-Text} channel that employs a Global Voting strategy from visited graph nodes to re-rank and refine textual evidence, filtering out semantic noise; and (ii) a \textbf{Text-to-Graph} channel that utilizes the \textbf{Memory-based Orphan Entity Bridging} algorithm. This algorithm utilizes textual cues to proactively resurrect valid but previously pruned reasoning paths from the search history without additional database overhead. Experimental results on multiple multi-hop reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that TGS-RAG significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a superior balance between retrieval precision and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [393] [Retrieval-Conditioned Topology Selection with Provable Budget Conservation for Multi-Agent Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05657)
*Abhijit Talluri,Pujith Anne,Bhagavan Choudary Pendiyala,Raghavendra Chilukuri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RGAO: Retrieval-Guided Adaptive Orchestration for Code-Agent, coupling complexity-conditioned routing with formal budget algebras to guide multi-agent code generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent LLM systems select orchestration topology without consulting the codebase, leading to misrouting and unpredictable budgets. There is a need to align topology with code structural complexity and to provide verifiable budget properties.

Method: Extract a hierarchical, six-dimensional structural complexity vector from a hierarchical code index; implement a complexity-conditioned topology router that selects orchestration topology based on this complexity; introduce a budget algebra with a structural-induction conservation theorem and formal contracts for sub-agents (six-dimensional budgets); integrate a hierarchical code retrieval engine.

Result: Proxy misrouting shrinks from 30.1% to 8.2%; sub-millisecond DAG construction; linear scalability of tree-index; empirical demonstration of budget conservation through the proposed algebra under dynamic topology selection.

Conclusion: By merging complexity-conditioned routing with formal resource algebras, RGAO achieves provable budget conservation and efficient, retrieval-guided orchestration, with scalable performance suitable for code-generation workflows.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems for code generation face a fundamental routing problem: the optimal orchestration topology depends on the structural complexity of the code under modification, yet existing systems select topologies without consulting the codebase. We present Retrieval-Guided Adaptive Orchestration (RGAO), an architecture that closes this loop by extracting a structural complexity vector from a hierarchical code index before selecting the orchestration topology. RGAO operates within Code-Agent, a multi-agent framework whose sub-agents are governed by formal contracts with six-dimensional budget vectors. Our headline contribution is the composition of two previously separate lines of work -- complexity-conditioned LLM routing and formal resource algebras -- yielding a property neither admits alone: provable budget conservation under retrieval-conditioned dynamic topology selection. Concretely we contribute: (1) a complexity-conditioned topology router that reduces proxy-measured misrouting from 30.1% to 8.2%; (2) a budget algebra with a structural-induction conservation theorem; and (3) a hierarchical code retrieval engine. Empirical evaluation demonstrates sub-millisecond DAG construction and linear tree-index scalability.

</details>


### [394] [Large Vision-Language Models Get Lost in Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05668)
*Gongli Xi,Ye Tian,Mengyu Yang,Huahui Yi,Liang Lin,Xiaoshuai Hao,Kun Wang,Wendong Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A unified information-theoretic and geometric analysis reveals a functional decoupling in LVLM decoders: attention preserves subspaces for reconfiguration while FFNs expand subspaces for semantic innovation; using fixed attention weights can match or exceed learned attention performance, indicating redundancy and misallocation in current architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a unified theoretical basis for understanding residual updates in LVLM decoders and to guide architectural optimization by separating the roles of attention and FFNs through information-theoretic and geometric insights.

Method: Introduce a framework that combines information theory (entropy and subspace information flow) with geometry to quantify residual updates in transformer blocks. Apply it to LVLM decoder backbones to separate the geometric/subspace effects of attention vs FFNs. Conduct controlled experiments by replacing learned attention weights with predefined values (e.g., Gaussian noise) and evaluate on multiple datasets to assess performance versus vanilla models.

Result: The framework reveals a fundamental decoupling: attention acts as a subspace-preserving operator geared toward reconfiguration, whereas FFNs act as subspace-expanding operators driving semantic innovation. In experiments, replacing learned attention weights with predefined values yields comparable or superior performance on most datasets, indicating severe misallocation and redundancy in current mechanisms and suggesting LVLMs often ‘get lost in attention’ rather than efficiently leveraging visual context.

Conclusion: The findings prompt architectural reconsideration of LVLM decoders. Attention should not be the primary driver of semantic transfer; instead, FFNs should handle semantic expansion and attention should preserve essential subspace structure. This points to potential efficiency gains by rethinking attention weights, possibly adopting fixed or simplified attention schemes, and focusing architectural designs on more effective subspace manipulation.

Abstract: Despite the rapid evolution of training paradigms, the decoder backbone of large vision--language models (LVLMs) remains fundamentally rooted in the residual-connection Transformer architecture. Therefore, deciphering the distinct roles of internal modules is critical for understanding model mechanics and guiding architectural optimization. While prior statistical approaches have provided valuable attribution-based insights, they often lack a unified theoretical basis. To bridge this gap, we propose a unified framework grounded in information theory and geometry to quantify the geometric and entropic nature of residual updates. Applying this unified framework reveals a fundamental functional decoupling: Attention acts as a subspace-preserving operator focused on reconfiguration, whereas FFNs serve as subspace-expanding operators driving semantic innovation. Strikingly, further experiments demonstrate that replacing learned attention weights with predefined values (e.g., Gaussian noise) yields comparable or even superior performance across a majority of datasets relative to vanilla models. These results expose severe misallocation and redundancy in current mechanisms, suggesting that state-of-the-art LVLMs effectively ``get lost in attention'' rather than efficiently leveraging visual context.

</details>


### [395] [Attractor Geometry of Transformer Memory: From Conflict Arbitration to Confident Hallucination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05686)
*Qiyao Liang,Risto Miikkulainen,Ila Fiete*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A unified geometric account explains two LM failure modes—conflict (PM/WM disagreement) and hallucination (unlearned facts). In hidden-state space, memorized facts form attractor basins; the geometric margin (distance to the nearest basin) separates correct recall from hallucination, outperforming entropy-based detection. A synthetic, controllable setup with LoRA-installed parametric memory isolates causal roles; results generalize to natural queries without fine-tuning. A scaling law C = exp(-c/Δ̄) governs confident hallucinations; frozen heads erase epistemic information, worsening with scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Output-only monitoring often fails to detect epistemic failures. The authors seek a mechanistic, geometry-based account of two distinct failure modes, to improve reliability and detection of hallucinations and conflicts in autoregressive LMs.

Method: A controlled synthetic task mapping entity identifiers to unique codes with parametric memory installed via LoRA adapters. Analyze the hidden-state geometry of autoregressive generation, define a geometric margin as distance to the nearest memorized basin, and compare margin-based detection to entropy-based methods. Validate on natural-language factual queries with no adaptation and study scaling of confident hallucinations with model size.

Result: The geometric margin cleanly separates correct recall from hallucination, outperforming entropy-based detection which rejects many correct outputs. The separation persists on natural factual queries without adaptation. The fraction of confident hallucinations follows C = exp(-c/Δ̄) and grows with scale even as overall error drops. Hidden states reliably encode epistemic state; the frozen output head erases it, with erasure worsening as scale increases.

Conclusion: Attractor geometry is structural rather than a fine-tuning artifact. Monitoring should leverage hidden-state geometry rather than output alone. The findings suggest architectural/training interventions to preserve epistemic states and improve detection of confident hallucinations, with implications for model-scale governance.

Abstract: Language models draw on two knowledge sources: facts baked into weights (parametric memory, PM) and information in context (working memory, WM). We study two mechanistically distinct failure modes--conflict, when PM and WM disagree and interfere; and hallucination, when the queried fact was never learned. Both produce confident output regardless, making output-based monitoring blind by design. We show both failures share a unified geometric account. In the hidden-state space of autoregressive generation, learned facts form attractor basins. Conflict is basin competition: WM disrupts convergence to the correct basin without raising output entropy. Hallucination is basin absence: the hidden state drifts freely when no memorized basin exists. The frozen LM head, designed for next-token prediction, cannot distinguish these cases and fires confidently either way. We verify this account in a controlled synthetic task--entity identifiers mapped to unique codes with PM installed via LoRA adapters--where ground truth is exact and component roles can be causally isolated through targeted adapter placement. Geometric margin--the hidden state's distance to the nearest memorized basin--reads this geometry directly and separates correct recall from hallucination far more cleanly than output entropy, with zero false refusals where entropy-based detection cannot avoid rejecting the vast majority of correct outputs. The separation holds on natural-language factual queries from the pretrained model with no adaptation, confirming attractor geometry is structural rather than a fine-tuning artifact. The fraction of confident hallucinations follows a scaling law $C = \exp(-c/\barΔ)$, growing with scale even as overall error rates fall. Hidden states reliably encode epistemic state; the frozen output head systematically erases it--and this erasure worsens with scale.

</details>


### [396] [DataDignity: Training Data Attribution for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05687)
*Xiaomin Li,Andrzej Banburski-Fahey,Jaron Lanier*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes FakeWiki, a large synthetic benchmark to evaluate pinpoint provenance for LLM outputs, and introduces two provenance-ranking methods, including a supervised ScoringModel and a zero-shot SteerFuse fusion, showing strong gains in Recall@10 across multiple models and prompt conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auditors need to identify which source documents support a given response, not just whether the response is correct. Existing baselines often exploit lexical shortcuts or topical similarity. A robust evaluation requires datasets that preserve true provenance while challenging superficial cues and adversarial prompts.

Method: Create FakeWiki with 3,537 fabricated Wikipedia-style articles preserving ground-truth provenance but weakening lexical shortcuts. Generate QA probes, source-preserving paraphrases, retro-generated variants, hard anti-documents, and five query conditions (clean plus four jailbreak-inspired transformations). Evaluate seven retrieval baselines, SteerFuse, and a supervised ScoringModel. ScoringModel maps response and document features into a shared space and is trained with InfoNCE using in-batch, retrieval-mined, and anti-document negatives. Test across nine open-weight instruction-tuned LLMs.

Result: ScoringModel raises mean Recall@10 from 35.0 to 52.2 without inference-time fusion, wins 41/45 model-by-condition cells, and SteerFuse often ranks second. On jailbreak-like queries, ScoringModel improves Recall@10 by 15.7 points on average over the best baseline.

Conclusion: Evaluation of provenance attribution requires settings that decouple true answer support from topical or lexical resemblance; the proposed dataset and scoring approach yield robust improvements in provenance ranking across diverse models and prompt perturbations.

Abstract: Auditing language-model outputs often requires more than judging correctness: an auditor may need to identify which source document most likely supports the knowledge expressed in a response. We study this as pinpoint provenance: given a prompt, a target-model response, and a candidate corpus, rank the documents that best support the response. We introduce FakeWiki, a controlled benchmark of 3,537 fabricated Wikipedia-style articles designed to preserve ground-truth provenance while weakening lexical shortcuts. FakeWiki includes QA probes, source-preserving paraphrases, retro-generated variants, hard anti-documents that remain topically similar while removing answer-critical facts, and five query conditions: clean prompting plus four jailbreak-inspired transformations. We evaluate seven retrieval baselines, a training-free activation-steering retrieval-fusion method, SteerFuse, and a supervised contrastive provenance ranker, ScoringModel. ScoringModel maps response and document features into a shared space and is trained with InfoNCE using in-batch, retrieval-mined, and anti-document negatives. Across nine open-weight instruction-tuned LLMs and five query conditions, ScoringModel improves mean Recall@10 from 35.0 for the strongest retrieval baseline to 52.2, without inference-time fusion, and wins 41/45 model-by-condition cells. SteerFuse is usually second-best despite requiring no supervised training, showing that activation-space evidence can efficiently complement text retrieval. On jailbreak-inspired transformed queries, ScoringModel improves Recall@10 by 15.7 points on average over the best baseline. Overall, our work shows that robust training data attribution requires evaluation settings that separate true answer support from topical or lexical resemblance.

</details>


### [397] [GCCM: Enhancing Generative Graph Prediction via Contrastive Consistency Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05689)
*Shaozhen Ma,Wei Huang,Hanchen Wang,Dong Wen,Wenjie Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes GCCM, a graph diffusion model with contrastive consistency and feature perturbations to prevent shortcut collapse in graph prediction, achieving stable sampling and improved performance over deterministic baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address shortcut collapse in consistency-training for diffusion-based graph prediction and stabilize/increase performance while reducing inference steps.

Method: Introduce GCCM: a graph contrastive consistency objective with negative pairs, plus input feature perturbations to break identical conditioning; extend beyond simple self-consistency to include negatives, increasing separation requirements.

Result: Empirical results on benchmark graph datasets show GCCM mitigates the shortcut and yields consistent performance gains over deterministic predictors.

Conclusion: GCCM effectively mitigates shortcut solutions in diffusion-based graph prediction, providing more robust and reliable predictions with improved performance.

Abstract: Conditional generative models, particularly diffusion-based methods, have recently been applied to graph prediction by modeling the target as a conditional distribution given the input graph, yielding competitive results compared to deterministic predictor. However, existing diffusion-based prediction methods typically require expensive iterative denoising at inference and often suffer from unstable sampling, which motivates recent efforts to reduce inference denoising steps and enable stable sampling via techniques such as consistency training. Despite this progress, we find that existing consistency training methods for graph prediction could potentially fall into a shortcut solution: the model may attempt to satisfy the self-consistency constraint by ignoring the noisy target (i.e., assigning it negligible weight), ultimately collapsing into a purely deterministic predictor. To mitigate such shortcut solution, we propose GCCM, a graph contrastive consistency model that goes beyond isolated pairwise matching between the same target at different noise levels by introducing negative pairs into a contrastive consistency objective. This adds an additional separation requirement, making the shortcut solution no longer trivially sufficient to satisfy the proposed objective. Moreover, we apply feature perturbation to the input node/edge features to break identical conditioning on the input graph, so that the shortcut no longer yields the same predictions across noise levels and becomes less attractive. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that GCCM mitigates the shortcut solution and yields consistent performance improvements in graph prediction compared to deterministic predictors.

</details>


### [398] [Saliency-Aware Regularized Quantization Calibration for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05693)
*Yanlong Zhao,Xiaoyuan Cheng,Huihang Liu,Baihua He,Xinyu Zhang,Harrison Bo Hua Zhu,Wenlong Chen,Li Zeng,Zhuo Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SARQC adds a saliency-aware regularization term to post-training quantization (PTQ), guiding quantized weights to stay close to full-precision weights. This unified objective improves generalization during inference across scale-search and Gram-based PTQ methods, yielding better perplexity and zero-shot accuracy for dense and Mixture-of-Experts LLMs without extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibration-based PTQ optimizes layer-wise reconstruction error on limited calibration data, which can push quantized weights away from the original full-precision weights and worsen generalization. A regularization term that respects weight saliency can constrain quantized weights, reducing generalization risk.

Method: Introduce a saliency-aware regularization term into the PTQ objective, creating a unified framework (SARQC) that augments standard PTQ losses. The approach is compatible with both scale-search and Gram-based PTQ and enforces closeness to the original weights during calibration, thereby improving generalization.

Result: Empirical validation on dense and Mixture-of-Experts LLMs shows consistent improvements in perplexity and zero-shot accuracy. Importantly, the method incurs no additional computational overhead during inference and complements existing PTQ pipelines.

Conclusion: SARQC provides a simple yet effective regularization augmentation to PTQ that reduces generalization risk by keeping quantized weights close to their full-precision counterparts. It is applicable across PTQ methods and improves downstream performance without increasing inference cost.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is an effective approach for deploying large language models (LLMs) under memory and latency constraints. Most existing PTQ methods determine quantization parameters by minimizing a layer-wise reconstruction error on a predetermined calibration dataset, usually optimized via either scale search or Gram-based methods. However, from the perspective of generalization risk, existing calibration objectives of PTQ based only on empirical reconstruction error on limited or unrepresentative calibration data could move the quantized weights away from the original weights. This may cause the generalization risk to diverge, potentially degrading downstream performance. To address this issue, we propose \emph{Saliency-Aware Regularized Quantization Calibration} (SARQC) a unified framework that augments the standard PTQ objective with a saliency-aware regularization term. This term encourages quantized weights to stay close to the original weights during calibration, leading to improved generalization during inference. SARQC integrates seamlessly into existing PTQ pipelines, enhancing both scale search and Gram-based methods under a unified formulation. Extensive experiments on dense and Mixture-of-Experts LLMs demonstrate consistent improvements in perplexity and zero-shot accuracy, without additional computational overhead during inference.

</details>


### [399] [Inference-Time Budget Control for LLM Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05701)
*Zhengru Fang,Senkang Forest Hu,Zhonghao Chang,Yu Guo,Yihang Tao,Hongyao Liu,Mengzhe Ruan,Jun Huang,Yuguang Fang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Two-stage inference-time budget control for LLM search agents in multi-hop QA using VOI-based action scoring, with a selective finalizer; yields gains under dual budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Under simultaneous limits on tool calls and token generation, allocate budget to actions that maximize marginal task value and decide when evidence suffices to commit to an answer.

Method: During search, assign each feasible action a task-level VOI score (value per budget unit) and select among retrieval, decomposition, and answer commitment; after search, use a selective evidence-grounded finalizer to compare and rewrite if a low-risk residual error remains.

Result: Across four multi-hop QA benchmarks, three LLM backbones, and four budget levels, the approach achieves positive gains over four baselines under the same dual-budget protocol; ablations show search-time budget control (esp. budget-dependent penalty) drives main gains, while answer-time control helps when the retrieval path is already adequate.

Conclusion: Inference-time budget control should govern both how budget is spent during search and how the final answer is committed for LLM search agents.

Abstract: LLM search agents increasingly rely on tools at inference time, but their trajectories are often constrained by hard limits on both tool calls and generated tokens. Under such dual budgets, better answers require not only stronger models, but also explicit control over which search action should receive the next budget unit and when the accumulated evidence is sufficient to commit a final answer. We study this problem in multi-hop question answering (QA) and formulate it as two-stage inference-time budget control. At search time, our controller assigns each feasible action a task-level Value-of-Information (VOI) score, defined as an operational estimate of marginal task value per unit budget under the current search state and remaining dual budget, and uses this score to choose among retrieval, decomposition, and answer commitment. After search, a selective evidence-grounded finalizer compares the trajectory answer with a refined candidate and rewrites only when the residual error appears to be a low-risk answer-form error. Across four multi-hop QA benchmarks, three LLM backbones, and four budget levels, the method yields positive aggregate gains over four audited baselines under the same hard dual-budget protocol. Ablations show that search-time budget control, especially budget-dependent penalty, provides the main performance gain, while answer-time control helps mainly when the retrieval path is already adequate. These results suggest that inference-time budget control for LLM search agents should govern both how budget is spent during search and how the final answer is committed.

</details>


### [400] [Proactive Instance Navigation with Comparative Judgment for Ambiguous User Queries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06223)
*Junhyuk Kwon,Seungjoon Lee,Hyejin Park,Kyle Min,Jungseul Ok*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ProCompNav uses a two-stage, pool-based strategy for instance navigation: first build a candidate pool, then identify the target via comparative judgment by asking discriminative, binary questions that split the pool and prune inconsistencies, reducing dialogue length while boosting success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods either stop at the first plausible candidate or react to each candidate independently, leading to inefficient disambiguation and long user responses. A pool-level, discriminative questioning framework can minimize user burden while ensuring reliable target distinction among distractors.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) construct a candidate pool; (2) perform comparative judgment to identify the target. At each round, extract an attribute-value pair that splits the current pool, ask a binary yes/no question, and prune all inconsistent candidates. This reframes disambiguation as pool-level discrimination rather than per-candidate attribute queries.

Result: On CoIN-Bench, ProCompNav improves Success Rate over interactive baselines with the same minimal input and over non-interactive baselines with detailed descriptions, while substantially reducing Response Length. It also achieves state-of-the-art Success Rate on TextNav, indicating the general usefulness of comparative judgment for instance-level navigation among similar distractors.

Conclusion: Comparative judgment is broadly useful for disambiguation in instance-level navigation. The two-stage pool-based approach reduces user burden and improves success rates, suggesting applicability beyond the evaluated datasets.

Abstract: Natural-language instance navigation becomes challenging when the initial user request does not uniquely specify the target instance. A practical agent should reduce the user's burden by actively asking only the information needed to distinguish the target from similar distractors, rather than requiring a detailed description upfront. Existing approaches often fall short of this goal: they may stop at the first plausible candidate before sufficiently exploring alternatives, or, even after collecting multiple candidates, ask about the target's attributes derived from individual candidates rather than questions selected to distinguish candidates in the pool. As a result, despite the dialogue, the agent may still fail to distinguish the target from distractors, leading to premature decisions and lengthy user responses. We propose Proactive Instance Navigation with Comparative Judgment (ProCompNav), a two-stage framework that first constructs a candidate pool and then identifies the target through comparative judgment. At each round, ProCompNav extracts an attribute-value pair that splits the current pool, asks a binary yes/no question, and prunes all inconsistent candidates at once. This reframes disambiguation from open-ended target description to pool-level discriminative questioning, where each question is chosen to narrow the candidate set. On CoIN-Bench, ProCompNav improves Success Rate over interactive baselines with the same minimal input and non-interactive baselines with detailed descriptions, while substantially reducing Response Length. ProCompNav also achieves state-of-the-art Success Rate on TextNav, suggesting that comparative judgment is broadly useful for instance-level navigation among similar distractors.

</details>


### [401] [Knowledge-Graph Paths as Intermediate Supervision for Self-Evolving Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05702)
*Huyu Wu,Jun Liu,Xiaochi Wei,Yan Gao,Yi Wu,Yao Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Grounds Proposer with LLM-guided knowledge-graph subgraphs and introduces Waypoint Coverage Reward to provide graded partial credit, improving self-evolving search (SSP) on seven QA benchmarks, especially multi-hop tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two bottlenecks in SSP: (1) Proposer generates questions from isolated entities without context, leading to invalid/unverifiable questions; (2) Solver only receives binary rewards, losing signal from partial progress.

Method: Ground question construction on knowledge-graph subgraphs guided by LLMs to provide relational context for the Proposer. Introduce Waypoint Coverage Reward (WCR) that gives graded partial credit to incomplete Solver trajectories based on coverage of construction-path entities, while preserving full credit for correct answers.

Result: Applying the approach across seven QA benchmarks and nine model configurations yields improved average scores over standard SSP in all configurations, with notable gains on multi-hop QA tasks.

Conclusion: Knowledge-graph paths can be reused as lightweight intermediate supervision to provide relational guidance and process feedback in SSP without extra task-specific annotations or designed process steps.

Abstract: Self-evolving search agents reduce reliance on human-written training questions by generating and solving their own search tasks. We build on Search Self-Play (SSP), a representative Proposer and Solver framework in which questions are generated and answered via multi-step search and reasoning. In practice, however, SSP faces two bottlenecks: the Proposer constructs questions from isolated answer entities without relational context, yielding many invalid or unverifiable questions in early self-play training, while the Solver receives only a binary outcome reward that discards useful signal from partially on-track search trajectories. We address both bottlenecks by reusing knowledge-graph paths as construction-derived intermediate supervision for both question construction and reward shaping. First, we ground question construction in LLM-guided knowledge-graph subgraphs, providing relational context for the Proposer. Second, we observe that constructing and solving a multi-hop question can involve overlapping intermediate entities: the factual bridges used to formulate the question may provide approximate waypoints for answering it. Exploiting this overlap, we introduce Waypoint Coverage Reward (WCR), which grants graded partial credit to incorrect Solver trajectories according to their coverage of entities on the construction path, while preserving full reward for correct answers. Across seven QA benchmarks and nine model configurations, our approach improves the average score over standard SSP in all configurations, including notable gains on multi-hop QA tasks. These results suggest that knowledge-graph paths can be reused as lightweight intermediate supervision, providing both relational guidance and process feedback without additional task-specific human annotations or manually labeled process steps.

</details>


### [402] [Resolving the bias-precision paradox with stochastic causal representation learning for personalized medicine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05706)
*Peisong Zhang,Manqiang Peng,Yuxuan Wu,Pawit Phadungsaksawasdi,Wesley Yeung,Ye Zhang,Trang Nguyen,Qiang Zhang,Nan Liu,Meng Wang,Kee Yuan Ngiam,Yih-Chung Tham,Ching-Yu Cheng,Tianfan Fu,Qingyu Chen,Rosemary Ke,Chang Li,Wenzhuo Yang,Zhenghao Lu,Chunyou Lai,Yu Zhang,Sheng Zhong,Hao Deng,Dianbo Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A bias-precision paradox-driven method sMMD for causal representation learning; replaces global balancing with subset-level sampling-based MMD; demonstrates improved counterfactual prediction and interpretability in ICU data, with up to 11.5% error reduction and accuracy gains in clinician evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the tension between reducing confounding bias and preserving clinically informative heterogeneity in causal representation learning; introduces subset-level distribution alignment to maintain heterogeneity for patient-specific predictions.

Method: Sampling-based maximum mean discrepancy (sMMD) that aligns distributions at subset level rather than globally; implemented in a counterfactual outcome prediction framework with attribution-grounded interpretability; evaluated on two large ICU cohorts (n = 27,783).

Result: Improved accuracy under distribution shift by up to 11.5%; higher recall in high-risk tasks; mechanistic analyses show selective preservation of decisive variables; human–AI evaluation: clinician accuracy up by 14.7% and reduced decision time; outperformed clinicians-in-training and large language models.

Conclusion: sMMD mitigates bias while preserving informative heterogeneity, enabling interpretable, real-time clinical decision support and better generalization under distribution shift.

Abstract: Estimating individualized treatment effects from longitudinal observational data is central to data-driven medicine, yet existing methods face a fundamental limitation: reducing confounding bias often suppresses clinically informative heterogeneity, degrading patient-specific predictions. Here, we identify this tension as a bias-precision paradox in causal representation learning and introduce sampling-based maximum mean discrepancy (sMMD), a stochastic alignment strategy that replaces global adversarial balancing with subset-level matching. We instantiate this approach in a framework for counterfactual outcome prediction with attribution-grounded interpretability. Across two large-scale ICU cohorts (n = 27,783), our framework improves accuracy under distribution shift, reducing error by up to 11.5% and substantially increasing recall in high-risk tasks. Mechanistic analyses show that sMMD selectively preserves clinically decisive variables. In human-AI evaluation, our method outperforms clinicians-in-training and large language models, and improves clinician accuracy by 14.7% while reducing decision time, enabling interpretable, real-time clinical decision support.

</details>


### [403] [Conceal, Reconstruct, Jailbreak: Exploiting the Reconstruction-Concealment Tradeoff in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05709)
*Md Farhamdur Reza,Richeng Jin,Tianfu Wu,Huaiyu Dai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A study on intent-obfuscation attacks on multimodal LLMs reveals a reconstruction-concealment tradeoff: transforms must hide harmful intent yet remain recoverable by the victim model. Current methods struggle to balance this, but character-removed variants improve the tradeoff. A concealment-aware variant construction approach selects low harmful-keyword alignment and mutually diverse variants, instantiated via five modality-aware prompting strategies. The authors introduce keyword-related distractor images to provide contextual safety challenges. Experiments across closed/open-source MLLMs show the proposed strategies outperform baselines, exposing a vulnerability: a model's own reconstruction can reveal hidden harmful intent and trigger unsafe responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and evaluate jailbreak risks in multimodal LLMs, identify the fundamental reconstruction-concealment tradeoff governing intent obfuscation, and develop strategies (variant construction, prompts, and distractors) to assess and mitigate these vulnerabilities.

Method: Reconstruction analysis of three representative black-box attack methods; development and evaluation of character-removed variants; Concealment-aware variant construction selecting variants with low harmful-keyword alignment and high mutual diversity; instantiation via five modality-aware prompting strategies; introduction of keyword-related distractor images; comprehensive experiments on closed-source and open-source MLLMs.

Result: Existing transformations struggle to balance concealment and recoverability; character-removed variants provide a better balance. The proposed concealment-aware construction and five prompting strategies outperform strong baselines. Keyword-related distractor images offer more effective auxiliary context than generic distractors. A key vulnerability is that a model’s reconstruction ability can reveal hidden harmful intent and lead to unsafe responses.

Conclusion: The work uncovers a previously underexplored vulnerability in MLLMs: their reconstruction capability can be exploited to recover hidden harmful intent, underscoring the need for more robust safety defenses and evaluation frameworks. The proposed strategies advance attack effectiveness and provide a basis for improving defenses against intent-obfuscation jailbreaks.

Abstract: Intent-obfuscation-based jailbreak attacks on multimodal large language models (MLLMs) transform a harmful query into a concealed multimodal input to bypass safety mechanisms. We show that such attacks are governed by a \emph{reconstruction--concealment tradeoff}: the transformed input must hide harmful intent from safety filters while remaining recoverable enough for the victim model to reconstruct the original request. Through a reconstruction analysis of three representative black-box methods, we find that existing transformations struggle to balance this tradeoff, limiting their effectiveness. In contrast, we show that character-removed variants achieve a better balance. Building on this, we propose \emph{concealment-aware variant construction}, which greedily selects character-removed variants that are low in harmful-keyword alignment and mutually diverse, and instantiates them through five modality-aware prompting strategies. We further introduce \emph{keyword-related distractor images} that depict the harmful keyword in diverse contexts, providing more effective auxiliary visual context than generic distractor images. Experiments across closed-source and open-source MLLMs show the proposed strategies outperform strong baselines, revealing an underexplored vulnerability: a model's own reconstruction ability can be exploited to recover hidden harmful intent and produce unsafe responses.

</details>


### [404] [Decodable but Not Corrected by Fixed Residual-Stream Linear Steering: Evidence from Medical LLM Failure Regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05715)
*Ming Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Linearly decodable failure signals in LLM hidden states do not enable correction via fixed linear steering, but support reliable post-generation abstention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test whether a linearly decodable failure direction can be used to correct or mitigate failures in a stable regime of medical QA (Overthinking), and understand why decodability does not translate into practical correction.

Method: 1) Identify and quantify Overthinking (OT) as a stable failure regime (Jaccard ≥ 0.81, 94% inter-annotator agreement). 2) Measure linear decodability of OT (71.6% balanced accuracy, p < 1e-16). 3) Evaluate five families of fixed linear steering (29 configurations, n=1,273) across architectures (Qwen2.5-7B) and domains (MMLU-STEM). 4) Examine representational entanglement via overlap with task-critical computation (85-88%), specificity ratio ≤0.152; non-targeted steering effects; LEACE erasure; random erasures. 5) Assess per-instance probe-steering correlation (r = -0.002, p=0.97). 6) Test selective abstention using the same probe (AUROC = 0.610, p=0.009) against uncertainty baselines.

Result: OT is clearly linearly decodable (71.6% balanced accuracy). However, five fixed linear steering configurations yield near-zero Δ, with null results across architectures and domains.
