Convergent evidence suggests representational entanglement: the OT direction substantially overlaps task-critical computation (85–88% overlap; specificity ≤0.152); non-targeted steering harms accuracy (−12.1 percentage points); LEACE erasure reduces accuracy (−3.6pp, p=0.01); random erasures yield slight improvement (+0.3pp). Per-instance probe–steering correlation is negligible (r=−0.002, p=0.97). Positively, the probe enables selective abstention (AUROC 0.610, p=0.009), outperforming all uncertainty baselines, indicating decodable failure structure can support reliability estimation post-generation even when it cannot be used for correction.

Conclusion: Fixed linear steering cannot exploit decodable failure signals to correct failures, likely due to representational entanglement. Nevertheless, decodable failure signals are actionable for reliability assessment via abstention, suggesting alternative strategies beyond fixed linear steering are needed to leverage such signals for correction.

Abstract: Can linearly decodable failure signals in LLM hidden states be leveraged to correct those failures? We investigate this classification-correction gap via Overthinking (OT)--a stable behavioral regime (Jaccard >= 0.81, 94% inter-annotator agreement) in medical QA where models answer correctly under resampling yet fail in extended chain-of-thought. OT is linearly decodable at 71.6% balanced accuracy (p < 10^{-16}). Yet five families of fixed linear steering (29 configurations, n=1,273) all yield Delta ~= 0, with identical null results cross-architecture (Qwen2.5-7B) and cross-domain (MMLU-STEM). Three convergent lines of evidence suggest representational entanglement: the OT direction has 85-88% overlap with task-critical computation (specificity ratio <= 0.152); non-targeted shared-direction steering damages accuracy (-12.1pp); and LEACE concept erasure damages accuracy (-3.6pp, p=0.01), while 10 random erasures produce Delta=+0.3pp. The per-instance probe-steering correlation is r=-0.002 (p=0.97). Positively, the same probe enables selective abstention (held-out AUROC=0.610, exceeding all five uncertainty baselines, p=0.009): decodable failure structure supports post-generation reliability estimation even when the fixed linear steering family cannot exploit it for correction.

</details>


### [405] [Detecting Time Series Anomalies Like an Expert: A Multi-Agent LLM Framework with Specialized Analyzers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05725)
*Hyeongwon Kang,Jeongseob Kim,Jinwoo Park,Pilsung Kang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SAGE is a multi-agent framework for structured anomaly diagnosis in univariate time series. It uses four specialized analyzers for point, structural, seasonal, and pattern anomalies, plus an evidence-grounded detector and a supervisor to produce analyst-facing reports. It creates synthetic in-context examples from normal data, without using real anomalies for in-context labeling. On three benchmarks, it outperforms strong baselines; ablations/human eval show higher reliability and practical usefulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current time-series anomaly detection often relies on a single general-purpose model, which can limit controllability, interpretability, and reliability for complex anomaly patterns. There is a need for modular, evidence-based diagnosis with interpretable outputs and minimal reliance on labeled anomaly data for in-context learning.

Method: Introduce SAGE, a four-analyzer framework (point, structural, seasonal, pattern) that uses family-specific numerical tools and diagnostic visualizations to generate evidence. An evidence-grounded Detector consolidates evidence into confidence-scored anomaly records with intervals and candidate types. A Supervisor converts structured records into analyst-facing diagnostic reports. Synthetic in-context examples are constructed from normal-reference segments, avoiding real anomalous examples and anomaly-type labels in prompts.

Result: SAGE achieves the best average performance among strong ML/DL and language-model baselines across three benchmarks. Ablation studies and human evaluation indicate improved detection reliability and greater practicality of diagnostic outputs.

Conclusion: Modular, evidence-grounded anomaly analysis with specialized agents enhances controllability, interpretability, and reliability in time-series anomaly detection; synthetic in-context learning from normal data offers label-free guidance for analysts.

Abstract: Recent studies have explored large language models for time-series anomaly detection, yet existing approaches often rely on a single general-purpose model to directly infer anomaly indices or intervals, limiting controllability, interpretability, and reliability for complex anomaly patterns. We propose SAGE (Specialized Analyzer Group for Expert-like Detection), a multi-agent framework for structured anomaly diagnosis in univariate time series. It decomposes anomaly analysis into four specialized Analyzers for point, structural, seasonal, and pattern anomalies. Each Analyzer applies family-specific numerical tools and diagnostic visualizations to generate evidence, while an evidence-grounded Detector consolidates the evidence into confidence-scored anomaly records with intervals and candidate types. A Supervisor then converts these structured records into analyst-facing diagnostic reports. SAGE further constructs synthetic in-context examples from normal-reference training segments, without using real anomalous segments or anomaly-type labels as in-context examples. Across three benchmarks, SAGE achieves the best average performance among strong ML/DL and language-model-based baselines. Ablation studies and human evaluation further show that the proposed framework improves detection reliability and the practical usefulness of diagnostic outputs.

</details>


### [406] [SkillRet: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Skill Retrieval in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05726)
*Hongcheol Cho,Ryangkyung Kang,Youngeun Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillRet: a large-scale skill retrieval benchmark for LLM agents with 17,810 skills, a 6-category and 18-subcategory taxonomy, 63,259 training samples, and 4,997 evaluation queries; current retrievers underperform; task-specific fine-tuning yields large gains; establishes a benchmark for retrieval in large-scale agent systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM agents accumulate extensive libraries of skills, selecting the appropriate skill under tight latency constraints becomes critical. Existing benchmarks are limited and do not capture retrieval behavior on realistic, large-scale skill libraries.

Method: Construct SkillRet with structured semantic tags and a two-level taxonomy (6 major categories, 18 sub-categories). Generate training samples and evaluation queries with disjoint skill pools. Benchmark diverse retrievers, then apply task-specific fine-tuning. Analyze why improvements occur (signal quality within long/noisy queries).

Result: Off-the-shelf retrievers struggle on realistic large-scale skill libraries; prior retrieval models show remaining headroom. Task-specific fine-tuning significantly improves performance, with NDCG@10 gains of +13.1 over the strongest prior retriever and +16.9 over the strongest off-the-shelf retriever.

Conclusion: SkillRet is a strong benchmark and a foundation for future research on retrieval in large-scale agent systems; it highlights clear room for improvement and demonstrates the efficacy of task-specific fine-tuning in improving skill retrieval.

Abstract: As LLM agents are increasingly deployed with large libraries of reusable skills, selecting the right skill for a user request has become a critical systems challenge. In small libraries, users may invoke skills explicitly by name, but this assumption breaks down as skill ecosystems grow under tight context and latency budgets. Despite its practical importance, skill retrieval remains underexplored, with limited benchmarks and little understanding of retrieval behavior on realistic skill libraries. To address this gap, we introduce SkillRet, a large-scale benchmark for skill retrieval in LLM agents. SkillRet contains 17,810 public agent skills, organized with structured semantic tags and a two-level taxonomy spanning 6 major categories and 18 sub-categories. It provides 63,259 training samples and 4,997 evaluation queries with disjoint skill pools, enabling both benchmarking and retrieval-oriented training. Across a diverse set of retrievers, we find that skill retrieval remains far from solved: off-the-shelf models struggle on realistic large-scale skill libraries, and prior skill-retrieval models still leave substantial headroom. Task-specific fine-tuning on SkillRet substantially improves performance, improving NDCG@10 by +13.1 points over the strongest prior retriever and by +16.9 points over the strongest off-the-shelf retriever. Our analysis further suggests that these gains arise because fine-tuned models better focus on the small skill-relevant signals within long and noisy queries. These results establish SkillRet as a strong benchmark and foundation for future research on retrieval in large-scale agent systems.

</details>


### [407] [Knee Osteoarthritis Severity Grading Using Optimized Deep Learning and LLM-Driven Intelligent AI on Computationally Limited Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05731)
*Dayam Nadeem,Neha,Safdar Mustafa,Adnan Alvi,Mohd Hussain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: On-device KOA severity grading using ResNet-18 with TensorFlow Lite; achieves ~94.5% accuracy; adds LLM-based interpretive outputs; offline, lightweight, interpretable AI-assisted knee screening.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address subjectivity and variability in KOA grading; enable accurate, timely diagnosis; deployable on resource-constrained devices with offline operation; enhance interpretability via LLM-generated insights.

Method: Transfer-learned ResNet-18 CNN trained on public KOA dataset to classify knee radiographs into five Kellgren-Lawrence grades; model optimized and converted to TensorFlow Lite for on-device inference; LLM Gemini-2.0-flash used to produce structured interpretive findings, as an interface not affecting classification.

Result: Test accuracy of 94.48% with stable convergence; on-device inference without continuous internet; LLM-generated interpretive outputs for symptoms, risk factors, prevention; demonstrates feasibility of on-device, interpretable AI-assisted KOA screening.

Conclusion: Shows feasibility of an offline, lightweight, interpretable decision-support tool for early KOA diagnosis; potential to improve accessibility and consistency of KOA assessment; requires external validation and assessment of robustness, bias, and interpretability of LLM outputs.

Abstract: Knee osteoarthritis (KOA) is among the musculoskeletal disorders that considerably restrict joint mobility, cause severe chronic pain and impact negatively on quality life. It is one of the persistent health issues worldwide. Generally, subjectivity and inter-observer variability undermine conventional practices and evaluation process that are adopted to address such health issues. Hence precise and timely diagnosis would be one of the effective ways for the assessment of its severity. This paper proposes an automated diagnostic approach for severity grading of KOA by blending a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) with a device-based inference platform powered by TensorFlow Lite. It proposes a model based on the ResNet-18 convolutional neural network. The designed model is trained on publicly available database. Through a transfer learning approach obtained knee images are first classified into five Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) grades. Further the developed model is optimised. During the training of the model test accuracy of 94.48% with stable convergence has been achieved. Subsequently the optimised model transformed into a lightweight TensorFlow Lite format, facilitating seamless deployment on resource-constrained devices. The designed model is capable enough to operate in the environment having no continuous internet connectivity. Also, an auxiliary Large Language Model (Gemini-2.0-flash) is applied to generate structured interpretive findings like potential symptoms, risk factors, and preventive majors etc. The LLM component functions as interface without influencing the classification process. The proposed model articulates the feasibility of an on-device, interpretable decision-support tools for early diagnosis and improve accessibility to Artificial Intelligence (AI)-assisted knee screening tool.

</details>


### [408] [SDFlow: Similarity-Driven Flow Matching for Time Series Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05736)
*Wei Li,Shibo Feng,Pengcheng Wu,Min Wu,Peilin Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SDFlow introduces a non-autoregressive time-series generator that operates entirely in frozen VQ latent space using flow matching, addressing exposure bias, high-dimensional latent spaces, and discrete supervision; it achieves state-of-the-art scores and faster inference compared with autoregressive models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional VQ+autoregressive (AR) models suffer from exposure bias during long-horizon generation. There is a need for a parallel, accurate, and efficient generation framework that handles high-dimensional latent tokens and discrete codebooks.

Method: 1) Replace step-wise token prediction with a global transport map (flow matching) to remove exposure bias. 2) Use a low-rank manifold decomposition with a learned anchor prior to mitigate high-dimensional latent spaces. 3) Incorporate discrete supervision by introducing a categorical posterior over codebook indices within a variational flow-matching framework.

Result: SDFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance, notably improving Discriminative Score and Context-FID, with pronounced gains on long-sequence generation. It also offers substantial inference speedups over autoregressive baselines while maintaining high fidelity.

Conclusion: SDFlow demonstrates that non-autoregressive flow-matching in frozen VQ latent space is a scalable, accurate, and efficient paradigm for time-series generation, effectively mitigating exposure bias and latent-space challenges; code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Vector quantization (VQ) with autoregressive (AR) token modeling is a widely adopted and highly competitive paradigm for time-series generation. However, such models are fundamentally limited by exposure bias: during inference, errors can accumulate across sequential predictions, leading to pronounced quality degradation in long-horizon generation. To address this, we propose SDFlow ($\textbf{S}$imilarity-$\textbf{D}$riven $\textbf{Flow}$ Matching), a non-autoregressive framework that operates entirely in the frozen VQ latent space and enables parallel sequence generation via flow matching. We tackle three key challenges in making this transition: (1) eliminating exposure bias by replacing step-wise token prediction with a global transport map; (2) mitigating the high-dimensionality of VQ token spaces via a low-rank manifold decomposition with a learned anchor prior over the latent manifold; and (3) incorporating discrete supervision into continuous transport dynamics by introducing a categorical posterior over codebook indices within a variational flow-matching formulation. Extensive experiments show that SDFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving Discriminative Score and substantially reducing Context-FID, particularly for challenging long-sequence generation. Moreover, SDFlow provides significant inference speedups over autoregressive baselines, offering both high fidelity and computational efficiency. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SDFlow-D6F3/

</details>


### [409] [ReFlect: An Effective Harness System for Complex Long-Horizon LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05737)
*Fan Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReFlect provides a model-agnostic, training-free error-detection and recovery harness that wraps LLMs to detect and recover from its own failures, improving long-horizon reasoning across diverse models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reasoning paradigms fail on long-horizon tasks due to error accumulation and brittle self-critique; need a deterministic recovery mechanism.

Method: Standalone error detection and recovery logic wrapped around the model at inference; uses reflection blocks, state and operators; model-agnostic; training-free.

Result: Across six domains and six models, reported gains: 41%-56% task success; +7 to +29 percentage points vs Direct CoT; SWE-bench patch-structural quality from 0% to 82-87%; inverse relation between baseline success and harness gain; limited gains when models too small to populate state; fixed at inference time.

Conclusion: ReFlect demonstrates a viable, training-free wrapper to improve long-horizon reasoning with model-agnostic error detection and recovery; effective but with model-dependent gains and constraints on state population; deployable at inference time.

Abstract: Current reasoning paradigms for LLMs include chain-of-thought, ReAct, and post-hoc self-critique. These paradigms rely on two assumptions that fail on long-horizon, multi-stage tasks. As a result, errors accumulate silently across reasoning steps, leaving an open question: can a reasoning system effectively detect and recover from its own failures? We present ReFlect, a \emph{harness} system for LLM reasoning that creates standalone error detection and recovery logic as a deterministic wrapper around the model. Controlled experiments across 6 reasoning domains show that prompt-level self-critique produces formulaic templates that flag no issues in 90 of 100 audited reflection blocks, and the investigated LLMs wrongly accept a wrong answer in at least 76\% of cases. Our ReFlect harness achieves task success rates ranging from 41\% on gpt-4o-mini to 56\% on Claude Sonnet 4.5 across six models spanning small and frontier scale, with per-model gains over Direct CoT ranging from +7 pp on Qwen2.5-72B to +29 pp on Claude Sonnet 4.5, and additionally raises SWE-bench patch-structural quality from 0\% (Direct CoT) to between 82\% (Qwen2.5-72B) and 87\% (GPT-4o). Notably, the harness gain is inversely proportional to the model's Direct CoT task success rate (the fitted slope is -1.69 with r=-0.76): each pp lost in baseline success rate is mechanically recovered by 1.69 pp of harness gain. We spot that adding structured reasoning state and operators yields only 15.0--18.7\% pair-mean on Llama-3.3-70B and Qwen2.5-72B because models at this scale cannot reliably populate the state its operators require. ReFlect is model-agnostic, training-free, and operates entirely at inference time.

</details>


### [410] [HyperLens: Quantifying Cognitive Effort in LLMs with Fine-grained Confidence Trajectory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05741)
*Chengda Lu,Xiaoyu Fan,Wei Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Author(s) identify an intrinsic magnification mechanism in transformer architectures where deeper layers amplify small shifts in layer-wise confidence, enabling a high-resolution confidence trajectory. They introduce HyperLens, a high-resolution probe to trace confidence trajectories and estimate cognitive effort during inference. Across LLMs and tasks, they observe task-dependent divergence in confidence trajectories that separates simple from complex tasks, leading to a quantitative cognitive effort metric. The main conclusions are that complex tasks entail higher cognitive effort, and SFT can inadvertently reduce cognitive effort and harm in-domain performance, offering a mechanistic diagnosis for this side effect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current analysis tools lack fine-grained resolution to understand inference dynamics in LLMs. A deeper, layer-wise view of confidence and its evolution could reveal how complexity drives cognitive effort and affect performance, guiding both model design and fine-tuning practices.

Method: 1) The authors identify a magnification mechanism in transformers where deeper layers magnify small changes in confidence. 2) They design HyperLens, a high-resolution probe to trace confidence trajectories across layers and quantify cognitive effort. 3) They empirically evaluate across multiple LLMs and datasets to characterize confidence trajectories and derive a cognitive effort metric. 4) They provide a mechanistic diagnosis for SFT side effects on cognitive effort and performance.

Result: HyperLens reveals a consistent divergence in confidence trajectories that separates complex from simple tasks. The proposed cognitive effort metric correlates with task complexity and can predict performance trends. A notable finding is that standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) can reduce cognitive effort, which in turn degrades performance on in-domain tasks, suggesting a mechanism for negative transfer or mismatch.

Conclusion: A fundamental principle emerges: complex tasks require higher cognitive effort, as captured by confidence trajectories. HyperLens offers a high-resolution diagnostic tool for inference dynamics in LLMs, and the observed SFT side effect provides a mechanistic explanation for certain in-domain performance drops, highlighting the trade-off between fine-tuning and cognitive load.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across diverse tasks, their inference dynamics remain poorly understood because of the limited resolution of existing analysis tools. In this work, we identify an intrinsic magnification mechanism in transformer architectures: deeper layers inherently magnify the small changes of layer-wise confidence, providing a fine-grained confidence trajectory. Building on this insight, we introduce HyperLens, a high-resolution probe designed to trace confidence trajectories and quantify the cognitive effort during inference. Across LLMs and datasets, HyperLens reveals a consistent divergence in confidence trajectories that separates complex from simple tasks. We abstract this pattern into a quantitative cognitive effort metric. Our analysis reveals a fundamental principle: complex tasks consistently require higher cognitive effort. Finally, we provide a mechanistic diagnosis of a common side effect of standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): it can reduce cognitive effort and consequently degrade performance on in-domain tasks.

</details>


### [411] [Best Arm Identification in Generalized Linear Bandits via Hybrid Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05745)
*Qirun Zeng,Xuchuang Wang,Jiayi Shen,Xutong Liu,Fang Kong,Jinhang Zuo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A likelihood-ratio confidence sequence for fixed-confidence best-arm identification in generalized linear bandits with hybrid absolute and dueling feedback; a Track-and-Stop policy over arms and pairs; delta-correctness with stopping-time bounds; cost-aware extension; empirical gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficiently identifying the best arm under GLMs when feedback comes from heterogeneous modalities (absolute rewards and duels) and acquisition costs differ, by unifying observations into a single confidence framework and adaptive design.

Method: Derive a likelihood-ratio–based confidence sequence under a self-concordant GLM assumption, yielding an ellipsoidal confidence set. Develop a hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm that adaptively allocates queries by tracking a minimax-optimal design over the joint action space of arms and pairs. Extend to a cost-aware setting with heterogeneous feedback costs.

Result: Proves δ-correctness and derives high-probability upper bounds on the stopping time. Demonstrates substantial sample-efficiency gains in empirical experiments compared with baselines.

Conclusion: The framework provides a general, efficient approach for fixed-confidence BAI in GLMs with mixed feedback modalities, with an extensible cost-aware variant and strong empirical validation.

Abstract: We study fixed-confidence best arm identification in generalized linear bandits under a hybrid feedback model: at each round, the learner may query either (i) absolute reward feedback from a single arm or (ii) relative (dueling) feedback from an arm pair, both governed by generalized linear models. We introduce a likelihood-ratio--based confidence sequence that unifies heterogeneous generalized linear observations and yields an explicit ellipsoidal confidence set under a self-concordance assumption. Building on this confidence set, we propose a hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm that adaptively allocates queries by tracking a minimax-optimal design over a joint action space of arms and pairs. We establish $δ$-correctness and provide high-probability upper bounds on the stopping time. We further extend the framework to a cost-aware setting that accounts for heterogeneous acquisition costs across feedback modalities. Empirical experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithms significantly improve sample efficiency over baseline methods.

</details>


### [412] [Evaluating Explainability in Safety-Critical ATR Systems: Limitations of Post-Hoc Methods and Paths Toward Robust XAI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05748)
*Vanessa Buhrmester,David Muench,Dimitri Bulatov,Michael Arens*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A taxonomy-driven, assurance-oriented evaluation of XAI for safety-critical ATR, revealing limitations of post-hoc methods and advocating for robust, causally-grounded, physically-informed explainability to support system-level assurance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainability is essential in safety-critical ATR where high predictive performance alone is insufficient; explanations must be interpretable, robust, and verifiable to enable validation and trust.

Method: Formalize explainability as an assurance-oriented assessment; identify major XAI paradigms (saliency-based, attention-based, surrogate models, detection-aware extensions); develop a taxonomy; evaluate methods along four dimensions: interpretability, robustness, vulnerability to manipulation, and suitability for validation/verification.

Result: Found systematic limitations in current post-hoc explanations, including spurious explanations, instability under perturbations, and overtrust due to convincingly visual outputs; many XAI techniques may be inadequate for safety-critical deployment; propose directions toward robust, causally grounded, and physically informed explainability to improve reliability and system-level assurance.

Conclusion: To enable safe ATR deployment, XAI must move beyond visually plausible explanations toward approaches that support reliable decision-making, incorporating causal and physical constraints to achieve true assurance.

Abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is increasingly rec ognized as essential for deploying machine learning systems in safety critical environments. In Automatic Target Recognition (ATR), where models operate on image, video, radar, and multisensor data, high pre dictive performance alone is insufficient. Model decisions must also be interpretable, reliable, and suitable for validation. This paper presents a structured evaluation of explainability methods in the context of safety-critical ATR systems: We identify major XAI paradigms, including saliency-based, attention-based, and surrogate ap proaches, as well as recent detection-aware extensions. Based on this, we formalize explainability as an assurance-oriented assessment problem, introduce a taxonomy, and assess these methods with respect to four key dimensions: interpretability, robustness, vulnerability to manipula tion, and suitability for validation and verification. The analysis identifies systematic limitations of current post-hoc explanation methods. In par ticular, we derive critical failure modes such as spurious explanations, instability under perturbations, and overtrust induced by visually con vincing outputs. These findings indicate that widely used XAI techniques may be insufficient for safety-critical deployment. Finally, we discuss implications for ATR systems and outline directions toward more robust, causally grounded, and physically informed explain ability methods. Our results emphasize the need to move beyond visually plausible explanations toward approaches that support reliable decision making and system-level assurance.

</details>


### [413] [Confidence is the key: how conformal prediction enhances the generative design of permeable peptides](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05770)
*Laura van Weesep,Sunay Chankeshwara,Leonardo De Maria,Florian David,Ola Engkvist,Gökçe Geylan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An RL-guided generative framework for designing permeable cyclic peptides uses an uncertainty-aware permeability predictor with conformal prediction to quantify and restrict predictive uncertainty, improving reliability and efficiency and limiting exploration outside the predictor's applicability domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predictive models used in RL-driven de novo design may fail outside their domain of applicability, leading to high-reward but unreliable designs. Cyclic peptides are understudied and present permeability challenges; integrating uncertainty quantification is crucial to reliable optimization.

Method: Combine an RL-guided generative model (e.g., for cyclic peptides) with a permeability predictor. Incorporate conformal prediction to provide calibrated, confidence-based uncertainty estimates, and reward designs based on CP-informed predictions to bias sampling toward domains with reliable predictions.

Result: Rewarding generations with CP-informed predictions improves reliability and efficiency of peptide optimization and discourages exploration outside the predictor's applicability domain, bridging generative modeling with conformal prediction for the first time in this context.

Conclusion: Uncertainty quantification via conformal prediction effectively guides RL-driven cyclic peptide design, enabling more reliable permeable designs and highlighting a general strategy to couple CP with generative RL to manage predictor-domain limitations.

Abstract: Generative models coupled with reinforcement learning (RL), such as REINVENT and PepINVENT, have emerged as a powerful framework for de novo molecular design. During the ideation process these generative frameworks utilize various predictive models as part of the optimization objectives. However, the utility of the predictive models can be limited by their domain of applicability. When RL is used to explore the chemical space with predictive models, it can suggest molecules that lie outside the predictor's domain of applicability. As a result, the predictions may become less reliable, potentially steering designs into high reward but also high uncertainty chemical spaces. This is particularly pronounced for cyclic peptides which show therapeutic promise due to their modifiability and large interaction surfaces but are understudied compared to small molecules. While passive membrane permeation in cyclic peptides has attracted interest, identifying optimal permeable designs remains challenging yet crucial for targeting intracellular sites. We present an RL-guided generative framework that designs permeable cyclic peptides using an uncertainty-aware permeability predictor as the scoring component. To address predictive uncertainty, especially impacted by novel chemistry, we integrate conformal prediction (CP) as our uncertainty quantification method. CP assesses designs based on the calibrated model under a user-defined confidence level. We demonstrate that rewarding generated peptides with CP-informed predictions improves both reliability and efficiency of peptide optimization process. This also discourages exploration outside the predictor's applicability domain. This approach bridges the gap between predictive uncertainty and RL-guided exploration, showing how generative modelling and conformal prediction can be combined for the first time.

</details>


### [414] [CircuitFormer: A Circuit Language Model for Analog Topology Design from Natural Language Prompt](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05773)
*Md Touhidul Islam,Sujan Kumar Saha,Farimah Farahmandi,Mark Tehranipoor*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A study introducing CircuitFormer, a circuit-specific LLM for analog circuit design, backed by a large annotated netlist-NLP dataset and a novel circuit tokenizer (CKT) with constant vocabulary growth, achieving high syntactic and functional correctness and surpassing open-source LLMs with far fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two bottlenecks in applying LLMs to analog circuit design: scarcity of paired NL descriptions and netlists, and inadequate graph-aware tokenization that fails to capture circuit topology efficiently.

Method: 1) Assemble a large dataset of 31,341 annotated analog netlists with NL descriptions. 2) Develop Circuit Tokenizer (CKT) that mines frequent subcircuits to encode connectivity, yielding constant vocabulary size (512) and 57% shorter sequences compared to BPE, with 2.3x better compression. 3) Train CircuitFormer, a 511M parameter encoder-decoder transformer, on the dataset with CKT representations.

Result: CKT reduces sequence length by 57% and achieves 2.3x compression with a fixed 512-vocabulary. CircuitFormer attains 100% syntactic correctness and 83% functional success across major analog circuit categories, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source LLMs by 10% in syntax and 14% in function, while using 240x fewer parameters.

Conclusion: The combination of a large, domain-specific dataset and a topology-aware tokenizer enables effective analog circuit design with a powerful, compact LLM, showing strong cross-category performance and substantial parameter efficiency.

Abstract: Automating analog circuit design remains a longstanding challenge in Electronic Design Automation (EDA). While Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized software code generation, their application to analog hardware design is hindered by two critical limitations: (i) the scarcity of analog design datasets containing natural language description of a design and its corresponding netlist, and (ii) the inefficiency of general-purpose tokenizers (e.g., Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)) in capturing the inherent graph structure of circuits. To bridge this gap, first, we curate the largest annotated dataset of analog circuit netlists to date, comprising 31,341 netlist-natural language description pairs across all major circuit classes. Furthermore, we propose Circuit Tokenizer (CKT), a novel circuit graph tokenizer designed to encode netlist connectivity by explicitly mining frequent subcircuits. In terms of scalability, CKT overcomes the bottleneck of prior circuit graph serialization methods where vocabulary size scales linearly with maximum number of components in the dataset, n_max, (O(n_max)); instead, CKT decouples vocabulary growth from circuit complexity, achieving a constant O(1) complexity. Empirically, CKT outperforms standard BPE on circuit topology representation, reducing sequence length by 57% and achieving a 2.3x superior compression ratio using a compact, fixed vocabulary of size 512. Leveraging this optimized tokenization, we train a circuit-specific language model, CircuitFormer, a 511M parameter encoder-decoder transformer. Our model achieves 100% syntactic correctness and an 83% functional success rate across all major analog circuit categories, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source LLMs by 10% and 14%, respectively, while requiring 240x fewer parameters. The dataset is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/touhid314/cktformer-dataset.

</details>


### [415] [HEDP: A Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt-based Framework for Domain Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05776)
*Yu Feng,Zhen Tian,Haoran Luo,Xie Yu,Diancheng Cheng,Haoyue Zheng,Shuai Lyu,Ping Zong,Lianyuan Li,Xin Ge,Yifan Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt (HEDP) for domain incremental learning, combining energy regularization and a hybrid energy-distance weighting mechanism to improve unseen-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain shifts degrade performance in incremental learning; requires a framework that adapts to new domains without retraining.

Method: Energy regularization loss to enhance separability of domain representations and a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that fuses energy-based and distance-based cues for domain selection and generalization.

Result: On benchmarks including CORe50, achieves 2.57% accuracy gain on unseen domains; mitigates catastrophic forgetting and improves open-world adaptability.

Conclusion: HEDP improves domain-incremental learning performance and generalization; code released.

Abstract: Domain Incremental Learning is a critical scenario that requires models to continuously adapt to new data domains without retraining. However, domain shifts often cause severe performance degradation. To address this, we propose Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt, a domain-incremental framework inspired by Helmholtz free energy. HEDP introduces an energy regularization loss to enhance the separability of domain representations and a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that fuses energy-based and distance-based cues to improve domain selection and generalization. Experiments on multiple benchmarks, including CORe50, show that HEDP achieves superior performance on unseen domains with a 2.57\% accuracy gain, effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting and enhancing open-world adaptability. Our code is \href{https://github.com/dannis97500/HEDP/}{available here}.

</details>


### [416] [Von Neumann Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05780)
*Shekhar S. Chandra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A diffusion-based cellular automaton-inspired neural architecture called Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) constructs learnable, specialized neurons on a cellular topology. Grounded in neural operators and Green's functions, VNNs form Cellular Machines, a universal computational framework. Preliminary experiments suggest VNNs outperform standard deep nets on basic tasks with greater parameter efficiency and task adaptability, and they propose hardware-aligned extensions of Von Neumann architectures to cellular substrates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Seek a neural architecture whose topology is determined by the input/output layout on a cellular array, inspired by von Neumann's diffusion-based cellular model, enabling specialized, learnable neurons and potential hardware-aligned efficiency.

Method: Develop VNNs on a cellular topology; view them as extended neural operators learning Green's functions via convolutions on a diffusion-signature lattice; formalize a broader Computational system, Cellular Machines, with universality; implement multi-layer perceptrons using VNNs and compare to conventional DL baselines; corroborate with initial experiments on basic tasks.

Result: VNN-based MLPs outperform equivalent DL models on basic tasks, with better parameter efficiency and the ability to learn new task types; the framework supports extending the Von Neumann hardware architecture to cellular substrates, suggesting new opportunities in ML hardware design.

Conclusion: VNNs provide a hardware-inspired, universal neural framework that unites neural operators, diffusion-based cellular topology, and cellular-machine computability; they offer improved efficiency and adaptability on foundational tasks and motivate hardware-conscious neural architectures.

Abstract: In the mid-twentieth century, mathematician and polymath John von Neumann created a computational system on an array of cells as a simple model of the human brain, where each cell had one of a finite set of roles or states that he predicted would be modelled by a diffusion process. In this work, we show that such a system, when developed in a modern deep learning setting, enables the construction of an artificial neuron having specialized roles that can be learnt. We refer to this neuron as the Von Neumann neuron, and the resulting neural network from such neurons result in a self-engineered design whose architecture is only dependent on the structure and locations of its inputs and outputs on this cellular array. The mathematical framework for these Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) is also constructed and shows that they are based on the extension of neural operators and the learning of Green's functions with convolutions on a cellular topology having a diffusion signature. We also prove that these VNNs are part of a more general computational system called Cellular Machines that are computationally universal. Initial experiments show that VNN based multi-layered perceptrons outperform their equivalent deep learning variant on basic tasks, while being more parameter efficient and are capable of learning new types of tasks. This includes the ability to solve for and construct an extension of the Von Neumann (hardware) architecture common to all modern computers to cells and suggests new opportunities that could be explored.

</details>


### [417] [Sheet as Token: A Graph-Enhanced Representation for Multi-Sheet Spreadsheet Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05811)
*Yiming Lei,Yiqi Wang,Yujia Zhang,Bo Guan,Depei Zhu,Chunhui Wang,Zhuonan Hao,Tianyu Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Sheet as Token: a sheet-level tokenization with graph-enhanced retrieval for multi-sheet spreadsheets, enabling cross-sheet understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address fragmentation caused by chunk-centric representations (rows/columns/blocks) and preserve global sheet-level semantics for scalable, accurate multi-sheet reasoning.

Method: Encode each worksheet into a compact dense token by extracting schema-aware records from sheet names, column headers, representative values, and layout features; employ a Graph Retriever to construct a query-specific candidate graph over sheet tokens using semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, and shape-compatibility relations; use a multi-stage graph transformer to retrieve supporting sheet sets.

Result: Experiments on a constructed multi-sheet corpus show that sheet-level tokenization yields stable representations; graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning improves listwise retrieval compared with a shallow graph baseline, with limited additional graph-side computation.

Conclusion: Sheet-level tokenization is a promising abstraction for scalable multi-sheet spreadsheet understanding.

Abstract: Workbook-scale spreadsheet understanding is increasingly important for language-model-based data analysis agents, but remains challenging because relevant information is often distributed across multiple sheets with heterogeneous schemas, layouts, and implicit relationships. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches typically decompose spreadsheets into rows, columns, or blocks to improve scalability; however, such chunk-centric representations can fragment worksheets into isolated text spans and weaken global sheet-level semantics. We propose Sheet as Token, a graph-enhanced framework that treats each worksheet as a unified semantic unit for multi-sheet spreadsheet retrieval. Our method extracts schema-aware records from sheet names, column headers, representative values, and layout features, and encodes each worksheet into a compact dense token. Given a natural-language query, a Graph Retriever constructs a query-specific candidate graph over sheet tokens using semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, and shape-compatibility relations, and composes these channels through a multi-stage graph transformer to retrieve supporting sheet sets. Experiments on a constructed multi-sheet spreadsheet corpus show that sheet-level tokenization learns stable representations, and that graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning improves listwise retrieval over a shallow graph baseline with limited additional graph-side computation. These results suggest that sheet-level tokenization is a promising abstraction for scalable multi-sheet spreadsheet understanding.

</details>


### [418] [Long-Horizon Q-Learning: Accurate Value Learning via n-Step Inequalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05812)
*Armaan A. Abraham,Lucy Xiaoyang Shi,Chelsea Finn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LQL stabilizes long-horizon Q-learning using a hinge-loss backstop based on optimality tightening bounds; no extra networks or forward passes, improving over 1-step and n-step TD on online and offline-to-online tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bootstrapping in TD learning causes compounded estimation errors for long-horizon tasks. There is a need for principled mechanisms to bound and mitigate error propagation when learning the optimal action-value function.

Method: Introduce a hinge-loss penalty derived from an optimality-tightening inequality: realized action sequences lower-bound the achievable value by the optimal policy; penalize violations of these bounds during Q-learning without extra networks or forward passes; the penalties are computed from existing TD outputs.

Result: When integrated with multiple state-of-the-art methods on online and offline-to-online benchmarks, LQL consistently outperforms 1-step TD and n-step TD at similar runtime.

Conclusion: LQL provides an efficient, principled stabilization mechanism for long-horizon Q-learning by enforcing optimality-bound constraints via a hinge loss, improving robustness and performance without additional computational burden.

Abstract: Off-policy, value-based reinforcement learning methods such as Q-learning are appealing because they can learn from arbitrary experience, including data collected by older policies or other agents. In practice, however, bootstrapping makes long-horizon learning brittle: estimation errors at later states propagate backward through temporal-difference (TD) updates and can compound over time. We propose long-horizon Q-learning (LQL), which introduces a principled backstop against compounding error when learning the optimal action-value function. LQL builds on a prior optimality tightening observation: any realized action sequence lower-bounds what the optimal policy can achieve in expectation, so acting optimally earlier should not be worse than following the observed actions for several steps before switching to optimal behavior. Our contribution is to turn this inequality into a practical stabilization mechanism for Q-learning by using a hinge loss to penalize violations of these bounds. Importantly, LQL computes these penalties using network outputs already produced for the TD error, requiring no auxiliary networks and no additional forward passes relative to Q-learning. When combined with multiple state-of-the-art methods on a range of online and offline-to-online benchmarks, LQL consistently outperforms both 1-step TD and n-step TD learning at similar runtime.

</details>


### [419] [AGPO: Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization for Verifiable Reasoning and Search Ads Relevance at JD](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05826)
*Yang Xu,Kun Yao,Yiming Deng,Zheng Fang,Kai Ming Ting,Ming Pang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AGPO mitigates boundary shrinkage in RL-powered value routing (RLVR) by combining a negative-dominant update for disfavoring incorrect reasoning paths with a group-advantage positive update that scales by intra-group variance, preserving exploration while emphasizing rare correct paths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR often narrows reasoning patterns; while it improves sampling efficiency, base models can achieve broader coverage at large sample sizes. There is a need to maintain exploration and avoid shrinking the reasoning space as models are trained.

Method: Introduce Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization (AGPO): a negative-dominant reinforcement strategy to suppress incorrect reasoning paths, and a positive refinement via a group-advantage mechanism that scales positive updates according to intra-group variance to emphasize rare correct paths and damp trivial ones.

Result: On five mathematical benchmarks, AGPO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and consistently improves pass@k as scale increases. In a large-scale industrial setting for search Ads relevance, AGPO improves data annotation quality and yields substantial downstream gains for student models.

Conclusion: AGPO counteracts boundary shrinkage in RLVR by preserving the base model’s exploration, while guiding learning toward rarer correct paths, leading to strong performance on benchmarks and in industry.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has demonstrated notable success in enhancing the reasoning performance of large language models (LLMs). However, recent studies reveal that while current RLVR methods improve sampling efficiency towards correct paths, they do not elicit fundamentally new reasoning patterns. Instead, the reasoning capability boundary of trained models often narrows compared to their base models, with base models achieving higher coverage at large sample sizes. In this work, we propose Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization (AGPO) to counteract this boundary shrinkage. AGPO adopts a negative-dominant reinforcement strategy to suppress incorrect reasoning paths, maintaining the base model's exploration capacity. For positive reinforcement, AGPO adopts a group advantage mechanism, which scales positive updates based on intra-group variance, allowing the model to focus on rare correct paths while suppressing updates from trivial paths. Our experiments on five mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that AGPO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while consistently improving pass@$k$ performance at scale. In a large-scale industrial application for search ads relevance optimization, AGPO effectively enhances the quality of the data annotation, leading to substantial performance gains in downstream student models.

</details>


### [420] [On the Role of Language Representations in Auto-Bidding: Findings and Implications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05833)
*Guanyu Zhu,Jining Luan,Hanwen Du,Xinyu Fang,Sibo Xu,Ersheng Ni,Hongji Li,Jincheng Fang,Ronghao Chen,Huacan Wang,Xuanqi Lan,Yongxin Ni,Yiqi Sun,Youhua Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SemBid presents a synthesis of semantic guidance and numerical bidding signals for auto-bidding in real-time advertising by injecting LLM-derived semantics as tokens into offline bidding trajectories, improving controllability, constraint satisfaction, and robustness over offline RL baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auto-bidding must optimize long-horizon value under constraints (budget, CPA) but existing methods rely on compact numerical states that miss high-level intents and evolving feedback. LLMs offer semantic encoding, but naive integration harms numerical precision; combining both could enhance controllability and generalization if done carefully.

Method: SemBid injects three semantic inputs—Task, History, and Strategy—as tokens alongside numerical trajectory tokens in offline bidding sequences. Self-attention fuses semantics with numeric data to produce controllable, objective-generalizable policies. Evaluated against offline RL and generative sequence baselines under diverse budget regimes; code released.

Result: SemBid achieves stronger performance than competitive baselines, with more consistent improvements in overall returns, constraint satisfaction (budgets/CPA), and robustness across scenarios. The semantic tokens enable better controllability and generalization without sacrificing numerical precision.

Conclusion: Integrating semantic information with numerical bidding signals via token-level injection and self-attention improves auto-bidding performance and controllability. Careful design of semantic integration is crucial; the approach opens avenues for leveraging LLM-derived guidance in numeric optimization tasks within real campaigns.

Abstract: Auto-bidding is a crucial task in real-time advertising markets, where policies must optimize long-horizon value under delivery constraints (e.g., budget and CPA). Existing methods for auto-bidding rely on compact numerical state representations: while they can implicitly capture delivery dynamics, they offer limited support for explicitly representing and controlling high-level intent, evolving feedback, and operator-style strategic guidance in real campaigns. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a powerful method for encoding semantic information, it remains unclear when LLMs help and how to integrate them without sacrificing numerical precision. Through systematic preliminary studies, we find that (1) LLM embeddings contain bidding-relevant cues yet cannot replace numerical features, and (2) gains emerge only with careful semantic--numeric integration rather than naive concatenation. Motivated by these findings, we propose \textit{SemBid}, a novel auto-bidding framework that injects LLM-encoded semantics into offline bidding trajectories at the token level. SemBid introduces three semantic inputs: \textit{Task}, \textit{History}, and \textit{Strategy}. It injects these semantics as tokens alongside numerical trajectory tokens and uses self-attention to integrate them, improving controllability and generalization across objectives. Across diverse scenarios and budget regimes, SemBid outperforms competitive baselines from offline RL and generative sequence modeling, with more consistent gains in overall performance, constraint satisfaction, and robustness. Our code is available at: \href{https://github.com/AlanYu04/SemBid-KDD2026}{\textcolor{blue}{here}}.

</details>


### [421] [Taklif.AI: LLM-Powered Platform for Interest-Based Personalized College Assignments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05842)
*Zaki Kurdya,Mohammed Zuqlam,Salem Amassi,Shady Telbany,Motaz Saad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Taklif.AI: an LLM-powered platform for generating personalized assignments using students' interests and cultural contexts, with guardrails and a serverless AWS stack; preliminary user feedback is positive; rigorous empirical learning-outcome evaluation is planned.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address student disengagement and plagiarism by tailoring assignments to diverse interests, cognitive abilities, and cultural backgrounds beyond performance-based personalization.

Method: Structured prompt engineering pipeline with input/output guardrails; serverless AWS architecture using Next.js; Llama 3.3 70B via LiteLLM for load balancing; LangChain orchestration; describes system architecture, prompt design, and guardrails.

Result: Preliminary user acceptance testing with 68 participants (65 students, 3 educators) showing 84% perceived personalization as beneficial; discusses current capabilities/limitations and outlines directions for rigorous empirical evaluation.

Conclusion: Provides architectural, methodological, and guardrails-based approach for personalized AI-generated assignments; future work should validate learning outcomes through robust empirical studies.

Abstract: Educators face significant challenges in creating engaging, personalized assignments that accommodate students' diverse interests and cognitive abilities. Traditional one-size-fits-all assignments frequently lead to decreased student engagement and increased reliance on unethical practices such as plagiarism. To address these challenges, we present Taklif.AI, a platform that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically generate personalized assignments tailored to individual student interests. Unlike existing AI-powered educational platforms that personalize based on academic performance metrics alone, Taklif.AI incorporates students' extracurricular interests and cultural contexts into the assignment generation process through a structured prompt engineering pipeline with input and output guardrails. The platform employs a serverless architecture on AWS with Next.js, using Llama 3.3 70B as the primary LLM via LiteLLM for multi-provider load balancing and LangChain for prompt orchestration. We describe the system architecture, the prompt design methodology, and the guardrails framework that ensures output quality. Preliminary user acceptance testing with 68 participants (65 students and 3 educators) indicates positive reception, with 84% of participants rating the personalization feature as beneficial. We discuss the platform's current capabilities and limitations, and outline directions for rigorous empirical evaluation of learning outcomes.

</details>


### [422] [AirQualityBench: A Realistic Evaluation Benchmark for Global Air Quality Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05854)
*Xing Xu,Xu Wang,Yudong Zhang,Huilin Zhao,Zhengyang Zhou,Yang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Global, multi-pollutant hourly air-quality benchmark with real observation masks to test forecasting under realistic missingness; shows sanitized-models don't generalize to fragmented global monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between sanitized, imputed datasets and real-world monitoring by capturing uneven coverage, structured missingness, heterogeneous pollutant scales, and deployment costs.

Method: Assemble 3,720 stations with hourly data over 2021–2025, six pollutants; preserve provider-native masks; treat missingness as part of the forecasting task; evaluate after inverse transformation to physical concentrations; provide data, code, and baselines.

Result: Sanitized-data performance does not reliably transfer to global, fragmented streams; benchmark highlights the need for mask-aware, physically interpretable forecasting; baseline implementations offered.

Conclusion: AirQualityBench offers a realistic, scalable evaluation platform for air-quality forecasting and is publicly available (GitHub).

Abstract: Air-quality forecasting models are commonly evaluated on regional, preprocessed, and normalized datasets, where missing observations are removed or artificially completed. Such protocols simplify comparison but hide the conditions that dominate real monitoring networks: uneven global coverage, structured missingness, heterogeneous pollutant scales, and deployment cost. We introduce \textbf{AirQualityBench}, a global multi-pollutant benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models under these realistic conditions. The benchmark contains hourly observations from 3,720 monitoring stations over 2021--2025, covers six major pollutants, and preserves provider-native observation masks. Rather than imputing a dense data tensor, AirQualityBench exposes missingness as part of the forecasting problem and reports errors on valid future observations after inverse transformation to physical concentration scales. Evaluating representative spatio-temporal models under this unified protocol shows that strong performance on sanitized datasets does not reliably transfer to global, fragmented monitoring streams. AirQualityBench therefore serves as a realistic testbed for scalable, mask-aware, and physically interpretable air-quality forecasting. All benchmark data, code, evaluation scripts, and baseline implementations are available at \href{https://github.com/Star-Learning/AirQualityBench}{GitHub}.

</details>


### [423] [SANEmerg: An Emergent Communication Framework for Semantic-aware Agentic AI Networking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05861)
*Yong Xiao,Haoran Zhou,Yujie Zhou,Marwan Krunz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SANEmerg enables emergent communication in semantic-aware AgentNet under bandwidth constraints using a bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter and an MDL-based complexity regularizer; it improves task accuracy while reducing bandwidth and computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome inefficiencies from rigid decoupling of communication and computation in large-scale agent-centric AI networks; enable task-relevant, resource-aware signaling under bandwidth limits.

Method: Propose SANEmerg framework with (i) a bandwidth-adaptable importance filter prioritizing high-contribution message dimensions, and (ii) an MDL-based complexity regularizer to promote computationally bounded signaling; integrate into an AgentNet prototype and conduct extensive experiments against state-of-the-art.

Result: Demonstrates significant performance gains: higher task accuracy and substantially reduced bandwidth and computational overhead; robust performance in bandwidth-limited scenarios.

Conclusion: Emergent communication with resource-aware signaling can enable scalable, semantically-informed collaboration in AgentNet; MDL regularization helps constrain signaling complexity; bandwidth prioritization enhances resilience under bandwidth constraints.

Abstract: Future networking systems are envisioned to become part of an agentic AI-native ecosystem in which a vast number of heterogeneous and specialized AI agents cooperate seamlessly to fulfill complex user requirements in real time. However, traditional networking paradigms are characterized by a rigid decoupling of communication and computation, which often leads to significant inefficiencies in large-scale agentic AI networking (AgentNet) systems. Emergent communication offers a novel solution by enabling autonomous agents that support task-specific signaling protocols for information exchange and collaborative coordination. In this paper, we consider a multi-agent emergent communication framework, tailored for semantic-aware AgentNet systems in which the user's semantic intent can be automatically detected, inferred, and linked to a set of sub-tasks to be assigned to a set of agents. We investigate how communication and signaling protocols can emerge among collaborative agents with computationally bounded intelligence under stringent bandwidth constraints. Our proposed framework, called SANEmerg, is designed to facilitate the emergence of communication for collaborative task fulfillment while adhering to the physical limits of AgentNet. SANEmerg incorporates a bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter that dynamically prioritizes the transmission of higher-contribution message dimensions, ensuring robust performance in bandwidth-limited environments. Furthermore, SANEmerg integrates a complexity-regularizer grounded in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to facilitate the emergence of computationally bounded signaling. Evaluated via an AgentNet prototype and extensive experimentation, SANEmerg demonstrates significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art solutions, achieving superior task accuracy while significantly reducing bandwidth and computational overhead.

</details>


### [424] [XDecomposer: Learning Prior-Free Set Decomposition for Multiphase X-ray Diffraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05866)
*Hanyu Gao,Bin Cao,Yunyue Su,Tong-Yi Zhang,Qiang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: XDecomposer is a prior-free, multiphase XRD analysis framework that jointly decomposes and identifies phase components without prior phase lists, using a set-prediction approach to recover phase-resolved structures and proportions with good generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multiphase PXRD analysis is a bottleneck due to complex mixtures; existing methods assume single-phase inputs and rely on priors; need data-driven, prior-free source separation.

Method: Formulates multiphase diffraction as a set prediction problem. Phase-query-driven decomposition; unified architecture outputs an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their proportions, and corresponding structural representations; diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction; does not require candidate phase lists, templates, or known phase count.

Result: Experiments on simulated and experimental PXRD data show substantial improvements in reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse systems; strong generalization to unseen mixtures; code available.

Conclusion: XDecomposer provides a practical, data-driven path for source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduces reliance on prior-guided, iterative phase matching.

Abstract: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis remains a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification, as real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases (components) cannot be reliably disentangled. While recent advances in representation-based crystal retrieval and generation suggest the possibility of inferring structures directly from PXRD, existing approaches largely assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings. Here, we present XDecomposer, a prior-free framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase number. We formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. A phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism, together with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction, enables accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity. Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures. These results provide a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduce long-standing dependence on prior-guided iteratively phase matching. The code is openly available at https://github.com/Licht0812/XDecomposer

</details>


### [425] [Agentic, Context-Aware Risk Intelligence in the Internet of Value](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05878)
*Basel Magableh,OmniRisk Research*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes an IoV risk architecture with five engines to handle composite risks: price/liquidity/volatility/route health prediction; a Bittensor-based verification subnet; sentiment fusion across text/on-chain/grey-literate data; an agentic governance layer under constraints; and an API-risk/scenario engine for pre-committed actions via Monte Carlo scenarios. Anchored by a 27h Solana liquidity stress test and a 168h prediction-router calibration with explicit class-imbalance honesty; claims deployability and a formal, falsifiable validator-loss decomposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoV is a heterogeneous, partially-trusted network where marginal risk is composite (route, sentiment, liquidity, policy). A risk primitive must integrate diverse signals and enforce constraints across chains.

Method: Architectural design outlining five engines: (1) prediction engine over price/liquidity/volatility/route health; (2) Bittensor verification subnet to decentralize and economically score predictions; (3) sentiment-fusion engine aggregating text/on-chain/grey-literature feeds; (4) agentic engine enforcing constitutional, role-bound constraints; (5) API-risk/scenario engine converting forecasts into pre-committed action programs via Monte Carlo scenarios. Empirical anchors include a 27-hour liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana and a 168-hour calibration arc with explicit class-imbalance honesty; formal validator-loss decomposition.

Result: Case study supports deployability; the validator-loss decomposition is stated formally and is falsifiable.

Conclusion: The multi-engine IoV risk architecture is plausibly deployable and testable, offering a structured approach to composite risk; however, concrete quantitative results and detailed implementation are needed for full validation.

Abstract: The Internet of Value (IoV) is a heterogeneous, partially-trusted network in which the dominant marginal risk is composite (route, sentiment, liquidity, and the policy a system is willing to commit to) rather than a property of any single chain. We argue that a risk primitive adequate for this regime is a composition of five engines: a prediction engine over price, liquidity, volatility, and route health; a Bittensor verification subnet that decentralises and economically scores prediction outputs; a sentiment-fusion engine over text, on-chain flow, and grey-literature feeds; an agentic engine under constitutional, role-bound action constraints; and an API-risk and scenario engine that converts forecasts into pre-committed action programs in the sense of Monte-Carlo scenario generation. We anchor the architecture in two empirical artefacts: a 27-hour policy-constrained liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana, and a 168-hour prediction-router calibration arc reported with explicit class-imbalance honesty. The case study supports deployability; the validator-loss decomposition is stated formally and is falsifiable.

</details>


### [426] [Null Space Constrained Contrastive Visual Forgetting for MLLM Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05909)
*Yuhang Wang,Zhenxing Niu,Haoxuan Ji,Guangyu He,Linlin Zhang,Haichang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel MLLM unlearning framework that forgets targeted visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual and all textual knowledge by freezing the LLM, fine-tuning the visual module, and employing Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) plus null-space constraints; extended to continual unlearning with sequential forgetting requests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the core challenge of unlearning in multimodal large language models, where knowledge is distributed across visual and textual modalities. The goal is to forget specific visual concepts without eroding non-target visual knowledge or textual knowledge, and to handle sequential forgetting demands.

Method: Freeze the LLM backbone and fine-tune only the visual module. Introduce Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) to separate target from retained visual knowledge in feature space and guide target representations toward appropriate regions. Identify and constrain the unlearning process within the null space of retained knowledge to minimize collateral degradation. Extend the approach to continual unlearning with sequential forgetting requests.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks show a strong balance between effective forgetting of targeted visual knowledge and robust retention of non-target visual and all textual knowledge, outperforming or matching baselines in forgetting-then-retention trade-offs.

Conclusion: The proposed CVF-based unlearning framework enables targeted forgetting in MLLMs while preserving broad knowledge, and its null-space constraint helps mitigate forgetting-induced degradation, with applicability to continual unlearning scenarios.

Abstract: The core challenge of machine unlearning is to strike a balance between target knowledge removal and non-target knowledge retention. In the context of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), this challenge becomes even more pronounced, as knowledge is further divided into visual and textual modalities that are tightly intertwined. In this paper, we introduce an MLLM unlearning approach that aims to forget target visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual knowledge and all textual knowledge. Specifically, we freeze the LLM backbone and achieve unlearning by fine-tuning the visual module. First, we propose a Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) mechanism to separate target visual knowledge from retained visual knowledge, guiding the representations of target visual concepts toward appropriate regions in the feature space. Second, we identify the null space associated with retained knowledge and constrain the unlearning process within this space, thereby significantly mitigating degradation in knowledge retention. Third, beyond static unlearning scenarios, we extend our approach to continual unlearning, where forgetting requests arrive sequentially. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves a strong balance between effective forgetting and robust knowledge retention.

</details>


### [427] [PREFER: Personalized Review Summarization with Online Preference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05911)
*Millend Roy,Agostino Capponi,Vineet Goyal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Online learning framework for personalized product-review summaries that updates user models via feedback from generated summaries; improves alignment with user interests while preserving summary quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users care about different product attributes and their preferences evolve with interactions; generic summaries fail to capture individual needs.

Method: Online preference learning loop that iteratively refines per-user preference models and generates summaries; feedback from the produced summaries guides updates; case study on Amazon Reviews'23 with controlled simulations.

Result: Better alignment with target user interests while maintaining summary quality in simulations.

Conclusion: Online adaptive summarization can personalize product reviews by learning latent user preferences over time.

Abstract: Product reviews significantly influence purchasing decisions on e-commerce platforms. However, the sheer volume of reviews can overwhelm users, obscuring the information most relevant to their specific needs. Current e-commerce summarization systems typically produce generic, static summaries that fail to account for the fact that (i) different users care about different product characteristics, and (ii) these preferences may evolve with interactions. To address the challenge of unknown latent preferences, we propose an online learning framework that generates personalized summaries for each user. Our system iteratively refines its understanding of user preferences by incorporating feedback directly from the generated summaries over time. We provide a case study using the Amazon Reviews'23 dataset, showing in controlled simulations that online preference learning improves alignment with target user interests while maintaining summary quality.

</details>


### [428] [Wisteria: A Unified Multi-Scale Feature Learning Framework for DNA Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05913)
*Weihua Wang,Haoji Li,Feilong Bao,Lei Yang,Guanglai Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Wisteria unifies local motif and global dependency modeling for DNA sequences using gated dilated convolutions, gated MLPs, and Fourier-based attention, achieving strong results across short and long-range dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DNA language models emphasize long-range token interactions but often neglect the interaction between local regulatory motifs and global dependencies, hindering multi-scale genomic sequence understanding.

Method: Extend a Mamba-based architecture with gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs, plus gated multilayer perceptrons to refine global dependencies; add a Fourier-based attention mechanism to enable frequency-domain modeling, periodic extension, and length generalization.

Result: Wisteria achieves strong performance on downstream benchmarks across four experimental settings with both short and long-range dependencies, outperforming competitive DNA language model baselines.

Conclusion: The approach effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi-scale genomic sequence analysis.

Abstract: DNA language model aims to decipher the regulatory grammar and semantic of genomes by capturing long range dependencies in DNA sequences. Existing methods emphasize long range token interactions but often ignore the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies. In this paper, we propose Wisteria, a genomic language model that integrates multi scale feature learning within a unified framework for DNA sequence. Specifically, Wisteria augments the Mamba based architecture with gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs and regulatory patterns, while gated multilayer perceptrons refine global dependencies. We further introduce a Fourier based attention mechanism to support frequency domain modeling, periodic extension and length generalization. Across four experimental settings with both short and long range dependencies, Wisteria demonstrates strong performance on downstream benchmarks against competitive DNA language model baselines. These results indicate that Wisteria effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi scale genomic sequence analysis.

</details>


### [429] [Intentmaking and Sensemaking: Human Interaction with AI-Guided Mathematical Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05921)
*Alex Bäuerle,Adam Connors,Alexander Novikov,Adam Zsolt Wagner,Ngân Vũ,Fernanda Viegas,Martin Wattenberg,Lucas Dixon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A formative study with 11 expert mathematicians using AlphaEvolve identifies 'intentmaking' as a distinct, iterative workflow for defining and refining experimental goals, complementing sensemaking; the finding argues for AI tools as collaborative instruments rather than opaque assistants to support scientific discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current interaction paradigms for AI-assisted scientific discovery are underexplored and often centered on Q&A models. There is a need to understand how experts formulate and refine goals (intentmaking) and interpret results (sensemaking) to better design cooperative AI tools.

Method: Qualitative formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians using AlphaEvolve to tackle advanced problems; thematic analysis to identify workflows and characterize 'intentmaking' and its relation to sensemaking.

Result: Identification and characterization of 'intentmaking' as an iterative process of discovering, defining, and refining experimental goals; demonstration that investigators cycle between intentmaking and sensemaking during investigations; conceptual extension of sensemaking to accommodate goal-driven exploration with AI.

Conclusion: Design AI tools for scientific discovery as collaborative instruments that support goal formation and iterative interpretation, rather than opaque black-box Q&A systems; implications for interface design to foreground intentional goal setting and iterative sensemaking.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence offers powerful new tools for scientific discovery, but the interaction paradigms required to effectively harness these systems remain underexplored. In this paper, we present findings from a formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians who used AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to tackle advanced problems in their fields of expertise. We identify and characterize a distinct workflow we term intentmaking, the iterative process of discovering, defining, and refining one's experimental goals through active system interaction.
