We frame this as a natural extension to sensemaking, the cognitive process of building an understanding of complex or novel data. We suggest that users enter a cycle of intentmaking (defining and updating their experiment) and sensemaking (interpreting the results) which repeats many times during the course of an investigation. Our documentation of these themes suggests an approach to designing AI tools for scientific discovery that goes beyond the existing question/answer model of many current systems, treating them as collaborative instruments rather than opaque black-box assistants.

</details>


### [430] [Which Are the Low-Resource Languages of the Semantic Web?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05929)
*Ndeye-Emilie Mbengue,Pierre Monnin,Miguel Couceiro,Fabien Gandon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a methodology and a preliminary multi-level categorization to quantify language resources in Open Access Data (OAD) across LOD KGs, using DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata to define low-, medium-, and high-resource languages for guiding cross-lingual transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the digital divide in Open Access Data between high- and low-resource languages and the lack of a quantitative, cross-validated definition of language resource levels in the context of multilingual LOD knowledge graphs.

Method: Analyzes language distribution across LOD KGs and develops a preliminary multi-level categorization anchored on established knowledge graphs (DBpedia, BabelNet, Wikidata) to formally define resource levels (low-, medium-, high-resource).

Result: A formal, preliminary definition of resource levels for languages in LOD KGs and a categorization framework that can be used to identify cross-lingual transfer candidates.

Conclusion: The framework provides a basis for selecting languages for cross-lingual transfer and mitigating the OAD divide, with future work expected to refine and validate the categorization.

Abstract: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from the global digital transformation. Multilingual Linked Open Data Knowledge Graphs (LOD KGs) could contribute to mitigating this divide through cross-lingual transfer; however, no clear quantitative definition of low-resource languages has yet been established in the context of LOD KGs. In this poster, we present a methodology to analyze the distribution of languages across LOD KGs and propose a preliminary multi-level categorization based on DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata. This categorization is leveraged to bring a formal definition of low-, high-, and medium-resource languages that could be later leveraged to select cross-lingual transfer candidates.

</details>


### [431] [In Data or Invisible: Toward a Better Digital Representation of Low-Resource Languages with Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05931)
*Ndeye-Emilie Mbengue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A PhD proposal assessing language distribution in LOD knowledge graphs (DBpedia, BabelNet, Wikidata) and proposing cross-lingual transfer and analogical reasoning to improve multilingual KG completion and Open Access Data coverage for low-resource languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the language-coverage divide in Open Access Data (OAD) and ensure inclusive participation in the global digital transformation. Current LOD KGs exhibit uneven language representation, which disadvantages communities with low-resource languages.

Method: 1) Identify language-distribution variables: number of Wikipedia articles per language edition and language-tagged entities in LOD KGs. 2) Analyze these variables across DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata to assess language representation. 3) Study cross-lingual transfer candidate selection for multilingual KG completion, using linguistic proximity and availability of curated alignments. 4) Explore analogical reasoning based on language similarities/dissimilarities to identify cross-language correspondences to improve KG completion and language coverage.

Result: As a PhD proposal, there are no empirical results yet. Expected outcomes include a quantified picture of language distribution across major LOD KGs, and a framework for selecting cross-lingual transfer candidates and using analogical reasoning to enhance multilingual KG completion and language coverage.

Conclusion: If successful, the work will provide methodological insights and practical strategies to reduce language inequality in LOD KGs, contributing to more equitable multilingual knowledge graphs and broader participation in the digital transformation.

Abstract: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from participating in the global digital transformation. In this PhD proposal, we aim to address this gap, focusing on the language coverage of Linked Open Data knowledge graphs (LOD KGs). First, we identify key variables that characterize language distribution in LOD, including the number of Wikipedia articles per language edition and the number of language-tagged entities in LOD KGs. These variables are analyzed across three major multilingual LOD KGs, DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata, providing insights into the representation and distribution of languages within LOD. Building on this analysis, we intend to study the impact of cross-lingual transfer candidate selection on the task of multilingual KG completion. In particular, we plan to investigate strategies based on linguistic proximity and the availability of curated annotated alignments between languages. Language proximity also motivates us to explore the benefits of analogical reasoning that relies on (dis)similarities and has not yet been investigated to identify correspondences across languages to improve KG completion performance and enhance language coverage in LOD.

</details>


### [432] [ICU-Bench:Benchmarking Continual Unlearning in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05938)
*Yuhang Wang,Wenjie Mei,Junkai Zhang,Guangyu He,Zhenxing Niu,Haichang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ICU-Bench introduces a continual multimodal unlearning benchmark built on privacy-critical documents, revealing that current unlearning methods struggle to balance forgetting quality, utility, and scalability across long task sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy risks from training multimodal LLMs on large-scale datasets and the lack of benchmarks for continual privacy deletion in realistic, multimodal settings.

Method: Construct ICU-Bench with 1,000 privacy-sensitive profiles from medical reports and labor contracts, totaling 9,500 images, 16,000 Q/A pairs, and 100 forget tasks; propose new continual unlearning metrics (forgetting effectiveness, historical forgetting preservation, retained utility, stability) and evaluate representative unlearning methods in continual settings.

Result: Experiments show existing unlearning methods under continual sequences struggle and fail to maintain a balance among forgetting quality, utility preservation, and scalability.

Conclusion: Multimodal unlearning methods must be explicitly designed for continual privacy deletion to handle realistic, long-term tasks.

Abstract: Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress across many domains, their training on large-scale multimodal datasets raises serious privacy concerns, making effective machine unlearning increasingly necessary. However, existing benchmarks mainly focus on static or short-sequence settings, offering limited support for evaluating continual privacy deletion requests in realistic deployments. To bridge this gap, we introduce ICU-Bench, a continual multimodal unlearning benchmark built on privacy-critical document data. ICU-Bench contains 1,000 privacy-sensitive profiles from two document domains, medical reports and labor contracts, with 9,500 images, 16,000 question-answer pairs, and 100 forget tasks. Additionally, new continual unlearning metrics are introduced, facilitating a comprehensive analysis of forgetting effectiveness, historical forgetting preservation, retained utility, and stability throughout the continual unlearning process. Through extensive experiments with representative unlearning methods on ICU-Bench, we show that existing methods generally struggle in continual settings and exhibit clear limitations in balancing forgetting quality, utility preservation, and scalability over long task sequences. These findings highlight the need for multimodal unlearning methods explicitly designed for continual privacy deletion.

</details>


### [433] [MAS-Algorithm: A Workflow for Solving Algorithmic Programming Problems with a Multi-Agent System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05949)
*Yuliang Xu,Xiang Xu,Yao Wan,Hu Wei,Tong Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAS-Algorithm is a modular, multi-agent workflow for algorithmic problem solving that enables structured reasoning and tool integration, delivering significant performance gains over baselines and prompting-only or fine-tuning approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of model-centric strategies and fragmented prompting by providing a unified, extensible framework with structured reasoning and external tool integration for algorithmic tasks.

Method: Decompose end-to-end solving into modular stages managed by multiple agents, enabling structured reasoning, tool integration, and coordinated collaboration. Evaluate on a self-constructed benchmark across multiple Qwen models and LiveCodeBench-Pro, with ablation and error analyses to explore upper bounds and generalization.

Result: Average acceptance-rate gains of 6.48% on Qwen-series models; 4.72% gain on LiveCodeBench-Pro; parameter-efficient fine-tuning yields only ~0.89% improvement. Ablation studies show individual agents contributing up to 27.7%. Across metrics, improvements in accuracy and efficiency with consistent analyses of error patterns and cross-scenario behavior.

Conclusion: MAS-Algorithm demonstrates strong potential for advancing AI-driven algorithmic reasoning, offering generalization across problem types and a rigorous, extensible framework that unifies reasoning, tooling, and coordination.

Abstract: Algorithmic problem solving serves as a rigorous testbed for evaluating structured reasoning in AI coding systems, as it directly reflects a model's ability to perform structured reasoning in complex scenarios.Existing approaches predominantly rely on model-centric strategies, such as architectural modifications and data scaling, which are costly and offer limited interpretability. Alternative methods leveraging external tools or prompting techniques (e.g., chain-of-thought) are often fragmented and lack a unified framework. In this paper, we propose MAS-Algorithm, a systematic multi-agent workflow for algorithmic problem solving inspired by the practices of competitive programmers and algorithm engineers. Our framework decomposes the end-to-end solving process into modular stages, enabling structured reasoning, tool integration, and flexible coordination among agents. The design emphasizes both rigor and extensibility, allowing it to generalize across diverse problem types.Experimental results on a self-constructed benchmark demonstrate consistent improvements across multiple Qwen series models, achieving an average gain of 6.48% in acceptance rate. In contrast, parameter-efficient fine-tuning on the same data yields only a marginal improvement of 0.89%. We further observe a 4.72% gain on LiveCodeBench-Pro, along with consistent improvements across additional accuracy and efficiency metrics.Beyond performance gains, we conduct comprehensive analyses to better understand the reasoning process within the workflow, including error patterns and cross-scenario behaviors. We further perform customized replacement and ablation studies to explore the upper bound of the framework, showing that individual agents can contribute improvements of up to 27.7%. These results highlight the strong potential of MAS-Algorithm for advancing AI-driven algorithmic reasoning.

</details>


### [434] [HaM-World: Soft-Hamiltonian World Models with Selective Memory for Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05951)
*Haoyun Tang,Haodong Cui,Keyao Xu,Kun Wang,Zhandong Mei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HaM-World (HMW) introduces a structured, memory-augmented world model that decomposes latent space into a canonical (q, p) subspace and a context c, using Hamiltonian-inspired dynamics and a history-conditioned memory (Mamba) to improve long-horizon planning and robustness to distribution shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World models for model-based planning struggle with long-horizon imagined rollouts and dynamics shift. The authors argue that two missing structures hinder planner-facing latents: (i) history-conditioned memory for approximate Markov completeness, and (ii) geometric organization separating configuration, momentum, and task semantics. These facilitate stable dynamics and more reliable planning under distributional changes.

Method: Propose HaM-World (HMW), a structured world model that (1) decomposes latent state into canonical (q, p) and context c, (2) employs Mamba selective state-space memory as the history-conditioned input, and (3) evolves (q, p) with an energy-derived Hamiltonian vector field plus learnable residual/control dynamics while letting c capture semantic, dissipative factors. The interface yields a single latent state used for dynamics prediction, reward/value estimation, imagined rollouts, and CEM action search.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four DeepMind Control Suite tasks shows HaM-World achieving the highest Avg. AUC (117.9, +9.5%), reducing long-horizon rollout error to 45% of a strong baseline, and winning 11/12 k in {3,5,7} MSE cells. Under 12 OOD perturbations (dynamics shifts, action delay, observation masking), HaM-World yields the highest return in all conditions with average OOD-return gains of 10.2% (Finger Spin) and 13.6% (Reacher Easy). Mechanism diagnostics indicate bounded energy drift in action-free Hamiltonian energy, structured energy variation during rollouts, and coherent energy transfer under control, supporting the Soft-Hamiltonian design.

Conclusion: The proposed structure provides a unified latent interface for dynamics, value, planning, and search, yielding improved long-horizon stability and robustness to distribution shifts. Empirical and mechanism diagnostics support the soft-Hamiltonian dynamics rationale and show that the memory-augmented, geometrically organized latent space can stabilize imagined rollouts and improve out-of-distribution performance.

Abstract: World models enable model-based planning through learned latent dynamics, but imagined rollouts become unstable as the planning horizon grows or the dynamics distribution shifts. We argue that this instability reflects two missing structures in planner-facing latents: history-conditioned memory for approximate Markov completeness, and geometric organization that separates configuration, momentum, and task semantics. We propose HaM-World (HMW), a structured world model that decomposes the latent state into a canonical (q, p) subspace and a context subspace c, while using Mamba selective state-space memory as the history-conditioned input to the same latent dynamics. Within this interface, (q, p) evolves through an energy-derived Hamiltonian vector field plus learnable residual/control dynamics, while c captures semantic, dissipative, and non-conservative factors. This gives the planner a single latent state shared by dynamics prediction, reward/value estimation, imagined rollouts, and CEM action search. On four DeepMind Control Suite tasks, HaM-World reaches the highest Avg. AUC (117.9, +9.5%), reduces long-horizon rollout error to 45% of a strong baseline model, and wins 11/12 k in {3,5,7} MSE cells. Under 12 OOD perturbations spanning dynamics shifts, action delay, and observation masking, HaM-World achieves the highest return in every condition, with average OOD-return gains of 10.2% on Finger Spin and 13.6% on Reacher Easy. Mechanism diagnostics further show bounded action-free Hamiltonian-energy drift, structured energy variation under policy rollouts, and coherent control-induced energy transfer, supporting the intended Soft-Hamiltonian dynamics design.

</details>


### [435] [Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust Learning for Debiased Knowledge Tracing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05958)
*Peilin Zhan,Wei Chen,Weilin Chen,Shuyi Pan,Ruichu Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) for Knowledge Tracing addresses selection bias by combining a propensity model with an imputation model in a DR framework, adds a temporal smoothness regularizer to control estimator variance, and yields stable, unbiased learning that improves KT backbones on real benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Observed educational logs are non-random due to selective exercise recommendations and student choices, causing severe selection bias. Standard empirical risk minimization on observed data yields biased mastery estimates and error accumulation.

Method: Introduce a doubly robust estimator that integrates a propensity model and an error-imputation model. Derive a variance-aware generalization bound showing variance-driven stochastic deviations in sequential KT. Propose Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) that jointly optimizes the KT predictor and the imputer with a smoothness regularizer to reduce variance while preserving DR unbiasedness. Empirically validate across multiple real-world benchmarks and KT backbones.

Result: TSDR consistently enhances state-of-the-art KT backbones on several real-world benchmarks, demonstrating improved accuracy and training stability due to principled bias correction and variance control.

Conclusion: DR-based bias correction with temporal smoothness regularization mitigates selection bias in KT, yielding unbiased learning under either model being correct and reducing variance growth over time, which improves performance and stability across KT architectures.

Abstract: Knowledge Tracing (KT) is fundamental to intelligent education systems, yet relies on educational logs that are selectively observed. The non-random nature of exercise recommendations and student choices inevitably induces severe selection bias. Most existing KT methods neglect this issue, training on observed logs using standard empirical risk, which yields biased mastery estimates and accumulates errors in subsequent recommendations. To address this, we introduce a doubly robust (DR) formulation for KT that integrates a propensity model with an error imputation model, theoretically guaranteeing unbiasedness if either model is accurate. Beyond unbiasedness, in the sequential setting of KT, we identify that the estimator's performance is compromised by variance-dependent stochastic deviations that accumulate over time, thereby causing training instability and limiting performance. To mitigate this, we derive a generalization bound that explicitly characterizes the impact of estimator variance and identifies temporal smoothness as a key factor in controlling it. Building on these theoretical insights, we propose the Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) framework. TSDR jointly optimizes the KT predictor and the imputation model with a smoothness regularizer, effectively reducing variance while preserving the unbiasedness guarantee of DR. Experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that TSDR consistently enhances various state-of-the-art KT backbones, underscoring the vital role of principled bias correction in KT.

</details>


### [436] [From Coordinate Matching to Structural Alignment: Rethinking Prototype Alignment in Heterogeneous Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05959)
*Xinghao Wu,Jianwei Niu,Guogang Zhu,Xuefeng Liu,Shaojie Tang,Jiayuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FedSAF introduces structural alignment for heterogeneous federated learning, aligning inter-class relational structure rather than absolute coordinates to global prototypes. This preserves client-specific feature subspaces and improves performance over coordinate-based prototype methods by up to 3.52%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HtFL clients have heterogeneous feature extractors, yielding distinct subspaces. Coordinate (coordinate-wise) alignment to global class prototypes forces a single subspace, suppressing learning capacity. Structural alignment targets semantic relationships, maintaining heterogeneity while aligning class structures.

Method: Propose FedSAF that reduces alignment to inter-class relational structure rather than absolute embedding coordinates. Uses class prototypes but aligns the relational structure across clients instead of enforcing identical embedding coordinates. Empirically evaluated against state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods on multiple benchmarks.

Result: FedSAF consistently outperforms existing prototype-based HtFL methods, with improvements up to 3.52% across benchmarks.

Conclusion: Shifting from coordinate to relational (structural) alignment substantially improves prototype-based HtFL under heterogeneity, suggesting relational alignment as a robust principle for heterogeneous FL.

Abstract: Heterogeneous federated learning (HtFL) aims to enable collaboration among clients that differ in both data distributions and model architectures. Prototype-based methods, which communicate class-level feature centers (prototypes) instead of full model parameters, have recently shown strong potential for HtFL. Existing prototype-based HtFL methods typically reuse the MSE-based or cosine-based alignment mechanism developed for homogeneous FL when aligning client-specific representations with global prototypes. These approaches are essentially coordinate alignment, where representations of clients are forced to match the global prototypes in the embedding space in an element-wise manner. Such alignment implicitly assumes that all clients should map their representations into the feature subspace defined by the global prototypes. This assumption is reasonable in homogeneous FL, where all clients share the same feature extractor. However, it becomes problematic in HtFL, since heterogeneous feature extractors naturally induce client-specific feature subspaces, and forcing all clients to optimize within a single global subspace unnecessarily suppresses their learning capacity. We observe that coordinate alignment implicitly couples two distinct objectives: aligning inter-class semantic structure, which is directly beneficial for classification, and enforcing a shared feature basis, which is unnecessary and even harmful under model heterogeneity. Building on this insight, we design FedSAF, which shifts the alignment objective from absolute coordinates to inter-class relational structure. We demonstrate that structural alignment consistently outperforms coordinate alignment in heterogeneous settings. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our structural alignment outperforms state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods by up to 3.52\%.

</details>


### [437] [TheraAgent: Self-Improving Therapeutic Agent for Precise and Comprehensive Treatment Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05963)
*Junkai Li,Yunghwei Lai,Tianyi Zhu,Zheng Long Lee,Weizhi Ma,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces TheraAgent, an iterative generate-judge-refine framework with an integrated TheraJudge to improve treatment-plan generation, achieving state-of-the-art performance on HealthBench and favorable expert evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs rely on one-shot generation without explicit verification for treatment planning, leading to rough, incomplete, and potentially unsafe plans. The work argues for an iterative reasoning process that mirrors human experts and a dedicated evaluation module to enforce clinical standards.

Method: An agentic framework (TheraAgent) employing a generate-judge-refine loop. A treatment-specific evaluation module (TheraJudge) is integrated into the inference loop to supervise and refine plans according to clinical standards.

Result: TheraAgent achieves state-of-the-art results on HealthBench with high Accuracy and Completeness. Expert evaluations report an 86% win rate against physicians, improved Targeting and Harm Control. Strong agreement between TheraJudge and HealthBench evaluations supports reliability.

Conclusion: Iterative, judge-guided generation yields more precise, comprehensive, and safer treatment regimens, with a reliable evaluation loop aligning outputs with clinical standards and outperforming traditional one-shot generation on treatment planning tasks.

Abstract: Formulating a treatment plan is inherently a complex reasoning and refinement task rather than a simple generation problem. However, existing large language models (LLMs) mainly rely on one-shot output without explicit verification, which may result in rough, incomplete, and potentially unsafe treatment plans. To address these limitations, we propose TheraAgent, an agentic framework that replaces one-shot generation with an iterative generate-judge-refine pipeline. By mirroring the actual reasoning process of human experts who iteratively revise treatment plans, our framework progressively transforms coarse and incomplete drafts into precise, comprehensive, and safer therapeutic regimens. To facilitate the critical judge component, we introduce TheraJudge, a treatment-specific evaluation module integrated into the inference loop to enforce clinical standards. Experiments show TheraAgent achieves state-of-the-art results on HealthBench, leading in Accuracy and Completeness. In expert evaluations, it attains an 86% win rate against physicians, with superior Targeting and Harm Control. Moreover, the highly agreement between TheraJudge and HealthBench evaluations confirms the reliability of our framework.

</details>


### [438] [BehaviorGuard: Online Backdoor Defense for Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05977)
*Yinbo Yu,Xueyu Yin,Jiadai Wang,Chunwei Tian,Sai Xu,Qi Zhu,Daoqiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Online, behavior-based DRL backdoor defense (BehaviorGuard) detecting trigger-agnostic action-distribution shifts and suppressing backdoor actions in real time; claims SOTA efficacy/efficiency across single- and multi-agent DRL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backdoor attacks in deep reinforcement learning (DRL) compromise policies; existing defenses rely on reward anomalies or fine-tuning, which struggle with complex triggers and incur high costs. A trigger-agnostic, online defense is needed.

Method: Proposes BehaviorGuard which analyzes action distributions to identify backdoor-induced drift, focusing on high-quantile regions and distribution tails. Introduces a metric capturing behavioral drift to detect and suppress backdoor actions at runtime, applicable to single- and multi-agent DRL in an online setting.

Result: Empirical evaluation across diverse benchmarks and backdoor attacks shows BehaviorGuard outperforms prior methods in both efficacy and efficiency.

Conclusion: First online defense against backdoor attacks in DRL, effective against various attack patterns and scalable to multi-agent settings, offering practical online mitigation.

Abstract: Backdoor attacks pose a serious threat to deep reinforcement learning (DRL). Current defenses typically rely on reward anomalies to reverse-engineer triggers and model finetuning to remove backdoors. However, complex trigger patterns undermine their robustness, and fine-tuning entails high costs, limiting practical utility. Therefore, we shift defense concerns to trigger-agnostic backdoor output behaviors and propose BehaviorGuard, an online behavior-based backdoor detection and mitigation framework for DRL. Specifically, we find that regardless of attacks, backdoored policies induce consistent shifts in action distributions to ensure reliable activation, leaving detectable traces in high-quantile regions and distribution tails, even in the absence of triggers. Based on this, we design a novel metric that captures behavioral drift in action distributions to identify and suppress backdoor actions at runtime. To our knowledge, this is the first online backdoor defense that counters attacks both in single- and multi-agent DRL. Evaluated across diverse benchmarks with different backdoor attacks, BehaviorGuard consistently surpasses prior methods in both efficacy and efficiency.

</details>


### [439] [TACT: Mitigating Overthinking and Overacting in Coding Agents via Activation Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05980)
*Yuan Sui,Yulin Chen,Yibo Li,Xue Jiang,Yufei He,Yihong Dong,Xiaoxin He,Tianyu Gao,Bryan Hooi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TACT introduces activation steering to detect and mitigate agent drift (overthinking/overacting) in long-horizon LMs by identifying drift axes in the residual stream and projecting activations to steer steps back toward calibrated behavior, yielding substantial gains on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon language model agents degrade during extended reasoning; two recurrent failure modes—overthinking and overacting—undermine reliability. There is a need to detect drift early and correct behavior without costly retraining.

Method: Label each trajectory step as calibrated, overthinking, or overacting. Observe that hidden states separate along two drift axes in the residual stream. Compute AUC ≈ 0.9 for linear separability between calibrated and failure modes. At test time, project each step's activation onto these axes and pull drifted steps back toward the calibrated region. Evaluate on SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and CLAW-Eval across models (Qwen3.5-27B and Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it).

Result: TACT outperforms unsteered baselines, increasing average resolve rate by +5.8 percentage points on Qwen3.5-27B and +4.8 pp on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it, while reducing steps-to-resolve by up to 26%. Shows that agent drift is a steerable direction in the residual stream.

Conclusion: Agent drift can be interpreted and controlled via residual-stream steering (TACT), offering a practical method to improve reliability of long-horizon agents without modifying training data.

Abstract: When language model agents tackle complex software engineering tasks, they often degrade over long trajectories, which we define as *agent drift*. We focus on two recurring failure modes *overthinking* and *overacting*, i.e., where the agent repeatedly reasons over information it already has, and where it issues tool calls without integrating recent observations or acquiring new evidence. In this paper, we introduce TACT (Think-Act Calibration via activation Steering), to detect and mitigate agent drift in the residual stream before it surfaces as a behavioral failure. In specific, we label trajectory steps as overthinking, overacting, or calibrated, and find that their hidden states can separate linearly along two *drift axes*, pointing from calibrated behavior toward each failure mode (AUC $\approx$ 0.9). To mitigate agent drift, we project each step's activation onto these axes at test time and pull drifted ones back toward the calibrated region. Experiments show that TACT outperforms unsteered baselines across SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and CLAW-Eval, lifting average resolve rate by $+5.8$ pp on Qwen3.5-27B and $+4.8$ pp on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it while cutting steps-to-resolve by up to $26\%$. These gains frame agent drift as a steerable direction in the residual stream, and position TACT as a viable handle for reliable long-horizon agents.

</details>


### [440] [BioResearcher: Scenario-Guided Multi-Agent for Translational Medicine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05985)
*Remigiusz Kinas,Joanna Krawczyk,Rafał Powalski,Przemysław Pietrzak,Agnieszka Kowalewska,Krzysztof Kolmus,Maciej Sypetkowski,Łukasz Smoliński,Tomasz Jetka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ingenix BioResearcher is a scenario-guided multi-agent system for translational medicine that coordinates 30+ tools, preserves provenance, and reconciles models for editorial assembly; it outperforms baselines on unit tests, biomedical benchmarks, and a clinical end-to-end benchmark, showing broad effectiveness across unit-level, open-ended, and clinical discovery tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundational models and generic agent systems are not tailored to the auditable, scenario-specific workflows and multi-source evidence integration (literature, trials, patents, multi-omics) required in translational medicine, including uncertainty handling and provenance tracking.

Method: Developed Ingenix BioResearcher, a scenario-guided multi-agent system that maps queries to versioned research playbooks, delegates to specialized subagents over 30+ tools and ML endpoints, combines structured database access with sandboxed genome-scale analysis code, and applies claim-level multi-model reconciliation before editorial assembly.

Result: On unit-level tests: 109 single-step tests with 83.49% pass rate and 0.892 average score. Biomedical benchmarks: 89.33% on BixBench-Verified-50 and top 0.758 mean score on BaisBench Scientific Discovery. Clinical end-to-end: 30-query benchmark with 74.7% ± 3.3% positive hit rate and 96.8% ± 0.2% negative clear rate.

Conclusion: The system demonstrates broad, competitive performance across unit-level, open-ended reasoning, and end-to-end clinical discovery, indicating viability of scenario-guided multi-agent systems for translational biomedicine and evidence synthesis with auditable workflows and provenance.

Abstract: Translational medicine turns underspecified development goals into evidence synthesis that must combine literature, trials, patents, and quantitative multi-omics analysis while preserving identifiers, uncertainty, and retrievable provenance. General-purpose foundation models and off-the-shelf tool-augmented or multi-agent systems are not built for this: they tend to produce single-shot answers or run open-endedly, and fall short on the auditable, scenario-specific workflows that heterogeneous biomedical sources demand.
  This paper introduces Ingenix BioResearcher, a scenario-guided multi-agent system that maps queries to versioned research playbooks, delegates to specialized subagents over 30+ tools and machine-learning endpoints, mixes structured database access with sandboxed code for genome-scale analyses, and applies claim-level multi-model reconciliation before editorial assembly.
  We evaluate BioResearcher across unit-level capabilities, open-ended biomedical reasoning, and end-to-end clinical discovery. It leads evaluated baselines on 109 single-step tests (83.49% pass rate; 0.892 average score), achieves strong biomedical benchmark performance (89.33% on BixBench-Verified-50 and the top 0.758 mean score on BaisBench Scientific Discovery), and leads on a 30-query clinical end-to-end benchmark with the highest positive hit rate (74.7% $\pm$ 3.3%) and negative clear rate (96.8% $\pm$ 0.2%). These results show broad, competitive performance across unit-level, open-ended, and end-to-end clinical evaluations.

</details>


### [441] [Strat-LLM: Stratified Strategy Alignment for LLM-based Stock Trading with Real-time Multi-Source Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06024)
*Wenliang Huang,Zengyi Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Strat-LLM studies how alignment and strategic reasoning in LLMs interact with model scale and market regimes in live trading, revealing regime- and size-dependent tradeoffs across Free, Strict, and Guided modes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill a gap in benchmarks by coupling architectural reasoning with strategy consistency under real-time, multi-source financial data, while eliminating look-ahead bias.

Method: Live-forward testing through 2025 on A-share and U.S. markets using heterogeneous data (sequential prices, real-time news, annual reports). Compare reasoning-heavy vs standard LLMs across mode settings (Free, Strict, Guided) and scales (e.g., 35B vs 122B). Evaluate via utility, alignment utility, drawdowns, win-rate, and total returns.

Result: Key findings: (1) Reasoning-heavy models excel in Free Mode; standard models need Strict Mode as a risk anchor. (2) Alignment utility is regime-dependent: Free/Guided capture momentum in uptrends; Strict mitigates drawdowns in downtrends. (3) Mid-scale models (≈35B) show best fidelity under strict constraints; ultra-large models (≈122B) incur an alignment tax under rigid rules but gain under Guided Mode. (4) Standard LLMs exhibit a high win-rate trap, favoring small gains over total returns; mitigation requires deep reasoning or strong guardrails.

Conclusion: Alignment strategy should be mode- and regime-aware. Dynamic or hybrid modes may offer the best balance across scales and market conditions. The work highlights significant tradeoffs between reasoning depth, risk control, and data integration in live trading LLM agents. Project details at Strat-LLM.github.io.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are evolving into autonomous trading agents, yet existing benchmarks often overlook the interplay between architectural reasoning and strategy consistency. We propose Strat-LLM, a framework grounded in Stratified Strategy Alignment. Operating in a live-forward setting throughout 2025, it integrates heterogeneous data including sequential prices, real-time news, and annual reports to eliminate look-ahead bias. Extensive stress tests on A-share and U.S. markets reveal: (1) reasoning-heavy models achieve peak utility in Free Mode via internal logic, whereas standard models require Strict Mode as a vital risk anchor; (2) alignment utility is regime-dependent, with Free and Guided modes capturing momentum in uptrending markets, while Strict Mode mitigates drawdowns in downtrends; (3) mid-scale models (35B) show optimal fidelity under strict constraints, whereas ultra-large models (122B) suffer an alignment tax under rigid rules but gain a performance premium in Guided Mode; (4) standard LLMs often fall into a high win-rate trap, optimizing for small gains at the expense of total returns, which can only be mitigated through deep reasoning or strict external guardrails. Project details are available at https://Strat-LLM.github.io.

</details>


### [442] [Pathways to AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06029)
*Gordon Fletcher,Saomai Vu Khan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A critical software studies analysis of AI trajectories that challenges inevitability, interrogates AGI definitional issues, and maps leverage points, trajectories, and alternative projects to propose governance-informed development programs toward AGI-adjacent capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To contest the presumed inevitability of the current AI trajectory, examine how social, political, and economic conditions shape AI development, critique AGI conceptualization, and provide policy-oriented recommendations.

Method: Critical, pathway-focused analysis drawing on three foundational-model trajectories (frontier proprietary, open-weight, domain/sovereign models); mapping causal pathways, leverage nodes, dead ends, and branch-off projects; cross-trajectory comparison; synthesis into socio-technical development programs.

Result: Identifies five research questions about pathways, leverage points, trajectory differences, alternative projects, and plausible governance-aligned development programs; provides structured analysis and recommended directions.

Conclusion: A conditional, critical framing can yield actionable recommendations for developing AGI-adjacent capabilities with transparency, moderation, wellbeing, and sustainable business models, while recognizing definitional and conceptual challenges around AGI and avoiding inevitability.

Abstract: Our focus are five related questions that stem from a critical software studies perspective. Underpinning this view is the acknowledged need to avoid assumptions regarding the inevitability of the current situation relating to AI. What we need to see is the closeness of the linkage between current commercial AI development and our prevailing social, political and economic circumstances. This does mean that the perspectives presented here are done so critically and conditionally. Most importantly, Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is seen as being problematic both conceptually and definitionally. This conditioning of any view regarding AGI does lead the discussion in specific directions and to certain conclusions regarding the future. However, adopting this perspective enables the work to offer some final recommendations. We set out to ask the following questions, 1. What are the critical pathways that produced the current dominant generative AI tools (capabilities, product forms, adoption patterns)? 2. Which decision points acted as leverage nodes (small changes that had large downstream effects), and which dead ends reveal alternative possibilities that did not become dominant? 3. How do pathways differ across three foundational-model trajectories such as the frontier proprietary models, open-weight models or specific domain and sovereign models? 4. Which alternative projects branched from key leverage nodes, what is their current state, and why did some succeed, stall, fail or become absorbed? 5. Based on this analysis, what socio-technical development programmes could plausibly move toward AGI-adjacent capability while meeting requirements for transparency, moderation, wellbeing and sustainable business models?

</details>


### [443] [Novelty-based Tree-of-Thought Search for LLM Reasoning and Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06040)
*Leon Hamm,Zlatan Ajanovic*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces novelty-based pruning for tree-of-thought reasoning in LLMs, reducing token cost by pruning low-novelty branches via an LLM-derived novelty metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address brittleness and high time/token costs of chain-of-thought/tree-of-thought and RL; leverage width-based search efficiency by adopting novelty as a pruning criterion in language planning tasks.

Method: Define a novelty metric for nodes in the search tree comparing a new thought against previously seen thoughts using LLM prompts and pretrained embeddings; compute novelty per candidate node, prune branches with low novelty; generate nodes with repeated prompting; analyze cost trade-offs and tree size.

Result: Experiments on language-based planning and general reasoning benchmarks show that novelty-based pruning reduces overall token cost by pruning the tree, even though per-state prompts may increase; performance is maintained or improved relative to baselines with reduced search space.

Conclusion: Novelty can be effectively transferred to language domains to enable width-based pruning in tree-of-thought reasoning, achieving token-cost efficiency and scalable planning.

Abstract: Although advances such as chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought or reinforcement learning have improved the performance of LLMs in reasoning and planning tasks, they are still brittle and have not achieved human-level performance in many domains, and often suffer from high time and token costs. Inspired by the success of width-based search in planning, we explore how the concept of novelty can be transferred to language domains and how it can improve tree-of-thought reasoning. A tree of thoughts relies on building possible "paths" of consecutive ideas or thoughts. These are generated by repeatedly prompting an LLM. In our paper, a measurable concept of novelty is proposed that describes the uniqueness of a new node (thought) in comparison to nodes previously seen in the search tree. Novelty is estimated by prompting an LLM and making use of embedded general knowledge from pre-training. This metric can then be used to prune branches and reduce the scope of the search. Although this method introduces more prompts per state, the overall token cost can be reduced by pruning and reducing the overall tree size. This procedure is tested and compared using several benchmarks in language-based planning and general reasoning.

</details>


### [444] [Visual Fingerprints for LLM Generation Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06054)
*Amal Alnouri,Andreas Hinterreiter,Christina Humer,Furui Cheng,Marc Streit*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces 'visual fingerprints' to compare LLM generation conditions by treating outputs as distributions of linguistic choices and visualizing them, enabling distribution-level analysis across repeated samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how prompts, system instructions, model parameters, and architecture bias outputs; to aid prompt design and model evaluation in the face of stochastic, open-ended generation.

Method: Define generation conditions; extract linguistic choices (content, expression, structure) via NLP pipelines from multiple samples; estimate distributions over choices; visualize as fingerprints; demonstrate via four usage scenarios.

Result: Shows that visual fingerprints reveal consistent condition-specific patterns not readily observable from single outputs or aggregate metrics; supports direct distribution-level comparisons.

Conclusion: Visual fingerprints provide a practical, scalable means to quantify and compare generation conditions, aiding analysis and design of prompts and evaluation across LLMs.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) outputs arise from complex interactions among prompts, system instructions, model parameters, and architecture. We refer to specific configurations of these factors as generation conditions, each of which can bias outputs in various ways. Understanding how different generation conditions shape model behaviors is essential for tasks such as prompt design and model evaluation, yet it remains challenging due to the stochastic and open-ended nature of text generation. We present an approach to visually compare LLM outputs across generation conditions by modeling responses as collections of linguistic choices, including content, expression, and structure. We extract these choices using natural language processing pipelines and represent their distributions across repeated samples. We then visualize these distributions as visual fingerprints, enabling direct, distribution-level comparison of condition-specific tendencies. Through four usage scenarios, we demonstrate how visual fingerprints reveal consistent patterns in LLM behavior that are difficult to observe through individual responses or aggregate metrics.

</details>


### [445] [VibeServe: Can AI Agents Build Bespoke LLM Serving Systems?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06068)
*Keisuke Kamahori,Shihang Li,Simon Peter,Baris Kasikci*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VibeServe introduces a multi-agent loop to auto-synthesize bespoke LLM serving stacks per usage scenario, achieving competitive performance in standard deployments and superior results in non-standard workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The conventional approach uses a single general-purpose stack optimized over years, which is inflexible to workload and hardware variations and requires extensive engineering effort. A generation-time specialization could exploit workload-, model-, and hardware-specific characteristics.

Method: An outer loop plans and tracks the search over system designs; an inner loop implements candidate stacks, verifies correctness, and measures performance on the target benchmark, yielding end-to-end LLM serving stacks. The evaluation contrasts standard deployment against baselines like vLLM and examines non-standard scenarios.

Result: In standard deployment, VibeServe is competitive with vLLM; in six non-standard scenarios (varying model architectures, workload knowledge, and hardware optimizations), it outperforms existing systems by exploiting opportunities that generic stacks miss.

Conclusion: Generation-time specialization broadens the design space for infrastructure software, suggesting a shift from runtime generality to bespoke, per-scenario serving stacks; code is available at the provided GitHub repository.

Abstract: For years, we have built LLM serving systems like any other critical infrastructure: a single general-purpose stack, hand-tuned over many engineer-years, meant to support every model and workload. In this paper, we take the opposite bet: a multi-agent loop that automatically synthesizes bespoke serving systems for different usage scenarios. We propose VibeServe, the first agentic loop that generates entire LLM serving stacks end-to-end. VibeServe uses an outer loop to plan and track the search over system designs, and an inner loop to implement candidates, check correctness, and measure performance on the target benchmark. In the standard deployment setting, where existing stacks are highly optimized, VibeServe remains competitive with vLLM, showing that generation-time specialization need not come at the cost of performance. More interestingly, in non-standard scenarios, VibeServe outperforms existing systems by exploiting opportunities that generic systems miss in six scenarios involving non-standard model architectures, workload knowledge, and hardware-specific optimizations. Together, these results suggest a different point in the design space for infrastructure software: generation-time specialization rather than runtime generality. Code is available at https://github.com/uw-syfi/vibe-serve.

</details>


### [446] [Safety Certification is Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06087)
*Oliver Schön,Licio Romao,Sadegh Soudjani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Kernel-embedding approach to directly estimate T-step safety probability from trajectory data, avoiding recursive DP; unifies and extends barrier certificates and robust Markov models; horizon-stable and applicable to non-Markovian dynamics; validated on a neural-controlled quadrotor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DP-based safety certification suffers from compounding errors over horizon and struggles with non-Markovian dynamics; need a non-recursive, horizon-stable method.

Method: Kernel embedding framework reframes safety certification as a trajectory-data classification problem to directly estimate the T-step safety probability; shows that existing methods are special cases; extends to non-Markovian dynamics; avoids recursion.

Result: Direct estimators are stable regardless of horizon length and handle non-Markovian dynamics; DP-based certificates become unsound with longer horizons; demonstrated in simulation on a neural-controlled quadrotor.

Conclusion: Direct, non-recursive kernel-embedding estimators provide robust safety certification for uncertain dynamical systems, overcoming horizon-induced errors and generalizing beyond Markovian assumptions; offer a unified perspective on existing approaches.

Abstract: The goal of this paper is certifying safety of dynamical systems subject to uncertainty. Existing approaches use trajectory data to estimate transition probabilities, and compute safety probabilities recursively via dynamic programming (DP). This recursion may lead to compounding errors in the certified safety probability, thus collapsing to a vacuous lower bound for growing horizons $T$. We propose a kernel embedding framework that treats safety certification as a classification problem on trajectory data, directly estimating the $T$-step safety probability without recursion. We show that the framework subsumes well-established approaches from the literature (e.g., barrier certificates, robust Markov models) as special cases, and allows us to go beyond their limitations. As the main consequence, it bypasses compounding error across the horizon and enables certification for systems with non-Markovian dynamics. We demonstrate that direct estimators remain stable independent of the certification horizon and in the non-Markovian setting, whilst DP-based certificates silently go unsound -- confirmed in simulation on a neural-controlled quadrotor.

</details>


### [447] [Shallow Prefill, Deep Decoding: Efficient Long-Context Inference via Layer-Asymmetric KV Visibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06105)
*Jungsuk Oh,Hyeseo Jeon,Hyunjune Ji,Kyongmin Kong,Jay-Yoon Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPEED reduces long-context cost in decoder-only LMs by a phase-asymmetric KV-visibility policy: keep prompt KV states only in lower layers during prefill, while decoding remains full-depth, using a minimal anchor. It preserves near-baseline quality on benchmarks while saving memory (25% active KV at 128K) and improving several metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context inference is costly in decoder-only models because long prompts are processed during prefill, cached at every layer, and repeatedly attended to during autoregressive decoding. The goal is to reduce memory and compute without sacrificing performance.

Method: Introduce Shallow Prefill, deep Decode (SPEED): a phase-asymmetric key-value (KV) visibility policy that materializes non-anchor prompt-token KV states only in lower layers; upper-layer prefill tokens are removed from visibility during Decode. Use a minimal Bag-of-Scale (BoS) anchor; 75% of layers used for prefill; evaluate on Llama-3.1-8B with long context (128K).

Result: In a controlled Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuning study, SPEED with 75% prefill layers achieves 51.2 average on OLMES-style benchmarks vs 51.4 baseline; TTFT up 33%, TPOT up 22%; active KV memory reduced by 25% at 128K context.

Conclusion: Long-context prompt tokens need not persist as full-depth KV-cache objects if Decode-phase tokens remain full-depth. The approach preserves core prompt-selection and representation-stabilization regions while delivering memory and compute savings, suggesting broader applicability to long-context inference with minimal quality loss.

Abstract: Long-context inference in decoder-only language models is costly because long prompts are processed during Prefill, cached at every layer, and repeatedly attended to during autoregressive Decode. We introduce \emph{Shallow Prefill, dEEp Decode} (SPEED), a phase-asymmetric KV-visibility policy that materializes non-anchor prompt-token KV states only in lower layers while keeping Decode-phase tokens full-depth. Unlike previous approaches that make upper-layer prompt KV states cheaper to store or construct, SPEED removes prefill tokens from the upper-layer Decode visibility set altogether. With a minimal BoS anchor, this simple change preserves broad benchmark quality while reducing long-context cost. In a controlled Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuning study, SPEED using only 75\% of layers for prefill tokens reaches 51.2 average score on OLMES-style benchmarks, compared with 51.4 for the full-depth baseline, while improving TTFT by 33\%, TPOT by 22\%, and reducing active KV memory by 25.0\% at 128K context. Layer-wise diagnostics suggest that this cutoff retains the main prompt-selection and representation-stabilization regions of the full-depth model. These results show that long-context prompt tokens need not always persist as full-depth KV-cache objects when Decode-phase tokens remain full-depth.

</details>


### [448] [On Time, Within Budget: Constraint-Driven Online Resource Allocation for Agentic Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06110)
*Xinglin Wang,Zishen Liu,Shaoxiong Feng,Peiwen Yuan,Yiwei Li,Jiayi Shi,Yueqi Zhang,Chuyi Tan,Ji Zhang,Boyuan Pan,Yao Hu,Kan Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Monte Carlo Portfolio Planning (MCPP) is introduced to maximize the probability that an agentic workflow completes within budget and deadline by online resource allocation and replanning based on simulated executions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In real deployments, workflows must meet hard budget and deadline constraints rather than optimizing average efficiency, prompting constraint-driven online allocation for agentic workflows with dependent subtasks.

Method: Formulates a finite-horizon stochastic online allocation problem. MCPP allocates models and parallel samples to concurrently executable subtasks, tracks remaining budget/time, and uses closed-loop simulations of workflow executions to estimate constrained completion probability and replans after observed outcomes.

Result: Empirical evaluation on CodeFlow and ProofFlow shows MCPP consistently improves constrained completion probability across a wide range of budget–deadline constraints compared to strong baselines.

Conclusion: MCPP provides a lightweight, reactive planner that increases the likelihood of constraint-satisfying completion in agentic workflows by online simulation-based replanning.

Abstract: Agentic systems increasingly solve complex user requests by executing orchestrated workflows, where subtasks are assigned to specialized models or tools and coordinated according to their dependencies. While recent work improves agent efficiency by optimizing the performance--cost--latency frontier, real deployments often impose concrete requirements: a workflow must be completed within a specified budget and before a specified deadline. This shifts the goal from average efficiency optimization to maximizing the probability that the entire workflow completes successfully under explicit budget and deadline constraints. We study \emph{constraint-driven online resource allocation for agentic workflows}. Given a dependency-structured workflow and estimates of success rates and generation lengths for each subtask--model pair, the executor allocates models and parallel samples across simultaneously executable subtasks while managing the remaining budget and time. We formulate this setting as a finite-horizon stochastic online allocation problem and propose \emph{Monte Carlo Portfolio Planning} (MCPP), a lightweight closed-loop planner that directly estimates constrained completion probability through simulated workflow executions and replans after observed outcomes. Experiments on CodeFlow and ProofFlow demonstrate that MCPP consistently improves constrained completion probability over strong baselines across a wide range of budget--deadline constraints.

</details>


### [449] [CrossCult-KIBench: A Benchmark for Cross-Cultural Knowledge Insertion in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06115)
*Zhen Zeng,Leijiang Gu,Feng Li,Jing Yu,Zenglin Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A benchmark and baseline for cross-cultural knowledge insertion in multimodal LLMs; evaluates adaptation to English, Chinese, and Arabic cultures; reveals current methods struggle to balance cultural alignment with behavior preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs are English-centric and can produce culturally inappropriate responses in cross-cultural settings. The work proposes a mechanism to insert culture-specific knowledge while preserving behavior in other cultures, addressing alignment and safety concerns.

Method: Introduce CrossCult-KIBench with 9,800 image-grounded cases covering 49 visual scenarios across English, Chinese, and Arabic cultures; supports single-insert and sequential-insert settings. Propose Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion (MCKI) that retrieves culture-relevant knowledge from external memory using frozen MLLM representations and prepends matched entries as conditional prompts when applicable.

Result: Extensive experiments show current approaches struggle to balance effective cultural adaptation with preservation of non-target cultural behavior, indicating a key challenge in culturally-aware MLLMs.

Conclusion: The work highlights cross-cultural adaptability as a crucial research direction for developing more culturally aware and responsible multimodal LLMs, providing a benchmark and baseline to drive progress.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), trained primarily on English-centric data, frequently generate culturally inappropriate or misaligned responses in cross-cultural settings. To mitigate this, we introduce the task of cross-cultural knowledge insertion, which focuses on adapting models to specific cultural contexts while preserving their original behavior in other cultures. To facilitate research in this area, we introduce CrossCult-KIBench, a comprehensive evaluation benchmark for assessing both the effectiveness of knowledge insertion and its unintended side effects on non-target cultures. The benchmark includes 9,800 image-grounded cases covering 49 culturally relevant visual scenarios across English, Chinese, and Arabic language-culture groups. It supports evaluation in both single-insert and sequential-insert settings. We also propose Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion (MCKI) as a baseline method. MCKI retrieves relevant cultural knowledge from an external memory using frozen MLLM representations, prepending matched entries as conditional prompts when applicable. Extensive experiments on CrossCult-KIBench reveal that current approaches struggle to balance effective cultural adaptation with behavioral preservation, highlighting a key challenge in developing culturally-aware MLLMs. Our work thus underscores an important research direction for developing more culturally adaptive and responsible MLLMs.

</details>


### [450] [Back to the Beginning of Heuristic Design: Bridging Code and Knowledge with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06123)
*Nguyen Viet Tuan Kiet,Bui Dinh Pham,Dao Van Tung,Tran Cong Dao,Huynh Thi Thanh Binh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A top-down, knowledge-first framing for automatic heuristic design (AHD) with LLMs, arguing that knowledge should be the search target rather than code alone; introduces a distortion–compression trade-off in a statistical-learning view and shows that knowledge-first search (in both population-based and tree-based AHD) improves efficiency, transfer, and generalization, often outperforming code-centric pipelines, with further gains when combining both strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bottom-up, code-centric AHD may obscure reusable knowledge and hinder generalization. A complementary top-down approach treats knowledge as the primary object of search, aiming for interpretable, reusable hypotheses that transfer across problems and trajectories.

Method: Formalize a statistical-learning view that exposes a distortion–compression trade-off. Instantiate this framework in both population-based and tree-based AHD systems. Conduct experiments across combinatorial optimization (and tasks beyond) to compare knowledge-first search with traditional code-centric pipelines and assess synergy.

Result: Knowledge-first search improves discovery efficiency, transfer, and generalization, often outperforming code-centric pipelines. Combining knowledge-first and code-centric strategies yields additional gains.

Conclusion: Progress in AHD should focus on constructing and evolving interpretable hypotheses that retain value beyond a single search trajectory; a knowledge-centric, top-down perspective can complement bottom-up code-centric methods and enhance generalization and reuse across problems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently advanced automatic heuristic design (AHD) for combinatorial optimization (CO), where candidate heuristics are iteratively proposed, evaluated, and refined. Most existing approaches search over executable programs and distill insights from execution feedback to guide later iterations. Because this process moves from low-level implementations to high-level principles, we refer to it as a bottom-up paradigm. We argue that this view is incomplete and introduce a complementary top-down perspective: knowledge becomes the primary search object and code merely instantiates and tests it, making what is learned explicit and reusable across problems and trajectories. We formalize this shift through a statistical-learning view that exposes a distortion--compression trade-off, and instantiate it in both population-based and tree-based AHD frameworks. Across CO and tasks beyond it, knowledge-first search improves discovery efficiency, transfer, and generalization, often outperforming code-centric pipelines, while combining both strategies yields further gains. Our results suggest that progress in AHD depends on iteratively constructing and evolving interpretable hypotheses that retain value beyond a single search trajectory.

</details>


### [451] [P-Guide: Parameter-Efficient Prior Steering for Single-Pass CFG Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06124)
*Xin Peng,Ang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: P-Guide enables single-pass guidance in flow-based generation by modulating only the initial latent, matching CFG performance with ~50% latency reduction; uses homoscedastic/heteroscedastic priors; first-order equivalence to CFG in prior space; robust and efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CFG provides high-fidelity conditional generation but incurs heavy computation due to dual forward passes per step; a faster alternative that preserves quality is needed.

Method: Introduce P-Guide that applies guidance through the initial latent in a single inference pass; argue first-order equivalence to CFG without explicit velocity field extrapolation; study both homoscedastic and heteroscedastic priors, jointly modeling mean and variance to enable adaptive loss attenuation and robustness.

Result: Experiments show ~50% reduction in inference latency with fidelity and prompt alignment competitive to standard dual-pass CFG baselines; heteroscedastic prior modeling improves robustness to data uncertainty.

Conclusion: P-Guide provides an efficient, CFG-like guidance mechanism for flow-based models via initial latent modulation; under a first-order view it is equivalent to CFG in guiding the prior; joint mean-variance priors offer adaptive robustness and better handling of uncertainty.

Abstract: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) is essential for high-fidelity conditional generation in flow matching, yet it imposes significant computational overhead by requiring dual forward passes at each sampling step. In this work, we address this bottleneck by introducing \textbf{P-Guide}, a framework that achieves high-quality guidance through a single inference pass by modulating only the initial latent state. We further show that, under a first-order approximation, P-Guide is equivalent to CFG in the sense that it steers generation from the prior space, without requiring explicit velocity field extrapolation during sampling. We consider both homoscedastic and \textbf{heteroscedastic} priors, and find that jointly modeling the mean and variance enables adaptive loss attenuation and improved robustness to data uncertainty. Extensive experiments demonstrate that P-Guide reduces inference latency by approximately 50\% while maintaining fidelity and prompt alignment competitive with standard dual-pass CFG baselines.

</details>


### [452] [Skill1: Unified Evolution of Skill-Augmented Agents via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06130)
*Yaorui Shi,Yuxin Chen,Zhengxi Lu,Yuchun Miao,Shugui Liu,Qi GU,Xunliang Cai,Xiang Wang,An Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Skill1 introduces a single-policy framework that co-evolves selection, utilization, and distillation of skills toward a single task-outcome objective, outperforming prior baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing skill libraries are maintained by optimizing capabilities in isolation or with separate rewards, leading to misaligned or conflicting evolution; a unified, outcome-driven approach is needed to coherently develop and reuse skills.

Method: A single policy: generates a query to search a persistent skill library, re-ranks candidates to select one, solves the task conditioned on the chosen skill, and distills a new skill from the trajectory. All learning is driven by a single task-outcome signal; low-frequency credit to selection, high-frequency credit to distillation; the three capabilities co-evolve toward improving task outcomes.

Result: Empirical evaluation on ALFWorld and WebShop shows Skill1 outperforms prior skill-based and reinforcement learning baselines. Training dynamics demonstrate co-evolution of selection, utilization, and distillation; ablations removing any credit signal degrade performance.

Conclusion: A unified, outcome-driven framework enables coherent co-evolution of skill selection, utilization, and distillation, yielding improved performance and more robust skill libraries; the single-task-outcome signal suffices to coordinate the three capabilities, with ablation studies confirming the necessity of all credit signals.

Abstract: A persistent skill library allows language model agents to reuse successful strategies across tasks. Maintaining such a library requires three coupled capabilities. The agent selects a relevant skill, utilizes it during execution, and distills new skills from experience. Existing methods optimize these capabilities in isolation or with separate reward sources, resulting in partial and conflicting evolution. We propose Skill1, a framework that trains a single policy to co-evolve skill selection, utilization, and distillation toward a shared task-outcome objective. The policy generates a query to search the skill library, re-ranks candidates to select one, solves the task conditioned on it, and distills a new skill from the trajectory. All learning derives from a single task-outcome signal. Its low-frequency trend credits selection and its high-frequency variation credits distillation. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop show that Skill1 outperforms prior skill-based and reinforcement learning baselines. Training dynamics confirm the co-evolution of the three capabilities, and ablations show that removing any credit signal degrades the evolution.

</details>


### [453] [Graphlets as Building Blocks for Structural Vocabulary in Knowledge Graph Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06154)
*Kossi Amouzouvi,Robert Wardenga,Jens Lehmann,Sahar Vahdati*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Graphlet-based token vocabulary for Knowledge Graph Foundation Models (KGFMs) enables transferable representations across diverse KGs, improving zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction by using small graphlets as tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knowledge Graphs are discrete like language/images but lack a fixed grid and have irregular, non-Euclidean topologies; there is no universal token set to promote transfer across unseen KGs.

Method: A model-agnostic framework that uses a vocabulary of graphlets (closed/open 2- and 3-paths, and star graphlets) as structural tokens; mines a KG between relations via pattern matching to obtain robust invariances.

Result: Evaluated on 51 heterogeneous KGs; in zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction, adding graphlets to the vocabulary yields models that outperform prior KGFMs.

Conclusion: Graphlet-based invariances provide a universal, transferable representation for KGs and improve cross-graph transferability of KGFMs.

Abstract: Foundation models excel at language, where sentences become tokens, and vision, where images become pixels, because both reduce to discrete symbols on a shared, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs share the discreteness, but not the geometry. Their entities and relations are discrete symbols, yet their arrangement is relational and lacks a common, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs (KGs) share the discreteness, but not the geometry. They form irregular, non-Euclidean topologies whose local neighborhoods differ from graph to graph. Therefore, Knowledge Graph Foundation Models (KGFMs) rely on identifying structural invariances to produce transferable representations. Without a universal token set, KGFMs are limited in their ability to transfer representations across unseen KGs. We close this gap by treating graphlets, small connected graphs, as structural tokens that recur in heterogeneous KGs. In this paper, We introduce a model-agnostic framework based on a vocabulary of graphlets that mines a KG between relations via pattern matching. In particular, we considered closed and open 2- and 3-path, and star graphlets, to obtain robust invariances. The framework is evaluated on 51 KGs from a wide range of domains, for zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction. Experiments show that adding simple graphlets to the vocabulary yields models that outperform prior KGFMs.

</details>


### [454] [Beyond Accuracy: Policy Invariance as a Reliability Test for LLM Safety Judges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06161)
*Shihao Weng,Yang Feng,Xiaofei Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Policy invariance is essential for LLM-based safety evaluators. The paper defines three invariance principles, introduces a stress-test protocol across multiple judge types, and reveals that current evaluators’ verdicts depend on prompt wording, not only agent behavior. It proposes Policy Invariance Score and Judge Card reporting and releases tools for auditing evaluators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensure that safety judgments reflect agent actions rather than evaluation prompts. Current benchmarks conflate agent behavior with how the evaluator is prompted, undermining trust in safety assessments.

Method: Propose three principles: rubric-semantics invariance under certified-equivalent rewrites, rubric-threshold invariance under intentional strict-to-lenient shifts, and ambiguity-aware calibration. Implement a stress-test protocol with four agent-class judges evaluating trajectories from ASSEBench and R-Judge. Analyze how content-preserving policy rewrites affect verdicts and measure judge reliability.

Result: Findings show policy rewrites flip up to 9.1% of verdicts; 18–43% of flips occur on unambiguous cases under rewrites. Judges respond to meaningful normative shifts and meaningless structural rewrites similarly, making current safety scores conflated with evaluator prompts. Introduce Policy Invariance Score and Judge Card reporting; reveal order-of-magnitude variance in judge reliability unseen by accuracy-based leaderboards.

Conclusion: Advocate auditing evaluators using the Policy Invariance Score and Judge Card; release protocol and code to enable future benchmarks to assess evaluator trustworthiness and robustness rather than rely on accuracy alone.

Abstract: LLM-as-a-Judge pipelines have become the de facto evaluator for agent safety, yet existing benchmarks treat their verdicts as ground-truth proxies without checking whether the verdicts depend on the agent's behavior or merely on how the evaluation policy happens to be worded. We argue that any trustworthy safety judge must satisfy a basic property we call policy invariance, and we operationalize it as three testable principles: rubric-semantics invariance under certified-equivalent rewrites, rubric-threshold invariance under intentional strict-to-lenient shifts, and ambiguity-aware calibration so that verdict instability concentrates on genuinely ambiguous cases. Instantiating these principles as a stress-test protocol with four agent-class judges on trajectories drawn from ASSEBench and R-Judge, we surface a previously unmeasured failure mode: today's judges respond to meaningful normative shifts and to meaningless structural rewrites with comparable strength, and cannot tell the two apart. Content-preserving policy rewrites flip up to 9.1% of verdicts above baseline jitter, and 18-43% of all observed flips occur on unambiguous cases under such rewrites, so existing safety scores conflate what the agent did with how the evaluator was prompted. Beyond the diagnosis, we contribute the Policy Invariance Score and the Judge Card reporting protocol, which expose an order-of-magnitude spread in judge reliability that is invisible to accuracy-only leaderboards. We release the protocol and code so that future agent-safety benchmarks can audit their own evaluators rather than trust them by default.

</details>


### [455] [BioMedArena: An Open-source Toolkit for Building and Evaluating Biomedical Deep Research Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06177)
*Jinge Wu,Hongjian Zhou,Mingde Zeng,Jiayuan Zhu,Junde Wu,Jiazhen Pan,Sean Wu,Honghan Wu,Fenglin Liu,David A. Clifton*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BioMedArena is an open-source framework that standardizes biomedical agent evaluation by decoupling evaluation layers, enabling fair, scalable benchmarking across many models, benchmarks, and tools, and achieving SOTA on several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the per-paper engineering tax and inconsistent evaluation surfaces that hinder fair comparison of foundation models in biomedicine, by providing a reusable, extensible evaluation platform.

Method: Decouples six layers of evaluation (benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, scoring); exposes 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 tools across 9 functional families; introduces six agent harnesses with six context-management strategies (yielding 12 backbones); requires only small adapter registrations to add new models/benchmarks/tools.

Result: Attains state-of-the-art results on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks with an average improvement of 15.03 percentage points over prior SOTA.

Conclusion: Reduces per-model engineering burden, enables fair and reproducible comparisons, and facilitates rapid integration of new models/tools; codebase and traces are publicly available at the provided GitHub repository.

Abstract: Building a deep research agent today is an exercise in glue code: the same backbone evaluated on the same benchmark can report different accuracies in different papers because harness and tool registry all differ, and integrating a new foundation model into a comparable evaluation surface costs weeks of model-specific engineering. We call this the per-paper engineering tax and release BioMedArena, an open-source toolkit that not only alleviates it but also provides an arena for fair comparison of different foundation models when evaluating them as deep-research agents. BioMedArena decouples six layers of biomedical agent evaluation -- benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, and scoring -- and exposes 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 biomedical tools across 9 functional families. Adding a new model, benchmark, or tool reduces to registering a few-line provider adapter. We further provide 6 agent harnesses with 6 context-management strategies, which provide 12 backbones with competitive research capabilities and significantly improved performance, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks, with an average lift of +15.03 percentage points over prior SOTA.
