The toolkit, configurations, and per-task traces are available at https://github.com/AI-in-Health/BioMedArena

</details>


### [456] [Rethinking Adapter Placement: A Dominant Adaptation Module Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06183)
*Suoxin Zhang,Run He,Di Fang,Xiang Tan,Kaixuan Chen,Huiping Zhuang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PAGE reveals a single dominant adaptation module where a lone LoRA-like adapter yields strong performance; DomLoRA places a single adapter at this module, achieving substantial parameter efficiency (~0.7% of vanilla LoRA) with better average task performance and supporting other LoRA variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify where to place a few trainable adapters in frozen pre-trained models for maximal performance, addressing the open problem of optimal adapter placement under extreme parameter efficiency.

Method: Introduce PAGE, a gradient-energy probe that estimates the initial trainable gradient energy for each candidate LoRA adapter. Find that energy concentrates on a single shallow FFN down-projection across model families and tasks. Define the dominant adaptation module and develop DomLoRA, which places a single adapter at that module. Evaluate on multiple downstream tasks (instruction following, mathematical reasoning, code generation, multi-turn conversation) and compare to vanilla LoRA and other variants.

Result: PAGE shows strong concentration on one shallow FFN down-projection, defining the dominant adaptation module. DomLoRA with ~0.7% of vanilla LoRA parameters outperforms vanilla LoRA on average across diverse tasks. The approach also improves other LoRA variants, supporting the dominant module as a practical placement guideline. Architecture-dependent but task-stable layer indexing.

Conclusion: A simple, efficient placement strategy—attach a single adapter to the dominant adaptation module—yields strong performance with minimal parameters and generalizes across tasks and LoRA variants. This provides a practical guideline for parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is a widely used parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that places trainable low-rank adapters into frozen pre-trained models. Recent studies show that using fewer LoRA adapters may still maintain or even improve performance, but existing methods still distribute adapters broadly, leaving where to place a limited number of adapters to maximize performance largely open. To investigate this, we introduce PAGE (Projected Adapter Gradient Energy), a gradient-based sensitivity probe that estimates the initial trainable gradient energy available to each candidate LoRA adapter. Surprisingly, we find that PAGE is highly concentrated on a single shallow FFN down-projection across two model families and four downstream tasks. We term this module the dominant adaptation module and show that its layer index is architecture-dependent but task-stable. Motivated by this finding, we propose DomLoRA, a placement method that places a single adapter at the dominant adaptation module. With only ~0.7% of vanilla LoRA's trainable parameters, DomLoRA outperforms it on average across various downstream tasks, including instruction following, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn conversation. This method also improves other LoRA variants, supporting the dominant adaptation module perspective as a practical placement guideline.

</details>


### [457] [Event-Causal RAG: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Long Video Reasoning in Complex Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06185)
*Peizheng Yan,Yu Zhao,Liang Xie,Juntong Qi,Mingming Wang,Erwei Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Event-Causal RAG, a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for infinite long-video reasoning using SES graphs and a global Event Knowledge Graph to enable memory-efficient, causal-consistent reasoning over extended video streams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address ultra-long/infinite video understanding where coherent memory and causal reasoning across distant events are required, overcoming the O(n^2) self-attention bottlenecks and clip-based memory limitations of existing methods.

Method: Segment streaming videos into semantically coherent events; represent each event as a State-Event-State (SES) graph capturing event and surrounding state transitions; merge graphs into a global Event Knowledge Graph stored in a dual-store memory enabling semantic matching and causal-topological retrieval; apply bidirectional retrieval to identify relevant event causal chains; supply evidence and chains to a backbone video foundation model for answer generation.

Result: Outperforms strong clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models on long-video understanding benchmarks, especially for multi-event integration and causal inference across long temporal gaps; improves memory efficiency and streaming performance.

Conclusion: The framework advances long-video reasoning by combining graph-based event representations with retrieval-augmented generation, achieving scalable, memory-efficient, and causally-informed reasoning over infinite video streams.

Abstract: Recent large vision-language models have achieved strong performance on short- and medium-length video understanding, yet they remain inadequate for ultra-long or even infinite video reasoning, where models must preserve coherent memory over extended durations and infer causal dependencies across temporally distant events. Existing end-to-end video understanding methods are fundamentally limited by the $O(n^2)$ complexity of self-attention, while recent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches still suffer from fragmented clip-level memory, weak modeling of temporal and causal structure, and high storage and online inference costs. We present Event-Causal RAG, a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for infinite long-video reasoning. Instead of indexing fixed-length clips, our method segments streaming videos into semantically coherent events and represents each event as a structured State-Event-State (SES) graph, capturing the event together with its surrounding state transitions. These graphs are merged into a global Event Knowledge Graph and stored in a dual-store memory that supports both semantic matching and causal-topological retrieval. On top of this memory, we design a bidirectional retrieval strategy to efficiently identify the most relevant event causal chains and provide them, together with the associated video evidence, to a backbone video foundation model for answer generation. Experiments on long-video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that Event-Causal RAG consistently outperforms strong clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models, particularly on questions requiring multi-event integration and causal inference across long temporal gaps, while also achieving improved memory efficiency and robust streaming performance.

</details>


### [458] [OPSD Compresses What RLVR Teaches: A Post-RL Compaction Stage for Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06188)
*Jaehoon Kim,Dongha Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OPSD in thinking-enabled math reasoning primarily compresses outputs when trained on correct rollouts; training on incorrect rollouts degrades accuracy; a revised pipeline SFT -> RLVR -> OPSD is proposed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why OPSD's token-level supervision underperforms or reverses gains in long-thinking tasks and whether hindsight supervision can improve short outputs, by isolating compression (correct rollouts) from correction (incorrect rollouts).

Method: Apply OPSD separately to correct and incorrect rollout groups in thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, assess impact on accuracy and response length, compare to baselines, and derive implications for pipeline design.

Result: OPSD acts as a compression mechanism in long-thinking math reasoning: training only on correct rollouts preserves accuracy while shortening responses; training only on incorrect rollouts harms accuracy.

Conclusion: Suggest a revised post-training pipeline: SFT then RLVR then OPSD. Highlights: OPSD can be beneficial for compression if trained on correct rollouts, but should be avoided on incorrect rollouts; future work could explore selective or adaptive OPSD strategies and broader task domains.

Abstract: On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) has recently emerged as an alternative to Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), promising higher accuracy and shorter responses through token-level credit assignment from a self-teacher conditioned on privileged context. However, this promise does not carry over to thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, where reported accuracy gains shrink and sometimes turn negative. We hypothesize that hindsight supervision can specify better token-level alternatives in short thinking-disabled outputs, but in long thinking-enabled traces it more readily identifies redundancy than supplies better replacements. To test this, we applied OPSD separately to correct and incorrect rollout groups, so that compression and correction can be observed in isolation. Our results show that in thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, OPSD behaves most reliably as a compression mechanism rather than a correction mechanism: training only on correct rollouts preserves accuracy while substantially shortening responses, whereas training only on incorrect rollouts damages accuracy. In light of these findings, we propose a revised post-training pipeline for thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning: SFT then RLVR then OPSD.

</details>


### [459] [Systematic Evaluation of Large Language Models for Post-Discharge Clinical Action Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06191)
*Shivali Dalmia,Ananya Mantravadi,Prasanna Desikan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Zero-/few-shot LLMs are evaluated for clinical action extraction from discharge notes using a two-stage prompting framework; they match or exceed supervised BERT in binary actionability, but supervised models outperform in fine-grained multi-label tasks; calls for reasoning-annotated datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve safety-critical care transitions by automatically extracting actionable clinical tasks from narrative discharge documentation and understand LLM limitations vs task-specific models.

Method: Two-stage extraction: decompose narrative discharge notes into explicit actionable tasks via staged prompting; compare zero-shot/few-shot LLMs to task-specific BERT; analyze annotation inconsistencies; perform qualitative error analysis.

Result: LLMs comparable or superior to supervised models on binary actionability; supervised better on fine-grained multi-label categories; privacy constraints limit fine-tuning; errors due to misalignment between model reasoning and annotation conventions; lack of rationales.

Conclusion: To advance clinical NLP, datasets should include reasoning annotations (rationales) to distinguish reasoning failures from annotation convention mismatches; evaluation should reflect clinical understanding, not just spans labeled.

Abstract: The work in this paper evaluates zero-shot and few-shot large language models (LLMs) for safety-critical clinical action extraction using the CLIP discharge-note dataset, with particular emphasis on transitions of care and post-discharge patient safety. To manage the complexity of clinical documentation, we introduce a two-stage extraction framework that decomposes discharge notes, that are written in narrative form, into fine-grained, explicitly actionable clinical tasks through a staged prompting strategy. Our contributions include a systematic assessment of generative LLMs for clinical action extraction, a detailed comparison between general-purpose LLMs and task-specific supervised BERT-based models, and an analysis of annotation inconsistencies across different action categories. We show that contemporary LLMs achieve performance comparable to or exceeding supervised models on binary actionability detection, while supervised baselines retain a meaningful advantage on fine-grained multi-label category classification, despite the absence of task-specific fine-tuning and under strict data-privacy constraints. Qualitative error analysis reveals that many failures stem from misalignment between model reasoning and dataset annotation conventions, particularly in cases involving implicit clinical actions and rigid structural labeling rules. These results indicate that reported performance reflects model limitations due to lack of clinical reasoning, that is not captured by plain annotations. Labels without rationales make it impossible to distinguish clinical reasoning failures from annotation convention mismatches. Advancing clinical NLP requires reasoning-annotated datasets that document why specific spans are actionable, not merely which spans were labeled, enabling proper evaluation of model clinical understanding.

</details>


### [460] [The Granularity Axis: A Micro-to-Macro Latent Direction for Social Roles in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06196)
*Chonghan Qin,Xiachong Feng,Ziyun Song,Xiaocheng Feng,Jing Xiong,Lingpeng Kong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Granularity is a dominant, structured latent axis in LLM role representations that encodes macro vs micro social-role granularity; this axis is causal and transferable across models and prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether LLMs internally encode social-role granularity and whether this latent structure is manipulable and generalizes across prompts, models, and datasets.

Method: Construct 75 social roles across five granularity levels; collect 91,200 role-conditioned responses over shared questions and prompt variants; extract role-level hidden states and project onto a defined Granularity Axis (difference between mean macro- and micro-role hidden states); analyze alignment with PC1, monotonicity across layers/prompts/splits/filters; test transfer to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct; perform activation steering along the axis to adjust response granularity.

Result: Granularity Axis aligns with PC1 (cosine 0.972) and explains 52.6% of variance in role representations; role projections increase monotonically across levels and are stable across layers, prompts, endpoints, held-out splits, and score-filtered subsets; axis transfers to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct; activation steering along the axis shifts granularity (e.g., 2.00 to 3.17 on a five-point scale); controllability differs between models, suggesting dependence on default operating regime.

Conclusion: Social role granularity is a structured, ordered, and causally manipulable latent direction in role-conditioned LLM behavior, not merely a surface feature, with cross-model generalization and potential for controlled alignment.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are routinely prompted to take on social roles ranging from individuals to institutions, yet it remains unclear whether their internal representations encode the granularity of such roles, from micro-level individual experience to macro-level organizational, institutional, or national reasoning. We show that they do. We define a contrast-based Granularity Axis as the difference between mean macro- and micro-role hidden states. In Qwen3-8B, this axis aligns with the principal axis (PC1) of the role representation space at cosine 0.972 and accounts for 52.6% of its variance, indicating that granularity is the dominant geometric axis organizing prompted social roles. We construct 75 social roles across five granularity levels and collect 91,200 role-conditioned responses over shared questions and prompt variants, then extract role-level hidden states and project them onto the axis. Role projections increase monotonically across all five levels, remain stable across layers, prompt variants, endpoint definitions, held-out splits, and score-filtered subsets, and transfer to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. The axis is also causally relevant: activation steering along it shifts response granularity in the predicted direction, with Llama moving from 2.00 to 3.17 on a five-point macro scale under positive steering on prompts that admit local responses. The two models differ in controllability, suggesting that steering depends on each model's default operating regime. Overall, our findings suggest that social role granularity is not merely a stylistic surface feature, but a structured, ordered, and causally manipulable latent direction in role-conditioned language model behavior.

</details>


### [461] [Towards Annotation-Free Validation of MLLMs: A Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06201)
*Ying Gu,Mei Chee Leong,Hui Li Tan,Shangbo Mao,Liyuan Li,Nancy Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces VL-LCM, a framework to assess vision-language logical consistency in multi-modal LLMs without ground-truth, evaluated on MC-VQA and NaturalBench across 11 open-source models; finds logical consistency lags behind accuracy but correlates with GT-based metrics and reliability, enabling model selection and justification in novel tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional accuracy metrics can reward unwarranted guessing and fail to validate models on tasks lacking ground-truth annotations. A logic-based consistency metric is needed to evaluate LLMs in vision-language contexts, including novel tasks.

Method: Define Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric (VL-LCM) using necessary and sufficient cause-effect relations on MC-VQA tests and NaturalBench; conduct systematic experiments on MMMU VL benchmark and NaturalBench; evaluate 11 open-source MLLMs from four families.

Result: VL-LCM reveals that although accuracy has improved, logical consistency remains significantly lagging. VL-LCM correlates with GT-based metrics, demonstrates reliability, and relates to response distributions, validating its applicability without GT. VL-LCM supports model selection, validation, and justification of answers in novel tasks without GT.

Conclusion: VL-LCM is a viable, GT-free metric for assessing logical consistency in VL models, complementing accuracy metrics and enabling reliable evaluation and justification in new tasks and model selection.

Abstract: Dominant accuracy evaluation might reward unwarranted guessing of Large Language Models, and it might not be applicable to novel tasks for model validation without ground-truth (gt) annotation. Based on basic logic principle, we propose a novel framework to evaluate the vision-language logical consistency of MLLMs on both sufficient and necessary cause-effect relations. We define Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric (VL-LCM) on traditional MC-VQA tests, and recent NaturalBench tests without the need for gt annotation. Through systematic experiments on representative VL benchmark MMMU and recent VL challenges like NaturalBench, we evaluated 11 recent open-source MLLMs from 4 frontier families. Our findings reveal that, despite significant progress of recent MLLMs on accuracy, logical consistency lags behind significantly. Extensive evaluations on the correlations of VL-LCM with metrics on gt, the reliability of LCM, and the relation of VL-LCM with response distribution justify the validity and applicability of VL-LCM even without gt annotation. Our findings suggest that, beyond accuracy, logical consistency could be employed for both accuracy and reliability. VL-LCM can also be employed for MLLM selection, validation, and reliable answer justification in novel tasks without gt annotation.

</details>


### [462] [Beyond Fixed Benchmarks and Worst-Case Attacks: Dynamic Boundary Evaluation for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06213)
*Haoxiang Wang,Da Yu,Huishuai Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Dynamic Boundary Evaluation (DBE) to place LLM evaluation on a boundary-based, globally comparable difficulty scale; provides item bank, boundary search algorithm, and adaptive protocol; evaluated across safety, capability, and truthfulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks saturate models and produce ceiling/floor effects; the most informative signal is at the boundary where per-prompt pass probability is near 0.5 under random-sampling decoding; DBE aims to locate this boundary and compare models on a common scale.

Method: Construct a calibrated item bank with per-item difficulty labels validated across 9 reference LLMs (covering safety, capability, truthfulness); introduce Skill-Guided Boundary Search (SGBS) to locate boundary items for a target LLM using only API-level queries; implement an evaluation protocol that maps a new LLM onto a unified ability scale and adaptively grows the bank if the target lies outside its coverage.

Result: DBE yields a broader, non-saturating evaluation of LLMs across four categories (harmful request refusal/over-refusal, constrained instruction following, multi-turn sycophancy resistance); maintains compatibility with existing datasets while expanding coverage across safety, capability, and truthfulness.

Conclusion: Boundary-centric evaluation via DBE enables more informative, comparable, and scalable assessment of LLMs, reducing saturation and enabling adaptive growth of the evaluation bank.

Abstract: Evaluating large language models (LLMs) today rests on fixed benchmarks that apply the same set of items to any model, producing ceiling and floor effects that mask capability gaps. We argue that the most informative evaluation signal lies at the boundary, where the per-prompt pass probability is near $0.5$ under random-sampling decoding, and propose Dynamic Boundary Evaluation (DBE), which actively locates each model's boundary and places it on a globally comparable difficulty scale. DBE delivers three artifacts: (i) a calibrated item bank covering safety, capability, and truthfulness, with per-item difficulty labels validated across $9$ reference LLMs; (ii) Skill-Guided Boundary Search (SGBS), a search algorithm that finds boundary items for a given target LLM using only API-level query access; and (iii) an evaluation protocol that places a new LLM on a unified ability scale and grows the evaluation set adaptively when the target falls outside the bank's coverage. We instantiate DBE on four categories spanning safety (harmful request refusal and over-refusal), capability (constrained instruction following), and truthfulness (multi-turn sycophancy resistance). The resulting evaluation covers a broader model spectrum without saturation while remaining compatible with existing datasets.

</details>


### [463] [Joint Consistency: A Unified Test-Time Aggregation Framework via Energy Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06219)
*Yunzhen Yao,Hongye Wang,Yahong Wang,Michael C. Gastpar,Bo Jiang,Lie He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Joint Consistency (JC): a test-time reasoning trace aggregation method framed as a constrained Ising-type energy minimization; external fields from independent evaluation signals and interactions from pairwise trace comparisons unify voting and weighted aggregation; scalable via efficient approximation and interaction modeling via LLM-as-a-judge; shows improvements on math/code reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing test-time aggregation methods largely rely on independent signals (e.g., trace quality or answer frequency) and ignore interactions among candidate traces. This can miss complementary information and joint consistency patterns across traces. A unified framework to model both signal and interaction can improve final answers and provide theoretical grounding.

Method: Formulate aggregation as a constrained Ising-type energy minimization where each trace is a node. External fields encode independent evaluation signals for traces; pairwise interactions encode comparative consistency between traces. The interaction matrix is constructed using LLM-as-a-judge comparisons, with a theoretical interpretation under answer-level homogeneity assumptions. An efficient approximation strategy is developed to scale to large trace budgets.

Result: Empirical results on math and code reasoning benchmarks show JC consistently outperforms baseline aggregation methods across tasks, judge models, trace budgets, and trace-generation settings.

Conclusion: JC provides a unified, principled framework for test-time trace aggregation that subsumes voting and weighted aggregation as special cases. Interaction modeling via LLM judgments and the efficient approximation enable scalable, robust performance improvements.

Abstract: This paper studies test-time aggregation, an approach that generates multiple reasoning traces and aggregates them into a final answer. Most existing methods rely on evaluation signals collected from candidate traces in isolation or answer frequencies, while ignoring comparative interactions among candidates. We propose Joint Consistency (JC), formulated as a constrained Ising-type energy minimization problem, where independent evaluation signals act as external fields and pairwise comparisons act as interactions. JC provides a unified framework for test-time aggregation that subsumes existing voting and weighted aggregation methods as special cases. Our construction of the interaction matrix leverages LLM-as-a-judge comparisons, and admits a theoretical interpretation under answer-level homogeneity assumptions. Moreover, we develop an efficient approximation strategy that makes interaction modeling practical for large-scale test-time aggregation. Experiments on math and code reasoning benchmarks show that JC consistently outperforms existing baselines across tasks, judge models, trace budgets, and trace-generation settings.

</details>


### [464] [A Versatile AI Agent for Rare Disease Diagnosis and Risk Gene Prioritization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06226)
*Tianyu Liu,Wangjie Zheng,Rui Yang,Benny Kai Guo Loo,Hui Zhang,Jeffries Lauran,Jianlei Gu,Botao Yu,Weihao Xuan,Kexin Huang,Nan Liu,James Zou,Yonghui Jiang,Hua Xu,Hongyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hygieia is a multi-modal AI diagnostic agent that integrates phenotypic, genomic, and clinical data to support precision diagnosis, reduces hallucinations with a router-based, knowledge-enhanced architecture, and provides confidence scores. It achieves state-of-the-art results on diagnostic benchmarks and improves physician performance while reducing workload, validated with Yale and Duke-NUS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address delays and inaccuracies in rare-disease diagnosis by leveraging diverse data sources, improving interpretability, and providing decision-support through confidence scoring.

Method: Develop a router-based, knowledge-enhanced multi-modal AI architecture that fuses phenotypic, genomic, and clinical data; prioritize risk-related genomic factors for rare diseases; generate and report confidence scores; validate on diagnostic benchmarks and in collaboration with clinicians for real-world case handling.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on multiple diagnostic benchmarks; improved diagnostic performance over physicians (reported improvement range 12%–60%); effective support for clinicians with medical records in real-world cases; notable reduction in clinician workload and enhanced interpretability.

Conclusion: Hygieia shows strong potential as a clinical decision-support tool for precision diagnosis, especially for rare diseases, due to high accuracy, interpretability, and workload reduction, with need for further prospective validation and workflow integration.

Abstract: Accurate and timely diagnosis is essential for effective treatment, particularly in the context of rare diseases. However, current diagnostic workflows often lead to prolonged assessment times and low accuracy. To address these limitations, we introduce Hygieia, a multi-modal AI agent system designed to support precision disease diagnosis by integrating diverse data sources, including phenotypic features, genetic profiles, and clinical records. Hygieia features a router-based and knowledge-enhanced framework that mitigates hallucination and tailors diagnostic strategies to different disease categories. Notably, it prioritizes risk-related genomic factors for rare diseases and provides confidence scores to assist clinical decision-making. We conducted a comprehensive evaluation demonstrating that Hygieia achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple diagnostic benchmarks. In collaboration with clinical experts from Yale School of Medicine and Duke-NUS Medical School, we further validated its practical utility by showing (1) Hygieia's superior diagnostic performance compared to physicians with an improvement from 12%-60% and (2) its effectiveness in assisting clinicians with medical records for handling real-world cases. Our findings indicate that Hygieia not only enhances diagnostic accuracy and interpretability but also significantly reduces clinician workload, highlighting its potential as a valuable tool in clinical decision support systems.

</details>


### [465] [Price of Fairness in Short-Term and Long-Term Algorithmic Selections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06227)
*Shahin Jabbari,Chen Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Dynamic fairness in sequential decisions reveals a tension between short-term utility and long-term equity: the Price of Fairness (PoF) can be large even when groups are nearly identical; however, simple investment policies can drive long-run disparities to near zero with low PoF. The results are supported by theory and experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static fairness constraints may worsen disparities in evolving populations. A sequential decision process lets decisions influence the population over time, necessitating a fairness framework that captures both immediate and future effects and quantifies the trade-off via PoF.

Method: The paper analyzes a stylized sequential selection problem where a decision-maker repeatedly chooses individuals, affecting immediate utility and population distribution over time. It develops notions of short-term and long-term group fairness, derives optimal and fair policies for the short term, defines and analyzes the Price of Fairness, and proposes simple investment policies to achieve low PoF. The results are complemented by empirical validation on synthetic and real datasets.

Result: The Price of Fairness can be substantial in the short term even when group distributions are nearly identical. In contrast, long-term disparities can vanish under simple investment policies that achieve a low PoF. The paper also characterizes optimal and fair short-term policies and provides empirical support for the theoretical findings.

Conclusion: Dynamic fairness considerations are essential in sequential decision-making. While static fairness constraints may incur large short-term costs, long-run equity can be achieved with investing policies that yield a low PoF. The work provides a framework to analyze fairness-utility trade-offs over time and guides policy design in high-stakes, time-evolving settings.

Abstract: Algorithmic decision-making in high-stakes settings can have profound impacts on individuals and populations. While much prior work studies fairness in static settings, recent results show that enforcing static fairness constraints may exacerbate long-run disparities. Motivated by this tension, we study a stylized sequential selection problem in which a decision-maker repeatedly selects individuals, affecting both immediate utility and the population distribution over time. We introduce notions of group fairness for both the short and long term and theoretically analyze the trade-off between fairness and utility via the Price of Fairness (PoF). We characterize optimal and fair policies in the short term and show that the PoF can be large even when group distributions are nearly identical. In contrast, we show that long-term disparities can vanish under simple investment policies that achieve a low PoF. We also empirically validate these theoretical observations using both synthetic and real datasets.

</details>


### [466] [Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06230)
*Xinquan Chen,Zhenyun Yin,Shan He,Bin Huang,Shanzhe Lei,Pengcheng Shi,Kun Cai,Bei Chen,Bangwei Liu,Zeyu Kang,Chao Huang,Yang Zhang,Wenjie Li,Ruijun Ge,Yajie Wang,Tianshun Fang,Tianyang Xu,Yiwen Cong,Meng Jin,Gaolei Li,Xuansheng Wu,Linhan Liu,Zijing He,An Li,Yan Teng,Xin Tan,ChaoChao Lu,Ji He,Jie Li,Chunfeng Song,Jinya Xu,Fan Song,Shujie Wang,Jianmin Qian,Jie Hou,Xuhong Wang,Yingchun Wang,Hui Wang,Xia Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Safactory is a scalable framework for trustworthy autonomous intelligence that unifies long-horizon decision making, tool use, and real-environment interaction by integrating simulation, data, and evolution into one pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As large models evolve into autonomous agents, challenges mount in long-horizon decision making, tool use, and real environment interaction. Existing agent infrastructures are fragmented across evaluation, data management, and agent evolution, hindering systematic risk discovery and continuous improvement.

Method: Safactory comprises three tightly coupled platforms: (1) a Parallel Simulation Platform for trajectory generation, (2) a Trustworthy Data Platform for trajectory storage and experience extraction, and (3) an Autonomous Evolution Platform for asynchronous reinforcement learning and on-policy distillation, forming a unified evolutionary pipeline.

Result: The abstract positions Safactory as the first framework to propose a unified evolutionary pipeline for next-generation trustworthy autonomous intelligence.

Conclusion: Safactory offers a scalable, integrated solution that links simulation, data management, and autonomous evolution to enable trustworthy and continuous improvement of autonomous agents.

Abstract: As large models evolve from conversational assistants into autonomous agents, challenges increasingly arise from long-horizon decision making, tool use, and real environment interaction. Existing agenticinfrastructure remain fragmented across evaluation, data management, and agent evolution, making it difficult to discover risks systematically and improve models in a continuous closed loop. In this report, we present \textbf{Safactory}, a scalable agent factory for trustworthy autonomous intelligence. Safactory integrates three tightly coupled platforms: a \textbf{Parallel Simulation Platform} for trajectory generation, a \textbf{Trustworthy Data Platform} for trajectory storage and experience extraction, and an \textbf{Autonomous Evolution Platform} for asynchronous reinforcement learning and on-policy distillation. As far as we know, Safactory is the first framework to propose a unified evolutionary pipeline for next-generation trustworthy autonomous intelligence.

</details>


### [467] [Data Language Models: A New Foundation Model Class for Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06290)
*Eda Erol,Giuliano Pezzoli,Ozer Cem Kelahmet*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces a Data Language Model (DLM) for tabular data; Schema-1 is a 140M-parameter model trained on 2.3M datasets, achieving native tabular understanding and outperforming baselines on prediction and imputation; can identify industry sector from raw values.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tabular data lacks native foundation models and current tabular AI relies on preprocessing pipelines; a true tabular modality model could enable direct, end-to-end AI on raw tables.

Method: Train Schema-1 (140M params) on >2.3M synthetic and real-world tabular datasets; evaluate on established row-level prediction benchmarks; compare to gradient-boosted trees, AutoML stacks, and other tabular foundation models; assess missing-value reconstruction and sector identification from raw cell values.

Result: Schema-1 outperforms gradient-boosted ensembles, AutoML stacks, and evaluated tabular foundation models on row-level prediction benchmarks; achieves lower missing-value reconstruction error than classical statistics methods and frontier LLMs; reliably identifies the industry sector of unseen datasets from raw values.

Conclusion: DLM acts as the native tabular understanding layer, eliminating preprocessing pipelines and enabling AI applications built on raw tabular data; establishes a foundation similar to language/vision/audio foundation models for tabular data.

Abstract: Every major data modality now has a foundation model that understands it natively: text has language models, images have vision models, audio has audio models. Tabular data, the modality on which many consequential real-world AI decisions are made, does not. Every approach to tabular AI today, from gradient-boosted trees to the latest tabular foundation models, requires a preprocessing pipeline before any model can consume the data. None of them understand tabular data as a modality. We introduce the Data Language Model (DLM), the missing foundation model for tabular data. A DLM understands tables the way a language model understands sentences: natively, without serialization or preprocessing, directly from raw cell values. It is the tabular data layer on which AI models, agents, and vertical AI applications can be built, eliminating the preprocessing pipelines that currently stand between raw data and every AI system that consumes it. We present Schema-1, the first DLM: a 140M parameter model trained on more than 2.3M synthetic and real-world tabular datasets. Schema-1 outperforms gradient-boosted ensembles, AutoML stacks, and the tabular foundation models we evaluate on established row-level prediction benchmarks. On missing value reconstruction it achieves lower reconstruction error than all classical statistical methods and frontier large language models on mean performance across conditions, establishing that structural understanding of a dataset's own distributional geometry is more useful for imputation than world knowledge encoded in language. It identifies the industry sector of any unseen dataset from raw cell values alone, reliably across any domain, a task no prior tabular model can perform. It is the native tabular understanding layer that has been missing from the AI stack.

</details>


### [468] [Addressing Labelled Data Scarcity: Taxonomy-Agnostic Annotation of PII Values in HTTP Traffic using LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06305)
*Thomas Cory,Axel Küpper*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs enable taxonomy-agnostic PII annotation in HTTP traffic via a multi-stage pipeline, plus an LLM-based synthetic data generator; successful across three taxonomies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing detectors rely on scarce manually labeled traffic and fixed taxonomies, limiting transferability across domains and evolving PII definitions; needs taxonomy-agnostic, flexible annotation and data generation for privacy auditing.

Method: A multi-stage LLM-based pipeline: deterministic pre-processing, label-level classification, targeted instance-level value annotation, and output validation. An LLM-based generator creates synthetic HTTP traffic with taxonomy-derived, manually validated PII annotations. Evaluation across three taxonomies with different granularity.

Result: The pipeline accurately detects PII types and extracts corresponding values for concrete taxonomies, demonstrating taxonomy-agnostic annotation and viable synthetic data creation for evolving privacy taxonomies.

Conclusion: LLMs can serve as a flexible foundation for taxonomy-agnostic traffic annotation and for generating labeled data under evolving privacy taxonomies, aiding privacy audits and dataset creation.

Abstract: Automated privacy audits of web and mobile applications often analyse outbound HTTP traffic to detect Personally Identifiable Information (PII) leakage. However, existing learning-based detectors typically depend on scarce, manually labelled traffic and are tightly coupled to fixed label taxonomies, limiting transferability across domains and evolving definitions of PII. This paper investigates whether Large Language Models (LLMs) can support taxonomy-agnostic annotation of explicitly transmitted PII values in HTTP message bodies when the taxonomy is provided at runtime. We introduce a multi-stage LLM-based pipeline that combines deterministic pre-processing with label-level classification, targeted instance-level value annotation, and output validation. To enable controlled evaluation and exemplar-based prompting without relying on sensitive real-user captures, we further propose an LLM-based generator for synthetic HTTP traffic with manually validated, taxonomy-derived PII annotations. We evaluate the approach across three taxonomies spanning different PII domains and granularity levels. Results show that the pipeline accurately detects PII types and extracts corresponding values for concrete PII taxonomies. Overall, our findings position LLMs as a promising foundation for flexible, taxonomy-agnostic traffic annotation and for creating labelled data under evolving privacy taxonomies.

</details>


### [469] [Mind the Gap? A Distributional Comparison of Real and Synthetic Priors for Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06343)
*Alex O. Davies,Telmo de Menezes e Silva Filho,Nirav Ajmeri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TabICL synthetic data occupies a narrow region of the real-table distribution; curated and web-scraped corpora are broadly interchangeable; the distributional gap between synthetic and real data affects performance in ways not captured by simple feature-based proximity or TabICL's internal representations; extensive hyperparameter tuning cannot close this gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how three canonical data classes used to pre-train tabular foundation models relate distributionally and how this relates to downstream performance.

Method: Characterize corpora (web-scraped T4, Kaggle-curated TabFM, synthetic TabICL) using aggregate table-, column-, and correlation-level features; compare distributions with discriminator AUCs and k-NN coverage; assess impact of TabICL hyperparameters (up to ~86k configurations).

Result: TabICL occupies a narrow region of the real-table feature space; there is a distributional gap that cannot be closed by hyperparameter optimization across 86k configurations; curated and web-scraped corpora are broadly interchangeable distributionally; the gap between synthetic and real data has a detectable impact on downstream performance that is not captured by feature-based proximity measures or TabICL's internal representations; coverage of the real-data distribution is not the primary driver of TabICL generalisation.

Conclusion: Synthetic priors like TabICL have limited coverage of the real-data distribution, and merely matching proximity in feature space or internal representations does not explain TabICL generalisation. Real-data distribution coverage may be insufficient to account for performance; other factors (e.g., distributional alignment with downstream tasks) likely govern generalisation.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models are pre-trained on one of three classes of corpus: curated datasets drawn from benchmark repositories, tables harvested at scale from the web, or synthetic tables sampled from a parametric generative prior. Despite the centrality of pre-training data to model performance, little is known about how these corpora relate to one another in distribution, and the impact this has on downstream performance. In this work we take three canonical, archetypal datasets used to train tabular foundation models; the T4 dataset represents web-scraped corpora, the TabFM dataset curated tables from Kaggle, and the TabICL dataset as the only well-used synthetic prior with publicly available parameters. We characterise each corpus using aggregate features over whole tables, columns and correlations, and compare them using discriminator AUCs and k-NN coverage metrics. We find that the TabICL synthetic prior occupies a narrow region of the space of real tables, that this mismatch cannot be closed by optimising prior hyper-parameters across more than 86 thousand configurations, and that curated and web-scraped corpora are broadly interchangeable on a distributional level in feature space. Surprisingly, the distributional gap between synthetic pre-training data and real tables has a clearly detectable effect on performance under neither feature-based proximity measures or TabICL's own internal representations, suggesting that coverage of the real-data distribution is not the primary driver of TabICL's generalisation.

</details>


### [470] [More Than Can Be Said: A Benchmark and Framework for Pre-Question Scientific Ideation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06345)
*Jie Yu,Song Qiu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces InciteResearch, a multi-agent system that converts tacit researcher friction into explicit, actionable insights via a five-dimensional profile, a 7-stage causal derivation trace, and validation that insights are necessary consequences; complements with TF-Bench, a tacit-to-explicit research benchmark across four scientific modes, showing superior novelty and impact over prompt-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between human tacit reasoning and explicit problem formulation in research, enabling AI to assist by making implicit misalignments and assumptions explicit.

Method: A multi-agent framework that: (1) builds a five-dimensional researcher profile anchored by friction points from vague inputs; (2) maximizes feasibility-novelty product while enforcing a 7-stage causal derivation trace to surface and challenge hidden assumptions; (3) checks whether a proposed method is a necessary consequence of the reframed insight. Introduces TF-Bench to benchmark tacit-to-explicit assistance across four scientific modes and to separate domain-related from domain-unrelated inspirations.

Result: Empirical gains on TF-Bench show InciteResearch surpasses a prompt-based baseline in novelty (4.250 vs 3.671) and impact (4.397 vs 3.806), indicating a shift from recombination to architectural insight in generated proposals.

Conclusion: AI can function as an extension of the researcher’s thinking, not merely as an execution engine, with InciteResearch and TF-Bench enabling tacit-to-explicit research assistance.

Abstract: AI research agents have shown strong potential in automating literature search and manuscript refinement, yet most assume a clear and actionable initial input, operating only after a research question has been made explicit. In contrast, human research often begins with tacit friction, a sense of misalignment before a question can be formed. We introduce InciteResearch, a multi-agent framework designed to make a researcher's implicit understanding explicit, inspectable, and actionable. InciteResearch decomposes the logical chain of Socratic questioning and distributes it across the entire pipeline that: (1) Elicits a structured five-dimensional researcher profile state anchored by specific friction points from vague, even domain-unrelated inputs; (2) Violates hidden assumptions by maximizing the feasibility-novelty product with enforcing a 7-stage causal derivation trace; and (3) check whether the proposed method is a Necessary consequence of the reframed insight. We further introduce TF-Bench, the first benchmark for tacit-to-explicit research assistance that distinguishes domain-related from domain-unrelated inspirations across four scientific modes. On TF-Bench, InciteResearch achieves leapfrogging gains over a prompt-based baseline (novelty/impact from 3.671/3.806 to 4.250/4.397), shifting generated proposals from recombination to architectural insight. Our work demonstrates that AI can serve as an extension of thinking itself, rather than merely automating downstream execution.

</details>


### [471] [Prediction and Empowerment: A Theory of Agency through Bridge Interfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06346)
*Richard Csaky*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A theoretical framework for agency under partial observability using deterministic world models with bridge sensing-action interfaces, revealing a separation between prediction, compression, and empowerment; identifies when perfect prediction is possible, proposes information-budget tradeoffs, and offers design principles for AI alignment centered on hidden-state identification, interface refinement, and controllability rather than mere overwrite control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how deterministic dynamics with partial observability generate apparent randomness and to derive principled guidelines for constructing and aligning AI agents through interface design that mediates human intent, internal states, tools, and world-channel conditions.

Method: Model sensing and actuation as bridge interfaces splitting agent-controlled parameters and environment-controlled channel state. Formalize a deterministic POMDP via a prior over latent microstates and many-to-one observation coarsening. Prove a separation between prediction, compression, and empowerment. Introduce refinable interfaces, sufficient memory, and action-conditioned observation compression to reduce posterior uncertainty and achieve target-conditioned interface empowerment. Develop a bit-string specialization with a conserved information budget to make tradeoffs explicit.

Result: Key theoretical results include: (1) perfect prediction is achievable by identifying the hidden quotient relevant to the task family or via overwrite control that makes the future action-determined; empowerment alone is insufficient; (2) with refinable interfaces and sufficient memory, observation compression reduces posterior uncertainty about latent quotients; (3) when refinement requires steering world-side channel conditions, this yields target-conditioned interface empowerment; (4) a bit-string information-budget framework equates prediction requiring internal capacity at least the latent entropy and overwrite requiring terminal action capacity over the controlled quotient; (5) practical design principle: distinguish hidden-state identification, interface refinement, task-relevant controllability, and overwrite/distractor control; implications for human-AI alignment as an interface-design problem.

Conclusion: The work provides a principled design lens for AI agents focusing on how interfaces mediate hidden-state identification, refinement of the interaction with the world, and controllability, rather than relying on mere overwrite or distractor control. It frames human–AI alignment as an interface problem bridging human intent, agent state, tools, and world conditions. The authors invite feedback as this is a working draft.

Abstract: We study agency under partial observability in deterministic physical or simulated worlds, where apparent randomness arises from uncertainty over initial conditions, fixed law bits, and unrolled exogenous noise. We model sensing and actuation as bridge interfaces split between agent-controlled parameters and environment-controlled channel state, inducing a deterministic POMDP through a prior over latent microstates and many-to-one observation coarsening. Within this framework, we prove a separation between prediction, compression, and empowerment. Perfect prediction can be achieved either by identifying the hidden quotient relevant to the target family or by overwrite control that makes the future target action-determined; high empowerment alone is insufficient. Under refinable interfaces and sufficient memory, action-conditioned observation-compression progress reduces posterior uncertainty about the latent quotient, and when refinement requires steering world-side channel conditions, this creates target-conditioned interface empowerment. A bit-string specialization with a conserved information budget makes the resulting tradeoff explicit: prediction by identification requires internal capacity at least the relevant latent entropy, whereas overwrite control requires terminal action capacity over the controlled quotient. For modern AI agents, the results suggest a design principle rather than a theorem of inevitability: objectives should distinguish hidden-state identification, interface refinement, task-relevant controllability, and mere overwrite or distractor control. Human--AI alignment is partly an interface-design problem, where the relevant bridge is between human intent, agent internal state, external tools, and world-side channel conditions. This is a working draft: feedback and criticism is most welcome.

</details>


### [472] [From Agent Loops to Deterministic Graphs: Execution Lineage for Reproducible AI-Native Work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06365)
*Josh Rosen,Seth Rosen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Execution lineage uses a DAG of artifact-producing computations to preserve stable work products across revisions, outperforming loop-based baselines on state preservation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Implicit conversational state in AI agent workflows makes it difficult to preserve work products, isolate irrelevant updates, and propagate changes through intermediate artifacts.

Method: Introduce an execution lineage modeled as a DAG with explicit dependencies, stable intermediate boundaries, and identity-based replay; compare DAG replay to loop-centric update baselines on two controlled policy-memo update tasks (unrelated-branch update and intermediate-artifact edit).

Result: DAG replay preserved the final memo exactly with zero churn and zero unrelated contamination in unrelated-branch updates; only DAG replay achieved perfect upstream preservation, downstream propagation, unaffected-artifact preservation, and cross-artifact consistency in intermediate-artifact edits, while loop baselines could still produce polished final outputs under bounded synthesis with all sources in context.

Conclusion: Final answer quality and maintained-state quality can diverge; execution lineage provides stronger guarantees about what should change, what should remain stable, and how work evolves across revisions, beyond immediate task success.

Abstract: Large language model systems are increasingly deployed as agentic workflows that interleave reasoning, tool use, memory, and iterative refinement. These systems are effective at producing answers, but they often rely on implicit conversational state, making it difficult to preserve stable work products, isolate irrelevant updates, or propagate changes through intermediate artifacts.
  We introduce execution lineage: an execution model in which AI-native work is represented as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of artifact-producing computations with explicit dependencies, stable intermediate boundaries, and identity-based replay. The goal is not to make the model a better one-shot writer, but to make evolving AI-generated work maintainable under change.
  We compare execution-lineage replay against loop-centric update baselines on two controlled policy-memo update tasks. In an unrelated-branch update, DAG replay preserved the final memo exactly in all runs, with zero churn and zero unrelated-branch contamination, while loop baselines regenerated the memo and frequently imported unrelated context. In an intermediate-artifact edit, all systems reflected the new constraint in the final memo, but only DAG replay achieved perfect upstream preservation, downstream propagation, unaffected-artifact preservation, and cross-artifact consistency.
  These results show that final answer quality and maintained-state quality are distinct. Strong loop baselines can remain competitive at producing polished final outputs when the task is a bounded synthesis/update problem and all current sources fit in context, but immediate task success can mask partial state inconsistency that may compound over future revisions. Execution lineage provides stronger guarantees about what should change, what should remain stable, and how work evolves across revisions.

</details>


### [473] [Debiased Multimodal Personality Understanding through Dual Causal Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06371)
*Yangfu Zhu,Zitong Han,Nianwen Ning,Yuting Wei,Yuandong Wang,Hang Feng,Zhenzhou Shao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes DCAN with Back-door and Front-door Causal Learning to deconfound multimodal personality understanding using a Structural Causal Model; introduces the Demographic-annotated Multimodal Student Personality (DMSP) dataset; demonstrates accuracy and fairness improvements on CFI-V2 and DMSP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fairness concerns in multimodal personality understanding due to demographic biases and spurious correlations; current methods may entangle observable and unobservable subject attributes with traits, leading to biased inferences.

Method: Build a Structural Causal Model (SCM) for personality understanding. Develop Dual Causal Adjustment Network (DCAN) comprising: (1) Back-door Adjustment Causal Learning (BACL) with a prototype-based confounder dictionary to block correlations from observable demographic factors; (2) Front-door Adjustment Causal Learning (FACL) with a learned mediator dictionary to intervene on latent/unobservable biases; perform causal disentanglement for deconfounded reasoning. Construct DMSP dataset for fairness analysis. Evaluate on CFI-V2 and DMSP datasets.

Result: DCAN yields higher prediction accuracy (92.11% on CFI-V2 and 92.90% on DMSP). Fairness metrics improve: equal opportunity +6.57% and demographic parity +7.97% on CFI-V2; +15.38% equal opportunity and +20.06% demographic parity on DMSP.

Conclusion: DCAN provides effective causal disentanglement for multimodal personality understanding, improving both accuracy and fairness; introduces a new dataset (DMSP) and releases code and data.

Abstract: Multimodalpersonalityunderstandingplaysacriticalroleinhuman centered artificial intelligence. Previous work mainly focus on learn-ing rich multimodal representations for video personality under standing. However, they often suffer from potential harm caused by subject bias (e.g., observable age and unobservable mental states), as subjects originate from diverse demographic backgrounds. Learn ing such spurious associations between multimodal features and traits may lead to unfair personality understanding. In this work, weconstruct aStructural Causal Model (SCM)toanalyze theimpact of these biases from a causal perspective, and propose a novel Dual Causal Adjustment Network (DCAN) to mitigate the interference of subject attributes on personality understanding. Specifically, we design a Back-door Adjustment Causal Learning (BACL) module to block spurious correlations from observable demographic factors via a prototype-based confounder dictionary, and subsequently ap ply a Front-door Adjustment Causal Learning (FACL) module to ad dress latent and unobservable biases throughalearnedmediatordic tionary intervention, thereby achieving causal disentanglement of representations for deconfounded reasoning. Importantly, we con struct a Demographic-annotated Multimodal Student Personality (DMSP) dataset to support the analysis and discussion of fairness related factors. Extensive experiments on the benchmark dataset CFI-V2 and our DMSPdataset demonstrate that DCAN consistently improves prediction accuracy, reaching 92.11% and 92.90%, respec tively. Meanwhile, the improvementsinthefairnessmetricsofequal opportunity and demographic parity are 6.57% and 7.97% on CFI-V2, and 15.38% and 20.06% on the DMSP dataset. Our code and DMSP dataset are available at https://github.com/Sabrina-han/DCAN

</details>


### [474] [Rethinking Vacuity for OOD Detection in Evidential Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06382)
*Claire McNamara*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Vacuity-based OOD detection in EDL is highly sensitive to the number of classes; mismatches in ID/OOD cardinality can falsely inflate AUROC/AUPR, confirmed for standard EDL and IB-EDL and discussed for LLM MCQA contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address evaluation artefacts in OOD detection using vacuity (UM) that arise because the uncertainty mass depends on the class count K; when K differs between ID and OOD, comparison metrics may be biased. The work seeks both empirical and theoretical understanding and guidance for clearer ID/OOD definitions.

Method: Theoretically analyze the dependence of vacuity on K and S (sum of Dirichlet parameters); perform empirical experiments comparing AUROC and AUPR for ID vs OOD with K differing by 1, across standard EDL and IB-EDL; discuss implications for LLMs with MCQA datasets and emphasize the need for equal K or adjusted evaluation.

Result: When K_ID and K_OOD differ by 1, AUROC and AUPR can change substantially, with up to 0.318 (AUROC) and 0.613 (AUPR) for standard EDL, and up to 0.360 (AUROC) and 0.683 (AUPR) for IB-EDL. These shifts occur without changes to model predictions, indicating a vacuity-based evaluation artefact.

Conclusion: Vacuity-based OOD detection in EDL fine-tuned LLMs is highly sensitive to uncontrolled differences in class cardinality; accurate OOD assessment requires equalized ID/OOD K or adjusted metrics, and clearer definitions of ID vs OOD in MCQA/causal language model settings.

Abstract: Vacuity, or Uncertainty Mass (UM), is commonly used as a metric to evaluate Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection in Evidential Deep Learning (EDL). It generally involves dividing the number of classes ($K$) by the total strength of belief ($S$) of the model's predictions, where $S$ is derived from summing the Dirichlet parameters. As such, UM is sensitive to the cardinality of $K$. In particular, it is unlikely in practice that there is a linear relationship between $K$ and $S$ as $K$ and $S$ increase due to the nature of EDL (suppressing incorrectly assigned evidence). As a result, when comparing In Distribution (ID) and OOD results, it is important that $K_{\mathrm{ID}}$ and $K_{\mathrm{OOD}}$ are equal; something that is not always ensured in practice. We provide an empirical demonstration of how results for AUROC and AUPR can substantially differ when class cardinality between ID and OOD differs by 1, with AUROC differing by as much as 0.318 and AUPR by 0.613 for standard EDL, and AUROC by 0.360 and AUPR by 0.683 for IB-EDL. More concretely, our findings isolate an evaluation artefact: when K differs between ID and OOD, AUROC/AUPR can be artificially inflated without any change in model predictions. We further discuss the evaluation of EDL over causal language models using Multiple-Choice Question-Answer (MCQA) datasets and argue for clearer definitions of ID and OOD in this context. Our primary contribution is an empirical and theoretical demonstration that vacuity-based OOD detection in EDL-fine-tuned LLMs is highly sensitive to uncontrolled differences in evaluated class cardinality.

</details>


### [475] [Automated alignment is harder than you think](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06390)
*Aleksandr Bowkis,Marie Davidsen Buhl,Jacob Pfau,Geoffrey Irving*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Automating alignment research with AI agents risks producing misleading safety assessments and misaligned deployments due to undetected errors in hard-to-supervise tasks; reliable generalisation and scalable oversight are crucial but face novel challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI capabilities rise, automating alignment research could accelerate progress, but it may also produce compounding errors and overconfident mis-safety judgments, risking misaligned deployment.

Method: Analytical argument outlining why automated research could fail: concentrated mistakes under optimization; non-human-like errors; evaluative claims beyond human judgment; high correlation from shared data/training. Discuss potential remedies (generalisation, scalable oversight) and their challenges.

Result: Identifies systematic, undetected errors in AI-generated outputs and the tendency toward overconfident safety assessments; explains why automation could be worse than human-only alignment work; highlights specific failure modes and the need for robust oversight.

Conclusion: Aggressive automated alignment requires agents trained for hard-to-supervise fuzzy tasks; generalisation and scalable oversight are promising but face novel obstacles in automated settings; further work is needed to ensure automated research improves safety rather than misleading assessments.

Abstract: A leading proposal for aligning artificial superintelligence (ASI) is to use AI agents to automate an increasing fraction of alignment research as capabilities improve. We argue that, even when research agents are not scheming to deliberately sabotage alignment work, this plan could produce compelling but catastrophically misleading safety assessments resulting in the unintentional deployment of misaligned AI. This could happen because alignment research involves many hard-to-supervise fuzzy tasks (tasks without clear evaluation criteria, for which human judgement is systematically flawed). Consequently, research outputs will contain systematic, undetected errors, and even correct outputs could be incorrectly aggregated into overconfident safety assessments. This problem is likely to be worse for automated alignment research than for human-generated alignment research for several reasons: 1) optimisation pressure means agent-generated mistakes are concentrated among those that human reviewers are least likely to catch; 2) agents are likely to produce errors that do not resemble human mistakes; 3) AI-generated alignment solutions may involve arguments humans cannot evaluate; and 4) shared weights, data and training processes may make AI outputs more correlated than human equivalents. Therefore, agents must be trained to reliably perform hard-to-supervise fuzzy tasks. Generalisation and scalable oversight are the leading candidates for achieving this but both face novel challenges in the context of automated alignment.

</details>


### [476] [Knowledge Graphs, the Missing Link in Agentic AI-based Formal Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06434)
*Vaisakh Naduvodi Viswambharan,Keerthan Kopparam Radhakrishna,Deepak Narayan Gadde,Aman Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A verification-centric knowledge graph (KG) built from structured IRs across specification, RTL, and tool feedback to guide SVAs generation; a multi-agent workflow performs three refinement loops (syntax repair, CEX-guided correction, coverage-driven augmentation). Evaluated on seven benchmarks, it improves grounding and yields compilable SVAs; formal coverage 78.5–99.4%, but complex temporal/arithmetic reasoning remains challenging for current LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address ambiguous/incomplete specs and RTL details, and to improve spec-to-RTL grounding for SVA synthesis by grounding decisions in a traceable, design-centered context using a KG that integrates IRs and formal-tool feedback.

Method: Construct a KG from IRs extracted from spec, RTL, and tool feedback (diagnostics, CEXs, coverage). Use a multi-agent workflow to query/update KG and generate SVAs; apply three refinement loops: (1) syntax repair guided by tool diagnostics, (2) CEX-guided corrections via trace links, (3) coverage-directed property augmentation.

Result: KG-based grounding improves spec-to-RTL grounding; consistently yields compilable SVAs with low syntax-repair overhead; formal coverage ranges 78.5%–99.4% across seven benchmarks; convergence is design-dependent; complex reasoning challenges persist.

Conclusion: A KG-driven, verification-centric workflow enhances grounding and synthesis of SVAs, but further improvements are needed for intricate temporal/arithmetic reasoning; the approach demonstrates strong potential for scalable, tool-informed SVA generation.

Abstract: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled workflows that generate SystemVerilog Assertions (SVAs) from natural-language specifications, with the potential to accelerate Formal Verification (FV). However, high-quality assertion synthesis remains challenging because specifications are often ambiguous or incomplete and critical micro-architectural details reside in the Register Transfer Level (RTL). Many existing approaches treat the specification and RTL as loosely structured text, which weakens specification-to-RTL grounding and leads to semantic mismatches and frequent syntax failures during formal parsing and elaboration. This work addresses these limitations with a verification-centric Knowledge Graph (KG) constructed from structured Intermediate Representations (IRs) extracted from the specification, RTL, and formal-tool feedback, including syntax diagnostics, Counterexamples (CEXs), and coverage reports. The KG links requirements, design hierarchy, signals, assumptions, and properties to provide traceable, design-grounded context for generation. A multi-agent workflow queries and updates this KG to generate SVAs and to drive three refinement loops: syntax repair guided by tool diagnostics, CEX-guided correction using trace links, and coverage-directed property augmentation. Evaluation across seven benchmark designs indicates that KG-based context retrieval improves specification-to-RTL grounding and consistently produces compilable SVAs with low syntax-repair overhead. The approach achieves formal coverage ranging from 78.5% to 99.4%, though convergence exhibits design dependence with complex temporal and arithmetic reasoning remaining challenging for current LLM capabilities.

</details>


### [477] [SCRuB: Social Concept Reasoning under Rubric-Based Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06444)
*Jamelle Watson-Daniels,Himaghna Bhattacharjee,Skyler Wang,Brandon Handoko,Antonio Li,Anaelia Ovalle,Mahesh Pasupuleti,Candace Ross,Vidya Sarma,Arjun Subramonian,Karen Ullrich,Will van der Vaart,Yijing Xin,Maximilian Nickel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SCRuB provides a rubric-based framework to evaluate social concept reasoning in LLMs, introducing SCRuBEval and SCRuBAnnotations with an ensemble of disciplinary perspectives. Frontier models reportedly outperform human experts in single-turn social reasoning tasks, suggesting an evaluation ceiling for this format.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap in evaluating LLM reasoning about social concepts (norms, culture, institutions). A robust, rubric-based methodology is needed to assess such non-technical reasoning, especially as models act as social agents.

Method: Three-phase pipeline: (1) prompt construction from established sources, (2) responses from experts and models, (3) evaluation using a five-dimensional critical thinking rubric. An ensemble Panel of Disciplinary Perspectives validates the prompts. Datasets released: SCRuBEval (n=4,711 prompts) and SCRuBAnnotations (300 expert responses, 150 expert judgments from 45 PhD-level scholars).

Result: Frontier models outperform human experts across all five rubric dimensions. In 1,170 pairwise judgments, expert evaluators chose model responses first in 80.8% of cases and preferred model responses overall 74.4% of the time.

Conclusion: This work provides the first expert-grounded evidence of evaluation saturation for social concept reasoning in a single-turn format, signaling the need for more nuanced, multi-turn or diverse evaluation tasks to push beyond current capabilities.

Abstract: While many studies of Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning capabilities emphasize mathematical or technical tasks, few address reasoning about social concepts: the abstract ideas shaping social norms, culture, and institutions. This understudied capability is essential for modern models acting as social agents, yet no systematic evaluation methodology targets it. We introduce SCRuB (Social Concept Reasoning under Rubric-Based Evaluation), a framework designed for this setting of task indeterminacy. Our goal is to measure the degree to which a model reasons about social concepts with the depth and critical rigor of a human expert. SCRuB proceeds in three phases: prompt construction from established sources, response generation by experts and models, and comparative evaluation using a five-dimensional critical thinking rubric. To enable generalization of the pipeline, we introduce a Panel of Disciplinary Perspectives ensemble validated against independent expert judges. We release SCRuBEval (n=4,711 evaluation prompts) and SCRuBAnnotations (300 expert-authored responses and 150 expert comparative judgments from 45 PhD-level scholars). Our results show that frontier models consistently outperform human experts across all five rubric dimensions. Across 1,170 pairwise comparisons, expert judges ranked a model response first in 80.8% of judgments and preferred model responses overall 74.4% of the time. Ultimately, this study provides the first expert-grounded demonstration of evaluation saturation for social concept reasoning: the single-turn exam-style format has reached its ceiling for models and humans alike.

</details>


### [478] [PrefixGuard: From LLM-Agent Traces to Online Failure-Warning Monitors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06455)
*Xinmiao Huang,Jinwei Hu,Rajarshi Roy,Changshun Wu,Yi Dong,Xiaowei Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PrefixGuard learns offline, typed StepView adapters to train lightweight prefix monitors over LLM traces, achieving strong AUPRC gains across multiple benchmarks and providing explicit diagnostics (observability ceiling, DFA footprint) to inform deployment; performance and usefulness vary by benchmark, and LLM judges remain weaker under the same protocol.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable online warnings for long-running, tool-using LLM tasks without brittle hand-authored schemas or costly real-time judgments; provide a scalable, monitor-based alternative that can operate on heterogeneous traces.

Method: An offline StepView induction step derives deterministic typed-step adapters from raw trace samples. A supervised monitor is then trained to learn an event abstraction and a prefix-risk scorer from terminal outcomes. The approach is evaluated across WebArena, τ^2-Bench, SkillsBench, and TerminalBench, with post-hoc DFA extraction and diagnostics for when prefix warnings translate into actionable interventions.

Result: Strongest PrefixGuard monitors achieve AUPRC of 0.900/0.710/0.533/0.557 on the four benchmarks, respectively. Across representations, using the strongest backend yields an average improvement of +0.137 in AUPRC over raw-text controls. LLM judges remain substantially weaker under the same prefix-warning protocol. An observability ceiling on score-based AUPRC is derived to separate monitor error from failures lacking evidence in the observed prefix. DFA extraction remains compact on WebArena and τ^2-Bench (29 and 20 states) but grows to 151 and 187 states on SkillsBench and TerminalBench. First-alert diagnostics reveal that strong ranking does not guarantee deployment utility: WebArena can rank well but produce low-false-alarm alerts, while τ^2-Bench and TerminalBench retain more actionable early alerts.

Conclusion: PrefixGuard offers a practical monitor-synthesis recipe with explicit diagnostics for translating prefix warnings into interventions. The framework handles heterogeneous traces via StepView adapters, supports post-hoc DFA analysis for finite-state observability, and provides deployment-aware insights, though performance and utility are benchmark-dependent.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents now execute long, tool-using tasks where final outcome checks can arrive too late for intervention. Online warning requires lightweight prefix monitors over heterogeneous traces, but hand-authored event schemas are brittle and deployment-time LLM judging is costly. We introduce PrefixGuard, a trace-to-monitor framework with an offline StepView induction step followed by supervised monitor training. StepView induces deterministic typed-step adapters from raw trace samples, and the monitor learns an event abstraction and prefix-risk scorer from terminal outcomes. Across WebArena, $τ^2$-Bench, SkillsBench, and TerminalBench, the strongest PrefixGuard monitors reach 0.900/0.710/0.533/0.557 AUPRC. Using the strongest backend within each representation, they improve over raw-text controls by an average of +0.137 AUPRC. LLM judges remain substantially weaker under the same prefix-warning protocol. We also derive an observability ceiling on score-based area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) that separates monitor error from failures lacking evidence in the observed prefix. For finite-state audit, post-hoc deterministic finite automaton (DFA) extraction remains compact on WebArena and $τ^2$-Bench (29 and 20 states) but expands to 151 and 187 states on SkillsBench and TerminalBench. Finally, first-alert diagnostics show that strong ranking does not imply deployment utility: WebArena ranks well yet fails to support low-false-alarm alerts, whereas $τ^2$-Bench and TerminalBench retain more actionable early alerts. Together, these results position PrefixGuard as a practical monitor-synthesis recipe with explicit diagnostics for when prefix warnings translate into actionable interventions.

</details>


### [479] [Beyond Task Success: Measuring Workflow Fidelity in LLM-Based Agentic Payment Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06457)
*Donghao Huang,Joon Kiat Chua,Zhaoxia Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic Success Rate (ASR) is a trajectory-level metric for LLM-based multi-agent payment workflows, decomposing into Transition Recall and Transition Precision to capture agent execution sequences. Applied to HMASP with 18 LLMs and 90k tasks, ASR uncovers checkpoints skipped by some models that TSR and HF1 miss; GPT-4.1 shows hidden shortcuts; GPT-5.2 achieves perfect ASR. ASR-guided prompt refinements and deterministic routing yield large TSR gains (up to 93.8 points). Emphasizes trajectory-level evaluation for regulated domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current metrics (Task Success Rate, Agent Handoff F1-Score) only capture final outcomes or unordered routing. There is a need to evaluate the fidelity of agent execution sequences to detect violations that affect safety/compliance in regulated workflows.

Method: Define a ground-truth expected sequence of agent transitions. Compute Transition Recall (fraction of expected transitions observed) and Transition Precision (fraction of observed transitions that are expected). Aggregate to Agentic Success Rate (ASR) across 18 LLMs evaluated on 90,000 task instances in the Hierarchical Multi-Agent System for Payments (HMASP). Compare ASR with TSR and HF1. Analyze checkpoint behavior in payment checkout. Apply ASR diagnostics to guide prompt refinements and deterministic routing guards, then measure TSR improvements.
